# Thread: light and circadian
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-06-19T01:21:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-32034>

so im trying to get my circadian adjusted but how extreme do you have to go? i mean is sitting in the living room with the lights on at 8pm going to throw you off??

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-19T01:23:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-32035>

If you look outside and it's daylight, then you're fine. If you look outside and it's dark, then you're throwing yourself off.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-19T01:31:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-32036>

I have an iPhone app that tells you what time the sun sets. I use this as my guide. It's called Sun & Moon, I think. I've had it for about 3 years and have used it for other things but it's great for this. Especially when you are out of town and the sunsets at a different time. You can look ahead on the calender and plan accordingly.

### msclman — 2012-06-19T02:03:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-32043>

what im saying is , r ur house lights bad after dark? laptops? tv?

### Melinda — 2012-06-19T02:04:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-32044>

> **msclman;32143 wrote:** so im trying to get my circadian adjusted but how extreme do you have to go? i mean is sitting in the living room with the lights on at 8pm going to throw you off??

When the sunlight fades I put on my orange glasses to block all blue light. We all wear them, kids too. We also put 25w bulbs everywhere. The refrigerator is too bright when we're going after ice for the ice bags so I'm glad to have the blue light blocking glasses. It's made an amazing difference: it makes me sleepy earlier and improved the quality of my sleep.

### Melinda — 2012-06-19T02:11:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-32046>

> **msclman;32152 wrote:** what im saying is , r ur house lights bad after dark? laptops? tv?

Yes, they will interfere with your circadian sleep cycle. I recommend you install f.lux.com on your computer, it changes the screen to be less blue at night and reverses it during the daytime.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-19T02:15:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-32047>

Sorry I misread your question.....still love the Sun/Moon app though. Helps to plan the sunset viewings too!

I go to the back room, turn all the lights off, and put on candles after the sun goes down. If I watch a movie I wear the orange "blue blocker" glasses and I too, have f.lux on the computer.

### chocolate — 2012-07-07T03:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-36020>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21802437](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21802437)

> Seasonal variation of salivary testosterone in men, normally cycling women, and women using hormonal contraceptives.
>
> Stanton SJ, Mullette-Gillman OA, Huettel SA.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Psychology and Neuroscience, Center for Cognitive Neuroscience, Duke University, Durham, NC 27708, USA. [steven.stanton@duke.edu](mailto:steven.stanton@duke.edu)
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Humans' endogenous testosterone concentrations vary over a number of temporal scales, with little known about variation longer than monthly cycles. Past studies of seasonal or circannual variation have principally used male participants and have produced inconsistent results. Thus, little is known about how testosterone concentrations fluctuate throughout the year, whether such variation differs between men and women, and whether there are influences of hormonal contraceptive use. The present study collected saliva samples from a large sample (N=718) of men and women, each collected at one time point within a relatively uniform distribution over a full calendar year. Both men and normally-cycling women displayed seasonal variation in salivary testosterone concentrations, such that testosterone concentrations are maximal in the fall and minimal in the summer. **Notably, normally-cycling women had testosterone concentrations that were over 100% greater at their maximum in fall compared to their minimum in summer.** Women using hormonal contraceptives not only had consistently lower endogenous testosterone concentrations, but also showed a flatter seasonal testosterone profile. The implications for studies of psychology and human behavioral endocrinology are discussed.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-07T03:44:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-and-circadian.1941/post-36023>

Mike...........total blackness. Tough out in NYC bro........but that is how we are designed.

# Thread: If you're considering a consult with Dr. K,
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-youre-considering-a-consult-with-dr-k.1945/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-06-19T04:24:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-youre-considering-a-consult-with-dr-k.1945/post-32067>

Just do it  Seriously! I had my consult this evening and not only did it give me hope that I am actually going to get well and regain my health and life, it's just very reassuring to have someone actually GET it for once. I feel like I have a solid path forward now and know exactly what I need to do. After fighting for 2+ years and feeling like I'm getting nowhere, it's unbelievably refreshing and uplifting to see a light at the end of the tunnel.

I know I have my work cut out for me both in being a "bulldog" to get my doctor's buy-in on my treatment plan and also to aggressively tackle my issues, but having it all spelled out so clearly for me makes it feel like it's truly within reach. I had read some of Dr. K's blogs over and over, but I just couldn't comprehend everything by reading it alone. Having him explain things based on my own lab results and then going back and re-reading the blogs after our talk just made it all click for me. I see my doctor on Thursday and have printed out several pages and written down extensive notes to make sure I get what I want when I walk into that office!

So if you're contemplating a consult and just aren't sure if it's worth it or what you'll get out of it, I would urge you to just go for it. Dr. K was amazing and so very helpful. For the first time in a very long time I have hope

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-19T04:44:45+0300

# Thread: Hunger VS Hunger Smackdown!

**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 13

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-19T07:15:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-32102>

For those of you who've gone a few rounds with HCG (har har) could you perhaps describe the hunger feelings?

I feel what I think is hungry - but it's different than cravings/eat the baby kind of hunger described with too big a dose.

It's more like my actual stomach is cramping up? I am excited when eating time rolls around - I wolf it down with relish, but it's not unbearable.

I just wonder if I could do something different to make it less uncomfortable.

Of course, I did try eating upon waking this a.m. trying to stick to leptin rx - but I wasn't hungry.

So perhaps I just go with the flow and eat lunch and then a dinner.

I'm down 11 lbds total, and 6 from my pre-load weight. Already can see a difference. Totally stoked.

### ColdBren — 2012-06-19T08:29:55+0300

I have been P3 for two weeks and have been hovering at the same weight with a massive amount of calories. I usually have more than 2000 with ratios of 50-70% fat 20-30% protein and 10-20 carbs. ### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-20T21:42:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-32486>

Hola All,

Just had to share. Adjusted dose down yesterday and again today. . .at about 125-135, I think it's the sweet spot! No hunger today  No major tummy empty feeling so far, I had zero problem waiting till about 12:30 for lunch - and I'm not hungry for it in the same way I was prior days.

Losses are happening, I was unable to use the scale this am - but I think everything is in sync and running smoothly.

My version is:

300ish grams of protien a day, with 1 meal seafood - somedays both are seafood. Jack's seafood mantra is in mind.

1 fruit

2 veggies.

I've been doing different veggies/protien for each meal - but I'm preparing for a 5 day vacation and cooking freezing extra portions for that. We are driving so I'm gonna get some dry ice and a cooler and just bring all the food along.

Do you know if having the same veggie/protein for both Lunch and Dinner is something that messes up the process - or is it just unwise because the variety gives you a better nutrient profile?

### colleencoble — 2012-06-21T06:00:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-32622>

Awesome, Cavemam! I was about to tell you that you needed to adjust downward.  Sorry I've been out of pocket. Been in meetings every day at my publishing house. It's my 10th anniversary with Thomas Nelson and it's been crazy busy every minute.

### colleencoble — 2012-06-21T16:38:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-32690>

She's doing 300 grams of weight in protein. 100 extra grams to compensate for the 1 fruit she's eliminated.  It's totally bad to have hunger on hcg and always indicates a problem. In fact, when I can't get someone to stop having hunger, I tell them to stop. You don't want to lose muscle and the wrong kind of fat because you can mess up your metabolism more.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-21T18:23:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-32728>

YUP! 300 grams weighed raw. I'm doing chicken, shrimp, white fish, and beef (2x a week only).

I'm not doing any fancy cooking or trying to make it palatable. It's like a supplement. I have been dieting for about 35 yrs so I'm real good managing the boredom and emotions involved with not eating w the world.

Of course with daily losses it's easy to stay motivated.

Today is day 7 and I'm down 10 lbs from my PRE load weight.

I think having a set time frame (40 injections) really makes a mental difference.

### DarleenMB — 2012-06-23T01:03:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-33055>

That's great. I've just ordered my hcg from that place in Canada mentioned in another thread. Just need to get some bac water and a couple of larger syringes for mixing ... found the water at Amazon, big surprise. I'm so excited Cavemam to see the success you're having.

When I tried it 3 years ago it took me 8 months to drop 20 pounds. Long story. After 18 months of low carb, and 9 months grain free, 8 months leptin RX I'm hoping for a different story.

Are you eating anything other than protein? How about fat intake. Have you dropped it to zero?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-23T01:51:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-33067>

Darleen,

I'm on day 10 now, still going strong. I have dropped almost 13 lbds from my pre-load weight (18 total). I know people like to count the after loaddays weight in losses, but I don't get that!

I'm as zero fat as one can be. I'm doing 300-350 grams per day. (meat weighed raw) of either chicken, shrimp, white fish. I did one beef day, and will do another this week. Don't know if that is a neutral or negative for me since I've only had it once towards the beginning.

I'm trying to do as much seafood as I can stand.

I'm doing one fruit a day and alternating between an apple, strawberries and orange.

I'm having a smallish serving of veggie.

I'm eating a full 500 calories a day, and filling in gaps in calories with protien.

No grains.

I tried doing protien in the a.m. ala reset, but I got too hungry later in the day. For me it's easy to eat lunch/dinner rather than bfast dinner.

I'm going with that.

I've been making lots of dose adjustments. . don't know if that is wise or not.

I got my TOM on day 3 but just kept going. . .and I'm happy so far!

### DarleenMB — 2012-06-23T03:11:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-33084>

### colleencoble — 2012-06-24T23:45:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-vs-hunger-smackdown.1947/post-33354>

It's normal to be cold on hcg.  I don't feel hungry on the right dose. I have a nice "empty" feeling that I love. But I don't feel saturated with food.

# Thread: Children and Circadian Rhythm
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tweety — 2012-06-19T14:57:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32120>

Spinoff from Cavemam's question to Dr. Kruse

So Dr. K answered kids should always follow the light. I am very curious about this one. My kids seem to do this naturally, because they are always up early in the summer and tend to sleep in for the winter months. Now mine are 9 and soon to be.... and a bedtime of 8:30 or 9pm is alright with me. But what about winter? It gets dark around 5:30 or 6 for a while, and I can't see them going to bed so early this coming winter.

I do manage to get them in bed closer to 7:30, but as they get older I think that will be more challenging. I know I have read about some of you going by candlelight once the sun goes down...

What are your plans or experiences? Kids ages? How do they respond?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-19T15:22:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32123>

Sleep for children is all about habit........this is why they invented Teddy Bears. Put them down and give no options.

### tweety — 2012-06-19T22:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32232>

Mine dont really have options! I am just thinking about winter time. We homeschool, so our schedule is usually up to us. However, I am finding the older they get, the later their activities get scheduled. Not by my doing, but by the establishments and studios that provide these activities. Our schedule is more and more being dictated by the outside world.

In fact, my kids are usually in bed in their room and we'll hear our neighbors kids outside until 10 or 11pm throughout the year. Its crazy to me, but I always wonder what my children think about going to sleep listening to their friends outside screaming and running around! They never say anything though, so it clearly doesn't bother them too much.

Actually, my kids have always followed this rhythm, just because we don't usually have anything to be ready for. And I will agree that their dispositions, and moods, and behaviors in general are awesome when we do this. When its interrupted for a late night or super early morning, they become bears!

### nonchalant — 2012-06-20T00:26:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32258>

I think running around screaming outside is nicer in the mornings, when the mosquitos aren't as bad.

(Not to say I run around and scream outside... Well, I'd like to sometimes.)

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-20T00:36:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32261>

My kids really follow the sun as well. But in the dead of winter it is dark here (in NH) around 4 - 4:30pm. There's no way they'll be in bed at that time (they are 11, 9, 6, and 3). I may need to invest in the yellow glasses, but it's not like my 6 year old will be wearing them during her team gymnastics practices. I don't really see a way around it, without withdrawing from their activities.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-20T00:38:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32262>

my youngest is up at 5/530 every day... oldest... we can put her to bed at 8pm, and she'll lie awake and talk until 10. I know its not good for her...just haven't figured out the optimal schedule for both of them yet!!! of course If I go back to work on site w/a client - they'll both be getting up at 5/530 every day!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-20T03:43:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32312>

We're in the middle of winter and it's dark at 5pm. I don't put my kids to bed at 5pm but we don't use any artificial lighting. It's candlelight only (and there is one salt lamp in the hallway). They do their homework by candlelight, brush their teeth and change clothes by candlelight, play games by candlelight, etc. We have dinner much earlier (usually by 4pm) and they are in bed by 7:30 or 8:00 at latest. My kids are 8 and 11. They both wake, on their own without alarms, just before sunrise. They know that come summer they will be able to stay up later if they wish and we'll have longer hours of light. My son has one evening of rugby practice per week - it lasts until 5:30pm. It's outdoors without artificial light. I whisk him home immediately he comes home to a house lit only by candlelight.

As I said on another thread, they complained in the beginning, but we've been doing this for many months now and it's just normal. They both sleep much better, fall asleep quickly and sleep solidly. We have not used the glasses - all screens go off at nightfall and it's strictly candles. They think I'm nuts and don't want their friends to sleep over until it's summertime, but they're going with it. I've also noticed that our evenings are much calmer, which is a bonus.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-20T03:49:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32314>

They think I'm nuts and don't want their friends to sleep over until it's summertime, but they're going with it. I've also noticed that our evenings are much calmer, which is a bonus.

You rock. That is so awesome.

They think I'm nuts and don't want their friends to sleep over until it's summertime, but they're going with it. I've also noticed that our evenings are much calmer, which is a bonus.

### kathylu — 2012-06-20T14:39:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32374>

Bedtime for our 5 year old is in bed no later than 8 pm year round and he's usually asleep by 8:15. He tends to get up shortly after sunrise for between 10 and 11 hours of sleep. He feels so much better (and we do too!) than when his sleep is irregular. He was still demanding a nightlight to keep the monsters away, so I gave in. However, I go in and turn it off shortly after he falls asleep. Once I started doing that, he almost immediately started sleeping an extras 45 minutes to 1 hour each night. Amazing how 4 watts of light can affect your sleep cycle!

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-20T15:12:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32382>

Kathylu

thats funny dd has to have a nightlight... ds will wake up in the middle of the night and turn it off...she doesn't notice, unless she wakes up to go potty... kinda funny...

### tweety — 2012-06-20T20:16:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32461>

I never thought about the nightlight! My kids share a room. My youngest falls asleep immediately and sleeps late. My oldest like Shijin said, goes to bed at 8ish, but sings or talks for a long time. But its still benefical because at least she's in a dark room, not in a room with artificial lights.

I posed your idea KiwiLauren about the candlelights for winter. I got more interest than I thought I would! I think I will have to try this out. We cut out TV a long time ago and their computer usage has always been daytime use, so its just letting go of our household lights.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-20T23:20:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/children-and-circadian-rhythm.1948/post-32505>

> **tweety;32576 wrote:**
>
> I posed your idea KiwiLauren about the candlelights for winter. I got more interest than I thought I would! I think I will have to try this out. We cut out TV a long time ago and their computer usage has always been daytime use, so its just letting go of our household lights.

Definitely not my idea but I was surprised, too, at how relatively easy the switch to no-lightbulbs actually was. I confess that I spend a LOT of time feeling incompetent about how to handle all the dietary issues and potential health concerns I have for both me and my family. I found the circadian cycles thing to be an area that was basically easy to implement, not at all confusing to understand/know what to do, and quick to see benefit from. No supplements need to be purchased, no convincing anyone to get in a 50 degree bathtub, and straightforward. My cup of tea!

# Thread: The Leptin RX is exactly like training a dog..... The Guide Dog Training is the same
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-19T15:28:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32124>

I finally figured out my confusion with everyone's difficulty.

The eating happens on schedule, the addictions get acknowledged and suffered through. You go to restaurants and have only club soda. You drink water only all day long. You go to bed at night and sleep. You take a couple of long perambulations.

Dogs can do it. People train them all the time. It's not that difficult. You just follow the rules.

Or you are dumber than a dog.

The suffering is just a given, knowing there is going to be die off and opioid withdrawals.

I finally realized why I don't understand. I still don't understand. Maybe folks can help me understand.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-19T18:06:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32152>

> **Jack Kruse;32243 wrote:** Think like a dog?

Food. Food ........ Food..... Did I sniff some food? I think someone's in the kitchen..... FOOD!!!

Food...... I'll sit for you if u give me food...... I'll stare at u until u share ur food .... Food. Is it dinner yet?

### chocolate — 2012-06-19T18:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32156>

How about you? did you understand it was your primitive brain ruling you and turning you into a fatass? did you retrain you primitive brain? 6 weeks for your kid.... balls to the wall....I understand that he was a guy and young....but it was cold cold water and he had time on his hands, with nothing to lose but weight. Was it mindset? Did he do something differently from what you are telling us works?

I figure it is like to retrain an untrained or poorly trained dog and you need to separate the situation. The foods are identifiable addictives... Its not like the old days where no one knew what to put in their mouths. Now figure out what an addict does for opioids and alcohol... I know b vitamins are needed for dt's... The opioids are just a nasty stretch, from what I read and was going through getting off wheat.

Its the primitive brain that is needing retrained. Isn't that what the leptin reset is all about? then when there are training difficulties... seek experience.

Please explain the difference?

The whole point of the body image training not working, is because of it being food addiction. Not because they look in the mirror and think they will never be fit. its just like any other addiction. It is scary. I love the renewed commitments and one day at a time. The fat is just side effect unless the hormones are trashed. There are no fat people in pow camps, hashis, bad hormones, or not. Fasting will start ketosis. What will stop it?

### ealachan — 2012-06-19T18:38:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32160>

>

>

> Dogs can do it. People train them all the time. It's not that difficult. You just follow the rules.

> I finally realized why I don't understand. I still don't understand. Maybe folks can help me understand.

Your ending statement of "I finally realized why I don't understand. I still don't understand. Maybe folks can help me understand." doesn't make any sense to me. Are you saying you still don't understand why other people might have bigger issues with sticking to the rules than you seem to? Also, I say this from a place of contructive criticism here - your tone sounds REALLY condescending.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-19T18:50:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32165>

I think what chocolate is trying to get at is what slows the Leptin reset. Simply reducing calories does not always equal weight loss. Read Quelsens's journal and you can see that. We are a system. Changing one thing changes everything. How do you come to your own personal optimal homeostasis when you cannot feasible have labwork every three months? It's trial and error. Paying attention. Taking notes. Praying. And no humans are not as easily trained as dogs because we don't always know what our main motivation is! A dog is motivated by food, to please its owner. Pretty simple. Is it memory? A dog has no ability to make any associations other than immediate. It's it our ability to reason and remember the past that trips us up?

### janagram — 2012-06-19T20:20:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32209>

most of us are made up of many parts...there is an animal part of us that is not so easy to train after running wild through fields of cheetohs all these years, and there is an emotional child part who says,"why can't I ever have dessert?" and there is a wild adolescent part who just needs a few more drinks to really party down, etc., etc, and I think that is why the mental part can be convinced of the right way to live, but getting all the other parts on board is another story. ### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-19T20:56:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32221>

> **Jack Kruse;32306 wrote:** Not trying to get into this mess but how does one access tone on the internet? Am I missing something?

I think you may be, which is good for you though - probably helped you develop into the kind of person who can brush off, get up, and start over - even when people are throwing stones. This isn't a bad thing - but the question might give you some insight into those times when you are misunderstood.

As a highly empathetic person, I evaluate the written word to detect meaning/emotions. It's served me extremely well in this text/email environment for business. Rare is the person that says what they are actually feeling/meaning - so most also tend to not take others at their literal word and interpret based on word choice, quickness of response, punctuation, capitalization, etc.

Having read all of Chocolates posts - I think there is a strong judgemental current (I don't think she would disagree, judging doesn't make an assessment wrong). It's not a negative about chocolate, many people both love and hate that environment.

A placement of word can easily turn the perception of comment from informative to bitchy.

Tailoring what you say, to a particular audience might serve you in some circumstances. It serves me everyday.

One thing I have noted - when Alpha males (JK, lookin at you) do the literal and brutal honesty, it comes across to most as strong and confident - people are drawn to it. Women for sure, but men too.

When it's a woman doing it, it's perceived by both men and women as something entirely different (bitchy etc).

I learned as time went on, that it's not being dishonest to communicate to others in a way that they are MOST likely to understand. Particularly in a one on one situation.

For some (Chocolate I suspect), there is too much else going on and little need to make that adjustment so why bother. I love the internets for that.

But Jack - don't dismiss that people DO read all kinds of tone/emotions into the written words here and everywhere. For everyone (lizard brain?) it's all about THEM, not what you meant.

### Ray — 2012-06-19T22:15:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32231>

> **chocolate;32236 wrote:** ...Dogs can do it. People train them all the time. It's not that difficult. You just follow the rules.
>
>
>
> ... I still don't understand. Maybe folks can help me understand.

Why don't people follow rules? A couple of observations

We are AMAZING at rationalizing, and many (most?) of us have had little recent practice adhering to discipline of any sort. Perhaps military training helps. Perhaps some are just good at trying new things... not just dabbling, but carefully, completely and accurately doing that thing. Anyhow, we all have our baggage that may help or hinder our effort.

Most of us have had a lifetime of improper training as to when, where, how, and why to eat. These old habits die hard. For example, the idea that there are "healthy" foods perhaps leads one to think that more of that good thing is better. Hence, you read on these forums about people are consuming surprising amounts of coconut oil daily or putting a half-stick of butter in their coffee, and wondering why weight loss is elusive. For what its worth, most people can easily disregard satiety cues and "push through the fullness" no matter what they are consuming.

Not everyone has the same ability to evaluate their own behavior. If you are not getting results, perhaps you should find a coach.

Finally, our bodies are in various states of disrepair. This results in different outcomes for various individuals doing the EXACT SAME THING.

best wishes to all...

### chocolate — 2012-06-20T00:29:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32260>

Its not about sticking to the rules.... its just that those are the rules and until you follow them you can't expect certain things. Addiction is absolutely exhausting to me..... but why pretend that it isn't addiction. You a can't even get started until you acknowledge addiction. And then to say fat is the new porn.... you can look at it but don't talk about it.... It makes me nuts. Those 15 passenger vans that keep crashing and killing kids is because no one is talking about weight. In a car, each seat belt is rated for 150 lbs. That's usually the payload. Why can you call out a heroin addict or meth addict or alcoholic for lying or endangering others... why do you think its dangerous to drive sleepy? We have an on board distillery that needs carbs and lots of them to make ethanol.... just because you are consuming alcohol indirectly, its the exact same addiction. I have dealt with nicotine addiction. 30 years ago, a beautiful 300 lb woman told me I was lucky, at least no one could tell by looking at me. I couldn't believe it was possible that she was addicted to food. When at the airport, a lot of folks would go to Vegas, standing in front of me shaking trying not to buy the ticket. One guy thought I was his lucky charm... how bad does that suck? anyway, I saw him one Friday with a bad tooth and I always enjoyed his smile, it was so beautiful. The next Friday the tooth was gone and he was headed to Vegas, trembling and shaking and buying his ticket....he didn't have the cash for a return ticket. I never saw him again. I hoped it was his rock bottom.

I have a house near Griffith Park in LA and one of my tenants' boss has a mission of getting mentally ill and unhelped folks help. My tenant uses his horse trailer and truck to distribute things and he's been all over LA for years. He knows a lot of folks. They show up at our house sometimes when they see the truck. Its definitely a challenge. Some folks hate themselves for being addicts. Then there are folks that are clingy and addicted to being addicts, that way little can be expected of them. Those are the ones that cost the effort of a dozen and will gladly jump line to get it. I guess its the victim mentality rather than the fight that wears on me. I love a good fight and love happy endings. I guess I landed amongst a different sort. Maybe things aren't easier for me, maybe I'm a harder fighter. I guess that makes you normal and me not. How does that make everyone feel? Is that a tone? lol...

And by the way, dogs are motivated by much more than food. And they love and have a sense of humor. Or at least mine do.

### chocolate — 2012-06-20T00:41:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32263>

I reread everything in this thread about the dogs.

We socialized a guide dog puppy and trained her. I honestly believe people should have a dog to train before they have kids. The world would be a better place. Its really true, you can go to a restaurant and the dog will behave better than folks kids. It makes me wonder how much time they actually spend with them. I have been in restaurants where people make those remarks. The dog would eat the same time twice a day the food bowl comes up after eating and water was down always. The dog was motivated by much more than food. When we would go places the ziploc of food was in my purse and she drank from a sport top bottle. Maybe someone that has experience should speak up here. The training manual is the leptin reset. The dog isn't hungry, it just want to chew. The dog needs to walk at the same time everyday. There were even puppy pool parties where the puppies went in and the kids didn't.

### chocolate — 2012-06-20T04:04:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-leptin-rx-is-exactly-like-training-a-dog-the-guide-dog-training-is-the-same.1949/post-32323>

No AN, not even ED.... that was the puzzler... 123 and me helped. She had a good doctor, just a preexisting misdiagnosis and no labs to imply otherwise... Born with oddball Asian innerds and we never questioned our organ status.... who would?

I don't think its much harder than pushing away from the table.

It isn't distaste .... There is an addiction involved and the evidence is the appearance... the mirror test reflects what everyone else sees. Year after year after year there is definitely something. And everything emerging about the human micro-biome implies that it doesn't take long to balance out (two weeks, just like ct). ( think that's coming up soon... which kinds of gut flora flourish under what diet kind of a thing, a hint was mentioned) It takes discipline and a prepared environment. After recognizing addiction. Or if it isn't that... what is it when you eat food you profess to not want or desire the consequences of?

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/06/this-is-your-brain-on-no-self-control-12307](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/06/this-is-your-brain-on-no-self-control-12307)

> However, the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC)—the part of the brain that manages self-control and says, “I really want to do the dumb thing, but I should overcome that impulse and do the smart thing”—fires with less intensity after prior exertion of self-control.
>
>
>
> He said that loss of activity in the DLPFC might be the person’s self-control draining away. The stable activity in the ACC suggests people have no problem recognizing a temptation. Although they keep fighting, they have a harder and harder time not giving in.
>
>
>
> **Which would explain why someone who works very hard not to take seconds of lasagna at dinner winds up taking two pieces of cake at desert. The study could also modify previous thinking that considered self-control to be like a muscle. Hedgcock says his images seem to suggest that it’s like a pool that can be drained by use then replenished through time in a lower conflict environment, away from temptations that require its use.
>
>
> Hedgcock says the study is an important step in trying to determine a clearer definition of self-control and to figure out why people do things they know aren’t good for them. One possible implication is crafting better programs to help people who are trying to break addictions to things like food, shopping, drugs, or alcohol. Some therapies now help people break addictions by focusing at the conflict recognition stage and encouraging the person to avoid situations where that conflict arises. For instance, an alcoholic should stay away from places where alcohol is served.**

What's the not simple part? fighting yourself? or forgiving yourself? I'm pity partied out, self or otherwise. Call it what it is, then you can fix it. Addictions are addictions.

# Thread: Weaning off of HC - looking for feedback
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaning-off-of-hc-looking-for-feedback.1953/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### vkiernan — 2012-06-19T21:00:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaning-off-of-hc-looking-for-feedback.1953/post-32222>

I am weaning off of my HC so I can have some tests run to see why I have adrenal fatigue in the first place. I am wondering what sort of problems I might run into and looking for feedback.

I am trying to wean off of 32.5 mg per day after having taken it for 2.5 months. This is the start of my second week and I am a little tired yesterday and today. But, I physically worked pretty hard this weekend and that usually takes its toll. My weekend was energetic though and that is after removing 12.5 mg. every day last week.

I think CT helping my adrenals. I wake up every day with the light and am tired by 9 every night. This is pretty consistent and my sleep is getting better and better.

Thanks all.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-19T22:05:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaning-off-of-hc-looking-for-feedback.1953/post-32230>

I weaned off 15mg HC starting in January. I had been taking it for a couple of years, and decided it was time to see if I still needed it. The advice I read was to drop the dose slowly, like 2.5mg/week, and watch for symptoms of adrenal problems along the way. I did it much faster - I took 5 weeks to get off. I never noticed a problem. I waited 2 weeks and then did the adrenal stress index saliva test. My results looked reasonable, not great, but better than where they were. I was pleased, and started with DHEA and rhodiola to support them.

However, Shortly after that I ran into massive thyroid dosing problems that I am still dealing with. If I remember correctly, you take Armour as well, right? My Armour dose was not sufficient, but upping the dose brought on adrenal fatigue symptoms (low blood pressure, high heart rate, orthostatic hypotension, feeling awful all the time). It took me a good long while to get the tests run and figure out what was going on. I had been taking 2 grains of Armour, and now I am up to 3.5. My doctor told me that with continued DHEA, and possibly pregnenolone supplementation, I may be able to drop the Armour dose a little. We'll see. It's difficult for me to tell if the fast heartrate is due to adrenal fatigue, or too much Armour. It is getting better though, with continued DHEA. I don't know if this helps any, but I thought I would share...

### freshveggies — 2012-06-19T22:20:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaning-off-of-hc-looking-for-feedback.1953/post-32234>

I might have missed something , but what test can show you why you have adrenal fatigue? I know saliva testing can show you the cortisol curve. I am just interested as I have never had a doctor tell me that they can show me why I have adrenal fatigue.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-19T22:59:44+0300

adrenal stress index

### vkiernan — 2012-06-19T23:23:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weaning-off-of-hc-looking-for-feedback.1953/post-32246>

You are correct. Jack is talking about the test to confirm adrenal fatigue. I don't believe there is a test persay to know exactly what is causing it but a lot of things one can rule out or know that is contributing. In my case, it could be just too much stress over the course of my life. I also read where if your mother is stressed, that can pass off to you as a baby. I was baby #6 and my mother was stressed so who knows. She is checking my pituitary and ACTH. I am also doing the GI Effects test for my gut. Dr. Kharasastan (sp.) has a great book on adrenals and the thyroid. He talks about the gut usually being the issue for dragging down the adrenals which in turn drag down the thyroid. In my consult with Dr. K., he told me to check the gut as well to see if that was culprit. I am digging at this point shall we say. Just making sure there aren't other things going on that I don't know about.

I highly recommend that book. I think it is called "Why do I still have thyroid symptoms when my lab results are normal." I will verify tonight and edit if need be. It really puts things into perspective for me. Why everything happens and lots of examples. Not sure where Jack stands on it but it does similate a lot of what he says; just puts it into laymen's terms for people like me.

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-06-19T23:48:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keep-stressing-over-high-crp-when-feeling-healthy.1954/post-32250>

My CRP labs are consistently in the red warning light range and have only gone up now as I've implemented many of the principals I have learned here.

10/24/11 - 3.54

3/15/12 - 3.34

6/14/12 - 6.93

I have expressed this concern with doctor. He also just ran Fibrinogen and Homocysteine:

Fibrinogen - 329 (240-450)

Homocysteine - 10 (4.0-15.4)

I have minimal knowledge of these labs, but understand elevated homocysteine is associated with atherosclerosis (heart health is a concern of mine based on family history) and fibrinogen is a blood clotting factor associated with heart attack.

Is there a genetic component here as I feel very healthy right now. What gives?

### kc_boxerman@yahoo.com — 2012-06-20T00:49:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/keep-stressing-over-high-crp-when-feeling-healthy.1954/post-32265>

I will do reasonable testing to try solve this, but doing boat loads of testing that my doctor doesn't even understand because of a hypothetically bad number on a piece of paper based on research that is probably corrupt while diverting these resources from my family who I care more about than myself while I feel fine so others can say just do everything better just seems over the top.

\- I am a believer in a quantified self and don't mean to be as snarky as that sounds

I thought the CRP might be associated with the high reverse T3 problem I learned of in October 2011. However, I have cleared this out and lowered thyroglobulin antibody and throid peroxidase by removing T4 from my thyroid treatment (first diagnosed hypothyroid at age 7).

I have been tracking some other lab values - but 90% of the bagel eating, canola oil drinking Joe Schmo's off the street probably don't have CRP near this high from what I gather.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-20T00:52:15+0300

I'm not worried about my doctor, he is trying to help me with my concerns (he did these two additional tests and did rT3 testing before I knew what it was, so he's trying to help). You've said high CRP can be artifactual. I'm curious when that is. Do these two labs address that? Are there really 'boatloads' of tests that need to be done to understand this number when I feel good?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-20T02:12:50+0300

Cortisol tests were saliva,

Thanks much,

### Destiny — 2012-06-22T22:28:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-going-to-a-new-functional-medicine-doc-thur.1956/post-33016>

> **janagram;33091 wrote:** Oh boy did I pay out of pocket.....! But, fine. I've got a packet of tests to go have done now. She took an extensive inventory of my physical health and diet, etc....so I feel like I won't be just flying by the seat of my pants so much, tho, she actually altered my supps but little. She told me to go get "activated" B supplements, and to add a carotene supp.....and not to take mag malate before bed (apparently it's energizing, which I don't need..) and she will zero in on my thyroid after tests.

Good luck with the doctor, hopefully, it is the one!

I heard that mag malate should be taken before bed because it makes you sleepy. According to what I found, it is OK to take it anytime.

How to take magnesium

Take your first dose of magnesium when you wake up in the morning and the last dose at bedtime. Magnesium is most deficient in the early morning and late afternoon. Most people find magnesium as good as a sleepin pill to help them get a good night’s rest.

Magnesium malate combines magnisium with malic acid, a weak organic acid found in vegetables and fruit, especially apples. The weak bond with magnesium makes it readily soluble in the body. Malic acid is a key component of several energy making chemical reactions in the body. Researchers have used magnesium malate succesfully to treat the chronic fatigue, pain and imsonia of fibromyalgia. Dimagnesium malate increases the amount of magnesium available to the body; it has the same properties as magnesium malate.

Magnesium can be taken with or without meals, but it is preferable to take it between meals for better absorption. Magnesium requires stomach acid to be absorbed. After a full meal, your stomach acid is busy digesting food and may not be avaialable to helpl absorb magnesium. Also magnesium is an alkaline mineral and acts like an antiacid: taken with meals, it may neutralize stomach acid and impair digestion.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-22T22:30:55+0300

# Thread: Hearing news with new ears....I know it's not just me,what about you guys?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TerrierMom — 2012-06-20T02:30:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32292>

When you hear a friend has newly diagnosed AI, or thyroid, or gastric issues.... Or when you hear about celebrities on the news (today they talked about Sharon Osbourne breaking down over her sons MS diagnosis...I live in LA so celebrities ARE the news, no escaping it)....

Do you just wanna send them all to Jacks site??!? If it's someone I know and they are open, I do. I feel almost like, I would never forgive myself for not saying something when I think there are answers here. I even sent one of my favorite bloggers here, hoping she finds some hope and help (if you read the hilarious bloggess.com posts you will know about the multiple AI and depression/anxiety wackiness she has going on, it is in there interspersed with the hilarity). She is the only person I don't know personally that I sent here so far but honestly... If people are looking for answers and come here and don't find anything to help them, they may be brain dead (or not really looking)

The other thing is reading those ladies magazine you get in the super Arlen, each with a diet of the week or amazing weight loss story. This week one ran a story about the wonders of magnesium. It got some stuff right and left other stuff out.... Everything I see or hear or read about health and diet just pops out at me now. That, and anything anthropological or KT related.... I'm taping things off science and history channel like crazy, still need to catch up on one about theropods and feathered dinosaurs.....

I know I can't be the only one seeing the world through an entirely different lens... What do YOU do with all this knowledge you are soaking up here...we can't keep it to ourselves! I'd like to hear how you approach folks and where you point them to begin, based on the issues they have.... Kinda like "start here if you have adrenal issues" or "MS" or...

It's so hard because it's all so interconnected (the quilt). The info here is mind blowing

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-20T02:51:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32297>

Oh, this happens to me all the time now!

Unfortunately, starting with my parents, no one wants to listen. My dad's cardiologist (dad has had quadruple bypass and multiple stents) continues to tell him to eat a "heart-healthy diet of whole grains and low fat". So my dad is "doing his best" by switching to whole wheat pasta and 5 days a week heart-pounding stairmastering. My mom, the diabetic, insists that "Diabetics NEED carbs", so she eats her 5 meals a day "to keep her metabolism going" with whole-grain crackers. Oh wait, now she's on NutriSystem, so she's eating their packaged garbage. But she's lost 15 lbs!! she will say. Sheesh.

I'm a single woman, and I recently had a date with a guy who turned out to be an Endocrinology professor at our medical school. He told me that his sister has MS so bad she can no longer work. I wanted so badly to point him to Dr. Terry Wahls' video and to Dr. Kruse's site, but I didn't want to appear first-date snarky.  I could tell, when I started to bring things up, he was not interested.

But here's some good news: Today, I saw a new OB/GYN from PaleoPhysiciansNetwork.com. A-w-e-s-o-m-e. She GETS it!!! Half her staff went to PaleoFX (I was there!!), and she started naming off all the tests she wants to run...basically a Dr. Kruse list! I almost cried.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-20T05:34:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32347>

I get it! I have told three friends of mine about Dr. Kruse and shared his site. One looked at it but has a comprehension problem so she couldn't understand the blogs. Plus she's a ultra marathon athlete (college scholarship) and at 52 looks 60 in the face, 30 in her body and feels like crap but she wasn't interested. Another I never heard back from and the third who has had a lumpectomy for breast cancer and is easily 50# overweight said it wasn't for her. I feel saddened by the ignorance of people but realize it is not their path, at least not right now....maybe later.

I have told numerous strangers too but who knows if anyone comes on board. I will keep trying to spread the message and if people are interested they will search on their own. For now I have one convert and my BF has another. His started the LRx and is doing CT! He is totally into it. I'm happy that at least some sharing has turned into success.

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-20T13:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32364>

> **PrimalPam;32411 wrote:**
>
> But here's some good news: Today, I saw a new OB/GYN from PaleoPhysiciansNetwork.com. A-w-e-s-o-m-e. She GETS it!!! Half her staff went to PaleoFX (I was there!!), and she started naming off all the tests she wants to run...basically a Dr. Kruse list! I almost cried.

Well that's awesome!!! I'm lucky that so far hubby is on board and my parents are kinda sorta following due to our results.... Mom says her dr tells her she will never be able to stop her statins....I know that's not true but not entirely sure how to get her there. She's got Kaiser HMO as her plan (in her 70's) so it will likely be very difficult to get her the right testing through them!!

Frustrating to see people pass it by but as we all know this requires radical change in thinking and beliefs and some people are not there yet...I suspect JK has been feeling this way for years now so we just gotta keep working with those who are open and hope for the best for the rest

### kathylu — 2012-06-20T14:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32371>

I'm always amazed by the amount of discipline people have when they latch onto something that works! Keep sharing your results and the message will spread, even if more slowly than we would like. I keep telling my sister-in-law about the ice baths...of course she thinks I'm crazy! However, the other day she mentioned that she had heard people talking about cold baths and icing on the radio, so maybe there is something to it? I said "Seems so..." Baby steps.

### Susan M. — 2012-06-20T18:52:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32436>

I also have trouble with comprehension. Sometimes I have to read a post a couple of times. I used to read all of the comments that are posted as well, but being as it is summer where I live, I am outside more than I am indoors for the time being so my education (via this site) has slowed down a little bit. I have had no luck at all in persuading anyone to look into this at all. None of my children or their spouses are interested, and one has a degree in genetics and another degree in biology. They all just kind of think I am a bit "wacked". But I am just mom, the one who never finished college. I have improved my health immensely and have lost 46 pounds but I still have absolutely no sway.

My aunt and uncle finally believe me that I have an intolerance to wheat because they heard it on The O'Reilly Show! Sigh.

I really love this site because it fully engages my desire to learn. I never in a million years thought I would be interested in bio-chemistry and anthropology though!

Susan M.

### Inger — 2012-06-21T11:05:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32637>

I see you.. I see you!!!! Crazy, I have to tell all people I meet about it! LOL

I do it carefully though not to offend, and if I feel the other have no interest I don't go on. But my parents and my brother that lives here too wants to do all this! They do too, partly, but they have so weak willpower and are so connected to their addictions and habits its is hard to break for them - it is far from perfect. They kind of need me to do all for them but I cannot - I have to care for myself first. But when I have the possibilities, I cook and do things like shutting off the lights at night and light candles instead.. and they love it all! It is kind of beeing in childcare.. haha..They also love to hear when I tell the news I read on Kruses blog for sure. They have no doubt it is all true! But then - it is also totally logical!

### SeaHorse — 2012-06-21T14:22:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32646>

I struggle with this a bit, because there is a real desire to reach out and help...but no one can be helped who isn't ready. The lead by example is what I aim for...but I'm also giving information about the website if asked and sometimes I just succumb and start raving a bit....that doesn't get me very far. I have one friend who is really interested and I trust she will open her mind and reap all the benefits.

Part of becoming healthier for me is to discern the difference between "fixing" and "showing." One leads to unhealthy worry and wasted energy and the other is how I want to teach.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-22T00:08:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hearing-news-with-new-ears-i-know-its-not-just-me-what-about-you-guys.1957/post-32816>

At my new doctor this week, she said it's all about telling one person at a time and living by example.

She's 48 and looks 38. She used to be an extreme runner (marathons), but she cut way back on the running and now does HIIT's instead. People thought she was crazy. She hacks herself all the time and adjusts her micronutrients accordingly. She said that when you look better than others your age, people will ask you what you are doing.

I told her about Dr. Kruse's opening PaleoFX speech: We are each a candle, and we need to light the other candles. She loved it.

Unfortunately still, most look at me like I'm nuts when I tell them how much fat I eat (sat. & MCT). I can even show them 5 years of cholesterol, showing lower tri's, higher HDL's, and they are still like...but aren't your arteries hardening? Makes me sigh.

BHRT is just one bullet in my arsenal... as is CT, epi-paleo, paying attention to light cycles, and seasonal eating.

from LEF Ref range

Glucose, serum 85 mg/dl 65-99

Uric acid, serum 5.1mg/dl 2.5-7.1

### kathylu — 2012-06-20T14:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-hack-my-labs.1962/post-32373>

From wikipedia: Osteocalcin is secreted solely by osteoblasts and thought to play a role in the body's metabolic regulation and is pro-osteoblastic, or bone-building, by nature.\[3\] It is also implicated in bone mineralization and calcium ion homeostasis. Osteocalcin acts as a hormone in the body, causing beta cells in the pancreas to release more insulin, and at the same time directing fat cells to release the hormone adiponectin, which increases sensitivity to insulin.\[3\]

Your iron looks high, you have a leaky gut (cholesterol numbers) and OMG...do something about your vitamin D level fast. I'll let others with more experience talk about thyroid function.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-20T14:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-hack-my-labs.1962/post-32376>

As Kathylu said, your cholesterol is bad due to your low hdl and trigs. Those numbers should be reversed. Probably pregnenolone steal going on. Your trigs are that of a carb eating person from what I know. Your D is extremely low - take D3 10000 plus K2 with a mk4 subfraction and A. They all work synergistically. Read Dr. K's blog on supplements. Your FT3 is too low. Looks like your testosterone is high for a female but don't quote me on that one. Was your CRP a highly sensitive one? It is very high too. You have a lot of inflammation going on. I don't see an RT3; did you get one? I am betting it is high too. You are probably not converting T4 to T3 but to RT3 instead. I would think you don't have an amazing amount of energy either.

Are you doing CT? Eating a lot of coconut oil? Upping your 03's and laying off the 06's? Staying off of carbs? Following the circadian rhythm and getting good sleep? These things will go along way to help you with those numbers. It doesn't happen overnight but it does happen. Good luck on your journey Joann.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-20T16:27:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-hack-my-labs.1962/post-32396>

Vitamin D real low, your cholesterol might be high b/c of your weight loss yet I wouldn't like the combination of high LDL, low HDL, high triglycerides and real high hs CRP.... that doesn't sound like a good combination. Yes to leaky gut.

Osteocalcin is a measure to assess bone formation status..... haven't found any ranges for that except for "in the teens". I'm pretty sure this one depends on your history and whether you have osteoporosis or are at danger of it. I think K2 gets it higher for more bone building.

### Owl — 2012-06-23T08:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-hack-my-labs.1962/post-33123>

Yes, they expect older people to lose more bone, hence the higher limit.

Same Mayo article: "Osteocalcin levels are increased in metabolic bone diseases with increased bone or osteoid formation including osteoporosis, osteomalacia, rickets, hyperparathyroidism, renal osteodystrophy, thyrotoxicosis, and in individuals with fractures, acromegaly, and bone metastasis."

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-05T16:57:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-hack-my-labs.1962/post-49588>

> **Joann;50092 wrote:** Anyone know about Alkaline Phosphatase?
>
>
>
> Just had a blood test from gyn which was over range. 33-130 mine is 137 from Quest labs
>
> Previous test in June was 131 but range was 25-165 from Labcorp
>
> doc had me come in for abnormal test....freaked me out. but then said to redo in 3 months ????. thinking it something with liver. wondering if antibiotics could have something to do with Alkaline Phosphatase....2 months ago.
>
> Thanks from a nervous wreck
>
>
> [http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/analytes/alp/tab/test?gclid=CLTj8dzLnrICFSVgMgodiEwAig](http://http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/analytes/alp/tab/test?gclid=CLTj8dzLnrICFSVgMgodiEwAig)
>
>
>
> The alkaline phosphatase test (ALP) is used to help detect liver disease or bone disorders. In conditions affecting the liver, damaged liver cells release increased amounts of ALP into the blood. This test is often used to detect blocked bile ducts because ALP is especially high in the edges of cells that join to form bile ducts. If one or more of them are obstructed, for example by a tumor, then blood levels of ALP will often be high.
>
>
>
> Any condition that affects bone growth or causes increased activity of bone cells can affect ALP levels in the blood. An ALP test may be used, for example, to detect cancers that have spread to the bone or to help diagnose Paget's disease. This test may also sometimes be used to monitor treatment of Paget's disease or other bone conditions, such as vitamin D deficiency.
>
>
>
> If ALP results are increased but it is not clear whether this is due to liver or bone disease, then tests for ALP isoenzyme tests may be done to determine the cause. A GGT test and/or a test for 5'-nucleotidase may also be done to differentiate between liver and bone disease. GGT and 5'-nucleotidase levels are increased in liver disease but not bone disorders.

in %status, to be good, you should place within (-0.25 and +0.25) and have only green color. 50% is already way too much.

Each and every one of your individual Omega3 and omega 6 fatty acids

is way out of line.

You have way too much GLA

that should have trickled down to AA

no, you have very little AA

You have a lots of EPA

that should have been converted to DHA

no you have very little DHA

your Desaturases are not really working

DHA and AA are two of the most important within O3/O6 family (and you are severely lacking on both).

There is more omegas (fatty acids) than just Omega3 and Omega6, they are in not better shape, but I am not able to form opinion because you do not have enough information on that test.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-13T02:29:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-hack-my-labs.1962/post-51306>

desaturases compete for omega 3's and 6's. The biggest issue is the proper amounts in a dietary context. This is why the optimal range is tight for a human with a big brain......3:1 or 6:1 you go over or under you aint optimal. This is why Wolf's book fish oil calculator was a big problem for me when the book came out. But you get a lot of bad press when you point out the mistakes in paleo land.

>
>
>
> If you overwhelm the pathway with an oversupply of Omega-3's that dominate the delta 6 desaturase enzymes, and omit or don't take more Omega-6 than Omega-3 you're IM-balancing the system by not providing the base substrate oils for synthesis of all the eicosanoids derived from Omega-6 on that side of the chart & pathway, including the body's most powerful anti-inflammatory agent PGE1 prostaglandin and PGI2 prostacyclin which is the body's most powerful anti thrombotic and anti platelet adhesive which works as a blood thinner & helps maintain proper blood viscosity, and therefore pressure, as well as blood circulation velocity (speed).
>
>
>
> Omega 6/3 balancing requires stopping the intake of ALL adulterated, oxidized TRANS molecule forms of Omega-6 (TRANS fats) because TRANS molecule fats disrupt the body's entire hormone production & regulation system by blocking the absorption & utilization of all the essential fatty acids the hormone system requires, and then supplementing with the correct ratio of omega-6 & omega-3 at around a 1 to 1 ratio up to a 2.5 or 3 to 1 ratio.

### JanSz — 2012-09-19T16:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-hack-my-labs.1962/post-52686>

The Fatty Acid Profile: Dietary Intake and Beyond

[http://www.metametrix.com/files/learning-center/articles/The-Fatty-Acid-Profile-Dietary-Intake-and-Beyond.pdf](http://http://www.metametrix.com/files/learning-center/articles/The-Fatty-Acid-Profile-Dietary-Intake-and-Beyond.pdf)

Figure 4. This pattern was found in a 54 year old male who, prior to specimen collection, had been eating **salmon 4-5 times per week

for 1.5 months plus supplementing with 2,000 mg of fish oil and 600 mg of flax oil per day. All four members of omega-3 fatty acids are

found in the fifth quintile. Fifth quintile linoleic acid with arachidonic acid in the first quintile shows a significant suppression of delta-6

desaturation. The AA/EPA ratio is in the first quintile, but not yet 2 standard deviations from the mean. However, continuing this level of

omega-3 oil supplementation can further suppress AA formation to generate a very low AA/EPA ratio. There are no clear adverse clinical

consequences at this stage, but the data provides a warning of the potential for crossing into clinically suppressed** AA status and various

consequences of sustained high peroxisome status, so supplementation of omega-3 enriched oils should be monitored.

> consequences at this stage, but the data provides a warning of the potential for crossing into clinically suppressed** AA status and various

> consequences of sustained high peroxisome status, so supplementation of omega-3 enriched oils should be monitored.

I really like the info in link above!!!

from what I can make out: my EPA: DGLA = .575

AA : EPA = 14.04

LA : DGLA = 40.55

I dont know what Triene :Tetraene means?

also thinking out loud......eating grass fed meat $$$, and pastured eggs $$$ and butter $$$ is different in AA? if eating grain fed meat maybe more AA?

I could be over thinking this

Joann

### CarolMorris — 2012-06-20T21:14:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-hackers-help-please.1966/post-32473>

I'm such a lurker, I can't get enough of this. Thank you all for your input, I wannabee one of you, first I have to figure out where to put my postsI didn't even know the difference between a blog and a forum--yikes. I see most of you are so young, I'm 61 female, menopausal for about 18 months.

Sorry this is so looong, I have so much to learn...

Before Leptin Rx:

Desperate for sleep

Food sensitivities up the gazoo, dairy, eggs, pork, nitrates, nitrites, red dye #40, yellow dye #5..on and on

(Anaphylactic to shellfish, mollusks and swordfish)

Inflammation, swollen hands and feet, bloating, skin, welts and itching

leaky gut, this took me awhile to figure out, I had no â€œtypicalâ€ symptomsâ€”never any pain or diarrhea

30-35 lbs overweight, skinny kid, within 10lbs of ideal â€˜til menopause, but always thought that was â€œfatâ€ (Twiggy generation)

Intense sweet and carb cravings as long as I can remember

Several borderline glucose tests 109-111â€”I refuse to get diabetes!!!

On metformin for 7 years for Insulin Resistance(supposedly as a preventative)

Always told I donâ€™t eat enough(avg. 1200-1400 cal)â€”I rarely ever over ate on proteins & fats, with sweets once I ate a bite there was never enough

Fat phobic(dietary)â€”seriously scared to eat any fats!!

Could not get my waist back or lose more than 10 lbs even for my daughterâ€™s wedding!

fatigue

10 weeks into Leptin Rx:

Have lost 4 Inches in my waist, 2 on leptin reset!!!

Maintained 12lb weight loss( on WB prior to LeptinRxâ€”thought I would gain on reset, but am maintaining).

NO cravings of any kind & eat lots of fat!! First time in my life that I can remember!!

Skip lunch most days or have bone broth or coffee with 5TBS coconut oil

Off metformin since 4/15 fasting bg in 80â€™s, non fasting rarely above 90 â€“yeah!!! I think the metformin may have contributed to lousy b-12

swollen hands and feet, bloating, skin, welts and itching---gone!

leaky gutâ€”I think is healing, was able to increase coconut oil to 15tbs a day with no negative effects

sweat pattern changing-not so sure about this, but I am warm

still desperate for sleep, although I have had a few nights of 6-8 hours

fatigue mostly gone except for really bad sleep nights

Am I correct that leptin Rx will heal the rt3 problem?

I will see my PCP in a couple of weeks and she said we would learn together 

I was not taking any supplements. Iâ€™ve added:

10,000 VitD3

500 kelp,

Fish oil-will try again(ripped up stomach)

Probiotic (225 billion)

Buffered vit C with mg, cal.pot

Mag malate

Labs 5/2/12---Started Leptin Rx and Keto Paleo April 12th.

WB since Feb 4,

hsCRPâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦3.69 mg/L (0.00-3.00 mg/L)

Leptinâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦. 32.5 ng/ml (8.0-38.9 )

Cortisol AM (serum)â€¦14.91 (4.3-22.40 mcg/dl))

rT3â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦.30 ng/dl (11-32)

fT3 to rT3 ratio â€¦â€¦â€¦.7.7

TSHâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦..1.035â€¦(2.2-4.0mIU/mL) taking 90mg Armour

T4 freeâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦..81â€¦..(0.7-1.5 ng/dL)

T3 freeâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦.2.3â€¦..(2.3-4.2 pg/mL)

B-12â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦..385 pg/ml (up from 278)

MMAâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦. 239 nmol/L (87-318)

Hba1câ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦.5.6â€¦â€¦(130 mg/dl)

TRIGLYCERIDESâ€¦â€¦â€¦95â€¦â€¦.(30-149 mg/dl)

HDLâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦..69

ESTRADIOLâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦....517 ( daily 1mg estradiol)

PROGESTERONEâ€¦â€¦..26.69ng/ml (daily 200mg prometrium)

TESTOSTERONEâ€¦â€¦..23ng/dl(2-45)

CORTISOL (AM)â€¦â€¦.14.91(4.3-22.40)

VIT.D (25-HYD)â€¦â€¦â€¦...45(>30 ng/mL)

DHEA-Sâ€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦â€¦..106 ug/dL (246-911)

### JanSz — 2012-06-21T21:21:03+0300

**CarolM;32588 wrote:** I'm such a lurker, I can't get enough of this. Thank you all for your input, I wannabee one of you, first I have to figure out where to put my postsI didn't even know the difference between a blog and a forum--yikes. I see most of you are so young, I'm 61 female, menopausal for about 18 months.

Sorry this is so looong, I have so much to learn...

Before Leptin Rx:

Desperate for sleep

Food sensitivities up the gazoo, dairy, eggs, pork, nitrates, nitrites, red dye #40, yellow dye #5..on and on

(Anaphylactic to shellfish, mollusks and swordfish)

Inflammation, swollen hands and feet, bloating, skin, welts and itching

leaky gut, this took me awhile to figure out, I had no “typical” symptoms—never any pain or diarrhea

30-35 lbs overweight, skinny kid, within 10lbs of ideal ‘til menopause, but always thought that was “fat” (Twiggy generation)

Intense sweet and carb cravings as long as I can remember

Several borderline glucose tests 109-111—I refuse to get diabetes!!!

On metformin for 7 years for Insulin Resistance(supposedly as a preventative)

Always told I don’t eat enough(avg. 1200-1400 cal)—I rarely ever over ate on proteins & fats, with sweets once I ate a bite there was never enough

Fat phobic(dietary)—seriously scared to eat any fats!!

Could not get my waist back or lose more than 10 lbs even for my daughter’s wedding!

fatigue

10 weeks into Leptin Rx:

Have lost 4 Inches in my waist, 2 on leptin reset!!!

Maintained 12lb weight loss( on WB prior to LeptinRx—thought I would gain on reset, but am maintaining).

NO cravings of any kind & eat lots of fat!! First time in my life that I can remember!!

Skip lunch most days or have bone broth or coffee with 5TBS coconut oil

Off metformin since 4/15 fasting bg in 80’s, non fasting rarely above 90 –yeah!!! I think the metformin may have contributed to lousy b-12

swollen hands and feet, bloating, skin, welts and itching---gone!

leaky gut—I think is healing, was able to increase coconut oil to 15tbs a day with no negative effects

sweat pattern changing-not so sure about this, but I am warm

still desperate for sleep, although I have had a few nights of 6-8 hours

fatigue mostly gone except for really bad sleep nights

Am I correct that leptin Rx will heal the rt3 problem?

I will see my PCP in a couple of weeks and she said we would learn together 

I was not taking any supplements. I’ve added:

10,000 VitD3

500 kelp,

Fish oil-will try again(ripped up stomach)

Probiotic (225 billion)

Buffered vit C with mg, cal.pot

Mag malate

Labs 5/2/12---Started Leptin Rx and Keto Paleo April 12th.

WB since Feb 4,

hsCRP………………3.69 mg/L (0.00-3.00 mg/L)

Leptin………………. 32.5 ng/ml (8.0-38.9 )

Cortisol AM (serum)…14.91 (4.3-22.40 mcg/dl))

rT3…………………….30 ng/dl (11-32)

fT3 to rT3 ratio ……….7.7

TSH……………..1.035…(2.2-4.0mIU/mL) taking 90mg Armour

T4 free…………..81…..(0.7-1.5 ng/dL)

T3 free………….2.3…..(2.3-4.2 pg/mL)

B-12…………………..385 pg/ml (up from 278)

MMA…………………. 239 nmol/L (87-318)

Hba1c………………….5.6……(130 mg/dl)

TRIGLYCERIDES………95…….(30-149 mg/dl)

HDL……………………..69

**ESTRADIOL…………....517 ( daily 1mg estradiol)

PROGESTERONE……..26.69ng/ml (daily 200mg prometrium)

TESTOSTERONE……..23ng/dl(2-45)

CORTISOL (AM)…….14.91(4.3-22.40)**

VIT.D (25-HYD)………...45(>30 ng/mL)

DHEA-S………………..106 ug/dL (246-911)

\------------

Supplement with DHEA

goal Women 275-400 μg/dL

Likely your pregnenolone is very low, measure it at LabCorp.

When you establish that your pregnenolone is actually low, consider supplementing with pregnenolone

and

lower your prometrium=micronized progesterone

Prometrium comes in 100mg gelcaps

Ideally you would replace all prometrium with pregnenolone.

I am talking pregnenolone Micrinized Lipid Matrix 150mg tabs, scored from Nutricology.

Dose needs to be titrated but expect (150mg-1200)mg/day

The idea is that

you have low cortisol.

Cortisol is made pregnenolone---->progesterone---xxxx-->cortisol

You are supplementing progesterone-----but----supplemental progesterone, even it is called bio-identical,

have difficulties down metabolizing (to cortisol)

If you supplement with pregnenolone

it should convert to progesterone

but

this timeit is actual progesterone that your body made

should have no problem to raise your cortisol.

When this works (like in my case it does)

I adjust pregnenone dose to get my cortisol=(20-22)

look up where is my progesterone ---->then tweak estradiol, estrone, total Estrogen

When/if you do all this, (and it is successful)

your will want lower progesterone (because current level may be too much) (goal 15-20-25)

and

to balance P/E you will lower also E2 supplementation.

\---------------

increase Vit D, B12, Folate

\---------------

Get IgE and IgG4 alergy tests,

E-95 Food Allergy Panel (Basic)

A-95 Food Allergy Panel(Extended)

Total 190 foods checked for IgE and IgG4 alergy tests

\-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Review and use information in Post #1

http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171942#post171942

p=166492#post166492">http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?15433-JanSz-Labs-amp-Tests&p=166492#post166492

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?18578-Natural-Cholesterol-Adjustment-Theraphy-(NCAT)&p=179723#post179723](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?18578-Natural-Cholesterol-Adjustment-Theraphy-\(NCAT\)&p=179723#post179723)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-21T22:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-hackers-help-please.1966/post-32799>

Your RT3 means your TSH is worthless.....your free T3 says a lot.....and your Pg/E2 ratio is not good. And your DHEA-S is a mess. You definitely need your doc to help this set of issues. I think you need to consider a salivary cortisol level because my bet is your diurnal levels are off and I think Phase 1 and Phase two estrogen detox is a major issue for you with those labs.

>
> PROGESTERONE……..26.69ng/ml (daily 200mg prometrium)****

****

[http://new.instacalc.com/421](http://http://new.instacalc.com/421)

P/E Ratio calculator

R1= 26.69

R2= 517

R3= 84,874.2

R4= 1,897.907

R5= 44.7

P/E=44.7**

\-----------------------------------------------

Now all we need is to figure out desirable P/E ratio.

I have seen 20-170

as desirable

What is your preference dr Kruse?**

...

For men I see

prog=1.4

E2=20

ratio=61 as rather ideal ratio.

This may also be not bad for ladies on their day 2-4 of cycle

\--------------------------------------------

Going by values in Genova diagnostics

[http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/Rhythm-Plus-Sample-Report.pdf](http://http://www.gdx.net/core/sample-reports/Rhythm-Plus-Sample-Report.pdf)

P/E2 ratio

Follicular 23-159

Luteal 25-141

Menopausal 33-116

...

# Thread: What is the Kruse opinion (anyone who knows it) on supplementing with calcium?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-kruse-opinion-anyone-who-knows-it-on-supplementing-with-calcium.1969/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
