>
>
>
> Inger, I really wish I could fast forward and see you at 90 yrs old!

LOL. Me too!

I got this idea from Aajonus Wonderplanitz, he healed himself long ago from autism and cancer with high meat.

He is a rawfooder that eats almost only raw animal food, but he eats dairy too, raw, this I disagree with. I guess he would be better off without.. and I do not think the diet needs to be 100% raw at all. Just something raw every day, because it has benefits because of the bakteria. I feel meat fits my gut a bit better raw, when fish goes great cooked, especially low fat fish. I love salmon and fatty fish like mackerel raw, as sashimi. This is intuitional to me.. how my body feels, how I like the taste.

The high meat is really like a real old gorgonzola.. that you forgot for a while in your fridge.. believe me. If you like very old cheeses, you will have no problem whatsoever to eat the high meat.

I always loved the strongest tasting cheeses, that might be why high meat tastes great to me!

### Inger — 2012-06-26T22:01:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-33749>

> **Qodesh;33855 wrote:** Inger,
>
> Can I make high meat from pre-frozen lamb liver? Or does all high meat need to be from fresh. It's very hard to find fresh grassfed organ meat here.

Qodesh,

I have never tried that. I have eaten lambsliver that have been sitting in my fridge open to air for weeks though, it tasted sour, very sour but not too bad and I digested it well.

You do not need organs, you can make it from grassfed meat too! Just cut all fat away, so there is only meat no visible fat. Or you could try game-meat! That would make great high-meat too!

Better not use prfrozen because freezing kills some enzymes and bakteria and then it will not age well...

You could try for sure.. But I would try it the right way first time, then you know how it should look and taste, and you have something to compare with!

### Michael — 2012-06-26T23:12:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-33759>

You're a brave woman, Inger!

> **Inger;33633 wrote:** LOL. Me too!
>
> I got this idea from Aajonus Wonderplanitz, he healed himself long ago from autism and cancer with high meat.
>
> He is a rawfooder that eats almost only raw animal food, but he eats dairy too, raw, this I disagree with. I guess he would be better off without.. and I do not think the diet needs to be 100% raw at all. Just something raw every day, because it has benefits because of the bakteria. I feel meat fits my gut a bit better raw, when fish goes great cooked, especially low fat fish. I love salmon and fatty fish like mackerel raw, as sashimi. This is intuitional to me.. how my body feels, how I like the taste.
>
>
> It's been said that Native Americans actually preferred their meat high. Without refrigeration it got that way pretty soon anyway, and they just got used to it.
>
>
>
> It's surprising just what they would eat at times. Francis Parkman says in one of his histories of a band of Algonquin Indians:
>
>
>
>
> > The French gave them food, which they devoured with a frenzied avidity, and, unappeased, fell upon a dead dog left on the snow by Champlain for two months past as a bait for foxes. They broke this carrion into fragments, and thawed and devoured it, to the disgust of the spectators, who tried vainly to prevent them.
>
>
>
> [http://www.gutenberg.org/files/3721/3721-h/3721-h.htm](http://http://www.gutenberg.org/files/3721/3721-h/3721-h.htm)
>
>
>
> I'm afraid I find Aajonus Wonderplanitz a little too much to believe. I was skeptical when I heard him on Jimmy Moore, but I nearly laughed outright when I read this interview:
>
>
>
> [http://www.whale.to/a/interview7.html](http://http://www.whale.to/a/interview7.html)
>
>
>
> One tall tale too many, I think.
>
>
>
> I should think he came across the idea of raw meat in people like Francis Pottenger and thought it would make a striking basis for a diet plan, but whether he actually cured himself of anything with it ...
>
>
>
> But, sure, I should think raw meat could be good. It certainly did Pottenger's cats nothing but good. Native peoples all round the world have eaten part of their meat uncooked; the French eat steak tartare; the Japanese sushi and sashimi. Indeed, I'm tempted to wonder if its only highly modernized people that don't.
>
>
>
> And why not ferment raw fish or flesh, too? My cousin went to a "rotting fish" festival in, I think, Bergen. Being an English girl I think she passed on sampling anything.
>
>
>
> The high meat is really like a real old gorgonzola.. that you forgot for a while in your fridge.. believe me. If you like very old cheeses, you will have no problem whatsoever to eat the high meat.
>
> I always loved the strongest tasting cheeses, that might be why high meat tastes great to me!

I remember reading something about the bacteria present in game that's been "hung till high" as people who shoot used to do, and still do a bit, in England. This would be birds like pheasants and grouse. I believe some people left them so long that they didn't take much plucking - the feathers would come away in lumps. So IIRC this source said that the bacteria that grew in the game were similar to the ones used fro blue cheeses in cheese-making.

### Inger — 2012-06-26T23:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-33762>

> **Michael;33897 wrote:**
>
> I'm afraid I find Aajonus Wonderplanitz a little too much to believe. I was skeptical when I heard him on Jimmy Moore, but I nearly laughed outright when I read this interview:
>
>
>
> [http://www.whale.to/a/interview7.html](http://http://www.whale.to/a/interview7.html)
>
>

### janagram — 2012-06-27T08:18:40+0300

### Michael — 2012-06-27T09:29:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-33892>

> **msclman;33965 wrote:** what is the difference between this and rotten meat?

I'd say the type of bacteria you get.

Freesia mentions the recipes in the GAPs book. I looked them up. Dr. Campbell-McBride uses quite lot of salt and she puts a cup of kefir whey in as a starter, too. The fish are also completely covered with the liquor.

Now the salt tends to inhibit the "wrong" bacteria, but doesn't affect the "good" ones. This is why it's added to sauerkraut.

The kefir may not be necessary, but by supplying a living culture of bacteria that she knows to be safe, she ensures the balance is in her favor. (Like swallowing a probiotic to do the same for your insides.)

Finally, because the fish is under the liquid, it doesn't encourage aerobic bacteria, which can be fairly nasty. IIRC, the ancient Icelanders used to bury sheep's heads -- again you'd be controlling the amount of oxygen that's available. I guess Inger does, too. Going by the video, Inger does open her jar regularly, but it spends most of the time closed (and it is in a cold place - her fridge).

I'd guess that you can do the whole thing in a less controlled manner than Natasha Campbell-McBride does. Algonquin Indians, and British squires with pheasant coverts, for example, just left their meat in a cool place. I should think Dr. Campbell-McBride's method is probably safer.

### Endless — 2012-11-05T07:13:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-62155>

Here's a good explanation from the above forum:

> "High Meat"
>
>
>
> \- meat that is partially pre-digested by bacteria (and possibly other microbes)
>
> \- traditionally made and consumed by Inuits ("Eskimo")
>
> \- most "modern western people" would call high meat "spoiled" or "rotten" - however
>
> \- as long as the source raw meat is healthy, the resulting "high meat" is also healthy.
>
>
>
> Why would we/do we eat it?
>
> \- it's a great natura "probiotic supplement" for meat-digesting bacteria & a very easily/quickly digestible protein at the same time
>
> \- it is recommended ESPECIALLY for people with brain-function ("psychological/emotional") problems and people with likeliness of insufficient "digestive flora" (poisoned by anti-biotics / flushed by some "colon cleansing" procedure / other reasons)
>
>
>
> What is it NOT:
>
> \- it is NOT equal to sour milk (which could be called "high milk" ) - sour milk is pre-digested by different bacteria types
>
> \- it is NOT a reliable source for "whipworm"/"Trichuris suis" (doctors call them a "parasite" ) - for that, AV recommends eating intestines of healthy pigs
>
>
>
> Why do/would someone NOT eat it?
>
> \- it has a strong smell and an intense taste compared to fresh meat! That's why it's usually NOT recommended to "RPD beginners".
>
> \- you might still believe the propaganda that "natural microbes cause diseases"- for letting go of this, I recommend any and all materials and interviews with AV (Aajonus Vonderplanitz) - he frames that propaganda with both common sense approach and links to some other information sources worth having "mapped".

### LinD — 2012-11-09T19:33:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-63164>

Someone (Leanne Ely) must be reading here, too!:

[https://savingdinner.com/articles/holy-mackerel/](http://https://savingdinner.com/articles/holy-mackerel/)

We know that we should be adding fish to our diets for optimal health, but which fish should you be reaching for?

It’s hard to go wrong as long as it’s wild fish caught in a sustainable, responsible manner, but one of the healthiest fishes you can buy is mackerel, a fatty deep water fish with a very firm flesh and a strong flavor.

Mackerel is easy to cook, it tastes great and it’s very good for you.

Let’s look at some of the health benefits of this delicious fish:

Omega-3. Mackerel is famous for being a very oily fish. It’s very high in those omega-3 fatty acids that we all should be eating more of.

Heart health. Mackerel can actually improve the condition of the heart. It lowers blood pressure and can prevent stroke.

Low in calories. A 3-ounce serving of this firm fish contains roughly 230 calories (providing it’s been cooked without added butter or sauces), which makes a fillet lower in calories than other meats.

High in protein. There are 21 grams of protein in that 3-ounce serving of mackerel and like other fishes, it contains no carbs, making it a wonderful item to add to your dinner plate.

Minerals. Mackerel contains calcium, potassium, phosphorous, magnesium and selenium. It’s a good source of folate, choline, Niacin, Vitamins A, D, K, B12 and C.

Mackerel lowers cancer-causing agents in the body, improves our immune systems and is great for brain health. Mackerel can help ease symptoms of arthritis, arthrosis and even migraine pain. Holy mackerel!

Now, one thing to be aware of when purchasing mackerel is that it is a deep water fish and depending on where it came from it may contain mercury. Pregnant women should not eat mackerel or any food that may contain mercury because it may harm the developing baby’s nervous system.

To cook mackerel, simply pan fry it at medium heat until it flakes with a fork!

Have you ever tried mackerel?

[http://www.facebook.com/savingdinner](http://http://www.facebook.com/savingdinner)

### nonchalant — 2012-11-29T12:33:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-65838>

I've started trying my high meat lamb's heart, yesterday and today.

I'm surprised at the taste.Â  Not hard to eat at all.Â  And it doesn't smell like regular (commercial) meat that has been in the fridge too long.Â  That stinks.Â  Yuk.Â  Nasty.

My high meat has been fermenting since Nov 3.Â  I didn't put anything with it.Â  Just thawed out the pastured lamb heart, cut off the outer membrane, cut it up into small pieces, and put it in a jar.Â  I stirred it 4-5 times.Â  The aroma began to get nice lately, so I just had to try it.Â  It is interesting tasting, not bad, actually.Â  Not as strong as blue cheese, but it does have sort of an aged-cheese taste.Â  Soft texture, though.

I think I'll thaw out another heart, and cut it up into larger pieces, and add it to the jar.Â  I can continue eating the smaller pieces daily while the larger ones ... ripen.Â Â 

Yes, Inger, one can do this with frozen hearts.Â  Obviously fresh would be better, but this will do.

eating raw meat is one of the best things I have done for myself. I just put a piece of rump steak on a plate and let it sit in the fridge for a few days. It has no smell really and tastes pretty bland. I find it very easy to digest - not like a cooked steak which I find very hard to digest now. I guess not for everyone tho.

### freesia — 2012-11-29T21:50:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-65906>

YesÂ I think "aged, raw" and "high" are two differentÂ beasts though. My grassfed Angus beefÂ is aged (one month I think)before being vacuum packed for the market where I buy it off the shelf. It has no smell, or "aged" taste. It's very tender /wp-includes/images/smilies/icon\_smile.gif
I eat it rare. Without meaning to I found I was always leaving the middle of my steaks raw...they're a New York Sirloin cut and _very_ thick! Previously I would have ordered my steak to be cooked "medium" in a restaurant but I found I really enjoy it rare/raw.Â Â 

I get Piemontese beef from another supplier, the farmer himself.Â It's different from the Angus,Â also aged and has no distinctive "old" smell or taste either.

I guess this is a world away from Inger's high meat...but I sure am intrigued to know what those two mitochondrial rx compounds are!
I'm pretty resourceful at finding out extra info...butÂ on these compounds I'm coming up blank! Caroline, Darleen, Inger, anyone..want to help me figure it out?

/wp-includes/images/smilies/icon\_biggrin.gif Â Â

### Inger — 2012-11-30T05:27:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-65921>

Anna,

your aged Angus is just the same process.. every steak you eat in any steakhouse is a bit "rotten" too...

Or it would be stone hard and not delicious at all. The high meat is just a step further. Much more bacterias. Much more alive..

Â 

I have no idea how to explain how it works, I just know it does. I know people have used this for so long, as a remedy, as a delicacy too.

You need to test it out Anne! Sometimes when we have no data we just need to test it on our selves. If you are scared, test just a tiny tiny piece at first. And I promise it taste nothing worse than very aged certain cheeses do. First it gets a more egg like taste and later as it becomes older it becomes more like old cheese, gets a much more stronger flavor but not bad at all!

But do not forget to air it once in a while.. I once forgot and ate 2 pieces anyways and got sick. Very high fever and I vomited once and got diarrhea but just once, all this in one evening. I went to sleep and next morning I felt totally awesome! I do not recommend to try this to anyone though, I was a bit scared too back then. It happened years ago.

Â 

You know I let my raw meat open in the fridge on a wooden plate and it get some whitish mold on the surface as it dry ages. I just cut thin slices off when I want some.. like dry aged ham. It does not stink though and is a bit different then that in the glass jar. But it has a touch of salami taste! Salami is aged too...

### Inger — 2012-11-30T07:14:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-65934>

Anne one more thing I forgot to say or maybe said unclear...
Dry aged do not get smelly. Wet aged "smells".
I guess because when wet it gives a so much better conditions for the bacterias to grow. So it literally rottens. This would not happend when hanging in air, it just dries out and ages much more slow (this is how your butcher does it), so slow it gets just a tiny salami taste and does not smell any. But we actually want lots and lots of bacterias, and that is why it needs to be in a jar closed and just get some air in a while so it can "rotten" or get "high".
This turns it into a much more effective remedy! It turns it into a superfood actually.

And yes, Caroline, I would love if Jack told us more about this raw/bacteria stuff and explained in depth why it is good!

### nonchalant — 2012-11-30T11:00:11+0200

> But do not forget to air it once in a while..Â 

Yes, we want aerobic bacteria, not anaerobic.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-08T13:18:28+0200

I cannot believe I did what I just did. After reading your post, being thrown about like a ping-pong ball from "oh yes, this makes perfect sense" to "OMG this is gros" I jumped into my car, drove to the next organic supermarket and got lucky. They had beef heart. I got one, chopped it up and put it in the fridge. So now I only hope that somewhere along the next four weeks I don't lose heart (virtually) and throw it on my compost for the cats to eat. I would hate myself for them having better gut microbes than me.

### Inger — 2012-12-08T16:42:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-66840>

Oh Caroline that would be just so much fun...! We could dip into my hole in the ice togehter..lol

Â 

Why they taste different? Hmmm.. I have no idea really! They are both from the same package I got from Germany.. grassfed beef hearts. There was 3 hearts though. They were vacuum packed but on package had gone open and was already a bit smelly. I did not cared about it but I just cut them up for high meat. Now I cannot remember at all if and wich jar is wich heart.. and then one more thing.

I have them both in glass jars. One is 100% air tight but the other is not maybe 100% because one piece of rubber is missing... and it is the one that is not airtight that is so yummy. And it is much moister too. Very moist. When I air it I have to dig around in it with a spoon otherwise the bottom pieces would get no air at all because of all the juice.

I want to explore further on this.

There is a book which covers both plant and animal food fermentation processes. Sandor Katz, Art of Fermentation.

I would love to buy it but I have not yet.

### Inger — 2012-12-09T11:28:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-66930>

I just read this..huh

> "...in Roman times, long before refrigeration, fish guts were placed in a barrel with sea water and allowed to ferment. What came out the bottom of the barrel was a watery fermented fish sauce called garam, widely used as a seasoning (probably the precursor of Worcestershire sauce). The oil floated to the top and was collected carefully. This fermented fish oil was undoubtedly the civilized world's first health elixir, reserved for the soldiers and nobility. It is said that the soldiers refused to march without their daily ration of liquidum." [http://www.westonaprice.org/cod-liver-oil/update-on-cod-liver-oil-manufacture](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/cod-liver-oil/update-on-cod-liver-oil-manufacture)

Â 

Now.. fermented fish guts? Huh. And they were healthy as horses.

Fermented meat is just a easy ride. Fermented fish guts.. I have to try these.

### Inger — 2012-12-09T11:37:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-66931>

C.Adler,

I get my grassfed meat from Max Metzger. They have a internet shop, or just email angela.galbierz@max-metzger.de and ask for grassfed beef heart. I buy grassfed beef fat there too sometimes. It is great! Very cheap and great quality IMO!

Â 

Caroline... I do dip naked in my ice hole but I am not that pretty eather..lol we must go when it is dark.. it is so early dark here and no one will see anything and guess what, it is even more optimal! lol

And the book.. I really have no idea what is the best book but I think it is real hard to find any books about fermented meat.. they are all about vegetables and such.. But if you were to buy it you could search a bit first for sure if there are others! But make sure it has fermented animal foods in it too..

If anyone reads the book, please share your wisdom with us..

### fitness@home — 2013-01-01T16:33:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68934>

Had my first taste of high meat today (appropriate for breaking in the New Year, right?). Not bad...even after a month the meat looks fresh and does not smell bad. Washed it down with some coconut flavored LaCroix.

I started the high meat on December 2. Using a grass fed beef heart, I cut it into smaller bite-sized pieces and made sure to air it every three days or so. The funny thing is how our dog gets so darn excited when he sees me take the jar out of the fridge { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

He prances and jumps all around me while I'm outside airing it. Think I will eventually have to share some with him...

So now that this jar is to the eating stage, I will have to start another batch of meat soon. Never thought in my lifetime that I would actually try something like this.

Happy New Year All!

### Inger — 2013-01-02T01:52:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68963>

Nice fitness@home and Caroline! My aged heart is already about 3-4 months old and it is quite like ammonia..lol But I like it as I always liked those "ammonia" cheeses too! I am too sad when my jars are empty.. I need to order some new grassfed heart soon so I can make new batches.

If someone does not feel any difference eating their high meat it might be you need a larger amount! There is no upper limit AFAIK..
I had fresh oysters yesterday and then some high heart (about 4 "mouth large" chunks).. and my sleep was just AMAZING.

I woke up at 6 PM and felt like in heaven..huh!

### caroline — 2013-01-02T02:34:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68969>

I have gotten sick twice now from oysters ..... JK says to stick with it. It kind of gives me the creeps - but I will try again. The last time I had them on Christmas day - no one else got sick so it couldn't have been a bad batch I don't think.
I am thinking - Inger .... if you had oysters and high meat before going to bed ..... does that give you jungle mouth???? It might be good that you are sleeping alone for the moment anyway ...LOL

### Inger — 2013-01-02T03:23:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68975>

> **caroline said**
>
> I have gotten sick twice now from oysters ..... JK says to stick with it. It kind of gives me the creeps - but I will try again. The last time I had them on Christmas day - no one else got sick so it couldn't have been a bad batch I don't think.
> I am thinking - Inger .... if you had oysters and high meat before going to bed ..... does that give you jungle mouth???? It might be good that you are sleeping alone for the moment anyway ...LOL

Caroline...LOL no! I always rinse and take care my mouth does not smell like high meat..huh! I often brush my teeths after too.
I actually love how my breath never smell anymore. I recognize it too well when others breath smell.. It is weird because if one thinks about that I eat high meat and such it could make ones breath smell but it does not. So nothing to fear!

Just rinse your mouth well after eating it!

Ha. Guess what, I as not sleeping alone. My ex slept in my bed. He likes to stay by my place quite often and he is allowed too when he follows my rules, I cannot say no if someone wants the good things you see. My rules are quite harsh, early bedtime, cold and dark home, and some more.

About oysters. I wonder if it could be a sort of detox too, or adjustment to bacteria or such? If no others got sick it still might have been it is not you, because often it might be just one oyster from the batch that is not good. Try to always smell it first, if it smells like ocean and fresh it is good. If it smells not so good it could have died and oysters that are dead more than one hour start to secret a poison that makes us sick. It is nothing serious or deadly but you will throw up and get sick in your stomach.
Just be careful the oysters are fresh and smell them carefully before eating. If they are almost dried out they are older too. The more water they have still inside the fresher they are. So that is a good sign. I have been eating almost dried out oysters too without issues but it is riskier. I am just not scared at all my stomach digests almost stones me thinks..lol
I do once got nausea after eating oysters a few weeks ago and I throw them up. I was fine again immediately after.

Do not ever give up Caroline, it gets better and better!

### caroline — 2013-01-02T03:32:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68977>

Inger - the funny thing is .... The oysters are sitting on rock salt - and it is the rock salt \[or whatever it is\] that gives me the creeps!

### caroline — 2013-01-02T03:34:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68978>

I don't just throw up - I get severe bloating and the runs too - so it has to be something.

### Inger — 2013-01-02T04:02:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68982>

> **caroline said**
>
> I don't just throw up - I get severe bloating and the runs too - so it has to be something.

Oh Caroline.. I feel for you!
Hmmm.. on rock salt? Are you eating them in a restaurant? Next time can you ask to get them without the salt? In Europe the serve oysters on ice.. not on salt. Are you sure it is the salt? Because it should not touch the oyster if it is in the one half shell, like they use to be served.. still "swimming" in the seawater that are inside.

How are you exactly eating the oysters Caroline? Are they fresh shucked? It is very important you shuck them right before eating and that they are still alive.

About the runs and bloating.. I guess I did not got that because I threw up so fast after. I did it immediately after I felt the nausea coming. Might have saved me from more troubles..
I know people get serious "food poisoning" for days from shellfish and that is why I am cautious and if I feel bad after eating them I do not hesitate to get the foods up. I am not too willing to suffer for days. But it has only happened once so far.

Caroline if you are sensible I would be very careful that the oysters are fresh and better buy them yourself and shuck them right before eating! It is quite fun and a challenge to be great at shucking oysters! One can never be sure what is going on in restaurant kitchens..lol I say that as a waitress that have seen a lot...

### Inger — 2013-01-02T04:08:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68984>

Caroline do you always got into troubles eating oysters? Or are there times you have eaten them raw and felt fine after?
Not that you have a allergy or something?

### caroline — 2013-01-02T04:56:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-68988>

I have been eating them for about a year now. I buy them from a very large and busy fish market. They are shucked and sitting on a bed of salt. Actually - the first time I got sick about 6 weeks ago - was the worst. The second time - I was at a friend's and she got them from the same fish market - already shucked. This time I knew to throw up right away and get rid of it so I was less sick. Jack says to keep trying - so I will. I don't think it is an allergy but the salt really did give me the creeps. funny. Remember that we had a restaurant for 35 years - LOL . However we had an open kitchen - you could always see everything that was going on!

### Inger — 2013-01-02T13:26:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69024>

Caroline, they were both times shucked? Hmmm.. how long time does it went from they beeing shucked unril you ate them? Should never be more than an hour max! In restaurants I worked in, where fresh oysters were on the menu, the chef shucked them only after a guest had ordered some and they was in front of the guests, on ice, as soon as possible.

Caroline, your restaurant with the open kitchen sound so cool! I was often in the most famous and high quality sushi restaurant in Hamburg, Henssler&Henssler (my ex lover, the most lovable man I ever met, always brought me there) and there was an open kitchen also! I loved it! The food was incredible.. Nice memories.

### kathylu — 2013-01-02T15:19:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69035>

I used to work on a science vessel where we had a small oyster dredge (on the Chesapeake Bay). One of my fond memories was taking the oysters we dredged back to the lab and then steaming them just until they opened (in the autoclave) and eating them with lemon, horseradish and hot sauce. Washed down with vodka we distilled ourselves...

Sometimes it's good to be a scientist, even though we are mostly geeks...lol!

### kathylu — 2013-01-02T15:24:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69038>

I've tried eating a few oysters raw from the pints of shucked oysters you get at the seafood counter in the grocery store. They taste fine and I haven't gotten sick, but it does make me a bit nervous. I know a lot more about what's IN oysters than I would like too...lol. It's a little hard psychologically to eat them (not knowing how long ago they were shucked), so I have just been swallowing them whole. No problems so far, knock on wood.

### diane — 2013-01-03T19:13:14+0200

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-05T23:09:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69537>

I just wrote an article for the Vermont Sheep and Goat Assoc. newsletter on organ meats recipes. This was requested by my shearer, who is also a butcher. She hates to see so many good organ meats going to waste. When she found out I was a fan, I got this writing assignment...I used to free-lance, but never did any cooking articles! Next year I'll offer to write one on eating RAW organ meats....we'll see how it goes.

My new favourite oyster is the Thatch Island oysters from Mass. I really love their taste....they just happen to be the hardest to shuck. I'm now wearing gauntlets to prevent bloodshed.

### nonchalant — 2013-01-07T16:21:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-69771>

Inger, over the Christmas holidays I didn't stir my jar of high meat for quite a while. I had left the jar lid on loosely, like you mentioned, but when I did check on it, I didn't care for the smell. So I stirred it again, and this time I put a few layers of cheesecloth between the jar and the lid, and again put the lid on loosely. Once again I went out of town, and neglected the jar for several days. But this time when I checked it, it smelled wonderful and inviting.
Leaving the lid off the jar is not an option for me, but the cloth seems to work quite well.

### fitness@home — 2013-01-28T00:40:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-72931>

> [Nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=69771)
>
> Inger, over the Christmas holidays I didn't stir my jar of high meat for quite a while. I had left the jar lid on loosely, like you mentioned, but when I did check on it, I didn't care for the smell. So I stirred it again, and this time I put a few layers of cheesecloth between the jar and the lid, and again put the lid on loosely. Once again I went out of town, and neglected the jar for several days. But this time when I checked it, it smelled wonderful and inviting.
> Leaving the lid off the jar is not an option for me, but the cloth seems to work quite well.

I also used Inger's advice and left my jar lid just a bit cracked when we had to go out of own for a week. I will admit to being scared to open the fridge when we got back. Was expecting a foul smell when I opened the door, but it was fine. The meat itself was definitely "riper".

It is now about two months old. My goal is to take a couple pieces out to eat each time I stir and air it (about every three days). Now, by eat I mean pop it into my mouth and wash it down quickly with a beverage...have to admire those of you that actually chew it.

Has anyone used free range pork heart for this purpose?

Nonchalant, I will try your cheesecloth method next time we are heading out of town for week or more.

### Inger — 2013-01-28T12:30:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-72982>

Cool idea with the cheesecloth! It looks to prevent the meat drying out because that is what will stop he ferment process me thinks. It needs to be moist..

Anna,
the high meat has no real time to be "ready" I guess.. because it just ferments more and more so it gets more and more effect.. I think after 1-2 months it already has a pretty good effect. But one needs to try out this. Might be the younger the meat the more you need to eat to get the benefits.

If it has gone bad.. I gues when it smells off. It should smell a bit like eggs.. or an smelly cheese or so. But not like a cow barn..hmm hard to explain. I did a batch with elk tongue but I ditched that. It smelled like a barn. I guess the tongue was dirty, I should have washed it well or take off the skin.. it made wrong bacterias grow.
People eat 1 year old high meat and older so there is not really any time when it goes bad in that sense me thinks.. if you only have the good bacteria in it. It just starts to taste pretty strong like ammonia. Like very old cheeses do too!

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-01-28T13:50:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-72983>

While in Whole Foods I saw "dry aged" ground beef that was darker than the other beef. I asked about the dry aged, and was told it was aged for 30 days in a specially controlled container. .... Might have to buy some next time I go to WF. Meanwhile I am sticking w/ high meat of 3 to 5 days in my refrigerator.

### diane — 2013-01-28T15:44:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-72988>

### Inger — 2013-01-28T17:05:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-73001>

Oh Diane.. that's too sad. Do you used prefrozen meat?

### diane — 2013-01-28T17:21:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-73003>

I did - it is fresh grassfed heart - but the butcher freezes before delivery. I don't think I was diligent enough in airing it. Will have to try again!

### Inger — 2013-01-28T17:32:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-73006>

You know Diane.. I kind of suspect it is because it was prefrozen. I would personally not use prefrozen.. When freezing some enzymes die and we need them all to make nice high meat. You can make high meat from muscle meat too! It does not need to be heart if you can only get that prefrozen.
I think prefrozen hearts are superfood too but for high meat they are less ideal.. make a carpaccio out of them instead!

### Inger — 2013-01-30T14:07:48+0200

I just got my hearts from Germany and I immediately made a beef heart carpaccio.. rescue!
I ate two plates of it.. I used raw grassfed beef fat instead of olive oil.

It was a bit weird but after eating it I really got a boost of energy and my mind was finally thinking clear - but I overdid it and payed the price and I am still exhaustet. If it is the lamb hearts that gives me so much energy I have to eat a smaller amount next time

### nonchalant — 2013-03-18T03:36:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-me-eating-high-meat-so-good-for-our-gut.2036/post-80930>

> [GlenPDQ said:](/goto/post?id=80830)
>
> Let's say I cube up some raw heart for high meat and wanted to spike it with some probiotics the same way milk is innoculated to make yogurt. Any suggestions what one would use? I have a bottle of probiotics onhand which contains Lactobacillus, Leuconostoc, Kluyveromyces, Brettanomyces, Debaryomyces, Saccharomyces, and Torulospora strains.
> .

Glen, I think if the meat were cooked, then you would need to add probiotics. Pasteurized milk needs them to make yogurt, but raw milk does not. I have made yogurt from raw milk several times, often unintentionally while giving some to my chickens. What they don't drink becomes yogurt with the heat of the sun. They seem to love that yogurt even more than the milk. I also would use the whey from making raw yogurt in my pickles. (I don't heat the raw milk much, only to body temp.) But recently I have considered leaving it out, because perhaps dairy bacteria aren't necessarily the proper microbes for vegetables. Salt seems to work fine, along with the normal bacteria present in the cucumbers.

Raw meat already has the beneficial bacteria needed to get off to a good start. Making sure it has oxygen keeps the anaerobic bacteria at bay, thankfully.

### JanSz — 2012-06-26T02:55:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-33557>

**Who sells them (at reasonable price)?

I see them sold freeze dry, meant for aquarium fish food.**

I think they can be eaten whole.

Some Philipino's that I used to work with, eat shrimps whole, shell and all.

[http://ecoscope.com/krill/index.htm](http://http://ecoscope.com/krill/index.htm)

[http://ecoscope.com/krill/krill5/index.htm](http://http://ecoscope.com/krill/krill5/index.htm)

> Special: some scientists describe Euphausia superba as the once most abundant multicellular species of the planet - some scientists describe Euphausia superba as one of the most flexible of our planet - some describe Euphausia superba as the most physiologically dynamic - some as a most interestingly designed - a famous scientist as "of almost extraterrestrial appearance, endowed with magical powers ", another: "The high importance of lowly krill"- and most of those few humans who were lucky to observe Euphausia superba alive, under water, lights still on, with the right optics, as utmost beautiful and fascinating
>
>
>
> estimation of biomass between 50 - 900 million tonnes (>900 000 000 000 000 individuals), some estimates are as high as 1.5 billion tonnes - estimation of annual production as high as a billion tonnes - central predator/prey organism in the antarctic ecosystem - key stone organism - current fishery ca. 80 000 tonnes per year.

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:44:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-34098>

Why eat fresh, whole krill? Why not just take a krill oil supplement?

### JanSz — 2012-06-28T05:47:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-34108>

> **purplemonster;34253 wrote:** Why eat fresh, whole krill? Why not just take a krill oil supplement?

Whole krill is a sea food, like shrimp or fish (but likely much better).

When we eat shrimp or fish we have pollutants in mind.

Krill is on the bottom of food chain, I expect minimal pollutants.

That should make krill the best sea food there is, (not sure how it tastes but garlic, sea salt @ hot pepper can help a lot).

Fish oil contains EPA & DHA that we treasure. Fish does not make them, it gets them thru food chain from krill.

EPA & DHA are made by Krill.

Fish oil does not have phospholipids, krill oil does have phospholipids.

Not sure, I guess phospholipids also originate within krill.

We do not discuss phospholipids much.

Everybody but specially older people and sick, should supplement with phospholipids.

Bottle of PS from BodyBio (300 pills) costs $150 , PS=(PhosphatidylCholine + other phospholipids and ALA & LA in 4/1 ratio)

Either fish oil or krill oil have proportionally too much DHA for the amount of contained EPA.

Supposedly ideal ratio is EPA/DHA=3/1

Krill or fish oil are human products, EPA/DHA proportions are likely skewed due to manufacturing process.

Eating krill directly would likely overcome this. Krill is as natural food as it gets.

/////////////////

//

### JanSz — 2012-07-09T01:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-36260>

[http://www.rimfrostkrill.com/press-release-olympic-seafood-as-introduces-its-flagship-brand-the-rimfrost-krill-collection-at-vitafoods-2012/](http://http://www.rimfrostkrill.com/press-release-olympic-seafood-as-introduces-its-flagship-brand-the-rimfrost-krill-collection-at-vitafoods-2012/)

PRESS RELEASE: Olympic Seafood AS Introduces its Flagship Brand, the RIMFROST KRILL COLLECTION at Vitafoods 2012

on MAY 4, 2012 Â· in PRESS RELEASES

FosnavÃ¥g, Norway – April 26th, 2012 — Norwegian firm Olympic Seafood AS launches their flagship brand, the RIMFROST KRILL COLLECTION at the 2012 Vitafoods trade fair from May 22-24 in Geneva, Switzerland.

The RIMFROST KRILL COLLECTION is a new premium range of products from the shrimp-like marine crustacean **Euphausia Superba (Antarctic krill)** that includes krill meal, krill powder and krill oil that are high in omega-3 fatty acids in the bio-efficient phospholipid form. The RIMFROST COLLECTION maintains its intrinsic goodness by immediate and gentle two-step enzymatic processing on-board their specialised krill-collecting vessel ‘Juvel’.

─Olympic Seafood AS focuses exclusively on Antarctic krill ingredients for consumer applications and is deeply committed to sustainable fishing practices and eco-responsibility, explains sales and marketing director Even T. RemÃ¸y.

─We take great pride in ‘Juvel,’ the newest krill vessel on the ocean, which utilises industry-leading technology and aims for low emissions, low energy use and low climate impact.

Having their own vessel guarantees 100% traceability and is also certified by Friend of the Sea to ensure it reaches the highest possible standards in sustainability. In addition, Juvel is always carrying an international observer from the Convention on the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR), which is reporting observations and scientific data to CCAMLR. It is important to note that many other krill vessels do not carry these observers, which can result in non-sustainable practices.

The Antarctic waters are the cleanest in the world and krill from these waters are naturally extremely low in pollutants, giving pure products where no extra purification is needed. Antarctic krill (Euphausia Superba) can tolerate the icy conditions due to their rich content of nourishing nutrients: phospholipid bound omega-3 fatty acids, other lipids, protein and antioxidants. In humans omega-3 phospholipids are more efficiently absorbed and utilised by the body as well as causing significantly less after taste than other forms of omega-3.

─Our exceptional onboard processing technology allows us to produce highly stable and unique raw material that is the best possible for SFE extraction, explains RemÃ¸y.

─This gives our distinct clear red minimally processed oil and powders with superior quality. No extra purification steps are necessary since the SFE extraction gives oil with high purity and favorable viscosity.

─We are proud of our products but we are also proud to provide a stable supply of goods with an established stock of raw materials as well as finished products, says RemÃ¸y.

─Our reserve of raw materials and ready products ensure all orders have a high delivery precision and security for customers, details RemÃ¸y.

Product Range

RIMFROST GENUINE is Antarctic krill meal where only the fluoride containing krill shells and water are removed before mild low-temperature drying and packaging of the product while still on board the Juvel. RIMFROST GENUINE has a characteristic reddish-brownish colour and fresh shrimp-like odour and taste. Just the way it should be!

RIMFROST PRISTINE is Antarctic krill powder that is not only high in omega-3 fatty acids in the bio-efficient phospholipid form but also lush with nourishing marine nutrients like peptides and antioxidants. RIMFROST PRISTINE also uses a rapid enzymatic processing on-board to derive its characteristic reddish colour and fresh shrimp-like odour.

RIMFROST SUBLIME is Antarctic krill oil high in omega-3 fatty acids in the bio-efficient phospholipid form, with a characteristic clear red colour and low odour with a hint of fresh shellfish. These traits are captured with a two-step process including gentle SFE extraction.

### JanSz — 2012-07-14T22:09:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-37825>

> **Anna K.;37858 wrote:** Is Krill better then shrimp???

Speaking of food chain, the higher the more poluted.

Krill is on very bottom of food chain.

Krill eats micro-algae that contain (valuable) DHA

Krill have inside factory that produces EPA and phospholipids

cell membranes are made of phospholipids

phospholipids are mostly PhosphatidylCholine also little of PhosphatidylEtanolamine and PhoshadtidylInositol and minor Glycolipids.

\----------

asking for help

BodyBio.com sells PC (PhosphatidylCholine)

it contains not only above phospholipids

but also

ALA(O6) and LA(O3) at the ratio of 4:1

both are vegetable oils

I would like to figure if

phospholipids in krill contain any other Fatty Acids (other than EPA & DHA), specially if there is any ALA & LA

important, I got curious when learned that grass fed beef contains ALA & LA (vegetable oils)

(mistigi how are you doing, tx for this info)

### JoeBranca — 2012-08-13T21:34:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/should-we-eat-krill-whole.2044/post-44748>

not bad... threw together some rehydrated/boiled wakame and sh-taake with garlic, ginger, coconut aminos; thawed and rinsed the frozen krill and threw them on top... drizzled a little avocado oil and evoo and went to town for an epic breakfast.

also for good measure, shared a couple of frozen whole sardines with the dog lol. i have no problem chewing up raw sardine heads, but something extremely bitter in the front-bottom gut section really throws me off.

# Thread: Out,  out damn light!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-26T15:29:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33639>

I'm trying to make the bedroom as dark as humanly possible. I also want it stylish and attractive. I've got nice room darkening shades, but even then it still lets light in at the sides.

What products/types of window coverings are you using that work really well. If you are covering the whole opening with aluminum foil, I'd love to hear about it - but not exactly the direction I want to go in. I want my house to be attractive to future buyers.

I see lots of products in my line of work, but people tend to get weirded out if I ask if we can shut all the shades and turn out the lights so I can see how dark it is

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-26T15:34:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33642>

We got room darkening shades from blinds to go or next day blinds. they do an amazing job. the were really expensive though b/c our windows are close to 6ft in width, and 3ft in height in the bedrooms! the do let some light in from the side - but its far less than what we used to deal w/our old crappy world market shades, and the neighbors spotlight which is on all freaking night! not sure how to eclipse the side light - ok here's an idea - what about putting some decorative valances down the side - so that when the darkening shades are pulled - the valances bloc what little light might come through the side???? decorative and useful - especially if you get a heavy fabric w/light blocking paneling?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-26T15:36:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33643>

> **Shijin13;33780 wrote:** We got room darkening shades from blinds to go or next day blinds. they do an amazing job. the were really expensive though b/c our windows are close to 6ft in width, and 3ft in height in the bedrooms! the do let some light in from the side - but its far less than what we used to deal w/our old crappy world market shades, and the neighbors spotlight which is on all freaking night! not sure how to eclipse the side light - ok here's an idea - what about putting some decorative valances down the side - so that when the darkening shades are pulled - the valances bloc what little light might come through the side???? decorative and useful - especially if you get a heavy fabric w/light blocking paneling?

I should clarify, I already have the expensive room darkening shades - it's those slivers of light on the side that are driving me nuts (ocd much?). Our neighborhood is lit up at night and now that I'm all optimal and what-not, those are glaring at me all night reminding me that "caves and burroughs" don't have slivers of light.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-26T17:12:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33666>

I ordered a covering that adheres via velcro strips to the window frame. It blocks all light and can be removed during the day if you want to. The velcro strips are either black or white (which have a sticky back, so that'll stick to the window frame.... not sure how it comes off).

[http://www.blackoutez.com/](http://http://www.blackoutez.com/)

There won't be any air circulation either... just so you know. A/C required at least in the summer if you use it. And if you decide to try it, order it big enough to go all the way to the corners of your window frame to ensure all light is blocked.

### johnnyb — 2012-06-26T17:25:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33670>

Someone with my last name was Mayor years ago. Once the polls closed I snagged a bunch of the signs that only said for Mayor. I can put one in the window to cast a shadow on my head if the neighbors turn on their too bright street lights. I have been thinking about framing some thick white PVC I have. Then setting it on the sill and using magnets attracted to the cornerbead in the window frame to hold it up right. The windows are large enough I may not have a good place to set the frames during the day. Maybe a genius idea will show up on this thread.

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T17:55:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33684>

> **Cavemam;33797 wrote:** I do have the rest of that blue painters tape left over after doing the tub drain. How shall I list this in the MLS:
>
>
>
> Features: Coveted decorative painters tape accents in master bed.

Painter's tape might not actually be a bad idea, since it's designed to come off of painted surfaces without damaging them. I don't know how it holds up to long-term use though, ie: whether or not it starts to leave a sticky residue like duct tape will after a while.

Are you actively showing the house now? If not, then just use the tape until you DO start to show the house?

Also, not sure if it's been mentioned already, but are drapes a possibility? You could pull them over enough that they blocked out the edges of the blinds where the light is showing through.

I need to get a blind for the window in my room. We live out in the country so it's fairly dark at night anyway, but in the winter time especially, if there's a bright moon it's pretty bright in our room. Full moons when there's snow on the ground make it almost daylight brightness in there. I tack a bath towel up over the window on nights like that (it's a casement window, so it's tall and narrow).

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-26T18:04:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33687>

I don't think that's a mismatch.... if it doesn't come from artificial light. My body loved the natural light cycles when I lived in the mountains. Even my cycle was in tune with them. I purposely left the window covers open... and looked at the pretty moon while falling asleep.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-26T18:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/out-out-damn-light.2050/post-33688>

Years ago when I made "warm window" curtains, I wanted a good tight seal. I put thin adhesive magnetic strips on the long edges of the curtains and the walls adjacent to the window. The magnets do a great job of holding the curtain tight against the wall and blocking light. The strips can be painted to match the wall and they blend in fairly well. That might not be acceptable for showing your house; if not, I'd remove them and repaint the walls (which you might do anyway before selling).

### tweety — 2012-06-27T15:37:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-with-dhs-labs.2056/post-33917>

My husband:

5'7 1/2", weight 275lbs

very heavy dense body frame; very heavy large muscles, very stocky and compact

I don't know his BF%

Past medical history:

\-many sports played, several concussions and has residual neck and shoulder pain probably from these sports injuries

\-tendency to pop ligaments and muscles (inflexible, tight tendons)

\-one bicep is completely torn (has 80% recovery potential)

\-ruptured achilles tendon, repaired surgically 1996

\-prone to injury in general

\- high stress as child

\-overweight since childhood, with the exception of high school

\-stressful last five years (moving, career change, starting his own business, father died...)

he has a high pressure job, and loves it but it requires alot of him. he has started addressing his stress- turning off his phone at home, limiting his strenuous duties when possible, ... we don't watch TV, he reads before bed and goes to bed around 10 when he can;

he loves CT

We are strict with diet because he has celiac. Dairy has been gone about a year. When he tried it after a time he had alot of inflammation, so he is good about keeping it out. The only thing I can imagine would be inadvertent gluten on his restaurant food. Really his appetite is not there. I know a large part of the issue is his stress. His appetite returns when he is not under stress, and he sees this now too. We had a small family vacation over a weekend and he lost 8lbs in three days. As soon as we get home, he puts it back on... from the stress. Fortunately, he is aware of this and has been more diligent about stress reduction since.

Typical days food:

B: 3 eggs, side of pasture raised ham/bacon/GF hamburger and licorice tea

L: broth and a little bit of leftovers

D: small salad, fish/GF beef, and veggie, licorice tea

Anything else?

[http://www.amazon.com/Ozeri-Bathroom-Hydration-Recognition-Technology/dp/B004NXUAXW/ref=sr\_1\_14?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1340816853&sr=1-14&keywords=scale+with+body+fat+percentage](http://http://www.amazon.com/Ozeri-Bathroom-Hydration-Recognition-Technology/dp/B004NXUAXW/ref=sr_1_14?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1340816853&sr=1-14&keywords=scale+with+body+fat+percentage)

\---

No restaurant food.

In restaurant always order plain water and triple order of shrimp scampi with triple butter each or couple pounds of a king crab with 8 tbsp=1/2cup=1stick=800cal butter.

At least 50% of daily food intake within one hr off wakeup.

Remember 70% fat + 30% proteins + incidental carbs.

Look (almost) at sun first 30 min after wakeup.

After he have results from Spectracell, supplement deficiencies, but drop licorice tea, replace with green tea.

...

1 dropperfull/day

### cookworm — 2012-06-26T20:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hoping-to-beat-ai.2059/post-33730>

I've been doing paleo for 8 weeks (started a weight loss program previously by following the Slow Carb Diet for a total of 13 weeks changed eating/exercising), following leptin Rx and feeling better, losing weight. I am hoping to try to stop autoimmune/chronic issues:

-   Ulcerative colitis (previously; have had a J-pouch)
-   Crohn's disease
-   Sjogren's syndrome
-   Interstitial cystitis
-   Insulin resistance
    Fatty liver
    Polycystic ovarian syndrome
    Anemia and B12 and D vitamin deficiencies
    Widespread chronic body pain

I'm hoping to get stronger and healthier and lose my goal weight (40 pounds). I'm halfway to my goal weight, but discouraged to learn that if my weight loss continues at the pace it is, it will take half a year to lose my total goal weight! I know; I didn't gain it overnight! I'm looking forward to the day when I can stop being cold all the time!

# Thread: Healthy Seafood ideas for people who dislike seafood
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 41
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cody — 2012-06-26T20:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33732>

I need some ideas for healthy recipes/products for seafood.

Here's what I like:

-   shrimp cocktail
-   clam chowder
-   raw salmon
    any fish deep fat fried and breaded.
    Tuna salad
    salmon salad

What do you all recommend?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-26T21:23:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33735>

I'd suggest you begin to get used to discomfort. You'll see why by BG 5 I bet.

### ealachan — 2012-06-26T21:23:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33736>

Try tuna without the mayo? I like it with lemon juice and a little tiny bit of olive oil, plus some chopped roasted red peppers (or banana peppers, if you like hotter stuff), kalamata olives, capers, red onion...all scooped onto a bed of greens. Shrimp scampi is easy to adapt to a paleo diet, just ditch the pasta and serve it over spaghetti squash or even plain. Shrimp is also wonderful in coconut-milk-based curry, and it's easy to load a curry up with lots of veg and turmeric as well.

If you want to expand your fish horizons a little, cod is super good for you and it's very, very mild...it doesn't taste "fishy" at all. Haddock is similar, though it's more finely grained than cod and a little harder to cook filets of. Both cod and haddock are fantastic in curry, or roasted and served with a tapenade over them.

Have you tried mackerel or sardines at all? If you like salmon, you'd probably like mackerel and sardines. Sardines packed in water seem to be milder-tasting to me than ones packed in oil. Canned mackerel tastes kind of like a cross between tuna and salmon to me. It wasn't nearly as strong as I thought it would be.

Hopefully that helps some?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-26T21:58:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33746>

Try making paleo mayo with coconut oil for your tuna salads. I can't eat eggs so not a rush for me to make it but I've read others have liked it.

Also Dover Sole is an inexpensive, very thin/flat, white fish that has almost no-existent flavor. I pan fry quickly in a little ghee then just put a little dill on the fish and pour some melted ghee with lemon rind over the fish. YUM! From start to end you should be done in 10-15 min.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-26T23:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33765>

Hi Cody, how about shrimp grilled in ghee or butter?

Eat a bunch of those items you already listed (sans the deep fry and breading) and I think you will find your interest quickly peak. It's like your body has discovered a wonderful new source of nutrition.

### djjadam@aol.com — 2012-06-27T00:14:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33774>

> **Jack Kruse;33873 wrote:** I'd suggest you begin to get used to discomfort. You'll see why by BG 5 I bet.

What is BG 5?

### nonchalant — 2012-06-27T00:53:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33790>

Hi Daniel, the title of Jack's current blog article is "BRAIN GUT 2: VIRAL MARKETING". Or BG2 for short. Jack is referring to the yet to be published 5th part of the series.

### Cody — 2012-06-27T06:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33881>

You guys/gals are awesome! Thanks for the idea.

I went ahead and had 4 ounces each of salmon, cod, and shrimp pan fried in Nutiva with a little garlic, salt, and pepper. Not bad!

I'm going to suck it up and try to find a way to eat seafood at least once per day for the next month.

Thanks for the ideas!

Any ideas for canned clams?

### janagram — 2012-06-27T08:07:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33888>

I used to love the creamy clam sauce over spaghetti, but maybe heat them up in (reduced) cream and serve over caulirice...

### janagram — 2012-06-27T08:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33889>

I'm in the same boat as you (not fishing)

### johnnyb — 2012-06-27T08:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-33891>

You might try cooking seafood differently. One of the reasons "I didn't like seafood" is having had most of it generally overcooked while growing up. My brother taught me that only a couple years ago. I keep trying more and more seafood. I recently cooked a salmon fillet in butter. It was very tasty until the thickest part was too under done. I had already cleared the pan so I hit it with 30 seconds in the microwave. That was too long. It was better undercooked. It is amazing how different seafood can be depending on how it's preparred. Try the widest variety you can in different ways. You will find more you foods you like. Keep going with what you do like too.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-06-28T16:22:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-34149>

I need some ideas too. After my consult with dr kruse last night I've become more convinced that I have start eating the stuff. I used to like shrimp and tuna, but since I had my first son I don't like any of the stuff. The smell of it nauseates me. When I make fish for my family for dinner, if I don't eat first I totally lose my appetite because it smells so bad to me. Soooo... Wondering about maybe a very mild fish with some heavy spices to cover up any smell? Other ideas?

### janagram — 2012-06-28T18:17:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-34180>

[http://perfecthealthdiet.com/2011/12...-2-the-recipe/](http://http://perfecthealthdiet.com/2011/12...-2-the-recipe/)

Try this recipe, Trisha! (the bengali fish curry, if you haven't tried it yet....its a winner, you don;t get the fishy thing)

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-28T20:45:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-34207>

Shrimp sautÃ©ed in Ghee and garlic

Fish taco salad (sautÃ© fish of choice in garlic, cumin, coriander, cilantros & lime juice, serve over romaine lettuce and jicama w/guacamole and poco de gallo)

Fresh mussels, clams & oysters

Sardines mixed up like tuna salad.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-06-28T21:16:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-34217>

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-28T21:47:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-34220>

I saute (with lots of CO) cod with scallops, and add green onion, fresh ginger, garlic, and shredded cabbage, carrots (the coleslaw bag). I add the veggies once the fish is mostly cooked, and push the fish to the side. It's fantastic! I eat it everyday, sometimes twice, changing up the meat/fish. The scallops are the best IMO.

### janagram — 2012-06-29T02:02:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-34277>

### Cody — 2012-07-02T16:10:01+0300

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-02T20:23:00+0300

DH has started making "Sardine Salad" - very much like tuna salad w/a can of smoked, and plain sardines in water - very tasty...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-03T04:18:06+0300

When I make fish for my family for dinner, if I don't eat first I totally lose my appetite because it smells so bad to me. Soooo... Wondering about maybe a very mild fish with some heavy spices to cover up any smell? Other ideas?

How about sashimi, ceviche and other forms of raw fish (like Inger's salmon)? No cooking or smells required!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-03T04:20:43+0300

Shellfish are totally different taste-wise, so if you're not fond of fish, go for the shellfish. Dr K told me they were superior to fish anyway, so here's to permission to gobble up all the scallops and mussels and shrimp you desire!

### allen001 — 2012-07-04T06:54:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-35435>

### Cody — 2012-07-23T14:46:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40372>

I found a winner, winner, fishy dinner recipe to solve my problem. I love eggs, first of all. Really, my favorite food ever.

**1\. Fisherman’s Eggs**

_Easy and very tasty. Try them for breakfast, or pair with a leafy salad for a quick supper. Serves 4. Adapted from_[The Silver Spoon](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0714845310?ie=UTF8&tag=c037-20&linkCode=xm2&camp=1789&creativeASIN=0714845310).

Heat the oven to 500Â°F and preheat an ovenproof serving dish for five minutes. Place a can of sardines, a small sliced shallot, a few sprigs of chopped parsley, and two cloves of finely chopped garlic in the warm dish. Add some black pepper and put the dish back in the oven for six minutes. Remove the dish from the oven, break four eggs into a bowl, and pour them gently on top of the sardine mixture. Season with salt and pepper and put the dish back in the oven for seven minutes, until the egg whites are cooked but still jiggly. Remove the dish and let it sit for five minutes (the eggs keep on cooking even out of the oven). Serve with toast and hot sauce.

[](http://http://www.chow.com/blog-media/2010/05/sardine_fishermans_eggs_600.jpg)

From [http://www.chow.com/food-news/47126/7-things-to-do-with-canned-sardines/](http://http://www.chow.com/food-news/47126/7-things-to-do-with-canned-sardines/)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-23T14:58:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40374>

nice backround of wheat bread.......this is the ying and yang of food.......

### ealachan — 2012-07-23T15:11:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40382>

>
>

> 1\. Fisherman’s Eggs**
>
> _Easy and very tasty. Try them for breakfast, or pair with a leafy salad for a quick supper. Serves 4. Adapted from_[The Silver Spoon](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0714845310?ie=UTF8&tag=c037-20&linkCode=xm2&camp=1789&creativeASIN=0714845310).
>
> Heat the oven to 500Â°F and preheat an ovenproof serving dish for five minutes. Place a can of sardines, a small sliced shallot, a few sprigs of chopped parsley, and two cloves of finely chopped garlic in the warm dish. Add some black pepper and put the dish back in the oven for six minutes. Remove the dish from the oven, break four eggs into a bowl, and pour them gently on top of the sardine mixture. Season with salt and pepper and put the dish back in the oven for seven minutes, until the egg whites are cooked but still jiggly. Remove the dish and let it sit for five minutes (the eggs keep on cooking even out of the oven). Serve with toast and hot sauce.
>
> [](http://http://www.chow.com/blog-media/2010/05/sardine_fishermans_eggs_600.jpg)
>
> From [http://www.chow.com/food-news/47126/7-things-to-do-with-canned-sardines/](http://http://www.chow.com/food-news/47126/7-things-to-do-with-canned-sardines/)

Hot damn that looks good! I'm a big fan of runny-yolked eggs on top of just about anything - kale, burgers, and now fish...

### Cody — 2012-07-23T15:33:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40383>

> **Jack Kruse;40562 wrote:** nice backround of wheat bread.......this is the ying and yang of food.......

The article I linked to is full of wheat!

I eat mine without toast and with pastured eggs.

By the way, ignore the serves four part! I throw in a fifth egg and it serves one. Me!

Also, works great with canned oysters! Even tastier.

### MartiD — 2012-07-23T15:56:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40386>

My husband laughs at my eating habit now. I was always a just shrimp or crab legs seafood eater, hated all other fish. I've been craving salmon since I started LRX in October and now eat tilapia, cod, halibut, etc. This weekend we went to Sams and got a big bag of sea scallops and big section of wild caught salmon. I've never had scallops because my Mom was deadly allergic to them. I wrapped in bacon last night and covered them in lemon butter and they were great. No fishy taste at all! And no reaction!

I'll keep venturing out more and more with time. Not sure I can do the raw thing just yet but who knows!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-23T17:49:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40394>

and getting to the point of the thread......if you dislike seafood learn to get used to your dislike long term........it is the reason you are sick.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-23T17:55:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40396>

This weekend we went to the local international mkt - I bought 2x1lb bags of frozen mixed seafood for $3.99/bag - that same bag at Tjoes is $11.99!

we also got fresh blue crabs for $1.99/lb - score... unfortunately at that price they'd been picked over by 11am and there were only 6 live ones' left -

### MJ* — 2012-07-23T19:08:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40408>

I just purchased Marder Brands skewered shrimp, and we put them on the BBQ brushed with butter and garlic, YUM!

Another fish I like is Monkfish sometimes referred to as the poor man's lobster. It's a meaty white fish. I like to broil it, you can also grill it. It's a juicy fish so you can put a lemon zest and salt rub on it an hour before you cook it to bring down some of the excess juice. Serve it with drawn butter, it's delicious!

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-24T16:39:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-40675>

It seems to me that salmon is usually the most difficult fish for people to like - at least in my experience.

If you like raw salmon - how can you go wrong just eating that?

I've been buying up wild caught alaskan salmon like crazy this summer - it is pricey.

But I just grill it in butter and add some blackened spices on the top.

### Cody — 2012-07-26T18:30:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-41214>

Is it safe to eat raw salmon from the grocer's freezer section?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-26T18:52:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/healthy-seafood-ideas-for-people-who-dislike-seafood.2061/post-41230>

If I eat salmon raw I make sure it is sushi grade, although I have to say I'm not sure what makes it "sushi grade", and wild. Lived in Seattle too many years to even think of eating farm raised. Just cannot do it with salmon. I used to get raw salmon from this Japanese store that never smelled of fish. So clean. Now I haven't eating raw except at sushi restaurants in a year because I'm not near that store. Making a trip next week to that area so going to check out their sea veggies too!

# Thread: Hey there!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-there.2067/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hilltowner — 2012-06-27T01:35:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-there.2067/post-33797>

So I' m here after taking over a month to read (okay, I skimmed a bit) the monster thread at MDA. I feel like I know some of you already.

In my 4th week of LR...I have eaten primally since spring 2009, and was strictly carnivore for 2 years. I added in some veggies last summer but do not seem to have success with hgher than 50 grams/ day carbs. Occasional alcohol and dark chocolate. In the same 3 lb range since December, and nothing seems to get me back under 120. I am 52, female, 5'2" and 124 as of today.

I am ordering a cortisol test soon, and looking into hormone testing. I never see my PCP so don't quite know how to broach this.

Laura

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-27T02:00:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-there.2067/post-33806>

Welcome and glad to have you.........

# Thread: Evolution
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution.2070/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-06-27T06:31:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution.2070/post-33882>

**In response to msclman's question:

"just finished your most recent blog. im no anthropologist but i do have a question, given the fast acting gene modification system we seem to have wont we evolve eventually to the standard american diet (and arent we selected now after 12k years of wheat to that type of diet)?"

**Jack posted this answer:**

"Here is our modern circle of life......we came from transitional ape with a small brain.....we walked up, and were not too bright. The cold came and the ocean crashed into our neighborhood and the forest and grassy plains died off.......the tectonic plates moved and the land shifted up and the coastal waterways floaded cutting us off from all out natural sources of food......IE carbs from fruit in the trees.......what was around? Lots of seaweed and shellfish......we ate it. Our legs got longer, our guts shortened, we got more viral infections from the clams......and we got even smarter. We stopped eating fruit, did not need it at all, and we ate more of what got us smarter....coastal vegetation and shell fish. Brains got bigger. We used tools and caught fish.......got smarter and we made tools to kill animals that were ten times our size......we got so smart we could make food for large packs of us.......we got bored ate more fish with a special virus that made us impulsive and we left the cradle that birthed us......we ran into some cold weather up North and we moved away from the sea and lost all our sources of seafood and meat. We decided to eat plants to survive because we were smart........or that is what we thought 13000 yrs ago........then our brains (cro magnon) shrank 130 grams and we became us......modern man......and we kept eating grains.....and then fruit.......and then we started feeding our animals the same \*\*\*\*......and we ate them and all the crap we fed them too........then we began making our own seeds and food, and sugar........and our brains shrunk. Our cognition got worse.......we listened to other humans who said whole grains and dairy from cows fed grains weee awesome for us. The smart humans who cared for us said eat what you want because a calorie is a calorie.....diet does not matter......we got fat, and dumb and wonf up with T2D and AD......the AD came earlier and earlier......now we cant think or do anything much. We stay in homes called elder care places where we eat fruit and grasses........as we watch apes and chimps on the discovery channel do the same thing........and we are as smart as them......once again because the TV said they have exactly the same genes we do........the only difference is they eat fruit all day and we dont? The orderly brings your grandmother a bowl of fruit covered in ice cream with a side of cookies. Medicare paid for it because its part of a balanced diet according to the FDA/USDA and has the stamp of approval of Washington DC who sell the grains to the entire world..........Everyone wants to be like us? That is made easy when you dumb it down for the masses to get because their brains no longer work....... That is how evolution leads to de evolution when you mess with your epigenetics. Go re read levee one right now.......epigenetics.......is what determines your cellular terroir......you eat like a chimp.......you think like one. Got it Mike? share it."

_Jack, and msclman I hope you don't mind me posting this here. It's too important to be buried in a closed thread!_**

### nonchalant — 2012-06-27T06:56:13+0300

Glad you posted this, Indigogirl! I was awed by his response also. I would have just replied "Because the SAD is unable to support good brains." Jack is a natural teacher.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T15:51:02+0300
