### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-27T19:51:50+0300

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-27T19:55:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evolution.2070/post-33994>

The power of marketing and "socially acceptable" is quite overwhelming isn't it.

I am struggling right now with a good friend. We actually bonded and became friends because we were both Paleo. She started as a client, and moved to #1 playdates and friends as we had boys the same age. They have fallen off and are now eating more sad. She got pregnant after not being able to for a while (yes, that eating got you preggers, lets stop it now!) So she is pregnant and eating bagels and I just can't stand it.

It really bothers me and I feel I've lost a partner in crime. I've found I just don't want to hang out as much, and I don't want my son seeing them going back to processed foods after we had made great strides in eating the same way and could swap kids with no crazy eating rules.

I'm mad. I want my friend back that has similar interest. Wah.

# Thread: Hello from the LCHF center of gravity - SWEDEN!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### CoolSwede — 2012-06-27T14:07:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33905>

Hello everyone!

I am a 33 year old Swedish guy that always is on the quest to improve my health. Three years ago, I friend of mine told me about the new LCHF diet and gave me a link to the Diet Doctor ([http://www.dietdoctor.com](http://http://www.dietdoctor.com)). The rest is history as we say!

My current goal is to lose more fat to reach 9%-10% BF. LCHF took me half-way but I have still those annoying love handles that just won't go away. I am currently at ~17% BF.

See ya'!

### janagram — 2012-06-27T17:48:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33944>

welcome! Come on in, the water's really cold! (you'll love it!)

### CoolSwede — 2012-06-27T18:10:14+0300

> **janagram;34083 wrote:** welcome! Come on in, the water's really cold! (you'll love it!)

I know, I have regularly done cold showers since March/April. I have not yet tried the cold tub since I do not own one. However, the Baltic is not boiling hot.

### .lina — 2012-06-27T18:12:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33954>

hejhej  nice to see more swedes here!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-27T18:16:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-33957>

Welcome.......we love high latitude folks here because cold matches with LCHF diets......its the Diet Doctors missing link.

### CoolSwede — 2012-06-28T14:04:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-34123>

Tjena Lina and thank you Jack for your warm welcome! Yes, we up north have access to cold weather naturally but as every neolithic being, I have been wearing warm clothes to avoid catching a cold. (Horrible expression by the way, should we rename it to "catch a warmth" instead?!)

Yes, I believe that cold is the missing link and maybe that will be the key to evolve into superhumans. I have read Barry's thread about fasted training and I am mighty impressed to say the least.

It is also funny to hear other people's comments changing from "Are you really going to eat that much butter" to "Don't you feel cold wearing only that t-shirt outside?". Well well, maybe those are the voices of a paradigm shift.

Lina: I am really impressed by your photos. They make me really hungry!

See you 'round!

### caroline — 2012-06-30T08:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-lchf-center-of-gravity-sweden.2073/post-34550>

Welcome to this great forum - Good luck!

# Thread: The Maasai and salt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-06-27T15:54:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/post-33922>

There's an interesting post by Chris Masterjohn on the Maasai.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/06/04/the-maasai-an-interlude-what-does-all-this-sociocultural-and-historical-stuff-have-to-do-with-heart-disease/](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/06/04/the-maasai-an-interlude-what-does-all-this-sociocultural-and-historical-stuff-have-to-do-with-heart-disease/)

Among other things it touches on the matter of salt, which has come up for discussion here recently.

Bruce Taylor insisted:

> The Masai do not use salt. Their language contains no word for salt. Whereas their vegetarian neighbors and their cattle crave salt, these carnivores seem to obtain sufficient salt from the flesh and milk which they eat.
>
>
> This statement that they had no word for salt actually seems to be part of an attempt to claim the Maasai as "carnivores" - _since everyone knew that a more plant-based diet needs more salt_. (In truth it seems the Maasai did sometimes eat non-animal foods, although it tends to be more the women that did.)
>
>
>
> In fact:
>
>
>
> According to Moritz Merker, who lived among the Maasai for eight years around the turn of the twentieth century (described in part 2 of this series), the Maasai word for steppe salt is e munjÃ¡n, which they added to their sweet potatoes and beans, and the Maasai word for natron salt is e magÃ¡t, which they added to their chewing tobacco.

I find that quite interesting.

What's that phrase Jack has used? "High carb paleo and metcons" was it?

Well, official paleo may be against salt, but it seems to me that if people are going to eat sweet potatoes, then they'd better put some salt on them like the Maasai knew to do.

### janagram — 2012-06-27T17:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/post-33947>

plus the masai used blood as a food......very salty.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-27T18:27:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-maasai-and-salt.2074/post-33964>

I think Masterjohn gets a pass for this missteps because the Masai do use salt on their carbs.....anyone on a LC diet needs salt.....but that salt needs to be good salt with iodine......

# Thread: Cooling Head = Great Night's Sleep
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2012-06-27T16:40:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-33930>

Had to comment here that I had the BEST night's sleep in a very long time last night. Living in Houston means very hot and humid temperatures this time of year.

Last night I placed an ice sheet inside my pillowcase and covered the pillow top with a hand towel. Went to bed as usual. For me, falling asleep is not a problem - _staying asleep_ after midnight is. Usually wake up 4-5 times.

Lo and behold, I only woke up once. The sleep was so deep and refreshing. The alarm woke me 4AM and it took longer than usual to feel alert. Almost like waking from a drug induced sleep.

I will be testing this method again tonight...

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-27T17:45:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-33943>

Hmmm keep us posted! Sounds like you're onto something!

### Mackay — 2012-06-29T13:48:53+0300

Any more reports on cold pillow sleeping?

### fitness@home — 2012-06-29T17:39:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-34365>

My little experiment...

I am an early to bed, early to rise kind of person; i.e. M-F turn in 8:30PM and get up at 4AM. It is not fully dark when I go to bed these days (use face mask to cover eyes). Don't watch TV in the evening but I do surf and read on my iPad. We don't turn on any extra lighting in the family room.

Night #2: Forgot to wear blue blockers. Surfed for about an hour between 7-8PM. Husband had TV on in room. Slept ok, but not as well as first night. Woke up briefly maybe 2-3 times and was able to fall back asleep quickly.

Night #3: Blue blockers on in evening during my surf time (with husband watching TV in same room). GOOD night's sleep. One wake up before midnight - back asleep quickly. Felt well-rested at 4AM when I got out of bed.

In Conclusion - **Dark and cold** are conducive to restful sleep. But then, we all know that anyway...it is applying it to our daily life that counts.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-29T18:43:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooling-head-great-nights-sleep.2076/post-34377>

When I was overweight I used to sleep with ice bags on my carotid arteries to cool the blood going to my brain.......it raised several hormones to help me sleep to loose the weight fast. I did so many outside the box things it was hilarious......but my family stopped laughing in 11 months when they saw me transform before their eyes. Sometimes unconventional gives exceptional results if you let it.

[http://www.amazon.com/Ace-Reusable-Cold-Compress-Large/dp/B00717YEO8/ref=sr\_1\_2?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1341073081&sr=1-2&keywords=ace+reusable+cold+compress+large](http://http://www.amazon.com/Ace-Reusable-Cold-Compress-Large/dp/B00717YEO8/ref=sr_1_2?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1341073081&sr=1-2&keywords=ace+reusable+cold+compress+large)

I've been doing spot CT with these as well.They stay cold a long time.

# Thread: What really kicked grain into the human diet...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-06-27T18:14:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-33956>

Talking with my son (degree in anthropology) about grain in the diet, he explained the surprising thing that really brought grain into prominent use. War! The first armies. When large armies were needed to mobilize, how to get soldiers? They were paid with a bag of grain, which could be given to the family to provide for them while the men were gone as well as feed the men while on the move. Before that grain was a lesser diet factor. Suddenly grain is money. And war....the shocking truth!!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-27T18:17:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-33958>

Its called the Younger Dyras.......it will be in the few blog posts and has been in several of mine.

### janagram — 2012-06-27T21:28:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34014>

Oh, I have heard that too....not so sure, but of course the colonists in new england, many being of scotch-irish descent, made rye whiskey from rye, but when the tax on whiskey (Whiskey Rebellion) drove them down into Appalachia rye didn't grow well, so they made corn whiskey. (moonshine, etc.) another factor, while corn will only last so long, whiskey is longlived, and can be shipped. Now you see the wisdom in their ways! Grains are for making whiskey! (I'm an appreciator, but imbibing is now limited to a titch on a holiday.

### Michael — 2012-06-27T22:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34022>

> **August;34150 wrote:**
>
> This is what they saw as defining the diet for a civilized man. How wrong they were! Strange the dietitians are still making the same mistake nearly four millennia later.

This was my joke, of course.

As a matter of fact, I guess cereal grains _are what made settled society -- and hence "all this" -- possible. And the _Epic_ was, in that sense, correct. And, of course, countless numbers of people would die, if they vanished tomorrow. This is why Loren Cordain called his famous paper on them "Cereal Grains: Humanity’s Double-Edged Sword":

[http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf](http://http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf)

Brilliant paper. It's a clumsy metaphor, though -- a double-edged sword, such as a knight's sword, is no more a danger to its wielder than a single-edged sword, such as a saber or falchion.

But one gets what the "cuts both ways" metaphor is getting at. They're bad for health, yet we rely on them (although, I guess, how long that reliance can continue and intensive arable farming can go on without destroying soil-structure is the big question.)

But, you don't actually need cereal grains to have civilization, including a high-level literary culture. Iceland proves that. The sagas are a major literary achievement even on a European-wide scale - and a unique one. In some respects they anticipate the novel.

Yet medieval Iceland got by without grain. People lived principally on meat, seafood, and dairy products such as skyr_. It was one of Vilhjalmur Stefansson's first achievements as an anthropologists to do a survey on Icelandic teeth and to demonstrate that in the early period they were mostly free from cavities at the time when bread was rarely available, because the climate was too cold to grow grain.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-27T22:43:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34032>

Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies by Jared M. Diamond states â€œThe adoption of agriculture, supposedly our most decisive step toward a better life, was in many ways a catastrophe from which we have never recovered.â€￾

Also, he explains how grain societies could support a ruling class.

"In contrast, once food can be stockpiled, a political elite can gain control of food produced by others, assert the right of taxation, escape the need to feed itself, and engage full-time in political activities."

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-27T23:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34036>

> **Nonchalant;34172 wrote:** Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies by Jared M. Diamond states “The adoption of agriculture, supposedly our most decisive step toward a better life, was in many ways a catastrophe from which we have never recovered.”
>
>
>
> Also, he explains how grain societies could support a ruling class.
>
> "In contrast, once food can be stockpiled, a political elite can gain control of food produced by others, assert the right of taxation, escape the need to feed itself, and engage full-time in political activities."

THis is a great book...from many perspectives... I was thinking about this last week

### Michael — 2012-06-27T23:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-really-kicked-grain-into-the-human-diet.2078/post-34048>

Oh, dear the board software has malfunctioned and lost an earlier post of mine when I attempted to add a comment. All that about beer, bread and Gilgamesh down the drain ....

Never mind. I had finished:

> This is what they saw as defining the diet for a civilized man. How wrong they were! Strange the dietitians are still making the same mistake nearly four millennia later.

This was my joke, of course.

As a matter of fact, I guess cereal grains _are what made settled society -- and hence "all this" -- possible. And the _Epic_ was, in that sense, correct. And, of course, countless numbers of people would die, if they vanished tomorrow. This is why Loren Cordain called his famous paper on them "Cereal Grains: Humanity’s Double-Edged Sword":

[http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf](http://http://www.direct-ms.org/pdf/EvolutionPaleolithic/Cereal%20Sword.pdf)

Brilliant paper. It's a clumsy metaphor, though -- a double-edged sword, such as a knight's sword, is no more a danger to its wielder than a single-edged sword, such as a saber or falchion.

But one gets what the "cuts both ways" metaphor is getting at. They're bad for health, yet we rely on them (although, I guess, how long that reliance can continue and intensive arable farming can go on without destroying soil-structure is the big question.)

But, you don't actually need cereal grains to have civilization, including a high-level literary culture. Iceland proves that. The sagas are a major literary achievement even on a European-wide scale - and a unique one. In some respects they anticipate the novel.

Yet medieval Iceland got by without grain. People lived principally on meat, seafood, and dairy products such as skyr_. It was one of Vilhjalmur Stefansson's first achievements as an anthropologists to do a survey on Icelandic teeth and to demonstrate that in the early period they were mostly free from cavities at the time when bread was rarely available, because the climate was too cold to grow grain.

# Thread: Vitamin D
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-28T03:59:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/post-34090>

[http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary\_supplements/vitamin\_d\_calcium](http://http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary_supplements/vitamin_d_calcium)

Vitamin D and Calcium for Better Blood Sugar

> New Recommendations
>
> But that caution has largely evaporated over the past several years, mainly because those old studies have been rejected due to poor-quality science. Since then vitamin D has entered a nutritional and medical renaissance of sorts.
>
>
>
> Now experts like Michael Holick, M.D., of the Boston University School of Medicine, and Walter C. Willett, M.D., of the Harvard School of Public Health, stress vitamin Dâ€™s safety â€” and recommend much larger amounts.
>
>
>
> An article in Archives of Internal Medicine (2009) reports that vitamin D levels among Americans have decreased over the past 10 or so years. Researchers note that three of every four people do not have adequate vitamin D levels. The numbers may be even worse during the winter months when the sun is low and people spend more time indoors, which interferes with vitamin D production.
>
>
>
> **The growing consensus is that every man, women, child, and infant should take 1,000 IU of vitamin D daily or 2,000 IU if they have a dark complexion. That may sound like a lot compared to the current 200 IU, but you would have to take 50,000 IU daily â€” practically a whole bottle a day â€” for months before you would develop any symptoms of toxicity.**

**

this article is a bit confusing for me... I believe I have calcium in cells versus magnesium because** of my sugar levels. I was hoping to kind of use the glucose testing as means to determine calcium. If anyone has a clue or thought, please share.

### chocolate — 2012-06-28T04:05:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/post-34092>

[http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary\_supplements/vitamin\_D\_calcium.page01](http://http://www.dlife.com/diabetes-food-and-fitness/dietary_supplements/vitamin_D_calcium.page01)

> Vitamin D & Calcium for Better Blood Sugar (continued)
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> In a 2007 review of the research, researchers noted that vitamin D and calcium work hand in hand to influence the body’s secretion of insulin, the hormone that controls blood sugar levels.
>
>
>
> In one study of 314 healthy adults, vitamin D appears to have offset or prevented age-related increase in blood glucose levels. In the control group, participants averaged a 6.1 mg/dl increase in fasting blood sugar over three years. _**However, people taking vitamin D (700 IU daily) and calcium (500 mg daily) supplements had virtually no change in their fasting blood sugar levels, according to the 2007 report in Diabetes Care.**_**
>
>
>
> Early in 2009, doctors at the Harvard School of Public Health found that low vitamin D and calcium levels were related to higher, unhealthy levels of C-peptide, a common marker of insulin function. A separate study by researchers at the University of California, Los Angeles, found that people with low vitamin D levels were more likely to have insulin resistance (a form of prediabetes) and also abnormalities in beta cells, which make insulin in the pancreas.**
>
>
>
> In 2008 Australian researchers reported on their use of vitamin D in the treatment of 51 patients with type 2 diabetes who had nerve problems, including numbness, tingling, and burning. After taking an average of 2,000 IU daily for three months, pain decreased by roughly half.
>
>
>
> Call Your Doc
>
> None of this means you should start treating yourself with a high dose of vitamin D. However, it’s probably worthwhile having your vitamin D and calcium blood levels tested and discussing with your doctor the possibility of taking supplements and an appropriate dosage for you. We live in the age of the proactive healthcare consumer: Call your doctor’s office and get the answers you need.

Does anyone know where the blood calcium levels are that they are referring to? If its in your blood, does that mean magnesium is in your cells? I know they didn't even mention the vitamin k2.

### Michael — 2012-06-28T21:02:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-d.2081/post-34212>

> **chocolate;34247 wrote:**
>
> Does anyone know where the blood calcium levels are that they are referring to?
>
>
> I read the article you linked, and it seemed to me that they were referring to _dietary levels of Vitamin D and calcium. (It turns out that was only partially true.)
>
>
>
> I tried to find the study by Harvard researchers that they refer to. So I googled:
>
>
>
> pubmed 2009 vitamin d calcium c-peptide
>
>
>
> This is what I came up with:
>
>
>
> [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19141703](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19141703)
>
>
>
> Note the title is:
>
>
>
>
> > Plasma C-peptide is inversely associated with calcium intake in women and with plasma 25-hydroxy vitamin D in men.
>
>
>
> So the article you originally found seems to be conflating calcium intake in women and plasma 25-hydroxy vitamin D in men.
>
>
>
> Not a good start.
>
>
>
> The Abstract begins:
>
>
>
> The consumption of calcium, vitamin D, and dairy products may be associated with a reduced risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus. However, whether this reduction is due to calcium, vitamin D, or other components of dairy products is not clear ...._

_

I'm not inclined to worry much about what they think "may" be true or take a lot of notice of which "component" they're hanging their hat on while admitting that nothing's "clear". Maybe I'm just a cynic.

I think the safest advice is to take some vitamin D if you can't get out in the sun much and to have your plasma level of 25-hydroxy vitamin D checked. You can look at people's recommendations for what that level should be online. Professor Cordain suggests to shoot for a level of 60 ng/ml. Maybe Jack suggests a level somewhere on the site.

If you do push your vitamin D level up, then you probably want to make sure that you're getting adequate vitamin A, too, since they seem to work synergistically. The occasional serving of liver from a grass-fed animal would be a way to get that.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/01/22/new-evidence-of-synergy-between-vitamins-a-and-d-protection-against-autoimmune-diseases/](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/blogs/cmasterjohn/2012/01/22/new-evidence-of-synergy-between-vitamins-a-and-d-protection-against-autoimmune-diseases/)

I wouldn't supplement calcium, whatever the article implies. (Heck, they didn't even seem to have read the Abstract of the Harvard study properly: "whether this reduction is due to calcium, vitamin D, or other components of dairy products is not clear ..._".) And calcium supplements certainly are associated with increased risk of stroke and heart attack.

# Thread: Vilhjalmur Stefansson and the Icelanders
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-06-28T10:35:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/post-34117>

There was an earlier discussion in which Vilhjalmur Stefansson and Iceland came up. I thought it would be interesting to post references for that, so I googled "stefansson iceland skulls teeth". Here's what I found.

There's this, which is an article Stefansson wrote for _Harper's Monthly Magazine_ that someone's put online:

> **My first anthropological commission was from the Peabody Museum of Harvard University when they sent John W. Hasting and me to Iceland in 1905. We found in one place a medieval graveyard that was being cut away by the sea. Skulls were rolling about in the water at high tide, at low tide we gathered them and picked up scattered teeth here and there. As wind and water shifted the sands we found more and more teeth until there was a handful. Later we got permission to excavate the cemetery, and eventually we brought with us to Harvard a miscellaneous lot of bone which included 80 skull, and as said, a great many loose teeth.
>
>
>
> The collection has been studied by dentists and physical anthropologists without the discovery of a single cavity in even one tooth.**
>
>
>
> The skulls in the Hastings-Stefansson collection represent persons of ordinary Icelandic blood. There were no aborigines in that island when the Irish discovered it some time before 700 A. D. When the Norsemen got there in 860 they found no people except the Irish. It is now variously estimated that in origin the Icelanders are from 10 percent to 30 percent Irish, 40 percent to 50 percent Norwegian, the remainder, perhaps 10 percent, from Scotland, England, Sweden, and Denmark.
>
>
>
> None of the people whose blood went into the Icelandic stock are racially immune to tooth decay, nor are the modern Icelanders. Then why were the Icelanders of the Middle Ages immune?
>
> ....

[http://www.biblelife.org/stefansson3.htm](http://http://www.biblelife.org/stefansson3.htm)

and this:

[http://blog.zeroinginonhealth.com/2009/01/25/visit-your-dentist-twice-a-year-part-1/](http://http://blog.zeroinginonhealth.com/2009/01/25/visit-your-dentist-twice-a-year-part-1/)

And some references get pulled up from Google Books.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-28T14:41:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/post-34129>

immune to cavities? nope they had no access to carbs.

### Michael — 2012-06-29T12:09:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vilhjalmur-stefansson-and-the-icelanders.2082/post-34332>

I suppose you're interested in the Icelander's access to cold, too, Dr. Kruse.

It's interesting that they seem to have a cultural fascination with feats of strength going back a long way - presumably back to viking times:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strength\_athletics\_in\_Iceland](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strength_athletics_in_Iceland)

### ealachan — 2012-07-21T12:38:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-39965>

My husband was just recently diagnosed as diabetic (he's only been doing paleo / optimal about half the time and eating SAD the rest of the time, so he didn't do himself any favors there), and since he pretty much has no clue what being diabetic means, I literally had to make a "stuff that will eventually kill you if you keep eating it" list for him yesterday. I also made a "things you SHOULD eat" list to go with it. I printed out two copies - one to hang on the fridge, and the other one he put in his wallet so that he could refer to it while at work (where most of the bad eating was happening).

Trying to explain to him this morning why bananas and a chocolate bar for breakfast are not a good idea was interesting. I've got my work cut out for me...oy!

### chocolate — 2012-07-21T17:23:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-39999>

> **ealachan;40152 wrote:** My husband was just recently diagnosed as diabetic (he's only been doing paleo / optimal about half the time and eating SAD the rest of the time, so he didn't do himself any favors there), and since he pretty much has no clue what being diabetic means, I literally had to make a "stuff that will eventually kill you if you keep eating it" list for him yesterday. I also made a "things you SHOULD eat" list to go with it. Maybe you could make him a smoothie. Its not optimal, but its baby steps. My son loves doing that. We bought one of those little cheapie individual blenders for like 14 bucks and it lasted a couple of years. That thing was rode hard. The cups are perfection. I bet you could make something so fantastic that he forgets all about ice cream. I used to do the Mars Venus milkshakes. They were pretty great.

### terryfischer39@yahoo.com — 2012-07-26T08:37:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-41166>

> **ealachan;40152 wrote:** My husband was just recently diagnosed as diabetic (he's only been doing paleo / optimal about half the time and eating SAD the rest of the time, so he didn't do himself any favors there), and since he pretty much has no clue what being diabetic means, I literally had to make a "stuff that will eventually kill you if you keep eating it" list for him yesterday. I also made a "things you SHOULD eat" list to go with it. I printed out two copies - one to hang on the fridge, and the other one he put in his wallet so that he could refer to it while at work (where most of the bad eating was happening).
>
>
>
> Trying to explain to him this morning why bananas and a chocolate bar for breakfast are not a good idea was interesting. I've got my work cut out for me...oy!

I used to teach diabetics and found that they hate to feel or be deprived, blood sugar issues hypo/hyper and all that, it's tricky especially when you have to take blood sugar lowering meds as well. So had some luck with adding on instead of taking away. Example: half a banana, 3-4 raw eggs, Tbs of chocolate, tsp to tbs of coconut oil and heavy cream and plenty of ice cubes and maybe some stevia for a morning milk shake. Go easy on the oil at first and build up. Get him to agree to eat some protein with every junk food snack. Perhaps a container of 4 glasses of water which he should finish before the end of work each day. (this doesn't include water he should be drinking before and after work). He shouldn't let himself feel hungry so give him plenty of high protein snacks for work. hard boiled eggs, pemmican, nuts, whatever you all can agree on. He should eat one before any junk food snack. You all should be reading everything you can on DM and he should be as knowledgeable as an MD. Dr Richard Bernstein has a very thorough book on DM basics. So as his nutrition improves he can gradually cut out the junk food and be more secure at work that he won't be having stomach pain. Writing down everything eaten every day for a while (like a couple of years) and how many ounces and how much protein, fat and CHO is immensely helpful. I use Fit Day as my online log as it calculates all that. Of course blood sugar monitoring with a meter before and after meals is a must. I'm a firm believer in the BAB and if it's not the perfect one at first at least it's a start. Pretty soon he'll realize he feels better and when he's telling you what to do then you know you've got him where you want him  Good Luck, I know it's not an easy task.

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T14:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54083>

> **ealachan;40152 wrote:** My husband was just recently diagnosed as diabetic (he's only been doing paleo / optimal about half the time and eating SAD the rest of the time, so he didn't do himself any favors there), and since he pretty much has no clue what being diabetic means, I literally had to make a "stuff that will eventually kill you if you keep eating it" list for him yesterday. I also made a "things you SHOULD eat" list to go with it. I printed out two copies - one to hang on the fridge, and the other one he put in his wallet so that he could refer to it while at work (where most of the bad eating was happening).
>
>
>
> Trying to explain to him this morning why bananas and a chocolate bar for breakfast are not a good idea was interesting. I've got my work cut out for me...oy!

I was declared pre-diabetic back in September 2011 with an A1c of 11.2. I was already gung-ho in my talks with the Endocrinologist about a lower carbohydrate intake and deleting everything from the grains-group. She had given me the green light.

Fast forward three months - November 2011, I had a 12hr fasting done and my A1c DROPPED to 5.2, my cholesterol did a massive flip that lit up my home telephone of three practitioners all begging me "what did you do to get such a great CHO and drop your A1c so fast?!?!?"... My PCP, My Endo and my Dermatologist all called me when they got the lab results, and I told them the truth: no breads, no rice, no pasta, no corn, no flour, no milk, no soymilk.... that is EXACTLY what I did. My husband lost 25lbs in that 3 month time frame with the food changes I made in the house and even he said he felt like he ate better! He limits his consumption of them to only when he is out on the road where he is a truck driver...

Im telling you - that whole category of foods is a definite issue... my HDL right now is 86 - my Endo just stood there and shook her head - she couldnt believe someone with a metabolic disorder could achieve that...

now if we can just figure out why my body wont drop the fat despite my dedication to much better food choices, Ill be happier... ugh...

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T15:00:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54093>

> **Jack Kruse;54790 wrote:** well dont tell some of the paleo bloggers this.......they will start a war with you over it. Congrats!!!

Dr.Kruse - I honestly could care less, LOL!!!!! The fact that one food group created such a hell for me, and was EASILY rectified by simply removing it from the equation has angered many of the RD's in the physician group I work in. The die-hard bread-lovers I work with in my AR department cant believe I could ever give up such a thing... Honestly, Id rather put a roll in the bell of my Bassoon, hit a low Bflat and watch that roll catapult into the air while someone takes a good swing with a bat to it! BWAHAHAHAHAHHAA!!!!

My CHO one year later (labs done in June 2012) still remain the same - maintained all of the numbers, but everything improved even more by a few points, A1c maintained.

To the reply for throw the scale away - the only scale I use religiously is the food scale when it comes to the carbohydrates. The 'other' scale is used once per month but Im not foolish to think its accurate.. I would much rather use measuring tape and current clothing as the true form of measurment.

I have been able to drop about 40+ pounds on my own, but I am in the worst plateau possible... that is why I am here on this site. Im hoping that the missing puzzle piece to my body holding on to the weight can be found here... I need to drop the remaining 100lbs.. but I sure as hell wont rely on an Endo telling me to take Qsymia... that is just frigging rediculous... Im on enough damn prescriptions right now and would love to get rid of all of them....

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T15:32:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54101>

### nonchalant — 2012-09-26T00:56:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54277>

> **cramernh;54797 wrote:** The fact that one food group created such a hell for me, and was EASILY rectified by simply removing it from the equation has angered many of the RD's in the physician group I work in. The die-hard bread-lovers I work with in my AR department cant believe I could ever give up such a thing...

I think it makes a lot of folks angry, because they imagine themselves trying to do without grains/starches, and it's a scary scenario for them. They don't realize that all those yummy starches can be replaced by unlimited yummy proteins and fats. They see it as a door closing, not a door opening.

### cramernh — 2012-09-26T12:29:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54380>

> **Nonchalant;55004 wrote:** I think it makes a lot of folks angry, because they imagine themselves trying to do without grains/starches, and it's a scary scenario for them. They don't realize that all those yummy starches can be replaced by unlimited yummy proteins and fats. They see it as a door closing, not a door opening.

The funny part is that, my former employers - a total of five Gastroenterologists FULLY supported me. One jokingly reminded me of my responsibility at age 50 despite my not eating breads/grains, but they were all for it. They also advocate the reduction/elimination of most dairy products as well... it was AWESOME knowing the surgeons I handled the AR for, were fully supportive. Especially when the drug reps would come in with lunches. UGHHHHHHHH drive me to drink, I cant believe the foods they were bringing in... what an oxymoron! The only time the surgeons would join in with the lunches, it would be when it was all fresh foods. I hardly ever saw them eat junk... so I know Im not the only crazy one! LOL!

### cramernh — 2012-09-26T12:42:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kitchen-reference.2089/post-54382>

The other side of this equation is the fact them damn RD's are so mind-screwed into the "MYPLATE" concept, you have to eat breads, grains, blah blah blah... Honestly speaking I could care less about that philosophy. Its a one-method plan that they _think_ is appropriate for everyone.. well sorry, thats not the kind of crack I get involved in, thank you very much. I had an argument with a supposed RD on myfitnesspal.com last month stating that RD's would probably be better off working in conjunction with a PCP, or a Specialist to collaborate nutritional care with the patient because their methods are absolutely askewed. She didnt like that. She feels her Master's Degree should hold the same weight as an MD.... I CRACKED UP LAUGHING. I asked her "are you versed in diagnosing patients? Can you honestly sit there - the keyboard commando you are trying to pull off, and tell me you know the complete medical history of Stein-Leventhal, how it was discovered, what it was referring to, the year it was declared, the proper treatments of a patient with Stein-Leventhal, the proper tests to run and actually to read and understand the labs with your female patients who are Stein-Leventhal??? Dont sit there and tell me your RD should weigh the same as an MD - it just doesnt work that way in real life.... She tried to rip me a new oriface with the whole elimination of the bread/grains group citing I am not reaping the benefits of whole grains and the much needed fiber for my colon. I asked her "I think you forgot about the fiber content of vegetables - its much more natural and not processed as the precious bread you are praising."

She thought it was damn near impossible to completely irradicate my A1c - I told her "keep on smoking that crack because Ive got labs to prove it - and Im trained to be able to read and understand them as well"... yeah.. I got cranky.. I dont 'do' well with Keyboard Commandos, LOL!

I scanned my labs onto the thread (and blackened out my personal info with the exception of my last name) and told her to mind her RD-mouth when she tells people 'its not possible', because it bloody well is possible.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Sources of Seafood for Those Who Live Far From the Sea
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Chrysanthemama — 2012-06-28T23:19:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-34234>

Does anyone have reasonably-priced sources of seafood they could recommend? I am in the mountains of NM, in a one-grocery town, and the quality of seafood here is the pits. I am considering doing bulk purchases of salmon, cod, halibut, and crab from AK or WA, but I thought I would ask to see if anyone is already doing this or something similar, and could make recommendations.

I found companies like this: [http://www.freshseafood.com/fresh-salmon.html](http://http://www.freshseafood.com/fresh-salmon.html)

which look awesome, but I'd also hope for a wholesale source, if possible. For the past two years I've bought a half a grass-fed, high-altitude raised steer, but this year we have permits for elk, so hopefully we'll be successful, and I'm thinking I could invest the grass-fed budget into seafood.

### vlynnb — 2012-06-28T23:30:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-34238>

Good question from my standpoint in the Rocky Mts. also. I will be interested in the responses. I also have the problem of budget in regards to purchasing the seafood. Your plan to hunt Elk has got me thinking. I grew up on deer and elk meat but as an adult I have an aversion to killing. I have always felt if I could buy beef instead that was somehow more acceptable to me. That could come from the incident when I shot my first deer, a little doe, that turned and looked at me with her big, soft, brown eyes just as I fired. I cried and have never hunted since. Maybe now that I can't afford the grass fed beef and good seafood I can justify hunting again and do as you suggest with applying the money saved to a seafood budget.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-29T01:42:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-34276>

Sardines in a can are available everywhere and dirt cheap.

### BJK77 — 2012-06-29T03:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-34301>

I have a local buying club I purchase wild Alaskan salmon through and I get some other random types like shrimp, crab, scallops etc at Whole Foods or Trader Joe's, but I've also stocked up on canned sardines, salmon and tuna from amazon. I like the brand Wild Planet - no bpa and good quality, sustainable products. You can get some decent deals too if you do the subscribe and save option: [http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb\_sb\_noss\_2?url=search-alias%3Daps&field-keywords=wild+planet](http://http://www.amazon.com/s/ref=nb_sb_noss_2?url=search-alias%3Daps&field-keywords=wild+planet)

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-06-29T18:45:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-34378>

Ahh...sardines. Hadn't even considered them. And oysters, too, I presume. I'm on it!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-30T23:56:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-34682>

Here are my sources for canned and frozen products. Unfortunately, they are for western Canada.

[http://eatkamloops.org/searching-for-seafood/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/searching-for-seafood/)

We also make regular trips to the coast and find local sources. Would this be possible for you to do? If so, you can find amazing sources. We just pick up 10 sockeye salmon from a Native food fishery, frozen prawns loaded with roe, and oysters picked off the beach. I can't begin to tell you how great it is to get the whole fish. You can eat all the "waste" parts of the fish such as: bones, head, roe and organs. Fillets and steaks become almost mundane after eating the waste parts. You can also collect seaweed and sea grass and dry them for later use.

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34842=288-native-food-fishery.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34842=289-whole-sockeye-salmon.jpg)

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/34842=290-iced-oysters.jpg)

Have you considered taking up fishing or finding a fisherman friend? In our local lakes around Kamloops we have beautiful trout. I am lucky to have fishermen friends that gift me trout on a regular basis.

### RobH — 2012-09-10T21:40:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-50766>

Do trout count as seafood? Lake water very different than ocean water. I'm just wondering where fresh water fish fit on the Eli-paleo template.

RobH, eating trout for lunch

### nonchalant — 2012-09-10T22:26:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-50772>

I was thinking about this issue for when I move from the city. I think I will be stocking various sorts of canned/frozen seafood available locally, and I will be making periodic trips to the Big City to visit relatives and fill the ice chests. Love that dry ice.

RobH, fresh water fish definitely count, along with crawfish and such. But I wouldn't want them as my only source.

### Jonathin — 2012-09-10T22:39:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-50775>

After searching for seafood options , I discovered THE seafood wholesaler for western Colorado about 45 min away. They sell to all the resorts and high end restaurants and they sell from a small shop to the public 3 days per week. They definitely have a wide range of product. For instance I was able to get a 1/2 gal of refrigerated shelled oysters for $40. I am hoping soon to get a substantial quantity of some fish that is in season, wild-caught, high DHA, etc for a discounted price and stash in one of my freezers like the 1/2 a cow or pig that we usually get. Otherwise I would agree that canned sardines (and canned mackerl) are a good buy, readily available and are not co-mingled with junk ingredients.

### btwimberly — 2012-09-16T06:44:35+0300

Jonathin. Can you say more about your Colorado seafood source? I am in Denver and would love to find raw unsalted unprocessed roe.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-16T14:37:09+0300

### Momma28 — 2012-09-16T16:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-51920>

The prices on fish are ridiculous! The only option for our family will have to be high quality fish oils. Dr. K, is their one you like best? Currently using Barleans Salmon oil and Mercola's Krill w/ Evening Primrose. Six kids still at home and one in college keeps the budget pretty tight. We do grassfed beef ( local farmer slaughters for us each year) and pastured chickens and sometimes eggs for our protein. I can get some fish at Sam's Club once in a while, but it doesn't seem to hold the kids and I have to cook so much of it to satisfy them. Everyone eats Paleo so no grains or sugars for any of us. We have done the Specific Carbohydrate Diet since 2007 for a couple of the kids that are on the spectrum and it works out fine for us and is pretty much Paleo style.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-16T17:42:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-51927>

The prices of modern medicine are even more sick. Do what you must. Sardines are dirt cheap! High quality fish oils are not going to help you.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-16T17:46:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-51928>

Here are two comments I made at the blog over a year ago about this issue:

Jack says

My concern is that fish oil supplements are becoming the “proxy” for good dietary choices in our

paleo/primal community (see Wolf's ridiculous fish oil rec's in his book). Once you get your diet repaired and back to ancestral balances of O6/O3 fats, then you can titrate the effective dose of supplemental O3 oils to your desired O6/O3 ratio

based upon your own blood work. This is the context I use for myself and patients.

Jack says:

Im a large fan of obtaining O3 from **food first as you know. But those who paleo with regular grain feed meats need to do something. And same thing with farmed fish……but sardines are dirt cheap. The only people I push fish oil with are the real sick ones. Jack realize who I see in my clinic is not representative of most people on MDA or PH etc…….You guys are way better off than most of my patients. I think y'all forget that context sometime.** Those people who usually have severe pregnenolone steal or seriously overweight need drastic refurbishing. there is no way to avoid the fish oil there. I am very cautious with fish oil in neurodegenerative diseases for the reason mentioned in the blog (AHS 2011 blog). Again this is not who we see on PH or MDA either

Ficat de cod - 58%, sare, foi de dafin si piper negru - 42%

Greutatea neta:121g masa neta a componetei solide:60g.

### AKMan — 2012-09-16T19:40:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-51946>

> **Momma28;52535 wrote: The prices on fish are ridiculous! The only option for our family will have to be high quality fish oils. Dr. K, is their one you like best? Currently using Barleans Salmon oil and Mercola's Krill w/ Evening Primrose. Six kids still at home and one in college keeps the budget pretty tight. We do grassfed beef ( local farmer slaughters for us each year) and pastured chickens and sometimes eggs for our protein. I can get some fish at Sam's Club once in a while, but it doesn't seem to hold the kids and I have to cook so much of it to satisfy them. Everyone eats Paleo so no grains or sugars for any of us. We have done the Specific Carbohydrate Diet since 2007 for a couple of the kids that are on the spectrum and it works out fine for us and is pretty much Paleo style.
>
>
> Jack Kruse;52542 wrote:** The prices of modern medicine are even more sick. Do what you must. Sardines are dirt cheap! High quality fish oils are not going to help you.

Can I try an alternative answer?

Momma28 - if your family's health is great, you are doing just fine. Teach your kids to make good food decisions and hopefully they will live long, healthy lives. If any of you have health issues, examine your diet, light exposure, and seasonal rythms. If none of you have health issues, try to eat seafood several times a week, even if just canned sardines. If you are doing all this and your hormones/labs aren't where you want, then consider adding in way more seafood and wearing the crazy yellow glasses while eating seasonally and getting cold. Good luck and let your labs be your guide.

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-16T19:59:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-51950>

> **jib;52559 wrote:** I was wondering if someone could please translate this from a can of King Oscar cod livers I just received in the mail:
>
>
>
> Ficat de cod - 58%, sare, foi de dafin si piper negru - 42%
>
> Greutatea neta:121g masa neta a componetei solide:60g.
>
> Temperatura de pastrare de la +4C la +25C.
>
> A se consuma de preferinta inainte de data: vezi capacul.
>
>
>
> Thanks much
>
>
>
>
> According to [Google Translate](http://http://translate.google.no/?hl=no&tab=wT#auto/en/Ficat%20de%20cod%20-%2058%25%2C%20sare%2C%20foi%20de%20dafin%20si%20piper%20negru%20-%2042%25%0D%0A%20Greutatea%20neta%3A121g%20masa%20neta%20a%20componetei%20solide%3A60g.%0D%0A%20Temperatura%20de%20pastrare%20de%20la%20%2B4C%20la%20%2B25C.%0D%0A%20A%20se%20consuma%20de%20preferinta%20inainte%20de%20data%3A%20vezi%20capacul.), that's Romanian for: Cod liver - 58%, salt, bay leaf and black pepper - 42%
>
> Net weight: 121g net weight of solid Components: 60g.
>
> Storage temperature from +4 C to +25 C.
>
> To be consumed preferably before date: see lid.

That's rather a lot of bay leaf and pepper.. This sounds like a different product than the cans of King Oscar "Lofot" canned cod liver oil I have in my fridge. The label on that says "Ingredients: Fresh cod liver. Salt" and then goes on to list the contents, per 100g:

Total fats: 60g

Saturated: 10g

Monounsaturated: 7.5g

Polyunsaturated: 22g

of which Omega-3: 7g

Salt: 0.7g

Protein: 7g

Carbs: 0%

I also checked a can of Bornholms (a Danish brand) canned cod liver, which was also contained only liver and salt, but with a bit different numbers:

Total fats: 40g

Saturated: 6.5g

Monounsaturated: 10.9g

Polyunsaturated: 13g

Omega-3: 9.1g

Protein: 8g

Carbs: 1g

(I read that as saying that total polysaturated is 9.1+13g)

A while back, I wrote to King Oscar customer support and asked them about BPA, and they informed me that all their cans are guaranteed not to contain any BPA. I did not ask if that holds true for all markets as well as for Norway. As they seem to have production facilities in many countries, so I guess there could be some variation in terms of compliance.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-16T23:46:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-51980>

For Colorado - A couple of the gals on here have mentioned H-mart (I've never been there!).

I have a place here in Longmont, where they will order whatever you want - and they stock quite a bit. It's all sustainable etc. . . but it's not cheap. Although, cheaper than whole foods most of the time.

We have a fun restaurant here that does $1 oysters for happy hour (same price I see them at WF and my fish place). . . so come on up for a happy hour

### Momma28 — 2012-09-19T01:48:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-52564>

> **AKMan;52561 wrote:** Can I try an alternative answer?
>
>
>
> Momma28 - if your family's health is great, you are doing just fine. Teach your kids to make good food decisions and hopefully they will live long, healthy lives. If any of you have health issues, examine your diet, light exposure, and seasonal rythms. If none of you have health issues, try to eat seafood several times a week, even if just canned sardines. If you are doing all this and your hormones/labs aren't where you want, then consider adding in way more seafood and wearing the crazy yellow glasses while eating seasonally and getting cold. Good luck and let your labs be your guide.

Thanks AKman...very encouraging post. A mother beats herself up enough when it comes to her children. I probably fair better than most, though. I have 8 of them and they have never been sickly kids. We stopped vaccines 15 years ago and 5 or the youngest 6 are nonvaxed except for the HepB given in the hospital against wishes. The youngest is addopted and has had 22 before we got him. He is full autistic. Didn't even know his name at 16 months old. It took me a week to change his diet of white bread toast, bananas and commercial milk microwaved in a bottle. No eye contact, no physical affection, just living in his own world. I put him on the Specific Carb Diet He had never had meat and was having minor seizures from lack of dietary fats. He had spastic palsy like movements with his hands also. Seizures stopped in first week with addition of organic butter and other fatty foods such as avocado. He wouldn't eat for three days because it wasn't his diet of choice. He finally gave in after waking up in a full shake from low blood sugar. Then he was ready to eat anything. Now at 23 months he's happy, no more spastic movements, plenty of eye contact and loves affection and attention. He has grown 5 inches in the 7 months I've dhad him, knows his name and is learning all of our names...no easy feat even for an adult. lol I paint him with Lugol's solution and magnesium oil as well. His Vit D3 is optimized and he wears a B12 patch by Vie once a week. I'm already doing everything I can and know to do and I'm still learning. Everybody that saw this little boy when he got here can't believe its the same child. Our other kids never have more than a cold. They had chicken pox once years ago. That's it in 22 years of parenting. They see the pediatrician at one week old and never see him again until I have another baby and I won't be having anymore. I have 12 year olds in my home who have NEVER been on an antibiotic. Never even had an ear infection. So I must be doing something right. I hate fish and have to make myself eat it so I used to rarely make it for the family. But I have made an effort to cook it more often. I have just always added a 15% Lugols Solution I had custom mixed for me from Ecuador...( herbhealers.com, once it became illegal here and I couldn't get it from Walmart pharmacy anymore) ... to fresh squeezed lemonaid or just painted it on their feet and thyroid area. I cured myself of uterine fibroids, fibrocystic breasts, PCOS and a goiter on my thyroid with it as well. Every bit of it gone. It may not be OPTIMAL but it works and is doable.

### Momma28 — 2012-09-20T00:20:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-52791>

Hi Caroline,

No, I wasn't raised healthy at all....mother was a single parent, welfare recipient, alcoholic and fed us out of cans or boxes. When I became a mother I wanted to be everything she wasn't. Somehow I just knew home cooking was best and it just must be God that has led me to ditch the vaccines and buck the system. I've had Docs get up in my face and challenge me on the vaccine issues and never backed down because I armed myself with information to fight with. I felt down inside that something was terribly wrong with the system and I am grateful every day that I saved my kids from any further damage. I haven't had a vax since 5 years old. Back then you got four or five of them when you were ready for kindergarten. That was it. Now it's at 36 and counting by 4 years of age. So, to answer your question...it must have been instinct. I new before I married that I would homeschool as well. Been at it 22 years now! ....Thanks

### caroline — 2012-09-20T02:01:22+0300

### Momma28 — 2012-09-21T04:32:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sources-of-seafood-for-those-who-live-far-from-the-sea.2095/post-53101>

Caroline,

Yes, hubby is very supportive. He's a simple man and trusts whatever I do. He's about to be 50 and has the body of an 18 year old, per his yearly physical with the govt. So I'd say he has a reason to trust me. When I met him at 22 he had just been diagnosed with Hypertension and his sugar level was right behind that. He couldn't sit anywhere for 5 minutes without falling asleep... a sure sign of high sugar. All of his family have both of these as well. Of course, he is free from all of it now and his family can't believe how slim he and I stay.

And yes, I agree it is truly a God thing for me to be nothing like my parents. They were very broken, dysfunctional people without a compass or boundaries to live by. God is the one thing that has made my life different than theirs or my siblings. In time I have been able to influence my two younger brothers to come out of the gangster life and drug dealing and become productive citizens. But sadly, my two sisters have not faired as well. The brokenness perpetuates in their lives. We were subjected to sexual abuse and that is something that is very difficult to overcome without a compass. You are very empty and you don't know why...until God shows you why.

And lastly, perhaps because of my past, I will fight to the death for my children's safety. I realized when the first one was born that his only protection in this world was his parents and I am well aware of what can happen when we aren't vigilant. But I never would have thought I would have to protect them from my own govt. My sex therapist told me last year that I had the highest degree of emotional intelligence of any human being she had ever come across. I don't know what that is...I just know what I feel. I guess it works.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: How can stalls even happen?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-stalls-even-happen.2096/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jmathisen — 2012-06-28T23:23:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-stalls-even-happen.2096/post-34235>

I've been on LR&CT for 5 weeks. Lost 37 lbs from 302 to 265 pretty steadily. Been stuck at 265 though for several days, and I guess I'm just unsure how that is possible?

I eat pretty much the same thing everyday. 50-60 grams of protein at breakfast, no lunch, 40 grams of protein (almost always sardines/fish) at dinner, and 1 tblspoon of CO at morning and at night. I try to keep carbs under 5g/day, most times zero. Only real carbs I get is from the sauerkraut a few times a week.

Energy is good, muscle tone is good, Sleep isn't as great as I would like (steadily getting worse), but I can't point to anything and say "this is seriously out of whack".

So in total, that has to be what, 1000 calories tops? Even if I've somehow become pretty stingy and burning calories, I find it hard to beleive that I'm not burning at least 1500/day, so even doing nothing else, after a week, I should drop at least one lb, let alone all the shivering after CT and stuff.

But the weight loss has just stopped, like the scale is stuck. Dr. scale yesterday at visit confirmed weight, and matches my scale at home.

I suppose on some level, it's possible I'm building some muscle somewhere I don't see it, but even then, I'd expect then to be losing some inches somewhere, and as near as I can tell, nada.

I don't mind sticking with it for a while longer, because I think I believe what Doc is saying, and everything else has worked, but this is frustrating. Contemplating some HCG or something to try to shake things up. Or maybe carbloading for a few days and starting the LR again.

I don't know, maybe I'm just posting hoping for some "hang in there's".... But I was so jazzed about how well it was going, and all the "CO shreds fat", and "CT blows fat cells away", and I'm doing everything I can, but stalled out...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-28T23:41:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-stalls-even-happen.2096/post-34245>

hormones....and ....inflammation and adaptation.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: How to explain to my doctor...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-06-28T23:31:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-34239>

So, I felt like I had a productive consult with Dr. Kruse last night. I am excited that I was able to reschedule my apt with my PCP today and get it moved up to this coming Tuesday. I have a list of labs that Dr. Kruse suggested, but I'm wondering how I explain this to my doctor... why I want these labs and reasons behind each of them. Or do I just hand him the list? Some of them are easy for me to understand, others... not so much. Here are the labs I want to request:

\- Test for celiac

\- Salivary Cortisol and DHEA

\- HS-CRP

\- Lipid Panel

\- Thyroid Panel (including Free T3 & Free T4)

\- Vitamin D

The ones I'm most concerned about my doctor hesitating on are the salivary cortisol & DHEA as well as the HS-CRP. Suggestions?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-28T23:33:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-34241>

go to primalmeded.com blog post of 11/8/2011 This primer will help you.

### freshveggies — 2012-06-30T00:45:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-34464>

The cortsol one can be done at ZRT labs. You do not need a doctors orders and I am not sure of an insurance company that would pay for it anyway. The others are pretty standard and you are paying for it so I don't see why your doc wouldn't order them. Tell them it is for your piece of mind.

### Destiny — 2012-06-30T03:53:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-34517>

I am excited that I was able to reschedule my apt with my PCP today and get it moved up to this coming Tuesday. I have a list of labs that Dr. Kruse suggested, but I'm wondering how I explain this to my doctor... why I want these labs and reasons behind each of them. Or do I just hand him the list? >
>
>
> \- Test for celiac
>
> \- Salivary Cortisol and DHEA
>
> \- HS-CRP
>
> \- Lipid Panel
>

>
> \- Vitamin D
>
>
>
> The ones I'm most concerned about my doctor hesitating on are the salivary cortisol & DHEA as well as the HS-CRP. Suggestions?

Just on the HS-CRP test - Aetna encourages to have that test done. Sadly, when I asked my doctor for it, I got back the CRP test. Many doctors don't realize there is a difference.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-30T04:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-34520>

huge difference.......most CRP's are normal Then the HS crp comes back like a night mare.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-30T04:30:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-34524>

I was just looking over my labs and came across this issue. I wondered why my new labs showed that my CRP is in the norm (mine was

### Huck — 2012-08-25T16:11:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-46998>

I assume you want to use your insurance to pay for labs. I have a high deductible, so I end up paying for my labs anyway.

What I do is order my own labs prior to my doc appointment. That gives me time to research what the labs mean so I can discuss with my doc. If I have my doc draw the blood, I get a call 2 or 3 weeks later from the nurse who doesn't understand what the labs means, so walking in and handing my doc a copy of the labs for discussion has proven to be my best option.

### Destiny — 2012-08-26T18:55:05+0300

### Lyndra — 2012-08-26T20:10:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-47291>

> **Huck;47422 wrote:** I assume you want to use your insurance to pay for labs. I have a high deductible, so I end up paying for my labs anyway.
>
>
>
> What I do is order my own labs prior to my doc appointment. That gives me time to research what the labs mean so I can discuss with my doc. If I have my doc draw the blood, I get a call 2 or 3 weeks later from the nurse who doesn't understand what the labs means, so walking in and handing my doc a copy of the labs for discussion has proven to be my best option.

I'm the same way. I'd rather have the labs in hand before my doc appointment so I can do research and come up with thoughts and questions. In addition, doing it that way gives me complete control of who has copies of my labs. My former PCP would have flipped had he seen my "bad" cholesterol panel - the one with HDL of 101 and trigs of 59 but "high" LDL. Some results are best not kept on an official record that may be shared with the insurance company.

### cramernh — 2012-09-25T12:55:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-54078>

Trisha.. that is exactly what I had to tell the Physician Asst 16 years ago in order to be tested for PCOS and IR. Both sides of the family have significant immediate family medical history for several reproductive/cycle related medical problems. BAM! 12hr Blood work was done for those two issues and my labs lit up like a Christmas Tree... it wasnt pretty.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-25T21:51:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-54221>

This is a long over due update, but my doctor agreed to every single test with no reservations. Several, he even remarked, yes I like that test, or that's a good idea. There were also a couple he suggested before I recommended them. There was no convincing, begging, or pleading that I had to do. I'm really liking this new doc, he seems very open to ideas!

### shah78 — 2012-09-25T23:22:55+0300

Why would anyone get a celiac test? If it's positive you'd be crazy to eat wheat et al. If it's negative, you'd be almost as crazy to eat wheat et al. Why would anyone willingly eat gluten? Would anyone get an arsenic test? or a plutonium test?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-09-26T00:06:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-54261>

> **shah78;54952 wrote:** Why would anyone get a celiac test? If it's positive you'd be crazy to eat wheat et al. If it's negative, you'd be almost as crazy to eat wheat et al. Why would anyone willingly eat gluten? Would anyone get an arsenic test? or a plutonium test?

It was one of the tests dr kruse recommended. I believe because it can give insight into whether or not I am dealing with auto immune diseases. Thankfully the test for hashis and celiac came back negative! I'm not 100% certain about Dr Kruses' reasoning in recommending the tests.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-26T01:19:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-explain-to-my-doctor.2097/post-54286>

> **shah78;54952 wrote:** Why would anyone get a celiac test? If it's positive you'd be crazy to eat wheat et al. If it's negative, you'd be almost as crazy to eat wheat et al. Why would anyone willingly eat gluten? Would anyone get an arsenic test? or a plutonium test?

# Thread: How much is a spike?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-a-spike.2101/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5

### paisley777 — 2012-06-29T00:50:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-a-spike.2101/post-34262>

I've done a few searches and couldn't find the answer to this...

I've seen it said that dairy and whey are ~ok~ for an individual IF it doesn't make their insulin spike.

What is the definition of a 'spike' in insulin?

I have a glucometer and can measure after eating, but how do I judge the results? 5mg/dl = "spike"? 10md/dl? 50? Etc.

Thanks for your time.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-29T01:42:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-a-spike.2101/post-34275>

a 20 point move ..........

### paisley777 — 2012-06-29T06:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-a-spike.2101/post-34320>

Thank you for the quick reply!

Cool. I made a keifer (home made) and whey shake for lunch.

I couldn't test every 15 due to work requirements, but pre-shake - 77, 20min - 85, 1hr - 87... then it dropped from there eventually back to 77.

Seemed like a reasonable amount, but I wasn't sure.

Thanks again.

### scotwilson — 2012-07-11T16:29:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-is-a-spike.2101/post-36813>

not sure buddy...,,,

# Thread: Do hormones smell..?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-hormones-smell.2109/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-06-29T11:28:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-hormones-smell.2109/post-34331>

I have a coworker that cannot stop telling how very nice he thinks I smell.. it is almost as he would get paralyzed when he is close to me really...

I use a nice perfume just a little in my hair to cover the "mud-scent" I guess my hair must smell like cause I wash it with ghassul. I let him smell my perfum but he said it was not that smell at all.. it was how my body, how I smelled that was so very attractive. That was intresting because I do not shower, I only go for a swim in the river every day, never use soap except a little under my armpits if I feel they smell any.. and use only coconut and oliveoil on my skin.

I never used any hormones in any form. I have the feeling all my "Kruse-lifestyle"  and all my tanning naked have optimized my hormones and that is what my coworker smells.. And no, we have not kissed or anything that close, he just smells it through my clothes and all.. so strange!!!

After I read that tanning certain bodyparts (the ones we cover with swimsuit usually) rises your testosteron up to 200 %... and works as a hormone booster / oprimizer for other hormones too, I use every moment I can to tan those parts. I wish people would know, there is such a powerful remedy for us to take for free.. the sun!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-29T13:55:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-hormones-smell.2109/post-34340>

when someone is attracted to your sent it means they have a dissimilar immune system and that was an early evolutionary signal for homo that they might be a good partner for an offspring.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-29T14:47:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-hormones-smell.2109/post-34341>

Inger, the quick answer to your question is Yes. I have been post-menopausal for 3 years now and began BHRT about a year ago. Now that I've added hormones back into my system, I can tell you without a doubt that they do smell. Even my husband has noticed it.

When I was in my child-bearing years, he said my skin smelled slightly sweet and warm and somehow comforting. He didn't notice that the smell had gone away, I guess because my hormones declined gradually as opposed to suddenly. But he says that I now smell like I did when we first met (13 years ago).

Elin

No.. LOL.. I do not tan at work and he did not know bout my tanning.. LOL.. (but I guess about the river.. yes)

But yeah.. he knows about my eating habits.. everyone knows at work, cause they see me eating and they want to know what that is. Like bloody raw elk liver, they know about mackerel head smoothies too. They think I am crazy but we have fun and I love my coworkers and they love me too.

Strangely enough my eating habits seems not so scare the men away at all..

NowI am waiting for the winter to come.. that never happened in my life before!!! I am SO exited. I have all great options to test it where I live!

I could write just a short story for sure about me how I was and what I did and how it evolved.. if it could be to any help I would be happy to do it! I had bulemia too and that might be helpful for women who suffer from ED to get to know what helped me.

### chocolate — 2012-07-29T01:09:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-hormones-smell.2109/post-41725>

not hormones, but a smell article

[http://www.livescience.com/21789-storm-scents-smell-rain.html](http://http://www.livescience.com/21789-storm-scents-smell-rain.html)

> Storm Scents: You Can Smell Oncoming Rain
>
>
>
> When people say they can smell a storm coming, they're right. Weather patterns produce distinctive odors that sensitive noses sniff out. This year's peculiar weather patterns—such as drought in the Midwest and a "super derecho" of thunderstorms earlier this summer—are no exception. In fact, as the rains return after a dryspell, many of these odors are stronger than ever. So, what are the scents of a summer storm? Here's a breakdown of three common odors:
>
>
>
> —Oh, ozone
>
> Before the rain begins, one of the first odors you may notice as winds pick up and clouds roll in is a sweet, pungent zing in your nostrils. That's the sharp, fresh aroma of ozone—a form of oxygen whose name comes from the Greek word ozein (to smell). Tropospheric chemist Louisa Emmons at the National Center for Atmospheric Research explains that ozone emanates from fertilizers and pollutants as well as natural sources. An electrical charge—from lightning or a man-made source such as an electrical generator—splits atmospheric nitrogen and oxygen molecules into separate atoms. Some of these recombine into nitric oxide, and this in turn reacts with other atmospheric chemicals, occasionally producing a molecule made up of three oxygen atoms—ozone, or O3. (Most atmospheric oxygen is made up of two atoms—O2.) The scent of ozone heralds stormy weather because a thunderstorm's downdrafts carry O3 from higher altitudes to nose level.
>
>
>
> —Petrichor potpourri
>
> Once the rains arrive, other odors come with them. Falling water disturbs and displaces odoriferous molecules on surfaces, particularly on dry ones, and carry them into the air. If you happen to be near vegetation, these molecules may come from plants and trees. For city dwellers, these odorants rise up from concrete and asphalt. Some are fragrant, others foul.
