# Thread: Bonjour from Paris
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bonjour-from-paris.2110/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Paleodocteur — 2012-06-29T13:12:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bonjour-from-paris.2110/post-34333>

\* I am a conventionally trained French family doctor who decided \*to change the course of his slowly eroding health at age 41.

I started VLCing with BABs a year ago. Within a month I dropped 15 kilos, the knee pain, the lowish mood and the brain fog. I randomly discovered I was wheat sensitive at reintroduction. So I went on with LC fromageo-paleo.\*

\*Through the process I discovered a new passion for understanding human physiology. I am so glad Dr Jack Kruse attempted to catch the attention of the paleo community because that's how I stumbled on his amazing blog a year ago. Nowhere else had I found such depth in \*clinical thinking. When I read the CT series and realized its power I decided that, if I were to use this fascinating tool to help my patients, I had to go through it.\*

So I did.

I got to enjoy the cold bath and cold air. I lost leftover fat and gained muscle volume and strength with little effort. I got tanned. My appetite diminished \*and I got as leptin and carb sensitive as my 3 year old !\*

My little personal journey uphill is quite nice but the implications for my sick patients are overwhelming.

However that's a tough sale to explain on a 15' consultation why and how to go wheat-free/dairy-free, in the home country of baguette and cheese. I keep trying, twicking the message to avoid sounding obtuse, nuts or preachy. I got a couple of good feedbacks on sleep resync and on dairy free. Wheat free appears more like a painful double edge sword, with some patients feeling awesome and some others worse for doing it halfway and probably suffering intermittent withdrawals and flareups. I wish I could discuss these topics with other docs, but so far my colleagues remain trapped in conventional thinking (this year we'll get bonuses to statinize a maximum of people...)\*

\* \*Hopefully I' ll find some other doctors on this forum, but there is already so much to learn from patients own experiences.

# Thread: Any ideas on what I can do with some stew meat?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-06-30T04:11:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34519>

Most of what I have left of the 1/2 cow we bought is stew meat. I'm trying to use as much of the meat up as possible before reordering. Any ideas on what I can do with it besides the obvious stew. My problem is I am on an AI diet and no FODMAPS. So no onions, garlic, tomatoes, tomato sauce, wine, coconut milk, coconut aminos..... Yep, very limited diet but it is really working for me, so I don't want to make an exception. I would like to cook it in the crockpot too. I do have some chicken stock that I made but not any bone broth right now. I need to make some more of that too.

Any ideas?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-30T04:20:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34522>

I make meat salad out of mine........and eat it for breakfast.

### janagram — 2012-06-30T04:24:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34523>

u could cook it with curry spices

### Melinda — 2012-06-30T04:48:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34529>

You might want to invest in a pressure cooker. I think it comes out with better flavor in the meat. Add whatever things you are allowed and lots of herbs.

### Michael — 2012-06-30T10:07:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34552>

If no onions, how about just carrots & celery & bouquet garni?

You could try dried mushrooms, too. Intense flavor.

You could also try mixing different meats in a stew for more flavor & interest. Lardons of bacon is classic. In France & Spain they do things like poultry or rabbit plus pork or spiced sausage.

### ealachan — 2012-06-30T15:42:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34572>

Do you have access to a meat grinder (or a butcher)? You could grind it up and then just use it for burgers, etc.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-06-30T16:47:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34587>

> **Jack Kruse;34681 wrote: I make meat salad out of mine........and eat it for breakfast.
>
>
> janagram;34682 wrote:** u could cook it with curry spices

I like both of these suggestions. I was definitely thinking of it as a breakfast food so I could do something a little different. Since it is such a tough piece of meat, would you simmer it in broth or something to make the meat salad? I found a mixture of spices I liked on chicken wings & it included curry powder. I could definitely use it on the meat. Again, how could I cook it so it would get tender. I have wanted a pressure cooker and have been thinking of buying one, but I already have so many small appliances I thought it would be a bit of overkill!

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-07-01T16:34:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34776>

Stir fry it with some spices or veggies.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-02T16:04:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-ideas-on-what-i-can-do-with-some-stew-meat.2117/post-34986>

I chop it up even smaller( maybe half size), quick stirfry with some seasonings or do it plain. I keep it in the fridge for snacking and taking to work. Caveat...I have to eat more than three times a day because of a pre-existing medical condition at this time. This is where a consult with Dr. K can make a big difference.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-30T04:18:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-know-why-factor-x-matters-now.2118/post-34521>

Phylogeny: Rewriting evolution:

[http://www.nature.com/news/phylogeny-rewriting-evolution-1.10885](http://http://www.nature.com/news/phylogeny-rewriting-evolution-1.10885)

The bone collectors have been wrong for a long timeâ€¦â€¦.and they will never believe it. But we must show them because our health and longevity depend upon following the sunlight of this new scientific information.

Anyone want to go back to re listen the Factor X webinar now? Read this gem from the Nature article, â€￾ The findings also shift the geographical origin of placental mammals, suggesting that they started in the Northern Hemisphere, where the first rodent fossils are found, not in the Southern Hemisphere, as many researchers have assumed on the basis of fossil and DNA data.â€￾

What did my Factor X, theory predict again?

Inquiring minds want to know. Closed minds will run for the cover of conventional wisdom of phylogeny. The rules are different folks and as this series rolls out more dragons are getting slayed. Just wait til Brain Gut 4 and 5.

### kathylu — 2012-07-08T22:02:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-want-to-know-why-factor-x-matters-now.2118/post-36230>

> **Jack Kruse;34680 wrote: Phylogeny: Rewriting evolution:
>
>
>
> [http://www.nature.com/news/phylogeny-rewriting-evolution-1.10885](http://http://www.nature.com/news/phylogeny-rewriting-evolution-1.10885)
>
>
>
>
>
> The bone collectors have been wrong for a long time…….and they will never believe it. But we must show them because our health and longevity depend upon following the sunlight of this new scientific information.
>
>
>
> Anyone want to go back to re listen the Factor X webinar now? Read this gem from the Nature article, ” The findings also shift the geographical origin of placental mammals, suggesting that they started in the Northern Hemisphere, where the first rodent fossils are found, not in the Southern Hemisphere, as many researchers have assumed on the basis of fossil and DNA data.”
>
>
>
> What did my Factor X, theory predict again?
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Inquiring minds want to know. Closed minds will run for the cover of conventional wisdom of phylogeny. The rules are different folks and as this series rolls out more dragons are getting slayed. Just wait til Brain Gut 4 and 5.**

**

Well, your factor X theory predicts rapid morphological and phylogenetic change in response to rapid, extreme changes in environmental conditions. Petersen's work certainly lends credence to your ideas. Apparently microRNA is highly conserved, found in the oldest, most primitive cell types and increases in incidence with increasing cell complexity. It is able to affect gene expression posttranslationally**. This is a HUGE deal because it blasts apart the dogma of DNA>RNA>protein expression. It also potentially allows for rapid recruitment of non-coding areas of DNA, i.e. our genomes may not be static, but infinitely more variable than we previously believed possible. Maybe your genetic inheritance is not necessarily your fate after all!

Definitions from wikipedia:

miRNAs are post-transcriptional regulators that bind to complementary sequences on target messenger RNA transcripts (mRNAs), usually resulting in translational repression or target degradation and gene silencing.\[1\]\[2\] The human genome may encode over 1000 miRNAs,\[3\]\[4\] which may target about 60% of mammalian genes\[5\]\[6\] and are abundant in many human cell types.\[7\]

miRNA research has revealed multiple roles in negative regulation (transcript degradation and sequestering, translational suppression) and possible involvement in positive regulation (transcriptional and translational activation). By affecting gene regulation, miRNAs are likely to be involved in most biological processes.\[17\]\[18\]\[19\]\[20\]\[21\]\[22\]\[23\] Different sets of expressed miRNAs are found in different cell types and tissues.\[24\]

Aberrant expression of miRNAs has been implicated in numerous disease states, and miRNA-based therapies are under investigation.\[25\]\[26\]\[27\]

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/15/discovery.chemical.affects.biological.clock.offers.new.way.treat.diabetes?utm\_source=feedburner&utm\_medium=feed&utm\_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm\_content=FaceBook](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/15/discovery.chemical.affects.biological.clock.offers.new.way.treat.diabetes?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm_content=FaceBook)

Discovery of chemical that affects biological clock offers new way to treat diabetes

Published: Sunday, July 15, 2012 - 13:31 in Biology & Nature

Biologists at UC San Diego have discovered a chemical that offers a completely new and promising direction for the development of drugs to treat metabolic disorders such as type 2 diabetes -- a major public health concern in the United States due to the current obesity epidemic. Their discovery, detailed in a paper published July 13 in an advance online issue of the journal Science, initially came as a surprise because the chemical they isolated does not directly control glucose production in the liver, but instead affects the activity of a key protein that regulates the internal mechanisms of our daily night and day activities, which scientists call our circadian rhythm or biological clock.

Scientists had long suspected that diabetes and obesity could be linked to problems in the biological clock. Laboratory mice with altered biological clocks, for example, often become obese and develop diabetes. Two years ago, a team headed by Steve Kay, dean of the Division of Biological Sciences at UC San Diego, discovered the first biochemical link between the biological clock and diabetes. **It found that a key protein, cryptochrome, that regulates the biological clocks of plants, insects and mammals also regulates glucose production in the liver and that altering the levels of this protein could improve the health of diabetic mice.**

Now Kay and his team have discovered a small molecule -- one that can be easily developed into a drug -- that controls the intricate molecular cogs or timekeeping mechanisms of cryptochrome in such a manner that it can repress the production of glucose by the liver. Like mice and other animals, humans have evolved biochemical mechanisms to keep a steady supply of glucose flowing to the brain at night, when we're not eating or otherwise active.

"At the end of the night, our hormones signal that we're in a fasting state," said Kay. "And during the day, when we're active, our biological clock shuts down those fasting signals that tell our liver to make more glucose because that's when we're eating."

Diabetes is caused by an accumulation of glucose in the blood, which can lead to heart disease, strokes, kidney failure and blindness. In type 1 diabetes, destruction of insulin producing cells in the pancreas results in the high blood sugar. In type 2 diabetes, which makes up 90 percent of the cases, gradual resistance to insulin because of obesity or other problems, leads to high blood sugar.

Kay and his collaborators discovered in 2010 that cryptochrome plays a critical role in regulating the internal timing of our cyclical eating patterns, timing our fasting at night with our eating during the day to maintain a steady supply of glucose in our bloodstream. Other researchers have recently discovered that cryptochrome also has the potential to reduce high blood sugar from asthma medication by adjusting the time of day a patient takes their medication. "We found that if we increased cryptochrome levels genetically in the liver we could inhibit the production of glucose by the liver," said Kay.

What he and his team found in their most recent discovery was that a much smaller molecule, dubbed "KL001" (for the first such compound from the Kay Lab), can regulate that activity as well. It slowed down the biological clock by stabilizing the cryptochrome protein -- that is, it essentially prevented crypotochrome from being sent to the cellular garbage can, the proteasomes.

The discovery of KL001 was serendipitous, a complete surprise to the scientists that came about from a parallel effort in Kay's laboratory to identify molecules that lengthen the biological clock. Two years ago, Tsuyoshi Hirota, a postdoctoral fellow in Kay's laboratory found a compound that had the greatest effect ever seen on circadian rhythm, a chemical the biologists dubbed "longdaysin" because it lengthened the daily biological clocks of human cells by more than 10 hours.

Continuing his search, Hirota resumed his efforts to find more chemicals that lengthened or slowed down circadian rhythms, enabling the scientists to understand more about the intricate chemical and genetic machinery of the biological clock. He and his colleagues in Kay's lab did this by screening thousands of compounds from a chemical library with human cells in individual micro-titer wells in which a luciferase gene from fireflies is attached to the biological clock machinery, enabling the scientists to detect a glow whenever the biological clock is activated. Their molecular fishing expedition came up with a number of other compounds, one of which was KL001.

"We found other compounds that like longdaysin slowed down the biological clock," said Kay. "But unlike longdaysin, these compounds did not inhibit the protein kinases that longdaysin inhibits so we knew this compound must be working differently. What we needed to know was what is this compound interacting with? And we were absolutely stunned when we discovered that what was binding most specifically to our compound,KL001, was the clock protein cryptochrome that our lab has worked on in plants, flies and mammals for the last 20 years."

Kay's team turned to biological chemists in Peter Schultz's laboratory at The Scripps Research Institute to characterize the compound and understand better chemically how it affected cryptochrome to lengthen the biological clock.

"Those biochemical studies showed us that KL001 prevents cryptochrome from being degraded by the proteasome system, which was another big surprise," said Kay. "It essentially interferes with the signal to send cryptochrome to the garbage can."

To understand how KL001 worked mechanistically with cryptochrome to control the biological clock, the team initiated a collaboration with Frank Doyle and his group at UC Santa Barbara. "They constructed a beautiful mathematical model of cryptochrome's role in the clock," said Kay. "That model was essential in allowing us to understand the action of the compound because the biological clock is very complicated. It's like opening the back of a Rolex and seeing the hundreds of tiny little cogs that are tightly integrated."

Based on that mathematical model, the scientists predicted that adding KL001 to mouse liver cells should stabilize cryptochrome and that the increased level of cryptochrome would inhibit the production of enzymes in the liver that stimulate the process of gluconeogenesis -- the generation of glucose during fasting. Experiments done together with the laboratory of David Brenner, dean of the UC San Diego School of Medicine and Vice Chancellor for Health Sciences, confirmed that prediction to be true.

"In mouse liver cells," said Kay, "we showed that KL001 inhibited gene expression for gluconeogenesis that is induced when exposed to the hormone glucagon, which promotes glucose production by the liver. It's a hormone we all produce in fasting states. And our compound, in a dose dependent way, inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis, the actual production of glucose by those liver cells."

Kay said the next step for the research group is to understand how KL001 and similar molecules that affect cryptochrome function in living systems, such has laboratory mice. The scientists also plan to probe how such compounds affect other processes besides the liver that may tie the biological clock to metabolic diseases. "As with any surprise discovery," he notes, "this opens the door to more opportunities for novel therapeutics than we can currently imagine."

Besides Kay, Hirota, Schultz, Doyle and Brenner, other scientists involved in the discovery were Mariko Sawa, Pagkapol Y. Pongsawakul and Tim Sonntag of UC San Diego's Division of Biological Sciences; Jae Wook Lee of TSRI; Peter St. John of UC Santa Barbara; Keiko Iwaisako, Takako Noguchi and David Welsh of UC San Diego's School of Medicine.

The study was supported by grants from the National Institutes of Health (GM074868, H051573 GM085764, GM096873, MH082945).

Isn't it good to lower our levels of IGF-1 so we don't burn through our stem cell depots and bring cell senescence closer? IGF-1 has been likened to throwing high octane fuel on a fire. If I remember correctly Jack or someone else here linked a video of a UK journalist who mentioned the South American dwarves who were immune to neolithic disease because their epigenetics coded for extremely low production if IGF-1. The same video talked about alternating feast and fast days which also lowered IGF-1 levels. Is it possible for someone to have long telomeres and constant high levels of IGF-1?

Incidentally I learned elsewhere that eating high fat requires at least 5 hours of no snacking between each high fat meal to avoid neolithic disease. Otherwise the body doesn't have time to prepare for the next meal. Mice put on high fat diets with plenty of time between meals did far better than mice allowed to browse on fat all day long.

# Thread: One little glass of Malbec wreaks havoc...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-06-30T16:41:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34584>

I was going to put the alcohol question in "Ask Jack" today, but I see someone else has, so he answered my question! Thanks Dr. K.!

Wow. So I've been doing the LR since Jan. 2012, and 3 weeks ago, began CT, and got right into it: 5x/week, 50-53F @ 40 mins in the tub. Did one face dunk, and I was in the tub, full body. Love it! Have followed the protocol exactly: the large glass of ice water, the high fat/high protein meal, no alcohol, strict keto-Paleo diet, seafood every day. Lost 6 lbs. in 3 weeks!

Wed. night, went to dinner with a friend, and decided that a glass of Malbec sounded nice. And so did the bread on the table, along with the pasta on my shrimp plate which I usually sub for veggies! Doh!). And so did the Sprinkles cupcakes afterwards.

I woke up Thursday, feeling drunk (from one glass!). I weighed 3 lbs. heavier. I was up half the night sweating. Miserable!!!

Thursday, I got right back on the keto-Paleo wagon with the CT, and feel good again.

Now I realize that I've eaten cleaner, so my liver lacks the enzymes to properly process the alcohol, but I had no idea it would happen that quickly, and that one glass of wine would make me feel THAT bad and help me make such bad food decisions. OK, now I get it...no more alcohol!

Probably wasn't the wine, unless that made it ok to have the bread, pasta and cupcake, and even then it wasn't a physical reason like your liver, but a physiologic reason.

And yes, the wine made my defenses against the sugar & wheat go down. It always does. I've never been a huge drinker; have never even tasted "hard alcohol", like vodka, scotch, etc. So even a little wine is now too much.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-01T15:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34765>

That was Wednesday; today is Sunday, and I'm finally down the 3 lbs. I gained from that one cheat meal. Sooooo not worth it. Have CT'd, eaten very clean keto-Paleo. And no more alcohol!

### johnnyb — 2012-07-01T17:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34787>

> **PrimalPam;34744 wrote:** I woke up Thursday, feeling drunk (from one glass!). I weighed 3 lbs. heavier. I was up half the night sweating. Miserable!!!

This sounds familiar. When I was eating 80/20 Primal I would eat Mexican food out every second or third Friday with friends. Sometimes that would include the works, a margarita, chips, rice, and beans. I would wake up the same way, all hot and sweaty. I wish I had actually taken my temperature. I'd swear if I had an infrared camera I would be illuminating the whole room. Losing the carbs was the best fix, but sleeping without any covers or a sheet on top seemed to relieve the sweating (probably weather dependent). I figured my body was doing a good thing by burning off the extra calories.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-01T17:39:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/one-little-glass-of-malbec-wreaks-havoc.2122/post-34792>

> **johnnyb;34947 wrote:** This sounds familiar. When I was eating 80/20 Primal I would eat Mexican food out every second or third Friday with friends.

The 80/20 rule just doesn't work for me, as I take too much leeway with it. My new doctor (who eats Paleo) says that for her, 80/20 means that if she's meeting friends for dinner, and it's a few hours later than she's used to eating, and the only choice on the table is bread, she'll have a piece to not starve before she orders her Paleo-type dinner. For me, that one piece leads to the whole meal.

Mexican food is a huge one for me, and I'm bringing in Tex-Mex for July 4th. I'm ordering fajitas, but I'll only eat the beef & shrimp, with some guacamole (I make it, so I know what's in it!). I have to avoid the rest like the plague. And ooooh, the margaritas that my sister-in-law makes....(slapping hand right now).

# Thread: good reason to get cold adapted
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-reason-to-get-cold-adapted.2123/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-30T17:37:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-reason-to-get-cold-adapted.2123/post-34602>

[https://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=cold-heart-attacks](http://https://www.scientificamerican.com/article.cfm?id=cold-heart-attacks)

Temperature Drops Put the Squeeze on Heart Attack Risk

A slight daily mean temperature decline can increase the number of heart attacks for up to a month, new research shows

> The cold facts
>
> Despite perennial tales about snow shoveling-induced heart attacks, there seems to be more to the cold story than physical exertion. **Researchers are still parsing out the precise physiologic effects of cold on the cardiovascular system, but some controlled lab studies run on volunteers have provided clues. These lab tests have shown that when the ambient temperature drops, blood viscosity increases, blood pressure changes, and the heart has to work harder, likely increasing the risk for clots and ultimately a heart attack.
>
>
>
> Although overexertion and deep freezes do seem to contribute to heart attack risk, the new data suggests that **overall temperature matters less than its variation. In other words, Bhaskaran explains, there did not seem to be a chilly threshold temperature after which the one-degree rule took hold. That means that even if the mean daily temperature is 10 degrees C one day, 9 the next, and 10 again the following day, there are still likely to be an extra 200 heart attacks during the next month.
>
>
>
> The striking lag time, which tended to be about two weeks after a temperature drop, although some** extended to 28 days,** seem to be more pronounced with cold weather heart troubles than in those spurred by heat waves. But "why that is, we're not entirely sure," Bhaskaran says. The cold might simply start broader changes in the body that can eventually lead to a heart attack, he suggests.

**Dr K's answer:

You need to go back and re listen to the Webinar and then re read the Hormone 101 blog......if cortisol/inflammation is an issue you might need BHRT long term. I not you wont. The answers are there and if your still confused there is always your doc to ask.

My doc's are a little useless so I'll ask you guys since you're all experts by now

>
>
>
> ALL REUNITE (MORE GEEKY THOUGH): THE WARM ADAPTED STORY of metabolism and biochemistry YOU HEAR REGURGITATED EVERYWHERE ON THE NET:
>
>
>
> Once leptin enters and binds to its receptors, it effects the lateral hypothalamic tracts to immediately send a second messenger signal to the thyroid to signal it to up-regulate thyroid function and efficiency. See whne we are warm adapted it require help to partially access this pathway from T3 hormone. If you are LR you never can access this pathway. This specifically is how we can raise our basal metabolic rate when we are leptin sensitive. These coupled events, matched with leptinâ€™s actions peripherally in muscles, occur at the UCP3 sitesâ€¦â€¦to burn fat as we sleep at a higher basal metabolic rate. This means electron chain transport does not make ATP as usual. When leptin allows this uncoupling to occur we make heat and not energy from normal metabolism. This means we will burn off our excess calories as pure heat. This is one reason why calories in and calories out argument makes no biologic sense once you understand how leptin works. Humans are built to burn fat at night as we sleep to loose excess weight we donâ€™t need. This is our modern day equivalent of hibernation. Out big fat amazing human brain got rid of winter sleep and took over a two hour window during sleep to replace it. CT-7 covers the gorgeous symphony of human circadian biology. Since it shrunk our risk of mismatches has risen exponentially. This is how your brain or feelings, can undercut you, when you are unaware of what a sped up epigenetics meant to human mammalian biochemistry. SKEPTICS: You wanted data, and now I am killing you with it.
>
>
>
> THE COLD ADAPTED HUMAN THYROID FXN: does not bother with T3 at all. Why? When you are supremely LS by cold you go straight the the source, the hypothalamus and make TRH from the brain. The brain controls all thyroid function in coldâ€¦â€¦.forget the Moose thyroid.
>
>
>
> SKEPTIC BOMB: You bypass all hormones and TSH too. TRH drives the whole show. The brain is completely in control and it up regualtes fat burning everywhere. This is how the Ancient Pathway lights your pilot light. The warm adapted human always complains about the cold and always feels coldâ€¦â€¦the cold adapted on is always pink to cherry in cold radiating heat like a furnace. You can thank TRH for this. This is does not even require a thyroid gland either. Is not life grand in the cold, folks?

**

> We have already established in earlier blogs that inflammation causes Leptin resistance. Leptin controls all energy production in the body. Inflammation stops T4 to T3 conversion in the liver and abruptly turns off your thyroidâ€™s ability to function properly despite normal thyroid labs.

**/>

> The high cortisol also directly affects the Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis too. Cortisol directly blocks 5′ deiodinase enzyme that converts T4 to T3 (this occurs in the liver). Cortisol Releasing Hormone (CRH) (seen elevated in high cortisol production states) directly blocks TSH. The implication is huge because Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH) and T3, the active thyroid hormone, are inhibited quickly in this process. Immediately, any excess T4 is then shunted to reverse T3. Reverse T3 is a COMPETITIVE inhibitor to T3, the active thyroid hormone. This basically turns off the thyroid!

So I guess the issue is inflammation and if I am understanding correctly, even if you're entirely cold-adapted, you can still have inflammation and that would prevent you from being "supremely" LS, since inflammation causes LR by definition. So therefore, you can be cold adapted and not LS, and that means you still need your thyroid BHRT fix despite it all?

What would cause someone to still have inflammation for the rest of their lives despite doing everything optimal?

And, using hs CRP as a marker for inflammation, what's the desired level for the cold thyroid bypassing to happen? Or LDL as that is another marker of inflammation as I'm understanding.

And then, at that point, does it even \*matter\*?? Since you're already supremely LS, your cortisol isn't running amok, and not blocking conversion of T4 to T3 and CRH isn't blocking TSH. So everything should be optimal no matter if cold-adapted or not, summer and winter.

So really, this would mean that this is a good thing for folks who have a damaged or missing thyroid for whatever reason but not b/c of LR, but for those who have other issues that would still cause inflammation for the rest of their lives, it would not matter at all? They will be stuck with BHRT for their thyroids no matter if cold or warm.

Sorry if I'm being dense here. The brain is actually cooperating a bit for a change today, so maybe I should be getting this on my own.....

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34809>

I did CT for 2-1/2 months and didn't feel like I improved a lot. My overall cold tolerance got a little better, but by the 25-30 minute mark at 50 degrees, I would start shivering and feel really cold. I was forcing myself to stay in. Post-CT warm up took a looooooong time.

My strategy now is to improve adrenal & thyroid function and then ease back into CT. Hopefully that can happen before the summer is over, as I'd love to start taking cold showers and then adapt myself naturally as the water temperature decreases into fall & winter.

### AKMan — 2012-07-01T20:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34836>

There are quite a few animal studies out there where they would destroy an animal's thyroid and then subject it to cold. In most cases, the animal wouldn't survive for long. However, if they 'cold-adapted' the animal first, the animal would survive. 'Cold adapting' usually consisted of housing the animal in 50 deg F temps for a week to 10 days.

In human terms, mild hypothyroidism usually presents in subtle ways; weight gain, hair loss, depression, constipation, etc... I've never heard of someone dying of hypothermia where the cause was hypothyroidism. In the animal studies, they used death by exposure as the end-point.

In my own case, I was diagnosed hypothyroid 7 years ago due to high TSH levels, with no real symptoms. After starting CT this year, I quit taking my Synthroid and my TSH, T4, and T3 levels have stayed normal. Prior to CT, I had been taking kelp and brazil nuts and eating a clean paleo mostly keto diet. So, impossible to say if the CT had anything to do with it, but I like to think it did.

My take on this is, if you are hypothyroid and don't have an autoimmune condition (hashi's), supplement with kelp and selenium, eat keto paleo high seafood, CT and test thyroid every 3 months.

I swim in the pool almost everyday to get my head under, I dunk my head in 35 degree water for the pain and crave it, I think a 45 degree bath is easier than a 65 degree one. ### ATL_Paleo — 2012-07-02T04:04:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34934>

PLEASE EXPLAIN THE CONNECTION BETWEEN LDL CHOLESTEROL AND PREGNENOLONE ?

We make our pregnenolone from LDL cholesterol, ie:

LDL cholesterol ---> pregnenolone

When LDL cholesterol is too high, then it's due to either:

a) pregnenolone is too low, cortisol is too low, and despite this: ACTH is also too low even though it should be high (hypothalamus mismanagement)

Ie: the liver enzymes which produce LDL cholesterol are upregulated by too low pregnenolone, but ACTH is too low to ensure the LDL cholesterol is then synthesized into pregnenolone, so LDL cholesterol gets "backed up".

or:

b) Cortisol is low, thus ACTH is high (as it should be), LDL cholesterol is high, pregnenolone is high, but pregnenolone is not synthesizing into cortisol. In this case pregnenolone is "backed up".

Source: [http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/content.php?118-cortisol-boost-101](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/content.php?118-cortisol-boost-101)

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-02T04:13:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-34935>

@ALT: Is that a question? LDL gets converted to preg but sometimes that conversion doesn't work well, the conversion needs T3 and vitamin A so if you're having trouble with thyroid you might not have enough T3 to make the conversion

LDL will be high if there is inflammation/stress....

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T14:32:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35445>

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?17834-Heart-Palpitations-on-oral-pregnenolone](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?17834-Heart-Palpitations-on-oral-pregnenolone)

> **Irregular heart rate (eg: palpitations) is a symptom of temporarily too low cortisol in relation to thyroid hormones, and it's severity is determined by the relative difference in cortisol and thyroid hormones.
>
>
>
> When it's triggered exclusively by an increase in dose of supplementatary preg, then it's due to the following process:
>
>
>
> 0) Initially resting metabolic rate is very low, so cortisol levels and thyroid hormones are relatively low - but they are balanced to each other.
>
>
>
> 1) supplementation with preg initially raises preg levels
>
>
>
> 2) adrenals synthesize preg along the cortisol-production-line into cortisol
>
>
>
> 3) cells absorb more cortisol and increase their metabolic rate (good)
>
>
>
> \[when cells increase their metabolic rate, they consume thyroid hormones faster, and they consume cortisol faster\]
>
>
>
> 4) cells consume T3 faster, cells consume cortisol faster
>
>
>
> 5) cells start to invoke their genetic programming which raises their resting metabolic rate, ie: they start to "stockpile" all hormones - especially T4 and cortisol (and they hypothalamus allows serum levels of all hormones to rise) THIS IS THE FUNDAMENTAL GOAL OF THIS EXERCISE
>
>
>
> 6) but when preg dose is too high for the initial T4 levels, cells won't absorb all of the extra cortisol, and some of that extra cortisol stays temporarily in serum, and the hypothalamus detects this as "too high cortsol" and backs off CRH, which tells the pituitary to back off ACTH, which tells the adrenals to back off cortisol production - by more than the amount of additional cortisol being synthesized by the supplementary preg.
>
>
>
> \[ The reason why the hypothalamus over-reacts this way is due to the speed cortisol levels increased. The faster cortisol levels increase, the more harshly the hypothalamus responds by lowering ACTH harshly. Ie: if you had slowed down the rate of absorption of preg, eg: via using transdermal preg, then cortisol would rise more slowly, and the hypothalamus would only react optimally to the excess cortisol \]
>
>
>
> 7) As a result of the hypothalamus' over-reaction, cortisol levels are now temporarily too low
>
>
>
> 8) At this time cells are loaded with more T4 and T3, but they've had their cortisol supply reduced, so they have their thyroid hormones in excess of their cortisol.
>
>
>
>
>
> In Hormone Modulation Therapy 101 I've improved the explanation of this phenomenon as follows:
>
>
>
> Tune in to the following symptoms of excess preg, excess cortisol and excess T3, ie:
>
>
>
> a) Symptoms of preg-is-in-excess-of-T3 (same as T3-is-too-low-compared-to-preg) are slightly spacey feelings - try reducing preg.
>
>
>
> b) Symptoms of T3-is-in-excess-of-cortisol (same as cortisol-is-too-low-compared-to-T3) are awareness of your heart beat / erratic heart beat - this has a complex explanation: it's due to an initial increase in metabolic rate causing an initial boost in cells absorbing both cortisol and thryoid hormones, followed by running low on thyroid hormones, followed by temporary ACTH suppression due to excess cortisol due to excess preg. Result is temporary excess of thyroid hormones in relation to cortisol. Try decreasing preg to reduce the likelihood of ACTH suppression, or switch from oral to TD preg to slow absorption of preg to prevent the excess cortisol.
>
>
>
> c) Symptoms of cortisol-is-in-excess-of-T3 (same as T3-is-too-low-compared-to-cortisol) are temporary excess cortisol causing ACTH suppression, causing too low preg, causing drowsiness and / or the yawns - try reducing preg.**

c) sounds a heck of a lot like what happens post CT to me.... I got 20-30min then I pass out for an hour or two.

b) sounds a heck of a lot like my every-day state no matter if I supp preg or not. I get worse after eating, after exercise, after upset, after anything that would increase my cortisol temporarily..... makes my heart thump even more.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T14:35:54+0300

>
> we are expected to take (proportional) amounts of (pregnenolone during day time and possibly progesterone at bed time).
>
>
>

>
>
>
> Dosing of pregnenolone and progesterone is newer ending guessing game.

Fascinating about cortisol, preg and T3. Some of these things happen to me and now I get it! Thanks for posting Souldanzer!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-04T21:26:05+0300

Can someone explain this part to me....

> ) but when preg dose is too high for the initial T4 levels, cells won't absorb all of the extra cortisol, ....

does this relate to not enough T4, so the body can't convert enough to T3 to counterbalance increased cortisol? There wasn't any talk of T4 before. To really it's a too low T3 b/c there isn't enough T4??

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-05T17:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35629>

Soul - thanks for posting this.

The other day I took a minimal thyroid dose in the morning in preparation for thyroid labs that afternoon. I took my regular amount of pregnenelone, and noticed that afternoon the weird spacey feeling described. Now I know what it was (not enough thyroid hormone on board). Tells me I'm still mainly running off of the T3 in Armour, and I will likely do better adding in a little T4. I am now spacing the preg doses and doing them at the same time as my Armour doses. I'm hoping this will help my morning symptoms. That link you posted really helped clarify what is going on when we're attempting to run hormones on manual, so thanks again!

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-05T17:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35631>

I also wonder if this means cortisol testing does not tell the gland's potential when someone is hypothyroid. That link seems to say that pregnenelone is made to match the level of thyroid hormone available. Less available thyroid hormone = less pregnenelone = less cortisol (but enough to balance out the amount of thyroid hormone, but a slower metabolic rate than optimal) no matter what the adrenal gland is capable of making. I always thought that in the face of hypothyroidism, a (healthy) adrenal response is to upregulate cortisol to cope with the stress of being hypothyroid. I'm not sure now how to assemble my little tidbits of knowledge...

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-05T18:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-and-ct.2137/post-35638>

My guess is it could go both ways possibly.... depending on where your issue is. If your problem starts with low thyroid, I could see how that could lower your cortisol. If your problem starts with HPA, that will affect your thyroid function. You could probably have them both trashed at the same time.

Finding this information really opened my eyes to what might be going on in my body.... whether or not I take preg. And CT would affect all of this, as well. I might have a hole in my heart that's causing all sorts of havoc.... but I'm pretty sure I've got some serious imbalance between HPA and thyroid as well and I'm starting to think that the heart palpitations might be caused by HPA/thyroid dysfunction much more than my PFO.

I dropped preg and DHEA yesterday (but also CT and leptin RX as I decided to have a wtf day) and I slept deeply, woke a couple of times but fell right back asleep. 10pm to 8am. Not bad at all. Not sure how I'm going to go about today yet.

# Thread: I'm sleeping!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### snapper — 2012-07-01T14:24:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34761>

I've seen numerous people here say that they are sleeping better in one way or another--longer, deeper, less awakenings.

I was sceptical of the claim that CT could make you sleep better. I'm blown away by how much better I am sleeping. I keep a daily log of how sleep-deprived I feel. I am extraordinarily sensitive to sleep even a little sleep deprivation. Even an hour or two to little sleep means torture the next day.

I got almost instant results from CT. From the time I began face dunking, sleep improved. After a couple of weeks, it had improved very consistently. It's getting to the point that I expect to sleep well. I've never had this in my adult life. It's just amazing.

Has anyone else with chronic sleeping difficulties seen them improve (or resolve) with CT? I guess that for a lot of people who sleep reasonably well, the difference may not be so obvious. For me, it promises to give a good proportion of my life back. To be precise, my records over a long period indicate that I sleep poorly on average about 1-2 nights per week. On those days, I feel horrible, function poorly, and life is just miserable. It's still early days--I've been doing CT for seven weeks. However, I can't believe this is placebo. I didn't even believe it was going to work.

This is marvelous. I just hope it lasts! I dont' know whether I need to progress through to intensive CT (I don't need to lose weight, I just tried it because someone suggested it could help sleep). I'd love to hear of any experiences, especially if anyone has had chronic sleeping problems and either seen or not seen improvement.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-01T15:26:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34767>

Yes, I sleep SO much better with the combo of CT and the keto-Paleo diet. And I'm one of those who was chronically sleep-deprived. I thought it was normal to wake up around 3 a.m., turn the t.v. on for 1-2 hours, then finally sleep again for about an hour before my alarm shook me.

While I still occasionally wake up in the middle of the night, I don't even look at the clock. I wake up, realize it's still dark, and fall back asleep. I never could do this before Paleo & CT! I'd toss & turn for hours.

For me, it's definitely the combo of CT & Paleo that does it. If I eat poorly, I still have a bad night's sleep. I'm doing CT 5 x/week now, and like Dr. K. says, the effects seem to be cumulative, because I still sleep well on my non-CT nights.

### MartiD — 2012-07-01T17:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34785>

If I over do carbs, even just veggie carbs ( sweet potato, mushrooms, etc) I can wake up once during night. If I eat my 25 gr carbs for breakfast or lunch I have no issues. I never had chronic sleep issues but now I sleep like a rock thanks to reset and CT.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-01T17:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34795>

Marti that is a sure sing Cortisol/DHEA and Melatonin axis is bad......

### Lyndra — 2012-07-01T18:45:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34811>

Congrats on the sleep! That is awesome. I did CT for 2-1/2 months and didn't see any sleep improvement . I'm still waking 4-5 times every night but expect that fixing adrenals & thyroid will get me back on track. Enjoy the good night's rest!

### vkiernan — 2012-07-02T02:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34910>

> **Lyndra;34971 wrote:** Congrats on the sleep! That is awesome. I did CT for 2-1/2 months and didn't see any sleep improvement . I'm still waking 4-5 times every night but expect that fixing adrenals & thyroid will get me back on track. Enjoy the good night's rest!

Lyndra, Are you taking DHEA? I really believe that is what is helping me sleep along with CT.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-02T03:06:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34922>

> **vkiernan;35070 wrote:** Lyndra, Are you taking DHEA? I really believe that is what is helping me sleep along with CT.

+1 on this. I'm sure the DHEA (combined with CT) has been critical to my improved sleep. I generally no longer wake in the middle of the night anymore and if I do on rare occasion, I'm able to fall back to sleep unlike previously where I'd toss and turn for hours. Now if only I could wake up feeling ready to crawl out of bed and not be so stinkin' sore!!

### snapper — 2012-07-02T03:44:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34928>

> **PrimalPam;34927 wrote:** Yes, I sleep SO much better with the combo of CT and the keto-Paleo diet. And I'm one of those who was chronically sleep-deprived. I thought it was normal to wake up around 3 a.m., turn the t.v. on for 1-2 hours, then finally sleep again for about an hour before my alarm shook me.
>
>
>
> While I still occasionally wake up in the middle of the night, I don't even look at the clock. I wake up, realize it's still dark, and fall back asleep. I never could do this before Paleo & CT! I'd toss & turn for hours.
>
>
>
> For me, it's definitely the combo of CT & Paleo that does it. If I eat poorly, I still have a bad night's sleep. I'm doing CT 5 x/week now, and like Dr. K. says, the effects seem to be cumulative, because I still sleep well on my non-CT nights.

Thanks. I'm the same: I still wake up during the night, but I usually fall back to sleep. I'm also the same on diet--I'm on a paleo diet. I'm not so fussed about carbs, but they're typically below 60 g and often quite a bit lower.

I hope that's correct about it being cumulative. I need to travel soon and may not find time or opportunity to do something each day. I hope the sleep continues to be good when I do! That would be fantastic.

### snapper — 2012-07-02T03:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34929>

That's really interesting. I eat most of my carbs in the evening. Back when I was on a fairly high carb diet, it seemed that I needed carbs to be able to fall asleep. Now, as long as I get my mind reasonably clear and "still", I just fall asleep when I'm ready. I do wake up during the night, but it really doesn't matter that much because I fall back to sleep. I understand that it's normal to come back to a waking or at least near-waking state during each cycle of sleep. As long as I don't wake up too much, I almost always drop back to sleep quickly. Still, I might experiment with avoiding carbs in the evening. Certainly, if I have a nice amount of fat in the evening, it seems to help induce sleep. Cheers.

### snapper — 2012-07-02T03:53:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34930>

> **Lyndra;34971 wrote:** Congrats on the sleep! That is awesome. I did CT for 2-1/2 months and didn't see any sleep improvement . I'm still waking 4-5 times every night but expect that fixing adrenals & thyroid will get me back on track. Enjoy the good night's rest!

Sorry to hear that. It's just awful not sleeping. I hope the suggestions are of some help. I thought DHEA was a bodybuilding supplement. I'm not sure we can get it in Australia. Can anyone suggest good suppliers?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-02T05:17:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-34943>

> **BJK77;35082 wrote:** +1 on this. I'm sure the DHEA (combined with CT) has been critical to my improved sleep. I generally no longer wake in the middle of the night anymore and if I do on rare occasion, I'm able to fall back to sleep unlike previously where I'd toss and turn for hours. Now if only I could wake up feeling ready to crawl out of bed and not be so stinkin' sore!!

Hmmm, why are you sore? What is up there?

### MartiD — 2012-07-02T19:17:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-35059>

Fortunately if I do wake up during the night I go right back out! That has definately changed over the last 10 months. Before I was just awake. I also like not needing an alarm clock! Although there are times where it would be nice to sleep past 6:30 a.m. on weekends!

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-02T19:38:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-sleeping.2141/post-35065>

# Thread: Hey Ya'll From WV
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### swedishhill — 2012-07-02T00:28:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-34893>

I am a born and bred city girl from Baltimore, moved to the mountains 4 years ago with my family. I love these hills and all the friendly people. I was thin very thin for that matter. Birth Control screwed me up in my late 20's. Through my thirties I continued to yo yo until at one point I weighted 203. Although I am a tall gal I was heavy. I am currently doing the LR with great success, I think because I was already mostly paleo it was easy to fall in place. The first thing I noticed was my feet aside from losing 8 pounds the first 4 days. They were full of callous' before I started. They are as smooth as a baby's bottom. LOL My skin has also became smoother and glowing. I started at 193 and am now 175. I have not been this weight in over 2 years. I would love to hear all of "Your" stories on this wonderful journey.

### Melinda — 2012-07-02T00:57:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-34897>

Welcome! My feet got very soft from all the wonderful butter but swimming every day is setting them back a little. Great jump on weight loss. I admire ppl that take the time to tell their story/journey...I like to read them, too.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-02T02:16:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-34909>

Welcome and awesome news on your weight loss!

### caroline — 2012-07-02T06:46:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-34960>

Welcome and thanks for telling your story. Great news on the weight loss - that hasn't been the case for a lot of us gals - so you are a super star!

### swedishhill — 2012-07-03T01:15:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-35159>

> **caroline;35120 wrote:** Welcome and thanks for telling your story. Great news on the weight loss - that hasn't been the case for a lot of us gals - so you are a super star!

I am as shocked as anyone. I have had a few issues with female stuff and will ask them under the Ask Jack section. I also want to post some photos of before and current progress. I am wanting to start working out, I think when that happens I will see big change. Not that I haven't already. I think that perhaps this will be the first year I actually wear a bikini. Good luck with your journey,

Swedishhill

### swedishhill — 2012-07-03T01:17:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-35162>

Couldn't have done it without the LR. I am currently 19 pounds down in three weeks.... Scary and exciting.

### swedishhill — 2012-07-03T01:18:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-yall-from-wv.2146/post-35164>

Thanks, Melinda.

I love reading the stories of others as well. It keeps me grounded and balanced when I feel frustrated.

# Thread: Noticed something bizarre last night after CT!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jmathisen — 2012-07-02T19:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35071>

So I've been faithfully CT'ing for several weeks, 40 degree or so water, 1 hour. Along with LR, lost 37 lbs.

Was standing in the bathroom after getting out last night and looking at myself in the mirror and noticing some loose skin from the weight loss on my thighs and abdomen, and what was tres bizarre, was that the line of loose skin just about exactly followed the line where my body is submerged in the cold water in the tub.

Kinda freaky. Now I gotta get a stock tank, can't have weird lines.

### fitness@home — 2012-07-02T20:12:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35080>

>
>
>
> Was standing in the bathroom after getting out last night and looking at myself in the mirror and noticing some loose skin from the weight loss on my thighs and abdomen, and what was tres bizarre, was that the line of loose skin just about exactly followed the line where my body is submerged in the cold water in the tub.
>
>
>
>
>
> Kinda freaky. Now I gotta get a stock tank, can't have weird lines.

Interesting! and another indication that the CT is doing its job. Keep going!

I live with a non-believer. Makes for some interesting conversations (and an occasional argument).

### youthfuliz — 2012-07-02T20:24:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35084>

Wow... I need to get my neck and face in the water more.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-02T20:34:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35089>

I just started using the plastic wine coolers to CT the tops of my arms because I can't get them in the tub. I wonder if I will get some strange line.....

[http://www.amazon.com/Vacu-Vin-Rapid-Silver-Chillers/dp/B0000638S9/ref=pd\_bxgy\_k\_img\_c](http://http://www.amazon.com/Vacu-Vin-Rapid-Silver-Chillers/dp/B0000638S9/ref=pd_bxgy_k_img_c)

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-02T21:34:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35097>

That IS freaky! But at least you know it IS working!

Since I can't get my entire body submerged either, I turn over halfway through so that my entire body does get submerged. I start on my back, with my shoulders & upper back covered in water. Halfway through, I turn over so that my entire front is submerged, and I prop myself up on my elbows in the tub.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-02T22:32:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35111>

I wish I knew we could do this before I started CT. I could back in time and just ice the left side of my body.

How cool would that look?

### nonchalant — 2012-07-02T22:59:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-35118>

Maybe we could get ice packs shaped like your team's logo? Hmm, would have to put the same side toward your tummy...

### msclman — 2012-07-11T20:17:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-36895>

an hour at 40 degrees!!!!!! i cant even get my tub to 40 and i put 30 pounds of ice in it! how bad do you shiver?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-11T23:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-36967>

You sure you are a New York mscleman? lol

### jmathisen — 2012-07-12T11:35:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-37115>

> **msclman;37061 wrote:** an hour at 40 degrees!!!!!! i cant even get my tub to 40 and i put 30 pounds of ice in it! how bad do you shiver?

What's weird is no shivering during CT. Few seconds of icy shock when getting in, and then I spend a few seconds getting my headphones and my book and getting the ice bag on my abdomen, and by the time I'm done with that, I've adjusted.

\*But\* once I get out, I get about 2-3 minutes to dry off and pick up my stuff, and then the whole body shivers start. I've never had my butt actually shiver and I'm from montana originally, but since CT, that thing looks like one of those ladies with the belts around their middle hooked up to the machine getting vibrated... And that lasts a good 10-15 minutes, I have to really concentrate to not shiver.

Then it all settles down and I'm just cold, but not frozen.

So far, benefits outweighing the annoyances.

### msclman — 2012-07-12T23:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/noticed-something-bizarre-last-night-after-ct.2160/post-37271>

> **Jack Kruse;37133 wrote:** You sure you are a New York mscleman? lol

im a ct girly man. is that handle available doc?

# Thread: Compounding bio-identical hormones: CW
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compounding-bio-identical-hormones-cw.2161/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-07-02T20:31:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compounding-bio-identical-hormones-cw.2161/post-35088>

An excerpt from my HMO about where they stand. Sad.

4/29/2009

**Bioidentical and Compounded Hormones

Furthermore, hormone levels measured at a moment in time are not a good measure of long term hormone levels because of the natural fluctuations throughout the day. Perimenopausal women (by definition!) may have low estrogen levels one moment, and then normal or high levels the next. Providers at Kaiser Permanente encourage you to rely on your individual symptoms and not blood or saliva levels to determine the effectiveness of any hormone replacement regimen, compounded or not. **Kaiser Permanente does not cover hormone testing for the purpose of monitoring hormone replacement therapy.**

Where can I read more about this?

The leading experts on womenâ€™s health have developed their own responses to this issue. Below is a summary of their positions along with a link to their full fact sheet on the topic:

â€¢

The American College of Obstetrician and Gynecologists (ACOG): â€œMost compounded products have not undergone rigorous clinical testing for safety and efficacy, and issues regarding purity, potency and quality are a concern. Compounded hormone products have the same safety issues as those associated with hormone therapy agents that are approved by the FDA and may have additional risks intrinsic to compounding.â€ (View press release by searching â€œbioidenticalsâ€ at [www.acog.org](http://http://www.acog.org) )

â€¢

North American Menopause Society (NAMS): â€œNAMS does not recommend custom-compounded products over well-tested, government-approved products for the majority of women- and does not recommend saliva testing to determine hormone levels.â€ (View full fact sheet at [www.menopause.org/bioidentical\_NAMS.aspx](http://http://www.menopause.org/bioidentical_NAMS.aspx))

â€¢

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration: â€œThe FDA is concerned that claimsâ€¦(that bioidenticals are safer, natural, can correct hormonal imbalances)â€¦ mislead women and health care professionals, giving them a false sense of assurance about using potentially dangerous hormone products.â€ (View the full fact sheet at â€œBioidenticals: Sorting Myth from Factâ€ at [www.fda.gov/consumer/updates/bioidenticals040808.html](http://http://www.fda.gov/consumer/updates/bioidenticals040808.html) )

What should I do now?**Many women feel confused about hormone therapy, especially if they thought compounded bioidentical hormones to be a more natural or safer option. The decision to use, or not use hormone therapy should be based on your own personal medical history and your own health goals. There is no â€œrightâ€ answer for every woman.

Consider the following examples:

For some women, the benefits of hormone therapy outweigh any potential risks. Their quality of life (whether that be a better night sleep, a better sex drive, or a better sense of well-being) are greatly enhanced on hormone therapy. Hormone users should consider: why am I using them, what are the benefits, and what are the potential risks for me? These questions should be covered with their healthcare provider at least every year. Many women will decrease their dose of hormones over time and then stop completely after several years. If a patient is considering bioidentical hormones, we urge her to start with those that are FDA-approved and have been investigated for purity, safety and efficacy.

For some women with bothersome perimenopausal and menopausal symptoms, the potential side-effects and potential risks of hormone therapy are not acceptable, and they choose not to take them at all. And, they may have a medical condition such as blood clots or heart disease, which makes the risks higher.

Some women have very minor or no symptoms of menopause. There is no recommendation to use hormones in this situation. Any claims for health benefits in the absence of symptoms should be viewed with great skepticism at this time.

Please talk to your healthcare provider about this information. We want to help you achieve your health goals, now and in the future. Please revisit your choices on hormone therapy at least every year, and help us advocate for reliable and responsible research in this area. There are multiple options to help individualize hormone replacement therapy if you decide this is the right treatment for you.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-02T20:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compounding-bio-identical-hormones-cw.2161/post-35090>

Sad....

Can you convince them to pay for the 30 day test? Something like this where it takes more tests [https://www.mymedlab.com/hormone/female-hormone-30-day](http://https://www.mymedlab.com/hormone/female-hormone-30-day) ? I'm trying to be hopeful....

### Glamazon — 2012-07-02T20:58:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compounding-bio-identical-hormones-cw.2161/post-35092>

I have got my results, independently tested. They are not going to cover bio identical hormones.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-02T23:48:45+0300

# Thread: Comparing Thyroid results
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-07-03T01:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35157>

I just found out that my family doctor ran a thyroid test on me last fall. At least, I have something to compare it with this year's one. Not sure what to make of it. The TSH result was better in 2011 but the rest was worse, no?

**Nov 2011

TSH **3.59 (0.4-4.5 mIU/L

T4, Total **7.0 (4.5-12.0 mcg/dL

T3 uptake **32 (22.35 %)

T4, Free, Calculated **2.2 (1.4-3.8 Units)

T3, Total **77 (76-181 ng/dL)

**April 2012**

TSH - 4.29** (0.27-4.20 uIU/ml)

T4 - 9.6** (4.5-12.0 ug/dl)

FT4 - 1.27** (0.83-1.62 ng/dl)

Free T4 Index 3.3** (1.5 -3.8)

T3 Total 86** (80-200ng/dl)

T3 Uptake 34.1** (24.3- 39.0 % )

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T01:18:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35165>

Oh boy, I'm not sure with these labs. If just looking at the TSH you are heading toward less thyroid function. It's too bad you don't have the FT3. But you do have FT4 which tells you how much you have that MAY convert to T3 but you don't know how much because you don't have that lab. The one value is calculated so I don't know if that is a valuable reading. Also just looking at your T3 it's way too low. I like my FT3 at the top of the range....top of the top quartile!  Hope that helps a little.

### Destiny — 2012-07-03T01:46:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35172>

> **Patty Cakes;35325 wrote:** Oh boy, I'm not sure with these labs. If just looking at the TSH you are heading toward less thyroid function. It's too bad you don't have the FT3. But you do have FT4 which tells you how much you have that MAY convert to T3 but you don't know how much because you don't have that lab. The one value is calculated so I don't know if that is a valuable reading. Also just looking at your T3 it's way too low. I like my FT3 at the top of the range....top of the top quartile!  Hope that helps a little.

It does! Thanks, Patty! Hopefully, my endo tomorrow will understand to all of it!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T02:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35175>

I don't want to discourage you but I have had my own personal struggle with endos. I hope yours goes better. But remember if he/she is useless just check the name off the list and try to find someone who can help. I ended up with a hormone specialist (anti-aging) for treatment of thyroid. I highly recommend an anti-aging doc as that is pretty much all they do...hormones! Sometimes an Ob-Gyn or even an Ob-Gyn NP can be helpful too. Good luck!

### Destiny — 2012-07-03T02:30:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35179>

But remember if he/she is useless just check the name off the list and try to find someone who can help. I ended up with a hormone specialist (anti-aging) for treatment of thyroid. I highly recommend an anti-aging doc as that is pretty much all they do...hormones! Sometimes an Ob-Gyn or even an Ob-Gyn NP can be helpful too. Good luck!

I picked a young endo hoping that she will be more up to date. I have an OB GYN who prescribes BHRT (not some common in the Tristate area). She also told me in April that my TSH was bordeline but still OK. I'll keep searching.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T21:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35320>

I would definitely ask for a TPO and Thyroglobulin antibodies test to rule out Hashimotos from your endo. If she won't authorize try and get it from your ObGyn. If you cannot get it there go get it yourself from an online lab. You just want to make sure. As I'm typing this I'm thinking you may have already had those done. Maybe not???

### Destiny — 2012-07-03T22:43:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comparing-thyroid-results.2168/post-35343>

> **Patty Cakes;35481 wrote:** I would definitely ask for a TPO and Thyroglobulin antibodies test to rule out Hashimotos from your endo. If she won't authorize try and get it from your ObGyn. If you cannot get it there go get it yourself from an online lab. You just want to make sure. As I'm typing this I'm thinking you may have already had those done. Maybe not???

I had my appointment with an endo today. The endo promised to run the entire thyroid test including the antibodies. I believe it when I see it. The whole thing with her is a no go for the future.

First, she crushed me when she told me that she is not really concerned about any TSH below 10! When I told her that I am sluggish all the time, she gave me a prescription for Synthroid 25 mg per day. I am not going to take it.

The next thing was that she had no idea about what the BHRT Triest cream is. When I told her, she told me Premarin is much better than some weak cream. Also my spiky nocturnal cortisol did not concern her at all. According to her, it was a sort of in line. The last straw was that she retested me for my toxic levels of vitamin D that is at 114. She was terrified by that number - her words not mine.

In addition, she looked herself very unhealthy like she had a very hyper thyroid. It is so disappointing but at least, let's hope, I will have some test results.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-03T23:24:14+0300

You could add the tri-est, bi-est question to it. It may help to screen through a lot of docs without a consultation visit.

# Thread: No cardio ever...ever?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-07-03T02:27:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35178>

It's quite clear one should not do cardio when one is LR (e.g., why is oprah fat post)...but is cardio reasonable when one is LS? I get the impression that Dr. K doesn't recommend cardio ever aside from sprints (and walking or swimming etc). I'm getting confused after reading some other threads in this forum mentioning 5ks and 10ks.

I really miss running/jogging. I'm still LR now, so wouldn't dream of doing it...but I was hopping when I finally become LS that I could start up again...nothing crazy...maybe a jog/run 40min 2x a week max. I find it really relaxing and a lot more fun than boring walking.

### Barry — 2012-07-03T16:48:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35274>

> **Eve;35338 wrote:** It's quite clear one should not do cardio when one is LR (e.g., why is oprah fat post)...but is cardio reasonable when one is LS? >
>
>
> I really miss running/jogging. I'm still LR now, so wouldn't dream of doing it...but I was hopping when I finally become LS that I could start up again...nothing crazy...maybe a jog/run 40min 2x a week max. I find it really relaxing and a lot more fun than boring walking.

You just answered your own question by your last sentence !! That thought process/experience is what counts, that = Epigenetics.

And if any paleo meatheads try to tell you otherwise, they haven't got a clue what they are talking about ;-)

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-03T16:53:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35275>

move slowly is the optimal way if you want to do cardio. that said if your adrenals are ok. and you're doing it at the optimal wo time btwn 3-6pm and all other medical issues are resolved - there's no reason why you can't do a long slow run occasionally - the thing you've got to watch out for is the "high" you get - and think its ok to do more. I love the HIITs I do - but I have to be careful - when I'm stressed I'll skip them. no need to do them and raise cortisol and inflammation. you're biggest gains in fitness will come from Lifting Heavy Things, and the occasional HIIT. with bike riding (not spin class), swimming, walking or hiking.

I've gotten back into swimming - and realized that I have to make myself go slowly - not push the intensity. Its easy to push the intensity in any form of exercise.

### AKMan — 2012-07-03T20:52:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-35316>

I used to love to run. Poor diet and metabolism problems caught up with me a few years back and I had to quit running. Now, thanks to Dr. Kruse and a few others, I am in the best shape of my life at 46. I started running again this summer and it feels great! I can run 3 miles in about 25 minutes. Now that I've seen what I can do, my plan for the rest of the summer is this: Brisk walk 1/2 mile (8 minutes), run all-out 1 mile (6-8 minutes), brisk walk 1/2 mile with a few 100yd sprints thrown in (6-8 minutes).

Total time is under 25 minutes for this workout. It doesn't feel like a 3 mile run at all, and I think I get way more out of it. I'm only doing it once a week, so getting plenty of recovery time. I think this is a great alternative to the chronic cardio style running most people do.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-07-26T18:07:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41209>

So what's the consensus here? I usually sign up for a annual 5k in my area. It is a fun time.

I'm not a fast runner, I just try not to come in last.

Is this harming me?

### Ray — 2012-07-26T18:24:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41213>

Isn't the hypothesis here something like: more cardio -> more oxidative stress -> more cell death -> more stem cell depletion

### quelsen — 2012-07-26T19:05:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41237>

as long as one understands that

"health provides energy for movement"

and that is IS NOT

"movement provides energy for health"

the rest is up to you.

Exercise has its place. however as with anything it can become damaging to you.

if you accept that then you will find your own sweet spot.

run is you want, or not.

bear in mind Body by Science ( for women ) in normal everyday stuff.

treat 5k's and above like your 20% note your 80%

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-26T19:14:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41242>

> **Ray;41430 wrote:** Isn't the hypothesis here something like: more cardio -> more oxidative stress -> more cell death -> more stem cell depletion

Precisely.......

### Lyndra — 2012-07-26T19:38:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41254>

One question for me is what is considered cardio? Is it only the high-paced endurance activity, say 75% of your max? Can one follow a Maffetone-type program (pure aerobic, low heart rate) more safely? Once I can get back to exercising, my plan is to do the Maffetone approach and build endurance at a low heart rate and not worry about being "fast."

### Stephen — 2012-07-27T04:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/no-cardio-ever-ever.2172/post-41433>

> **Lyndra;41471 wrote:** One question for me is what is considered cardio? Is it only the high-paced endurance activity, say 75% of your max? Can one follow a Maffetone-type program (pure aerobic, low heart rate) more safely? Once I can get back to exercising, my plan is to do the Maffetone approach and build endurance at a low heart rate and not worry about being "fast."

Personally I think this is an ok idea. My personal opinion is just don't go doing it every day of the week or for extended sessions. That is unless you want to compete in something. Then recognise that in general athletic performance does not = health.

For far too many the way they approach exercise is in a way such that their highs are too low and their lows are too high. 180bpm - age is a good place to target to stay below for pure aerobic, low heart rate training. Take another 5bpm off if you're just getting back into it or you get sick more than twice a year. Depending on the person this heart rate target (energy systems training) may well require walking, especially when going up hills.

Stephen
