# Thread: Risk of alcohol abuse may increase after bariatric surgery
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery.2175/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-03T03:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery.2175/post-35192>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/06/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery-12300](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/06/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery-12300)

Among patients who underwent bariatric surgery, there was a higher prevalence of alcohol use disorders in the second year after surgery, and specifically after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, compared with the years immediately before and following surgery, according to a study in the June 20 issue of JAMA. This study is being published early online to coincide with its presentation at the annual meeting of the American Society for Metabolic and Bariatric Surgery.

â€œAs the prevalence of severe obesity increases in the United States, it is becoming increasingly common for health care providers and their patients to consider bariatric surgery, which is the most effective and durable treatment for severe obesity. Although bariatric surgery may reduce long-term mortality, and it carries a low risk of short-term serious adverse outcomes, safety concerns remain. Anecdotal reports suggest that bariatric surgery may increase the risk for alcohol use disorders (AUD; i.e., alcohol abuse and dependence),â€￾ according to background information in the article.

The authors add that there is evidence that some bariatric surgical procedures (i.e., Roux-en-Y gastric bypass \[RYGB\] and sleeve gastrectomy) alter the pharmacokinetics of alcohol. â€œGiven a standardized quantity of alcohol, patients reach a higher peak alcohol level after surgery compared with case-controls or their preoperative levels.â€￾

Wendy C. King, Ph.D., of the University of Pittsburgh, and colleagues conducted a study to determine whether the prevalence of AUD changed following bariatric surgery, comparing reported AUD in the year prior to surgery with the first and second years after surgery. The prospective study included 2,458 adults who underwent bariatric surgery at 10 U.S. hospitals. Of these participants, 1,945 (78.8 percent female; 87 percent white; median \[midpoint\] age, 47 years; median body mass index, 45.8) completed preoperative and postoperative (at 1 year and/or 2 years) assessments between 2006 and 2011. The primary outcome measure for the study was past year AUD symptoms determined with the Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (AUDIT) (indication of alcohol-related harm, alcohol dependence symptoms, or score 8 or greater).

The researchers found that the prevalence of AUD symptoms did not significantly differ from 1 year before to 1 year after bariatric surgery (7.6 percent vs. 7.3 percent), but was significantly higher in the second postoperative year (9.6 percent). Frequency of alcohol consumption and AUD significantly increased in the second postoperative year compared with the year prior to surgery or the first postoperative year.

â€œMore than half (66/106; 62.3 percent) of thosereporting AUD at the preoperative assessment continued to have or had recurrent AUD within the first 2 postoperative years,â€￾ the authors write. â€œIn contrast, 7.9 percent (101/1,283) of participants not reporting AUD at the preoperative assessment had postoperative AUD. Nonetheless, more than half (101/167; 60.5 percent) of postoperative AUD was reported by those not reporting AUD at the preoperative assessmentâ€￾

The researchers also found that male sex, younger age, smoking, regular alcohol consumption, AUD, recreational drug use, lower score on a measure of a sense of belonging at the preoperative assessment and undergoing a RYGB were independently related to an increased likelihood of AUD after surgery. RYGB accounted for 70 percent of surgeries and doubled the likelihood of postoperative AUD compared with laparoscopic adjustable gastric banding.

The authors note that although the 2 percent increase (7.6 percent to 9.6 percent) in prevalence of AUD from prior to surgery to the 2-year postoperative assessment may seem small, the increase potentially represents more than 2,000 additional people with AUD in the United States each year, with accompanying personal, financial, and societal costs.

â€œThis study has important implications for the care of patients who undergo bariatric surgery. Regardless of alcohol history, patients should be educated about the potential effects of bariatric surgery, in particular RYGB, to increase the risk of AUD. In addition, alcohol screening and, if indicated, referral should be offered as part of routine preoperative and postoperative clinical care. Further research should examine the long-term effect of bariatric surgery on AUD, and the relationship of AUD to postoperative weight control.â€￾

My doctor just recommends eating as though you've had a bypass.... I have a facebook friend that lost 40 lbs in two months following the menu for bypass. She quit but she didn't gain it back. I think she quit when her clothes fit. I think being too broke for bigger clothes was her motivation.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-03T04:28:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/risk-of-alcohol-abuse-may-increase-after-bariatric-surgery.2175/post-35216>

bariatric surgery carries massive depression and suicide risks.....close to 40%. Pass on this option.

# Thread: Today's News Stories:  Leptin and Epigenetics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-news-stories-leptin-and-epigenetics.2191/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-07-03T22:57:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-news-stories-leptin-and-epigenetics.2191/post-35347>

Why hasn't Jack told us any of this before???

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-epigenetics-genes-rheumatoid-arthritis.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-epigenetics-genes-rheumatoid-arthritis.html)

"A research team at the University of California, San Diego â€“ led by Gary S. Firestein, professor in the Division of Rheumatology, Allergy and Immunology at UC San Diego School of Medicine â€“ investigated a mechanism usually implicated in cancer and in fetal development, called DNA methylation, in the progression of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). They found that epigenetic changes due to methylation play a key role in altering genes that could potentially contribute to inflammation and joint damage. Their study is currently published in the online edition of the Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases.

"Genomics has rapidly advanced our understanding of susceptibility and severity of rheumatoid arthritis," said Firestein. "While many genetic associations have been described in this disease, we also know that if one identical twin develops RA that the other twin only has a 12 to 15 percent chance of also getting the disease. This suggests that other factors are at play â€“ epigenetic influences." "

And: [http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-discovery-cellular-pathways-converge-food.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-discovery-cellular-pathways-converge-food.html)

"The Kahn laboratory was the first to describe AMPK's critical role in mediating the actions of leptin, the hormone produced by fat cells that serves as a master regulator of neuroendocrine, metabolic, vascular, sympathetic and immune function. In 2002, Kahn demonstrated that AMPK is activated by leptin in skeletal muscle, thereby enabling the hormone to metabolize fatty acids. Subsequently, in 2004, her laboratory discovered that an opposing scenario takes place in the brain's hypothalamus, where AMPK is inhibited by leptin.

"Having determined that leptin's effects on food intake and body weight depend on the inhibition of AMPK in the hypothalamus, we wanted to determine the signaling events that were responsible for this effect," she explains.

"

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-04T00:05:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-news-stories-leptin-and-epigenetics.2191/post-35365>

### janagram — 2012-07-04T00:38:34+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-13T19:41:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-news-stories-leptin-and-epigenetics.2191/post-37530>

> **AKMan;35510 wrote:** Why hasn't Jack told us any of this before???
>
>
>
> [http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-epigenetics-genes-rheumatoid-arthritis.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-epigenetics-genes-rheumatoid-arthritis.html)
>
>
>
> "A research team at the University of California, San Diego – led by Gary S. Firestein, professor in the Division of Rheumatology, Allergy and Immunology at UC San Diego School of Medicine – investigated a mechanism usually implicated in cancer and in fetal development, called DNA methylation, in the progression of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). They found that epigenetic changes due to methylation play a key role in altering genes that could potentially contribute to inflammation and joint damage. Their study is currently published in the online edition of the Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases.
>
>
>
> "Genomics has rapidly advanced our understanding of susceptibility and severity of rheumatoid arthritis," said Firestein. "While many genetic associations have been described in this disease, we also know that if one identical twin develops RA that the other twin only has a 12 to 15 percent chance of also getting the disease. This suggests that other factors are at play – epigenetic influences." "
>
>
>
> And: [http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-discovery-cellular-pathways-converge-food.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-07-discovery-cellular-pathways-converge-food.html)
>
>
>
> "The Kahn laboratory was the first to describe AMPK's critical role in mediating the actions of leptin, the hormone produced by fat cells that serves as a master regulator of neuroendocrine, metabolic, vascular, sympathetic and immune function. In 2002, Kahn demonstrated that AMPK is activated by leptin in skeletal muscle, thereby enabling the hormone to metabolize fatty acids. Subsequently, in 2004, her laboratory discovered that an opposing scenario takes place in the brain's hypothalamus, where AMPK is inhibited by leptin.
>
>
>
> "Having determined that leptin's effects on food intake and body weight depend on the inhibition of AMPK in the hypothalamus, we wanted to determine the signaling events that were responsible for this effect," she explains.
>
> "

My entire blog is about this.........GO read the IF and leptin blog and tell me again that I have not told you about it? I did.......i dont think you got the message......because you did not have enough data points then to make sense of it. This is how the Quilt works.......when you dont know the science.......I need to give you small bites of it.......that is called a blog post. Then I slowly add more and more pieces to fill in the gaps. The more bits of knowledge you get your own brain puts things together and you have this insight or bolt of intuition that maybe I might be correct. When bits of knowledge come together we call this wisdom. Wisdom and knowledge are not the same........knowledge does not always lead to understanding. If you do not believe that go read a scientist blog like Guyenet. There they argue constantly about bits of knowledge instead of putting the knowledge together into a framework of how it works.........for an organism. I do the opposite. Guyenet is a 30 ft guy (and he is quite smart by the way, but being smart can also allow you to fall thru many trap doors when your vision is constricted by facts not connected to a wisdom)........

I am a person who gets the 30 ft perspective but I connect the dots to get the 30000 ft view before I use it. Why? If I am wrong, I can get sued.......Guyenet can't, and he just goes on to another experiment.

This is why I have to really think in 6 dimensions to make sure things are safe before we apply them...........physicians and surgeons operate on the 30000 ft to help disease and wellness........But if some of the bits of knowledge we accept and acquire are off........the 30000 ft view is off (think cholesterol or Hormones as a great example) I hope this makes sense..........

### janagram — 2012-07-13T21:54:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-news-stories-leptin-and-epigenetics.2191/post-37577>

I didn't realize AKman was being facetious....but, yeah, he realizes you HAVE told us this!

And thanks for this further discussion, because it is very interesting. I really appreciate the 30000 foot view....the 30 ft. view is just news I can't use! And I like the way you discriminate between wisdom and knowledge....very important.

### Michalis — 2018-01-03T13:46:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/todays-news-stories-leptin-and-epigenetics.2191/post-231472>

I am really late to the party but things have started making sense a bit.

For the folks like me that were or still are obese and have cartilage issues see the links below.. Leptin and Cartiladge... Boom.

[http://ard.bmj.com/content/71/3/455](http://ard.bmj.com/content/71/3/455)

**Conclusions** Leptin acts as a pro-inflammatory adipokine with a catabolic role on cartilage metabolism via the upregulation of proteolytic enzymes and acts synergistically with other pro-inflammatory stimuli. This suggests that the infrapatellar fat pad and other WAT in arthritic joints are local producers of leptin, which may contribute to the inflammatory and degenerative processes in cartilage catabolism, providing a mechanistic link between obesity and osteoarthritis.

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bcpt.12160/full](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bcpt.12160/full)

**Conclusions**
In the light of the presented evidence, obesity as a risk factor for OA turns out to be more than just weight loaded on joints. Adipocytokine leptin emerges as a potential obesity-related systemic factor that could push intra-articular balance towards inflammation and destruction in the pathogenesis of OA. The whole pathophysiological network of OA involves multiple mediators, but obese OA patients seem to be more susceptible to the detrimental effects of leptin in the joints because of elevated levels of bioactive leptin in the synovial fluid and, further, due to increased responsiveness of the cartilage to the leptin effects when SOCS-3 levels are low (figs [2](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bcpt.12160/full#bcpt12160-fig-0002) and [3](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bcpt.12160/full#bcpt12160-fig-0003)). The evidence discussed in the present MiniReview supports the idea of leptin as a possible link between obesity and OA, and as a target for disease-modifying drugs for the treatment for OA.

This one as well might have been posted in the forum somewhere..

[http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03008207.2017.1385605?journalCode=icts20](http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03008207.2017.1385605?journalCode=icts20)

Abstract :
Leptin alone and in combination with interleukin-1-beta induced cartilage degradation potentially inhibited by EPA and DHA

# Thread: Lowering RT3 with desiccated thyroid?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowering-rt3-with-desiccated-thyroid.2212/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Luke — 2012-07-05T10:30:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowering-rt3-with-desiccated-thyroid.2212/post-35598>

My doctor last week gave me a prescription for desiccated thyroid after reading my lab results.

Free T4: 15.0 pmol/L (10.0-19.0)

TSH: 4.43 mIU/L (0.50-4.00)

Free T3: 6.0 pmol/L (3.5-6.5)

Reverse T3: 720 pmol/L (140-1200)

My ratio of T3/RT3 is 8 & im concerned about the T4 in my medication converting to extra RT3 & making things worse. I have no nutritional imbalaces that need to be adressed.

Has anyone seen RT3 problems treated with Armour/Desiccated thyroid alone or do you feel extra T3 is absolutley necessary?

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-07-05T14:47:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowering-rt3-with-desiccated-thyroid.2212/post-35606>

there are some that have treated an RT3 problem with NDT but it takes a very, very long time because you have the continuous issue of the incoming t4 converting to Rt3. If I were to try it with NDT I would make sure to take a decent amount of milk thistle and selenium as per Janie over at Stopthethyroidmadness.com

> Using a good liver cleanse/support product, plus Selenium: Since the bulk of RT3 is made in the liver, some have lowered their high RT3 by using a good liver cleanse, especially those with Milk Thistle. You may have to DOUBLE the recommended amount to get the lowered RT3. Milk Thistle can supposedly lower ferritin, so patients are adding iron to their supplementation, or raising what they are already on. Also make sure your Milk Thistle comes from the seeds. Milk Thistle not from the seeds can have an estrogenic effect. Also, there is a lot of research which proves that low selenium can increase RT3. So patients supplement with Selenium to stop that fact.

it\`s definitely faster to do only T3 and treat the concurrent problems that caused the RT3 situation.

your labs suggest you are in a commonwealth(?) country or country other than the USA, by chance are you in Canada and if so where did you get the RT3 done?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-05T15:23:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowering-rt3-with-desiccated-thyroid.2212/post-35609>

IT wont work.......any NDT still drives T3 and T4 to rT3 if CRH/ACTH/cortisol is out of its rhythm.

I've taken Milk Thistle over the last few months but didn't feel any change. I live in Australia. I return to my doc in 5 weeks time if I haven't improved I'm going to strongly suggest T3 only which I wanted at first but he persisted I try NDT first.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-06T14:14:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lowering-rt3-with-desiccated-thyroid.2212/post-35820>

> **Luke;35973 wrote:** I've taken Milk Thistle over the last few months but didn't feel any change. I live in Australia. I return to my doc in 5 weeks time if I haven't improved I'm going to strongly suggest T3 only which I wanted at first but he persisted I try NDT first.

Luke, just keep in mind what Dr. Kruse and Colleen just said about the NDT. You can go to T3 only but you have to find out why T4 is being converted to RT3 instead of T3. I was making progress with the milk thistle and t3 route only but as soon as my new doc took me off of t3 and put me on NDT, my ratio slid again. You need further testing to see what is actually driving it or you will won't solve the underlying problem. You might want to look at your cortisol levels thruout the day and see where things are at. You might be surprised at what you find. This will tell you a lot about how things are running. You can do this yourself if you have not already.

# Thread: Effects of pregnenolone
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-07-05T17:20:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35623>

I am going to see my OBGYN in the mid of August since I am supplementing my estrogen and progesterone. I am not great on it so I've decided to start taking 15 mg of pregnenolone in the morning. The idea is to start slow and see since I get heart palpitations a lot.

After two days, I know, it might be a placebo effect, but I feel calmer, almost no heart palpitations, less sluggish and more motivated to do the housework. In addition, my FBG went down to 94 from 104. I also slept better. Is it in my head or is this a result of supplementing with pregnenolone?

It this continues well for a week, I want to add 5mg of DHEA and then 25 mg of 7-Keto. Any experiences with this, or any suggestions?

### BJK77 — 2012-07-06T03:08:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35759>

15 mg is a pretty small dose, but you're describing some of the benefits I've noticed from pregnenolone and DHEA though I take much higher doses. I just have a general feeling of well being that I haven't had in a very long time. My sleep has improved, energy increased and I just feel more motivated and excited about life. I'm currently taking 200 mg pregnenolone and 150 mg DHEA split in 2 doses throughout the day.

### Destiny — 2012-07-06T15:35:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35830>

My last night was not good. I woke up at 12 and at 5. I felt that my heart pulse was running crazy, not the palpitations, just the heart pumping really fast. I felt very anxious with almost like the heart was in my throat. This morning I checked my heart rate -100 and BP was 117/77. I did a short CT and it went like this - 94 and BP 121/74. My pulse is normally in 80's and BP usually 112/70.

Any ideas?

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-06T17:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35851>

Take a look at this link that souldanzer posted on the thyroid and ct thread (here: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2214-Thyroid-and-CT&highlight=thyroid](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2214-Thyroid-and-CT&highlight=thyroid))

I'm still trying to get a drip on things, but it sounds like the extra pregnenelone landed you with excess thyroid hormones in your cells (see description below), which made you feel overstimulated. If your thyroid hormones are not a concern, he suggests taking a smaller dose (or maybe dividing the dose) of pregnenelone, to prevent this kind of over-response. Hope this helps!

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/...l-pregnenolone](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/...l-pregnenolone)

Irregular heart rate (eg: palpitations) is a symptom of temporarily too low cortisol in relation to thyroid hormones, and it's severity is determined by the relative difference in cortisol and thyroid hormones.

When it's triggered exclusively by an increase in dose of supplementatary preg, then it's due to the following process:

0) Initially resting metabolic rate is very low, so cortisol levels and thyroid hormones are relatively low - but they are balanced to each other.

1) supplementation with preg initially raises preg levels

2) adrenals synthesize preg along the cortisol-production-line into cortisol

3) cells absorb more cortisol and increase their metabolic rate (good)

\[when cells increase their metabolic rate, they consume thyroid hormones faster, and they consume cortisol faster\]

4) cells consume T3 faster, cells consume cortisol faster

5) cells start to invoke their genetic programming which raises their resting metabolic rate, ie: they start to "stockpile" all hormones - especially T4 and cortisol (and they hypothalamus allows serum levels of all hormones to rise) THIS IS THE FUNDAMENTAL GOAL OF THIS EXERCISE

6) but when preg dose is too high for the initial T4 levels, cells won't absorb all of the extra cortisol, and some of that extra cortisol stays temporarily in serum, and the hypothalamus detects this as "too high cortsol" and backs off CRH, which tells the pituitary to back off ACTH, which tells the adrenals to back off cortisol production - by more than the amount of additional cortisol being synthesized by the supplementary preg.

\[ The reason why the hypothalamus over-reacts this way is due to the speed cortisol levels increased. The faster cortisol levels increase, the more harshly the hypothalamus responds by lowering ACTH harshly. Ie: if you had slowed down the rate of absorption of preg, eg: via using transdermal preg, then cortisol would rise more slowly, and the hypothalamus would only react optimally to the excess cortisol \]

7) As a result of the hypothalamus' over-reaction, cortisol levels are now temporarily too low

8) At this time cells are loaded with more T4 and T3, but they've had their cortisol supply reduced, so they have their thyroid hormones in excess of their cortisol.

In Hormone Modulation Therapy 101 I've improved the explanation of this phenomenon as follows:

Tune in to the following symptoms of excess preg, excess cortisol and excess T3, ie:

a) Symptoms of preg-is-in-excess-of-T3 (same as T3-is-too-low-compared-to-preg) are slightly spacey feelings - try reducing preg.

b) Symptoms of T3-is-in-excess-of-cortisol (same as cortisol-is-too-low-compared-to-T3) are awareness of your heart beat / erratic heart beat - this has a complex explanation: it's due to an initial increase in metabolic rate causing an initial boost in cells absorbing both cortisol and thryoid hormones, followed by running low on thyroid hormones, followed by temporary ACTH suppression due to excess cortisol due to excess preg. Result is temporary excess of thyroid hormones in relation to cortisol. Try decreasing preg to reduce the likelihood of ACTH suppression, or switch from oral to TD preg to slow absorption of preg to prevent the excess cortisol.

c) Symptoms of cortisol-is-in-excess-of-T3 (same as T3-is-too-low-compared-to-cortisol) are temporary excess cortisol causing ACTH suppression, causing too low preg, causing drowsiness and / or the yawns - try reducing preg.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-06T17:36:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35860>

Thanks for that post, Bigknitwit! Very interesting.

Thank you, Bigknitwit, it makes me feel better. I will try a 7mg dose and go from there.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-06T19:42:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35894>

Start slow for sure. When I first started preg (100 mg) I had to lower the dose (50 mg) because the same thing happened to me..... heart palps, racing, and feeling overstimulated. I lowered it and felt fine. Now I take two doses of 50 mg each. I love the preg because it gives me a little energy without any of the negatives. Yesterday I forgot to take it until the afternoon and was tired by late morning. Then within 30 min of taking it I felt great and energized. Actually my "energized" is probably most people's normal feeling! LOL

### Destiny — 2012-07-06T20:01:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35901>

> **Patty Cakes;36058 wrote:** Start slow for sure. When I first started preg (100 mg) I had to lower the dose (50 mg) because the same thing happened to me..... heart palps, racing, and feeling overstimulated. I lowered it and felt fine. Now I take two doses of 50 mg each. I love the preg because it gives me a little energy without any of the negatives. Yesterday I forgot to take it until the afternoon and was tired by late morning. Then within 30 min of taking it I felt great and energized. Actually my "energized" is probably most people's normal feeling! LOL

That is what I need ..some energy during the day. Somehow yesterday, it all waited until the night to manifest.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-06T20:06:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/effects-of-pregnenolone.2215/post-35902>

That was probably your cortisol. That is usually how adrenal dysfunction start with high nighttime cortisol.  I can't remember but have you taken a cortisol saliva test to confirm a dysfunction? Plus you have thyroid issues that "zap your vital essence!" I ran around for 3 years completely hypo being tired all the time. I couldn't put my head down on the desk at work for fear I would never get up! Ohhhh....I feel your pain!

But you can slowly raise your preg over time. I don't know what I am taking about but I would think you could raise it a little every 2-3 weeks. I started too high then lowered it and two weeks later I added the second dose back in and was fine.

# Thread: Please help me like CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-07-06T03:09:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-35760>

I know there's health benefits, but, I kind of hate CT right now (still a newbie), even in this hot summer weather (I can't even fathom doing this in winter!). I HATE being cold, and I frequently am very cold--I go to bed in layers of clothes and blankets (even hat and mittens if I'm cold enough)...this is killing me. I don't have to \*love\* CT; I'd settle for just liking it. Any encouragement? Does it get easier? I was willing to give up my bad food habits, add exercise, take supplements, but this, I'm having a really bad time with.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-07-06T03:15:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-35764>

I ct'd today after mowing the lawn, felt refreshing, not cold. Try spot ct'ing with ice packs for a while, or make the tub water a little warmer til you get acclimated. Are you anemic? How are your ferritin levels?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-06T03:44:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-35780>

I started in February and still can't say I "like" it. I do it because I have to. I still shiver afterwards and am COLD for a bit. But, after time I warm up and get over it.

### cookworm — 2012-07-06T03:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-35784>

> **incorky;35927 wrote:** Are you anemic? How are your ferritin levels?

This is a good question--I have problems off and on with anemia, but haven't had my levels checked recently (I do take iron supplements daily though). I hadn't thought about the possibility of anemia.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-07-06T04:17:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-35788>

well your bat mitochondria need iron in order to function well. The iron is a morphological feature of brown fat. Cold intolerance, cold hands/feet is a feature of low iron stores (ferritin).

### jmathisen — 2012-07-09T20:54:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36372>

I look forward to CT each day. I use the hour to "center", contemplate, read a book, just get some quality "me" time. I find that doing it a couple hours before bed helps. I used to do it right before bed, but I didn't like laying there in bed cold, it seemed to take longer to get to sleep. Now I get out, get the robe on, take care of a few things, and get some movement in so I get warmer before I hit the sack.

Has made it much more tolerable.

### freesia — 2012-07-09T23:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36397>

It does get easier but I think it may take longer for some of us.

I started to like CT when my body started responding with a raised temperature.

It took me 10 weeks to get the kind of response Jack talked about achieving in 2 weeks. I just had to take it slow...detox was intense and that worked against progress if I pushed myself too far too fast. I started at almost 60F and came down slowly....over the past 3 months to 48F. I found reading all the CT blogs useful for motivation.

Like you I really, really HATED being cold. I have bad and very clear memories of times I was cold and miserable, right back from high school days. Other people weren't cold. Now it's mid-winter here I'm rapt to find I'm the one with warmer hands than others!

I don't know if CT is indicated for everyone, but if you really want to do it I'd recommend starting in Summer (maybe get ferritin checked as suggested first) then easing into it as the temperature goes down in Fall.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-10T00:31:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36405>

You do it regardless of your wants needs or emotions......and watch what happens.

### caroline — 2012-07-11T04:30:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36725>

> **Jack Kruse;36571 wrote:** You do it regardless of your wants needs or emotions......and watch what happens.

words to live by!

### shilohman — 2012-07-11T05:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36738>

Just ease into it. As suggested above, start with spot CTing, then once you get adapted to that try very short showers and expand from there.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-11T05:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36740>

> **Jack Kruse;36571 wrote:** You do it regardless of your wants needs or emotions......and watch what happens.

I think there must be more to this - this mindset of "just do it" reminds me of the ridiculous chronic cardio times. I did that regardless of my wants, needs, or emotions - 2-3 hrs a day, 6 days a week.

Shouldn't we be more alert to actual outcomes? With CT, it gets to feel so right and good for you - but if not, perhaps thats a clue?

### freesia — 2012-07-11T05:50:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36746>

Have to admit I _was on a kind of auto-pilot for the first 2 -3 weeks of full baths and that worked best for me.  I already knew_ I didn't like cold and didn't want to invest precious time and energy wrestling with what I knew vs what could be.

...I think I used to run the bath almost as if I was running it for someone else. Then I measured the temp then got right in while focussed firmly on whatever was playing on my laptop - at that time it was UK costume Dramas...  The whole thing could almost have been happening to someone else.

Then when my temperature started heating up while I was running the baths I really made friends with the whole process. Who said I didn't like cold???

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-11T06:09:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36748>

> **Cavemam;36906 wrote:** I think there must be more to this - this mindset of "just do it" reminds me of the ridiculous chronic cardio times. I did that regardless of my wants, needs, or emotions - 2-3 hrs a day, 6 days a week.
>
>
>
> Shouldn't we be more alert to actual outcomes? With CT, it gets to feel so right and good for you - but if not, perhaps thats a clue?

outcomes are in how you feel and your labs.......they should be congruent.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-11T18:36:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-like-ct.2224/post-36847>

> **Jack Kruse;36571 wrote: You do it regardless of your wants needs or emotions......and watch what happens.
>
>
> when you're looking at your health - an CT can help fix it - you look at the big picture - what is it you're doing this for - make the decision and execute, you may not like it but you will like the end results.
>
>
>
> Cavemam;36906 wrote:** I think there must be more to this - this mindset of "just do it" reminds me of the ridiculous chronic cardio times. I did that regardless of my wants, needs, or emotions - 2-3 hrs a day, 6 days a week.
>
>
>
> Shouldn't we be more alert to actual outcomes? With CT, it gets to feel so right and good for you - but if not, perhaps thats a clue?

how much of not feeling right is intuition, and how much is CW that's still lurking telling you're effing nuts?

there are times when I don't want to CT - but I do it anyway - it may not be full submersion - but even Spot CT keeps you moving fwd, even if you don't want to do it. There may also be points in your life where CT doesn't fit due to chaos etc. but I've found I'm happier when I CT - crazy huh? maybe its the 1hr I get just sitting in the tub, reading a book, or listening to a podcast etc. its my meditation I guess...

# Thread: What's the big deal about liver?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jmathisen — 2012-07-06T09:12:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/post-35815>

Looking on nutritiondata.com, except for ridiculously large excess of the RDA for things like copper, beef liver doesn't seem like that extraordinary a meat, compared to all the other choices. Yet I see people pushing it like chiclets in some of the diet discussions.

Is there some super-secret to offal that only the initiates understand? Get a buzz off it? ("Hey dude, just coming down off my liver buzz... Need to score some pate")...

I'll eat it (I hate liver), if I have to, but as wonder-food, it doesn't seem that extraordinary.

Just thinking about it is giving me the creeps. Wonder if I can just eat sardines all day every day and be happy...

What am I missing?

### Michael — 2012-07-06T11:17:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/post-35818>

IIRC, it has at least ten times as much folate as you'll get in any of the vegetables that are (strangely) widely-touted as **the** source of this nutrient.

It's also a good source of iron--so it's important for menstruating women.

But it's probably best known as a good source of vitamin A. Charts you see around, and services like Fitday, sometimes list stuff like carrots as containing A. Well, they don't. They contain beta-carotene, which is a precursor to it, and which, it turns out, some people cannot convert efficiently at all. The World Health Organization keeps finding, to its dismay, that many children in the 3rd world are drastically low in this nutrient, which threatens their eyesight among other problems, and can't get it from what it had assumed (incorrectly) were good sources. (The best of the cheapest hopes to solve that problem right now seems to be palm oil.)

There are probably other nutrients in there that I haven't remembered, and for all I know there may be ones that haven't even been isolated yet.

Suffice to say, liver was usually the hunter's portion. That's to say, in many societies it seems that the man who killed a game animal would be the first to get some of the choice cut - and that usually meant the liver. He'd probably cut it out and eat it a good portion of it raw, there and then. That's shown in _Dances with Wolves_ (and is probably one of the few historically authentic touches in the film). People didn't do that for nothing: they knew what did them good.

If you want more details on why liver - and other organ meats - should be in your diet, I should pop over to the Weston A. Price Foundation website and look up some articles there.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-06T14:18:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/post-35822>

> **jmathisen;35978 wrote:** Is there some super-secret to offal that only the initiates understand? Get a buzz off it? ("Hey dude, just coming down offJust thinking about it is giving me the creeps. Wonder if I can just eat sardines all day every day and be happy...
>
>
>
> What am I missing?

Yep to everything Micheal said but also from what Dr. K. is saying about seafood being top of his food chain, you probably would be fine with sardines. Interesting thought.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-06T14:20:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/post-35823>

It's also a great source of B vitamins. This link has a good chart detailing all the nutrients: [http://www.whfoods.com/genpage.php?tname=foodspice&dbid=129](http://http://www.whfoods.com/genpage.php?tname=foodspice&dbid=129)

### Michael — 2012-07-06T14:29:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/post-35825>

> **vkiernan;35986 wrote:** Yep to everything Micheal said but also from what Dr. K. is saying about seafood being top of his food chain, you probably would be fine with sardines. Interesting thought.

That's an interesting thought.

Do you know Ramiel Nagel's book _Curing Tooth Decay_? He references what Weston Price found with isolated populations in that and says that all the groups that Price found that had "immunity" to tooth decay regularly ate food from one of three groups, seafood, organs from land animals, or raw dairy products; or in some cases people had foods from two of these groups. Obviously, dairy's problematic for many people, but, yeah, get the seafood and the organ meats in, or at least one of the two.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-06T16:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/post-35839>

> **jmathisen;35978 wrote:** Looking on nutritiondata.com, except for ridiculously large excess of the RDA for things like copper, beef liver doesn't seem like that extraordinary a meat, compared to all the other choices. Yet I see people pushing it like chiclets in some of the diet discussions.

>
>
>

>

it is one of two foods that have serious choline in it.......and choline is needed to export fat out of the liver to reverse metabolic syndrome......in case youve been living under a rock.......147 million USA folks have this problem.

### caroline — 2012-07-13T14:19:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whats-the-big-deal-about-liver.2230/post-37455>

> **Cavemam;37491 wrote:** I ordered some chicken livers from uswellness today. I really hope I can do this! Getting the liverwurst down is a challenge - I may have to guild this lilly a bit. . .

If you follow the recipe carefully - it will be really smooth. The taste is very mild \[because of all the butter\] and the cognac is lovely.

Make sure you go over the liver and remove any of the green stuff. He tells you exactly what to do. I can't wait to try it on a hamburger or I guess on steak too. Alot like putting a slab of blue cheese on a steak me thinks!! Can't wait to hear how you go. Cheers!

### BJK77 — 2012-07-06T17:43:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/t3-cytomel-making-ct-more-difficult.2238/post-35861>

I just started taking T3 and am only at 50 gr per day right now, but I'm noticing my CT is getting much more difficult to do. I assumed the opposite would happen, but I'm struggling to stay in my 50-degree tub for my usual 30 minutes and am having a really hard time warming up afterwards too.

I finally felt like I was getting used to CT and would warm up rather quickly, generally in about 30 minutes or so. Now that I'm taking the T3 it seems to be going backwards. Any ideas why?

I can't think of anything else I'm doing new that would have caused this. I've been increasing my DHEA/pregnenolone, but that's been over the course of several weeks and I didn't notice this until a few days after starting the T3.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-06T18:31:33+0300

I wonder if putting someone into a deep sleep in a controlled, frigid condition for 2 weeks might be a future treatment for many "diseases". In other words, a forced hibernation.

# Thread: Hungrier In the Summer?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NotAnother30 — 2012-07-06T20:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-35906>

So I've been noticing that I tend to get hungrier as the temperture warms up. Granted I live in Miami and it's hot all year round however I'm noticing the change. To my understanding I thought you would be hungrier in the winter instead of the summer. Could anyone out there shed some light? Thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-07T00:07:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-35958>

yep......its related to light cycle on hypothalamus......and T3 and apetite

### Lyndra — 2012-07-07T00:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-35963>

Interesting, I am now hungry in the morning before breakfast. Maybe it's the adrenal support & thyroid I'm taking.

### Mackay — 2012-07-07T00:56:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-35971>

The ancient Chinese physicians simply observed that SUmmertime is the time for expansion and Winter the time for Contraction.

### LinD — 2012-07-07T01:10:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-35975>

My husband commented this morning that he's been getting hungrier, too. Guess I've noticed it, as well, but careful to not overeat after losing 40# last year.

I told him it was likely due to circadian rhythms/time of year. I was right! He is also wearing the special glasses in the evening like me. Lately, we both forget to put them on at sundown, though, so we are not timely.

(I know.... That's bad.)

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-07-07T01:27:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-35982>

I'm in this boat, too. Seem to want more veggies and fruit than normal, too.

### BJK77 — 2012-07-07T04:00:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-36034>

I've been noticing this as well. Thought it might have been related to my increasing DHEA supplementation, but considering we're in a heatwave right now maybe it is more of a summer thing. I also find I'm wanting more fruits and veggies though I'm really trying to limit them.

### NotAnother30 — 2012-07-07T15:58:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-36077>

Thanks everyone, I really thought it was just me, whew! Being my first real primal summer, I suppose my body is just getting adjusted. I'm wondering if this has anything to do with it being mango season? With there being more high carb fruits and vegatables around, could the body just naturally crave more? Yesterdays was 3 eggs and sliced steak at 8am then at 11am when my stomach started growling, I grabbed the sweet potatoes from the night before and I wasn't hungry again until about 6pm. Wouldn't more carbs = more work? I would hate to relapse backwards.

### allen001 — 2012-07-11T07:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungrier-in-the-summer.2244/post-36760>

I Think in hot climate the body loses water, salty and nutrients via sweat, or urine this may be the reason for being more hungrier.

My husband is 78+ years old and had his blood drawn by the Doctor. He went to the doctor because he is having severe pain in his hand, arm, shoulders at night. He has not been able to sleep a full night through for about 2 weeks. His hand was red, swollen, fingers had a bluish tint to them. He has had no injury or problem before this started. The inflamation medicine the doctor gave him does not help. The doctor said the lab results were good and no infection. Just prescribed pain pills.

WBC 9.2

RBC 5.6

Hgb 15.90

Hct 49.6%

MCV 89.0

MCH 28.6

MCHC 32.1

RDW 13.4%

PLT 314.0

MPV 7.1fl

Lymph% 21.6%

Lymph# 1.9

Mono% 5.7

Mono# 0.5

Gran% 72.7

Gran# 6.8

Uric Acid 5.3 mg/dl

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-comment-on-lab-results.2247/post-35961>

### skg — 2012-07-07T00:45:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-comment-on-lab-results.2247/post-35965>

Thank you for you answer Dr K. We have been following your blog since Feb.

We take the supplements you recommend and eat total paleo and have been for over 2 1/2 yrs. We are also doing the 30 Fish challenge. We do not take any medications and his blood pressure has been averaging 120/70. He is 6ft tall and weighs 202. This was a new doctor for us as we do not have one. What test should we try to have taken?

### skg — 2012-07-07T00:55:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-comment-on-lab-results.2247/post-35970>

I just wanted to add this.

The pain he was having was so great that we made an appointment with a chiropractor who also does acupuncture.

The doctor did the acupuncture on his hand, fingers, arm and shoulders. He also did ultrasound therapy and neuro stimulator. He said the problem was his lymph fluid. After the treatment my husband felt better, but during the night again woke up in extreme pain. He went back to the chiro today and had the same treatments again, but also had a shot of magnesium chloride. He told my husband to take 250mg Magnesium Chloride daily. We would like your comments on what we should do or where to go next .

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-07T00:58:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-comment-on-lab-results.2247/post-35972>

This cant be done over the internet I am sorry to say......he needs to go to a walk in clinic or an ER to get another set of eyes on him.

### skg — 2012-07-07T01:13:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-comment-on-lab-results.2247/post-35977>

Ok, thank you very much Dr K. We are enjoying reading everything you have written. You are giving so much of your time and we appreciate it.

# Thread: Sunbathing ups mens testosterone
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunbathing-ups-mens-testosterone.2250/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-07T03:21:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunbathing-ups-mens-testosterone.2250/post-36018>

[http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/health/8493042.stm](http://http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/health/8493042.stm)

Sunbathing ups men's testosterone

> A spot of sunbathing may be just the thing to lift a man's libido, say experts after an Austrian study finds testosterone is boosted by vitamin D.
>
>
>
> The **bulk of this essential nutrient is made by the skin on exposure to sunlight. The rest comes from the diet.
>
>
>
> A study in Clinical Endocrinology journal of 2,299 men found those with enough of the vitamin had more of the male sex hormone than those with less.
>
>
>
> Blood levels of both dipped in the winter and peaked in the summer.
>
>
>
> Low testosterone levels can impact on a man's libido as well as zap energy levels.
>
>
>
> It also performs essential functions in both men and women such as maintaining muscle strength and bone density.**
>
>
> Across the 2,299 men studied, testosterone and vitamin D levels appeared to peak in the month of August, and drop off in the winter, hitting their lowest levels in March.
>
>
>
> Professor Winfried Marz and colleagues, who ran the study, said scientists should now look at whether vitamin D supplements would have the same effect on testosterone.
>
>
>
> Ad Brand of the Sunlight Research Forum in The Netherlands, a non-profit organisation set up to inform the general public on latest medical research on sun and health, said: "Men who ensure that their body is at least sufficiently supplied with vitamin D are doing good for their testosterone levels and their libido among other things."

### chocolate — 2012-07-07T03:26:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunbathing-ups-mens-testosterone.2250/post-36019>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21154195](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21154195)

> Effect of vitamin D supplementation on testosterone levels in men.
>
> Pilz S, Frisch S, Koertke H, Kuhn J, Dreier J, Obermayer-Pietsch B, Wehr E, Zittermann A.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Internal Medicine, Division of Endocrinology and Metabolism, Medical University of Graz, Austria.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The male reproductive tract has been identified as a target tissue for vitamin D, and previous data suggest an association of 25-hydroxyvitamin D \[25(OH)D\] with testosterone levels in men. We therefore aimed to evaluate whether vitamin D supplementation influences testosterone levels in men. Healthy overweight men undergoing a weight reduction program who participated in a randomized controlled trial were analyzed for testosterone levels. The entire study included 200 nondiabetic subjects, of whom 165 participants (54 men) completed the trial. Participants received either 83 μg (3,332 IU) vitamin D daily for 1 year (n = 31) or placebo (n =2 3). Initial 25(OH)D concentrations were in the deficiency range (< 50 nmol/l) and testosterone values were at the lower end of the reference range (9.09-55.28 nmol/l for males aged 20-49 years) in both groups. Mean circulating 25(OH)D concentrations increased significantly by 53.5 nmol/l in the vitamin D group, but remained almost constant in the placebo group. Compared to baseline values, a significant increase in total testosterone levels (from 10.7 Â± 3.9 nmol/l to 13.4 Â± 4.7 nmol/l; p < 0.001), bioactive testosterone (from 5.21 Â± 1.87 nmol/l to 6.25 Â± 2.01 nmol/l; p = 0.001), and free testosterone levels (from 0.222 Â± 0.080 nmol/l to 0.267 Â± 0.087 nmol/l; p = 0.001) were observed in the vitamin D supplemented group. By contrast, there was no significant change in any testosterone measure in the placebo g**roup. Our results suggest that vitamin D supplementation might increase testosterone levels. Further randomized controlled trials are warranted to confirm this hypothesis**.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-07T03:47:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sunbathing-ups-mens-testosterone.2250/post-36024>

### Huck — 2012-08-07T19:17:35+0300

After 4 weeks of sun. On sunny days, I tried to spend 1-2 hours in the sun, most days without a shirt. On some days, I just put the top down on the car and went for a long drive.

Results:

April 2012 . . . . . Aug 2012​

DHEA-S . . . . . . . . . . . . 34.5 . . . . . . . 32.6 (51.7-295)

Vitamin D . . . . . . . . . . . 68.6 . . . . . . . 111 (30-100)

Testosterone went up nicely and so did Vitamin D. I don't understand why free testosterone went down with total going up so much.

Seems to confirm that sun will raise total testosterone and vitamin d.

ETA: What makes the testosterone increase so interesting is I stopped taking pregnenolone, 7-keto, and dhea about 6 weeks prior to the 2nd lab, so without the sun, testosterone probably would have either stayed the same or gone down.

Now I have to try to figure out how to slow down conversion of testosterone to Estradiol. Any ideas?

DIM makes me extremely fatigued so that's out. I eat broccoli and cauliflower several times a week. I use coconut oil daily. I take several herbs that are aromatase inhibitors plus magnesium.

What else can I try? My progesterone is at bottom of range .2(.2-1.4). Would progesterone cream help?

# Thread: Hello from Vancouver Island
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-vancouver-island.2254/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### zulu-sky — 2012-07-07T09:11:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-vancouver-island.2254/post-36063>

Hi all,

I am new to this site but not new to LR. I tried the leptin reset for a bit in the spring when personal situations sent me back to basic paleo. I was doing fine until I tried some Greek yogurt.... which only led me to ice cream and a full blown sugar binge. Now that I have made myself really good and sick, I am attempting to claw my way back to health and go back to what worked in the beginning. It seems I am a sugar addict. Like an alcoholic must stay away from all alcohol or risk being addicted again, I need to stay off of anything remotely related to sugar or risk getting addicted again. It seems funny that one little bit of yogurt could have such power over my  I did find that I never achieved a lot of energy even when I was successful with weight loss. I have had trouble absorbing iron and am now perpetually on iron supplements, accompanied with a hypo - thyroid and peri- menopause I have a lot to consider. I have learned that I need to closely watch my sleep habits as it has a big effect on my emotions when I am sleep deprived. -- All that to say, I am back.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-07T17:19:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-vancouver-island.2254/post-36088>

Welcome and glad to have you again!!

### caroline — 2012-07-08T08:00:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-vancouver-island.2254/post-36186>

Welcome from a former Canadian!

# Thread: Teach me how to ask questions that don't lead to a dead end.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-07-07T18:23:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36100>

> **Answers are someoneâ€™s terminusâ€™s explanation of an existing mystery. Theories are someone elseâ€™s answer, and in my view answers usually lead to dead ends. In this series I am going to focus on questions for you to ponder. Questions open mindful inquiry and may suggest that your mind expands in ways it may never have done before.** I am going to take many known facts today and I am going to connect the dots for your own mind to explore.

I need to learn to ask better questions.

I'm running in circles.... every doc is giving me a different answer including Dr K. If I follow the trail of answers I get back to where I started and around and around I go. I know I need questions not answers b/c I'm caught in a dead end.

How do I find the correct questions? What are the questions I need to ask? I am convinced I am in this spot b/c I cannot find the good questions. There's a chance I have the answer yet I cannot reach it b/c I'm not asking the correct questions to find the path to the answer.

Please teach me how to ask questions that will encourage doctors to mindfully inquire.

### Huck — 2012-07-07T18:49:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36104>

What answer are you looking for?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-07T18:53:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36106>

I'm looking for questions.... questions that will lead my doc's to look for better questions of their own.

### Huck — 2012-07-07T19:04:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36109>

But you have already asked questions and gotten answers. So evidently you didn't like those answers, so what answer do you want?

(I'm just suggesting you try coming at the question from a different direction)

Instead of "What causes me to feel tired all the time" maybe "Doc, if I suddenly started having normal energy, what changes would you expect to see in my labs"?

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-07-07T19:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36116>

> **Souldanzer;36272 wrote:** I'm looking for questions.... questions that will lead my doc's to look for better questions of their own.

Based on your many comments, it seems your intuition is spot on. Frustration, if channeled correctly, often leads to breakthroughs. You seem to be ready for a breakthrough. ... However, my view is that you might be suffering from analysis paralysis. Will more "better" questions really provide new info?? My hunch is no. Thus, my suggestion would be ... you probably have the best solution for you in your mind. ... Its time to trust your intuition, and make a decision ... and use your impressive skills to make a plan ... and then make the plunge (of a decision) and take action.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-07T20:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36119>

Bingo on the last two......ideation without execution leads to deletion of the concept.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-07T20:43:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36122>

> **Huck;36275 wrote:** But you have already asked questions and gotten answers. So evidently you didn't like those answers, so what answer do you want?
>
>
>
> (I'm just suggesting you try coming at the question from a different direction)
>
>
>
> Instead of "What causes me to feel tired all the time" maybe "Doc, if I suddenly started having normal energy, what changes would you expect to see in my labs"?

This is good . . . . . going to try this myself!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-07T21:23:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36125>

You guys are right. I have a pile of messy answers. And I'm trying to sort through them.

It's also true that in my heart (no pun intended) I know how I possibly want to go about this.

The problem actually is the plan. I cannot come up with a plan b/c I'm in the control of other people's answers.... which I'm realizing now, is nobody's but my own plan which creates the basis of my problem. So I actually keep running in to the dead ends b/c other people operate under their answers and theories, while I'm looking to execute my own plan.... and they don't agree. But I need them for execution.

So I guess my question really is - how do I execute my plan if it doesn't operate under the answers/theories of the people who I need for this to be executed?

### skline@peak.org — 2012-07-07T21:44:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36126>

Hi, Soul Danzer

I am frequently stuck. I am growing more aware of the power of mistakes. Mistakes indicate that one is actually DOING something, instead of getting stuck in "ideation", as doc suggests.

So long as the mistakes aren't too costly, the more the better, because if one can freely make mistakes one gets answers from the real world instead of pallid abstractions from endless theorizing.

To find out where a path leads, go down it. One foot in front of the other. And I try to cultivate a constant awareness of whether the path is leading somewhere one might wish to be, or not. At the very least, trying this and then that makes one familiar with the immediate terrain, so one can wander around it more easily.

Maps vary according to who made them. While certain major landmarks may accurately appear on many maps ("COLD!"), the local paths and obstacles vary according to one's history and surroundings. I've followed too many "one size fits all" recommendations and landed flat on my face too many times. Even a slow learner will eventually figure out that's not the way to go.

### NickieG — 2012-07-07T22:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36130>

Souldanzer....could it be that you are attempting to listen to too many "experts" whose theoretical underpinnings are in conflict? You might choose one expert you intuitively trust and stick to that road.

I know for myself I initially was attempting to follow Dr Kruse ideas at the same time I was attempting to follow Jon Gabriel's work...they are different and I felt like I didn't know what to do cause I was getting myself stuck between to seemingly opposites. I choose Dr. K. and life has been less stressful.

Good luck to you.

Nickie

### youthfuliz — 2012-07-07T23:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36136>

Is it because you haven't made up your mind on what is the best plan to deal with your problem? In that case, spend more time to study the problem, understand the answers of the several experts you consult with - obviously some may be more conventional, some more cutting edge, some suggest more invasive methods, while others more conservative... after you understand where each of them come from, weigh the pros and cons, think about the risks you take and the potential benefits... then choose the path that you feel most confident about, and give it a try. It's your body, it has to be your decision. Then just tell the doctors you have thought through and picked your plan, ask them to help you accomplish what you want to accomplish. Maybe some of them won't be your friend... but if you prepare your reasoning well enough maybe they will. Or if you trust your own informed decision enough, find another doctor who will understand you and help you.

### Mackay — 2012-07-08T14:54:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36194>

Some problems can be solved in a flash of insight, other problems require constant tinkering and require action so you can make mistakes and correct them to move forward. Choose your path. Stick with it for a year and tinker as you go along. If you were my patient that's what I would council.

The journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.

or

It's impossible to steer a parked car.

or

If you waited for all the lights to turn green before you left your driveway, you'd still be there.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-08T20:28:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teach-me-how-to-ask-questions-that-dont-lead-to-a-dead-end.2261/post-36216>

**

Yes, there's conflict within the expert's opinions. Some of that conflict is coming from the source, me. I have told my story too often, I skip things, I get bored and annoyed, I don't want to recall it all. I've decided that I'm the only expert I'm going to listen to for the final decision though I do need my docs to help me execute my decision. I am resisting the fact that once again I'm the only one who can help myself and I have to figure it out all on my own. I'm a bit exhausted and somewhere I wish I would find that doc who I can just hand it all over to and figure it all out for me. Not going to happen.

youthfuliz;36302 wrote:** Is it because you haven't made up your mind on what is the best plan to deal with your problem? In that case, spend more time to study the problem, understand the answers of the several experts you consult with - obviously some may be more conventional, some more cutting edge, some suggest more invasive methods, while others more conservative... after you understand where each of them come from, weigh the pros and cons, think about the risks you take and the potential benefits... then choose the path that you feel most confident about, and give it a try. It's your body, it has to be your decision. Then just tell the doctors you have thought through and picked your plan, ask them to help you accomplish what you want to accomplish. Maybe some of them won't be your friend... but if you prepare your reasoning well enough maybe they will. Or if you trust your own informed decision enough, find another doctor who will understand you and help you.

I think this is where I'm at though a bit messy. I've decided on the doc I want to work with for now - my first cardiologist, the one who found the PFO. The drawback is that I can't hand it over - still got to watch out for me special needs b/c of my unique history.

> **Mackay;36360 wrote:** Some problems can be solved in a flash of insight, other problems require constant tinkering and require action so you can make mistakes and correct them to move forward. Choose your path. Stick with it for a year and tinker as you go along. If you were my patient that's what I would council.
>

> or
>
>
