# Thread: Flight or fight in the water....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Amazonmagic — 2012-07-10T18:35:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36571>

So I have been on vacation in the Redwood forest for about a week. During our vacation we camped, ate by the fire and bathed in the River. It was wonderful. During our vacation we decided to take a Klamath Jet boat River tour. We had a couple of hours before the tour began and decided to go for an ocean swim. We got to the beach and noticed there were no swimmers. We decided to go swimming in the estuary a few hundred yards away. An estuary is where the mouth of the river meets the ocean. I got in the water and stopped at my knees. Fear filled me and I couldn't understand why. I have been swimming in the Puget Sound since March, why on earth was I so filled with dread. I squatted down and got the water to my neck, swam up and down the shoreline but couldn't get in past my knees. After about 15 minutes, I just HAD to get out of the water. My boyfriend was baffled at my fear, but was ok with us leaving. Well I found out why on the tour. We got on the boat and the first place they took us was the very same estuary I had been swimming in. They proceeded to tell us that this estuary and beach had the highest concentration of GREAT WHITE SHARKS on the west coast and that they actually came into the estuary all the time to eat seals and salmon. OH MY GOD!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! Talk about that natural survival instinct kicking in!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-10T18:37:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36572>

Always listen to your gut. This is a great lesson that I'm sure will help you "listen" in the future. Thanks for sharing this as it can be a lesson to us all. Love it!

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-10T19:16:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36579>

Your gut really was keeping you safe wasn't it!

### janagram — 2012-07-10T19:22:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36583>

You are blessed! Oh how great that you have inner guidance.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-10T19:38:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36591>

That is quantum biology at work.........another levee in the Quilt.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-10T20:09:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36596>

My dog taught me this... she's way more sensitive than I am though I do pay attention to this. I have made a U-turn hiking alone in the mountains a number of times (not really safe or recommended to hike alone in the mountains we've got wildlife...). She will take the leash in her mouth and lead me away from whatever is hiding in the bushes. I tried to not listen once.... she threw a fit. Jumping up on me, yapping, tugging the leash (which wasn't attached to her but in my hands!!), laying down flat... she would not move any further into the direction we were heading. I eventually listened to her and we turned away from what was scaring her. She taught me to listen to this. Now I do, even when I'm without her.

We've all officially gone off the deep end  I'm glad I'm not alone down there

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-10T20:25:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36603>

What's crazy is in our modern world we've been taught to ignore these feelings as BS, instead we rely on digital readouts, spreadsheets, etc. those feelings are not BS. we've forgotten how to listen to our inner-selves, our gut, our intuition, our 6th sense. its there for a reason. Trust your body it knows what its doing.

when I was in labor w/ds about 14hrs in - I had a feeling something was wrong. my MW pushed me to keep going w/the home birth, I asked my birth doula to help me into the shower - and inbtwn contractions I told her point blank - something is wrong, and I need help, I wasn't tired - but intuitively I knew something was wrong. I asked her for help convincing my MW and DH that I needed to go to the hospital - they agreed after they checked my blood pressure - Can you say Danger zone. the boy was happy as a clam and was enjoying labor - my body decided it needed support. I listened to it. I'm 100% positive if I'd ignored it, I wouldn't be here today.

heed the inner-voice, intuition, gut - what ever you want to call it. Its key to our survival.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-11T00:39:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36658>

> **Jack Kruse;36757 wrote:** That is quantum biology at work.........another levee in the Quilt.

Sorta like Deja Vu? I once read that if you experience deja vu, and then make a real emotional reaction to it, you reinforce it. Like time travel.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-11T02:13:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36685>

> **Nonchalant;36824 wrote:** Sorta like Deja Vu? I once read that if you experience deja vu, and then make a real emotional reaction to it, you reinforce it. Like time travel.

You can time travel forward, but you can not time travel back.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-11T05:41:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36744>

> **Cavemam;36851 wrote:** You can time travel forward, but you can not time travel back.

It's not really time travel backward. More like both forward and back at the same time. Like Amazonmagic's emotional response to the tour guide's information was very strong. So her feeling of dread back when she was wading in the water was equally strong.

Ok, this is getting wierd. I'll hush now.

### Amazonmagic — 2012-07-11T16:56:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-36817>

Yeah ya lost me on that, but that's ok

### nonchalant — 2013-03-04T04:45:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-78871>

Jack's QB1 blog post reminded me of this thread.

### SeaHorse — 2013-03-04T14:59:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-78904>

Yes it does, doesn't it. Often our thoughts block the information our bodies are resonating with. Unfortunately, for many people their bodies no longer have the ability to register this kind of information. It's like we get gummed up and all our amazing powers of perception and awareness are dulled down or squashed flat. It's why you can't learn this stuff intellectually...your body has to be able to feel and sense the environment and it can only start to do that when all our cells are cleared or clearing and we are tapped into the sources of energy like the sun and the earth. I think this is when being in nature becomes such an intense source of joy....which seems to be showing up here more and more!

Our brains say "I want to be healthy, or I want to be able to sense energy" but if we stay on the outside of these ideas looking in, then we know we still have work to do. It's living these thoughts that make the difference and actually turn us into finely tuned beings.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-03-04T15:03:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-or-fight-in-the-water.2321/post-78905>

Do you listen to your stream of consciousness every morning? The healthier one gets the more you control the duration of your present........When you live as such, it should be possible to find out when perceiving stops and remembering begins. I have found in my health it has not been possible...but, I try for it elusively. So far in my life, a perception in fact, does not have an end......perceiving just seems to continue on......

I am 72yo, not a doctor.

Fatty acids and phospholipids are heavily correlated with inflammation.

Check that first.

Second,

do wholesale micronutrient evaluation and follow it up with optimization.

third

iodine/iodide

You may get old trying to find doctor who will write you scripts for this two tests.

Alternatively use your own $$.

Essential & Metabolic Fatty Acids Analysis (EMFA) by Genova Diagnostics

[http://www.healthremedies.com/essential\_metabolic\_fatty\_acids\_analysis\_emfa\_genova\_diagnostics.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/essential_metabolic_fatty_acids_analysis_emfa_genova_diagnostics.html)

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

\=====================

Do both tests 2x/year, follow up with corrections, when good, repeat 1x/year

Spectracell, make corrections to outright defficiencies and close calls.

(EMFA), post results here, I will help you, PM me when ready,

the test is good but the commentary requires adjustments per post #1 here:

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171942#post171942](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171942#post171942)

\=======================

Eat, sleep and behave per dr Kruse's advice.

Cliff notes on that on top of above referred post.

...

When you good with the above, return for

Lipids, Steroid & Thyroid Hormone Optimization.

//////////

Specifically triglycerides correlate with:

Trig +76--Niacinamide

Trig +76--vit C

Trig + ---indicate carbohydrate intolerance

Trig +59 chromium

Trig +59 EFA 4:2 oil

Trig +59 Fish Oil

Trig +59 L carnitine

Trig +59 Phosphatidylcholine

Trig +59 selenium

Pay real close attention to what is written in post #1 on fats.

no fish oil, but fish ok

no Krill oil, but krill ok

..

Cholesterol is used to make steroid hormones.

High cholesterol correlates with low Steroid hormones.

Specially DHEAs and Cortisol and also Vit D.

..

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-11T17:43:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-36824>

2.5% for me....darn. I think my husband is pure though....he has a sagittal crest on his skull.. He was a big hit in the anthropology classes in college because..

" It serves as a muscle attachment area for the temparalis muscles that extend up both sides of the head from the mandible (jaw). The presence of a sagittal crest indicates that there are exceptionally strong jaw muscles. Some Australopithecus afarensis and the robust australopithecines (Australopithecus robustus, boisei, and aethiopicus) had sagittal crests. Among the living primates, the most prominent sagittal crests are found on adult male gorillas. Humans do not have them. " The Encyclopedia of Science

So I guess I married a gorilla! What does that make our daughters????

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-12T02:16:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37014>

> **SeaHorse;37039 wrote: This article was posted awhile back on the Factor X thread....pretty mind blowing.
>
>
>
> [www.rdos.net/eng/asperger.htm](http://http://www.rdos.net/eng/asperger.htm)
>
>
> not through yet but wow
>
>
>
> Dog
>
>
>
> Cooperation with wolves were most likely a key to the ability for Neanderthals to survive in the cold Europe. Genetic research shows that dog and wolf parted 135,000 years ago. 77 They also reveal that the dogs ancestor is the European gray wolf. There are wolf remains close to Neanderthals, but not with the pray. This probably means the wolf had a special relation with humans. There are also big similarities between Neanderthal prey and wolves prey. 78 Some people incorrectly believe that dogs must change morph to be dogs and domestic. The main problem with this reasoning is that there is no evidence for a special dog phenotype older than 14,000 years, and analysis of American historical dogs claim they have an Eurasian origin. They must then have been brought with the settlers that crossed the Bering strait at least 15,000 years ago. It's also unlikely that the common dog psychological traits can have evolved in just a few thousands years. It's quite interesting that dogs are sensitive to human cues, 79 and that some humans (probably autistic mainly) are sensitive to dog's cues. This tells us that the original relation between dogs and humans was not as domesticator / domesticated, rather like a symbiotic relation.**

I have an interesting relationship with dogs to say the least.

>
>
> That site was enlightening, I agree.

I get this question A LOT from anybody who knows me out side of Real Estate - specially friends, I'm the LAST person one would expect to do such a thing. I attribute it to a fascination with the study of human behavior. . . In an aspie type focus on one topic to the Nth degree, I learned how/why people say and do what they do. I made a study of words vs action and interpreting and predicting behavior.

I am terrible in a "party" setting, but one on one - when I have some interest in common, I thrive.

I went into real estate because I love architecture/remodeling/houses etc - but I ended up a good real estate agent because I look at each client like a game to solve. I MUST FIGURE THEM OUT AND FIND THE HOUSE THEY WILL LOVE. Usually our interests bond us. I want to find them a house, they want to find one - win/win.

Interestingly enough I do not get along with other real estate people. I find it so frustrating all the extroversion. They always want to gather and socialize even when nobody has anything in common! I'm always getting scolded by my broker for not coming to X event or participating in X.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-12T05:00:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37068>

I s\*ck at the small talk. I'm terrible on the phone or in person as well for that matter. It's even worse having to do it in English now as I didn't grow up learning what to say. I have a few memorized scenarios and I pray I will pick the correct one when I need to meet/talk. I get it wrong all the time  I get stared at a lot. I cannot figure out what the right thing to say.

But I'm great once you know me and I can be me  If you don't mind me going on and on about whatever my interest happens to be at that moment in time  And keep it quiet please. Careful about touching me and don't mind me wearing my clothes inside out. The tags/seams drive me crazy.

I do have quite a sense of humor which apparently is uncharacteristic.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-12T15:53:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37141>

So here's a question.... I wonder what N's Briggs-Meyer's quotient lends towards.

While I appear to be an extrovert - I'm not. I'm not comfortable in crowds, social situations etc. I've learned how to do it, as a matter of survival in my job. but its not my choice. I'm one of those people that prefers to be in the background and make things happen. I'm very good at it. when I was in the military, I had this ability to fad into the background and get things done...but when necessary I'd go toe-to-toe w/the big brass, and I'd usually come out on top, but once it was over I'd fad back into the wall flower again. Its weird confrontation doesn't bother me. I think in a past life I was a warrior of some sort.

I'm highly detail oriented on things I'm interested in, and I have a laser like focus on those things... things I'm not interested in - meh don't get that same level of attention.

on the briggs meyer's quotient I'm an INFP. given that I shouldn't even be in the career field I've chosen, nor should I be good at it, but for some reason I excel in it. I should be an artist or writer - but I'm not.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-07-12T21:05:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37234>

what an interesting thread! i'm @ 3%, (95th percentile)

that pic looks my kindergarten pic, but my nose is smaller and turned up and I have a pointy chin. My hair: just as wild, even today LOL

I have big thick fingers, and very muscular, even before I lifted weights. A big head, hats never fit! Big muscular calves, well defined, even my SHINS. Who has muscular shins?!?! When I was in high school i vividly remember a friend walking behind me up a flight of stairs, and gasp "like, oh my god, teenie! You have these, like, MUSCLES in your legs. I mean, not like a dude, but almost." (yes, say it like a valley girl) I was MORTIFIED. MAN MUSCLES? In my legs? yeesh. There is a pic of me, I NEVER exercised, and I have traps that are visible from the front LOL I always fought my muscular body, I so badly wanted to be LITHE. AY yi yi.

But at the same time as muscular, extremely curvy. Even when I was FORTY lbs less than I am now, in high school, I had an hourglass figure. Cute boobs and a butt. Still do, just a bigger hourglass LOL

I am intro and extroverted. Painfully shy @ times, and then ridiculously confident at others. How is that possible? Hard to fit in. Always felt like the water pistol that squirted jelly...

Cavemam, he is adorable!!

And ditto on the tanning for me too. It's like I'm tanning in between/under my freckles.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-12T21:28:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37242>

OMG some one else has the big muscular calves! I've been accosted in the gym - when I used to have a membership - and the body builders kept coming up and asking me "Who did your calves" they did a great job - I'd love your doc's info. WTF????? I can't wear boots my calves are that freaking BIG... Snowboarding/skiing boots barely fit. and the shins... OMG when I run in anything but vff - I get horrible shin splints... it feels like my leg bones are getting pulled away from each other!

over all I'm very muscular. I don't have to lift heavy often to put on muscle. I swear I look at or think about lifting heavy things and I get more muscular!

I read the link about other characteristics, and he fits alot of them. It's very intriguing. I'm thinking I am not very neanderthal myself, but he definitely seems to fit the bill. I have already told him I want us both to do the 23andme tests, but now I REALLY want to!

### ealachan — 2012-07-12T23:34:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37283>

I'm a member of the huge-calf club too, and it's not just because they're fat! Even as a little kid, I had "tree-trunk legs", and have always been weirdly strong. Big feet (size 11 and WIDE), big hands (for a girl), short stubby fingers, and I've TOTALLY got an occipital bun on my skull. After reading all this and a bunch of other stuff about Neanderthals lately, I'd frankly be shocked if I had the test done and found that I DIDN'T have a high percentage of Neanderthal in me.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-12T23:44:04+0300

I wonder if anything in the 23&me (damn the results aren't in yet!) will point to why I'm more coordinated in water than on land... I seriously have some mermaid/merman stuff going on... when I float I move. USA Swimming & the USOC found through research that the most efficient and fastest swimmers move forward w/o any propulsion naturally. we did a test w/them at school - and there were only 3of us who did this - of course all three of us were swimming at the national level by the ages of 11 and 12. even to this day when I get in the water - if I keep my body position properly and don't move I still move down the pool

for me land coordination requires effort, there are days where I seriously walk into walls... its embarrassing - especially when I'm at the office for work.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-14T15:02:22+0300

>

I feel your pain. Without working out I have always had well developed calves since I hit puberty. Always had a problem with shin splints. They develop very quickly. I also have a body that responds well to light weight training, almost instantly.It likes weight training. Always been inordinately strong without working out until lately when my hormones disappeared which deleted my energy and my reserves. I was the little 9 year old girl who could beat up 4 bigger boys at once and win on the playground. And don't even think about challenging me in dodge ball or tether ball because I was dangerous back then. Too bad it seemed to be always speed that was emphasized most in gym programs. I wonder what life would have been like if I started weight lifting at a young age.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-14T16:01:26+0300

When I lifted in college - the strength coach and to have me work with the lacross/hockey/football team - b/c no one on the swim team could lift what I lifted (including the guys) I had a special weight training program! at my leanest in college I still weighed 160-165, but was a size 4! fast I was not. distance was my cup of tea - especially in the pool. heck I could out run the cadre on rifle runs along the powerlines when my shin splints didn't kill me. ### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-14T16:04:18+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-14T17:04:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37758>

Here is the key point. It is about mtDNA. This is why they went extinct. My thoughts are tied to leptin because it controls the human placenta. Although modern humans share some nuclear DNA with the extinct Neanderthals, the two species do not share any mitochondrial DNA, which in primates is always maternally transmitted. This observation has prompted the hypothesis that whereas female humans interbreeding with male Neanderthals were able to generate fertile offspring, the progeny of female Neanderthals who mated with male humans were either rare, absent or sterile (in line with Haldane's rule). If mtDNA is not present then you eventually die out.........I think interbreeding is what killed them and the fact that their DNA allowed for impulsivity......so they would have been quite unstable deep into life because of some interesting features unique to them.

### kathylu — 2012-07-14T17:28:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-37765>

> **Jack Kruse;37925 wrote:** Here is the key point. It is about mtDNA. This is why they went extinct. My thoughts are tied to leptin because it controls the human placenta. Although modern humans share some nuclear DNA with the extinct Neanderthals, the two species do not share any mitochondrial DNA, which in primates is always maternally transmitted. This observation has prompted the hypothesis that whereas female humans interbreeding with male Neanderthals were able to generate fertile offspring, the progeny of female Neanderthals who mated with male humans were either rare, absent or sterile (in line with Haldane's rule). If mtDNA is not present then you eventually die out.........I think interbreeding is what killed them and the fact that their DNA allowed for impulsivity......so they would have been quite unstable deep into life because of some interesting features unique to them.

There's also the Rh neg gene too. If a female neanderthal interbred with a male Cro Magnon, Rh incompatibility would almost ensure no more than one viable offspring. So the population would slowly decline.

The mtDNA theory is intriguing, but it's hard to believe that NO neanderthal females produced a viable offspring with a Cro Magnon. I wonder if anyone has analyzed mtDNA from Basques? From my understanding, they seem to be the population that carries the largest remnants of neanderthal DNA.

### kathylu — 2012-07-15T15:35:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-38026>

> **Jack Kruse;37970 wrote:** There is not.....because we have been looking and we have found none. This strongly suggests why they died off.

Jack, I'm going to have to maintain agnosticism on this topic especially since the Neanderthal genome was sequenced to only 1.3x coverage using degraded DNA, lots of potential for error and misinterpretation there. And the mtDNA sequencing was performed by using 31 bp PCR amplified fragments out of an estimated 16,000 bp total genome. Enough to identify those unique fragments, but not much else. Degradation is a real problem when sequencing ancient DNA.

I don't think they were that much different than modern humans. They diverged from humans 250,000 (lots of disagreement on that number) years ago while Sub-Saharan Africans and Eurasians diverged 150,000 years ago. If they existed still today they would just be considered another ethnic group, not another species. There would be no surprise if there was interbreeding between two ethnic groups.

Here are a few fun ideas:

[http://www.aoi.com.au/bcw/neanderbasque.htm](http://http://www.aoi.com.au/bcw/neanderbasque.htm)

Kathy, you seem to know a little so hopefully you can direct me in my curiosity - what are the gold standard methods today for dating things. . .I'm very sceptical on timing. . .last time I looked (15 yrs ago) I was completely put off by the +/- and couldn't trust any conclusions or theories using those times.

What's happening now?

### kathylu — 2012-07-16T16:42:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-38351>

> **Cavemam;38254 wrote:** Kathy, you seem to know a little so hopefully you can direct me in my curiosity - what are the gold standard methods today for dating things. . .I'm very sceptical on timing. . .last time I looked (15 yrs ago) I was completely put off by the +/- and couldn't trust any conclusions or theories using those times.
>
>
>
> What's happening now?

Cavemam, I'm not a paleontologist, just a lowly molecular biologist. However, from my understanding of the techniques involved, radioisotope dating is considered very reliable, quantifiable and repeatable. That's one of the major ways scientists come to an agreement on anything...can it be repeated with the same results by other labs? If so, then the confidence index rises. So, the age of rocks and any fossils within those rocks can be pretty reliably dated. Is it absolute truth? Of course not, we weren't there after all, but the dates are considered reliable.

Here's a nice link discussing procedures:

[http://www.actionbioscience.org/evolution/benton.html](http://http://www.actionbioscience.org/evolution/benton.html)

Molecular biology is an evolving science (pun intended). DNA sequencing has been standardized to a large degree, but the results often depend on the quality of the material being sequenced. Thus my earlier comment about the problems in sequencing ancient biological specimens. It used to be that many labs had a talented in lab sequencer and he or she was better at producing reliable results than anyone else. Machines now do much of the work, but as it is a biological procedure, there is an inherent lack of specificity at times, therefore lots of room for interpretation and conjecture.

All that being said, divergence of genes from one another is fairly easy to determine, as gene sequences tend to be highly conserved over time. Therefore determining even differences of a few base pairs can be significant. Scientists then use the data to construct DNA phylogenetic trees. They then try to correlate this information with traditional means of dating ancient materials. There are no absolute answers, just informed guesses. So, if the DNA being sequenced is 400 years old, I'd give the results more credence than a sequence from DNA that is 40,000 years old.

I hope that helps!

### chocolate — 2012-08-14T02:55:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-am-neanderthal-hear-me-roar.2338/post-44803>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/13/research.raises.doubts.about.whether.modern.humans.and.neanderthals.interbred?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/08/13/research.raises.doubts.about.whether.modern.humans.and.neanderthals.interbred?)

Research raises doubts about whether modern humans and Neanderthals interbredNew research raises questions about the theory that modern humans and Neanderthals at some point interbred, known as hybridisation. The findings of a study by researchers at the University of Cambridge suggest that common ancestry, not hybridisation, better explains the average 1-4 per cent DNA that those of European and Asian descent (Eurasians) share with Neanderthals. The study was published Aug. 13 in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

In the last two years, a number of studies have suggested that modern humans and Neanderthals had at some point interbred. Genetic evidence shows that on average Eurasians and Neanderthals share between 1-4 per cent of their DNA. In contrast, Africans have almost none of the Neanderthal genome. The previous studies concluded that these differences could be explained by hybridisation which occurred as modern humans exited Africa and bred with the Neanderthals who already inhabited Europe.

However, a new study funded by the BBSRC and the Leverhulme Trust has provided an alternative explanation for the genetic similarities. The scientists found that common ancestry, without any hybridisation, explains the genetic similarities between Neanderthals and modern humans. In other words, the DNA that Neanderthal and modern humans share can all be attributed to their common origin, without any recent influx of Neanderthal DNA into modern humans.

Dr Andrea Manica, from the University of Cambridge, who led the study said: "Our work shows clearly that the patterns currently seen in the Neanderthal genome are not exceptional, and are in line with our expectations of what we would see without hybridisation. So, if any hybridisation happened -- it's difficult to conclusively prove it never happened -- then it would have been minimal and much less than what people are claiming now."

Neanderthals and modern humans once shared a common ancestor who is thought to have spanned Africa and Europe about half a million years ago. Just as there are very different populations across Europe today, populations of that common ancestor would not have been completely mixed across continents, but rather closer populations would have been more genetically similar to each other than populations further apart. (There is extensive genetic and archaeological evidence that population in Africa were 'structured'; in other words, different populations in Africa only had limited exchange through migration, allowing them to remain distinct from each other both in terms of genetics and morphology.)

Then, about 350-300 thousand years ago, the European range and the African range became separated. The European range evolved into Neanderthal, the African range eventually turned into modern humans. However, because the populations within each continent were not freely mixing, the DNA of the modern human population in Africa that were ancestrally closer to Europe would have retained more of the ancestral DNA (specifically, genetic variants) that is also shared with Neanderthals.

On this basis, the scientists created a model to determine whether the differences in genetic similarities with Neanderthal among modern human populations, which had been attributed to hybridisation, could be down to the proximity of modern humans in northern Africa (who would have later gone on to populate Europe) to Neanderthals.

By examining the different genetic makeup among modern human populations, the scientists' model was able to infer how much genetic similarity there would have been between distinct populations within acontinent. The researchers then simulated a large number of populations representing Africa and Eurasia over the last half a million years, and estimated how much similarity would be expected between a random Neanderthal individual and modern humans in Africa and Eurasia.

The scientists concluded that when modern humans expanded out of Africa 60-70K years ago, they would have brought out that additional genetic similarity with them, making Europeans and Asians more similar to Neanderthals than Africans are on average -- undermining the theory that hybridization, and not common ancestry, explained these differences.

Dr Manica added: "Thus, based on common ancestry and geographic differences among populations within each continent, we would predict out of Africa populations to be more similar to Neanderthals than their African counterparts -- exactly the patterns that were observed when the Neanderthal genome was sequenced; but this pattern was attributed to hybridisation. Hopefully, everyone will become more cautious before invoking hybridisation, and start taking into account that ancient populations differed from each other probably as much as modern populations do."

Progesterone: ========================================

\================================================================

Fighting high estradiol is another battle.

Levels depend on high production and or low metabolizing.

Estradiol(E2) is produced with help of aromatase, more fat=more aromatase

To inhibit aromatase ---> Anastrozole, Arimidex is used

To destroy aromatase --->Femara

> to speed up metabolizing E2 ..........
>
>
>
> metabolism is inhibited by ..............
>
>
>
> look up bottom chart

In the mean time

Loose any excess weight (body fat)

Take one Arimidex pill EOD

and

eat lots of uncooked raw homemade sauerkraut, broccoli and other calciferous vegetables.

Get this bathroom scale, look at it every day.

Ozeri Touch 440 lb Digital Bathroom Scale -- Measures Weight, Body Fat, Hydration, Muscle and Bone Mass with Auto Recognition Technology for 8 Personal Profiles

[http://www.amazon.com/Ozeri-Bathroom-Hydration-Recognition-Technology/dp/B004NXUAXW/ref=sr\_1\_8?ie=UTF8&qid=1342020589&sr=8-8&keywords=bathroom+scale](http://http://www.amazon.com/Ozeri-Bathroom-Hydration-Recognition-Technology/dp/B004NXUAXW/ref=sr_1_8?ie=UTF8&qid=1342020589&sr=8-8&keywords=bathroom+scale)

I'm taking DIM and milk thistle to help clear the estrogen. Also upping my progesterone to help balance. I can tell that things have changed in the past few months since finding out I had too much estrogen. My DDs have shrunk down a little and are not a swollen and I just feel better all around.

Thanks everyone. And for all that info, JanSz! Your brain is working overtime for me... thank you.

I know I'm E dominant... I had breast cancer. I was trying to figure out what my starting P:E ratio is so I can trend it. From what Dr K has commented, it sounds like I can't use this as a baseline because my E will be higher than in day 2-3, so too high for the ratio calculation. I still can't figure out what this current ratio is, though. Can anyone?

Patty Cakes, I took milk thistle etc for months. (This was drawn recently, after 6 months + of Leptin Rx, CT, light cycle attention, etc. Can you imagine what it would have been before???) Had a consult with Dr K... it's time for the big guns now. It's going to be winter for several years for me now! Along with IV glutathione, and some other mega supplementation, until my ratio is normalised.

Vkiernan, I'm pretty sure I have adrenal issues that I'm also trying to sort (no testing to confirm, though.) It's messy!

Thanks again everyone. (And if anyone can tell me how to actually calculate _this_ ratio, I'd be grateful... or is it impossible?)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-12T02:37:10+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-12T03:08:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/p-e-ratio-labs-help-please.2344/post-37027>

> **Patty Cakes;37182 wrote:** I'm getting labs then start CTing again. Honestly for me the Pregnenolone and DHEA have been the best additions to my health regime. I have felt the most energy and excitement from taking those than anything.
>
>
>

I'm curiously watching your progress especially if you go with BHRT as a person who has had cancer. I've read almost all of Suzan Summers books and really enjoyed her story. Good luck!

Thanks, Patty Cakes! What form of DHEA do you take? I notice most of the docs in Suzanne Somer's books recommend only creams/lotions. I am meeting with an alternative doc who does BHRT and plan to do it. I'll keep you posted.

Basically, I fit that category Dr K talked about in the webinar about how you become an E collector (via a leaky gut). So I need to do two things majorly: fix the gut (I have been doing the Leaky Gut Rx, bone broth, supplements, etc but now it's major stuff with IV glutathione, lots of NAC etc) and clear the estrogen (so mega doses of bio-curcumin and Calc Glucarate) as well as stopping aromatisation (which means eating only CO for fat pretty much and in huge quantities... as well as putting it all over my body!). That plus dealing to my underactive thyroid and hormones (DHEA or 7 Keto as well as BHRT... all to be determined soon I hope). Of course, the rx is also for mega daily CT for the next few years (no summer for me). I'm struggling with the fact that NZ is a small country and very few docs know about any of this stuff ... there are only 2 functional meds in the country that I can find and one of the main sources of BHRT refused to do half the tests I wanted (after consulting with Dr K). So lots of trade offs... a clean, green country with grass fed animals, organic eggs and fresh seafood... but little knowledge about cutting edge anything. My oncologist thinks surgery is the only Rx a person needs, plus maybe a little radiation or chemo for 'bad' cases. Good grief.

Thanks for the well wishes! It means a lot. Sometimes it feels a bit lonely trying to walk up this steep hill. I keep reminding myself that the view gets clearer all the time (even if part of the clarity is seeing the s\*\*t you've been standing in!

### Croak — 2012-07-11T10:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-36767>

Posted this already in Glamorama's "Flat tummy" thread, but figured it's time to do my own thread.

EDIT: Figured I should mention that I did the Leptin RX, then Post-Reset RX, with a couple solid months of CT post-reset (45-55f hour soaks in the tub). Stopped CT in May when I went to the UK for business, plan on getting back to it now that I've returned to Vancouver for the rest of the summer.

> Hey, I'm back!
>
>
>
> I've been in the UK for weeks, but thought I'd post an update picture. Tough staying primal living in a hotel surrounded by restaurants, and frankly I didn't sweat it too much. Stayed away from carbs as much as possible, stayed with a big fat-heavy breakfast, but also had more than a few pints of lager (it's London, I had to be sociable!).
>
>
>
> Just to recap, here's how I started in November:
>
>
>

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-11T16:30:25+0300

Am I doing the math correctly - 85ish pounds in 7ish months??! So roughly 10+ pounds/month dropping off of you. You must be absolutely ecstatic!! It's also great that you took the time to do the "before" pictures - not very fun to do, in my experience. Has the weight dropped off steady state, or gradually slowed, or other?

### Melinda — 2012-07-11T17:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-36825>

You look so much younger in the face, amazing! I'm looking forward to the cooler temps of winter for the first time ever.

### Croak — 2012-07-11T19:00:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-36857>

I feel younger too! At 48, I weigh less now than I did in 1999, which I find cool as hell.

Little things keep adding up, like my chronic rotator cuff pain (both shoulders) that is now gone, the pitting edema in my shins that is nearly gone, waking up with the sun easily (when I'm not jet-lagged), and of course finding clothes I couldn't fit into for years are actually too large now. General energy levels, mood and outlook much improved, as well.

I also want to mention that I'm getting "lucky" again, which complements and/or is because of my increased (or restored) testosterone and libido levels. Not kidding a bit, women must be able to smell it or something...while I'm no High Karate(that dates me!) or Axe Body Spray poster-boy, I'm getting "chatted up" and beyond an almost embarrassing amount these days. Almost.

It was very nice to not get dirty looks (or looks of despair!) from the people sitting beside me on an airplane.

I was also quite happy to finally fit between FDR and Winnie on my last trip to London...that's a fairly narrow gap on that bench in reality but both butt cheeks are making contact. I'm illustrating to the two bronze gentlemen how wide my ass _used_ to be.

**EDIT**: As for the weight loss, there simply wasn't a lot for the first couple months on the Leptin Rx. But almost like a switch being flipped (well, it was, really), it's been steady since, half pound or so a day post-Reset, on 2k+ calories a day.

### MJ* — 2012-07-11T19:53:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-36881>

Croak awesome you look younger!!! LoL and it looks like in a different way just as the famous quote tells us, the harder you work the "luckier you get" ;-)

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-11T23:05:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-36946>

Croak - this is amazing!! You look so vibrant in the after too. So glad you shared - I appreciate it.

### David — 2012-07-12T00:53:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-36989>

Congratulations, Croak! This is really inspirational! Your story makes me see that using the good information we get on this board can truly help each and every one of us if only we can stick with it!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-12T01:47:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-37004>

Great to have you back! You look radiant in every way. As I was on the beach this past weekend (40 degree temps and ice cold water) trying to get the courage to walk into the water, my husband stood (in his jacket) on the sand and yelled encouragement in the form of "just 'Croak it' honey!!... By which he meant one inch at a time and I'd get there (I did). Just thought you'd like to know your inspiration is being touted (and shouted) on the beaches of New Zealand.

### Tizawee — 2012-07-12T02:05:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/croaks-return.2345/post-37010>

You really do look years younger. You carry 221 well. I can understand why you're getting hit on.  Thanks so much for the before and after. You look great!

### caroline — 2012-07-13T14:51:21+0300

# Thread: Histamine Help and more
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaHorse — 2012-07-11T15:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-36797>

I would like to start a thread for those of us who are dealing with histamine issues from food. Also I've been trying to get a handle on other food components like oxalates, salicylates and FODMAPs.

I guess it was a revelation that histamines in food could be such a problem. If you don't have enough diamine oxidase enzyme to break down histamine it causes all the reactions that seem like classic allergy responses. But you won't test positive on an allergy workup. [www.allergyuk.org](http://http://www.allergyuk.org) is a good place to start research. Then you can have too many mast cells in your body which are all too happy to spill histamine at the drop of a hat. Google "histadalia" and "mastocytosis" to read about the connections.

I had my eyes opened this year when I started the GAPS diet and went into a huge decline. Aside from detoxing I knew something else had to be going on....well fermented foods, leftovers (think broths that sit in the fridge) eggs. etc. etc. all of these foods have high histamine content. Then there are the foods that can trigger histamine release in your body: tomatoes, chocolate, shellfish, canned fish, chicken, tea, vinegar...the list goes on.

Part of my road to optimal is healing my leaky gut and getting a grip on the food intolerances...I really want to be able to eat a lot of shellfish!

What I've done so far:

I'm experimenting with taking the enzyme diamine oxidase which you can buy as Histame. Also, I cut out all histamine foods for 2 months and then started with the seafood challenge. I couldn't do the whole 30 days, but keeping the seafood to super fresh and no canned stuff helped a lot. Fish is easier on me than shellfish right now.

I think CT is great because cold always brought relief for my hives which is another histamine condition.(Not sure how the cold uticaria that the bad o6/o3 ratios brings on is related ) Face dunks have been great for puffy eyes and sneezing in the a.m. which always comes the day after ingesting something with histamine.

Soo....just wanted to start up a conversation with others who might have some ideas about getting past this particular hump in the road. I'm anticipating that as other areas become more optimal i.e. hormones....that this hyper immune response will settle down. Would welcome suggestions!

On to oxalates and FODMAPs later...

### Eve — 2012-07-11T18:05:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-36830>

I started GAPS last February. I had big problems with histamine for the whole year. I couldn't eat any leftovers or even broth that was freshly made. I would get a few hives, beating heart, hot flashes, etc. You probably already know this but according to GAPS as I understand it, histamine issues can be a result of an overabundance of "bad" bacteria in the gut that release histamine as a byproduct.

I don't think I noticed any improvement in my sx during that year because I didn't start taking probiotics until Sept 2011 and I only recently realized that I was having reactions to the probiotics all along as there were traces of gluten in it. It was not until Jan of this year to the present that I have gone through several jars of my homemade sauerkraut. I've gotten a lot of die-off -- at first, sometimes moments after eating it, desperately needing a nap -- but now I have no more histamine issues. It's very nice not to have to cut and freeze all my meat after cooking it! I can't believe I can have broth now too! My improvements might also be contributed to glutathione or Myer's iv's that I've been getting once a week since May.

Unfortunately my other food intolerances still remain...As in FODMAPS, Polyols cause me a lot of nausea, although fructose is slightly getting better (e.g., cabbage) but is still not tolerable. The next thing I am going to do is get my hormones tested because I think they are what is impeding my progress.

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-11T19:25:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-36868>

Eve, that's really helpful to get your story...I was trying to do probiotics too, but I stopped all supplements to try and figure things out. I was taking BioKult.

I know exactly what you mean about the instant reaction to sauerkraut. I love the stuff, but I would get a weird kind of sedative feeling, like you've just had a huge amount of alcohol mainlined into your bloodstream and then complete fatigue. Yuck.

I'll talk to my ND about the iv myers and glutathione...it would certainly help to bypass the stomach and I'll try my BioKult again. Going back to why our guts got leaky in the first place...I think the hormone balances are key.

Have you seen the chart on paleo-dietitian.com?--somebody posted it here awhile ago and it's a FODMAPs chart, but once you take out the foods with oxalate, histamine and salicylates there are hardly any veggies left. I'm experimenting a little these past few days and I think I'm better with some of the polyols. Fructans are still totally off limits. I'm adding lots of lettuce to my dinners just to get a bit of greenery during the summer.

Thanks again for posting!

### BJK77 — 2012-07-11T19:35:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-36872>

I didn't notice any histamine reactions until I started GAPS. I seemed to do ok with fermented foods, but the broth (even 3-hour broth) sent me over the edge - extreme itching, massive fatigue and muscle weakness to the point I could barely walk, headaches, etc. I had to cut out the broth completely and avoid canned fish, avocados, and other high histamine foods.

Recently I've been really struggling with a severe case of rosacea that I think is related to the histamine reactions although it also happens when I'm too warm. I find the cold baths to be so refreshing when this is going on as my face gets super red and hot and I literally feel like I'm on fire.

I had a consult with Dr. K just a few weeks ago and he thought a lot of this was related to my low DHEA levels, so I've been increasing my supplementation lately and I \*think\* I'm seeing some improvement. I don't want to jinx it, but when I do have reactions now they aren't quite as bad. I'm hoping the trend continues . . . but I know I definitely still have some trigger foods that I need to be careful with, mostly leftovers right now.

Have either of you had your DHEA levels tested?

### Eve — 2012-07-11T19:56:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-36884>

Glad I could help, Seahorse. I think I have seen the Paleo Fodmap chart...I know what you mean about nothing left to eat. I've been eating pretty much the same thing everyday since January. The only local/organic veggies that I can eat and are available right now are broccoli, zucchini, and bok choy (hard to find). I can eat lettuce but can't find a safe oil for dressing.

B, I am going to get my DHEA levels tested. I'm going to do the Salivary Hormone Panel III by zrtlabs but am going to wait til 21st day of my cycle to test as per the instructions just in case. So I won't get results until August. I don't really have problems with sleep unless I've reacted to a food that day...but my DHEA is probably still wonky based on my severe leaky gut.

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-11T20:06:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-36890>

Ditto on the DHEA testing....I had a test that was lost this month en route to the Neuroscience lab. So I'm doing another one this week.

That's encouraging about the rosacea and the DHEA!

I had so much bokchoy in May and June I don't even want to look at it now. I just skipped veggies altogether for awhile and now I'm shredding lettuce and putting lemon and a bit of olive oil on top. The way I see it, I'm staying at the meat/fat/as much fish as I can tolerate stage until my gut heals enough to proceed. Hoping that some lifestyle changes will also contribute to a hormone revival.

Its another chicken or egg problem. The cysteine is so important for chelating.

### thelowhistaminechef — 2012-07-13T01:56:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-37326>

Hi there, I've been following your forum for a while now...I just had to post. I was diagnosed with histaminosis (histamine intolerance) two years ago - I have many of the symptoms a lot of people here are suffering from. It might be worthwhile researching it...I have low diamine oxidase and high histamine and two allergies that drive my overall levels higher. Re a few people saying that removing foods from their diet helped in the short term but then it seemed to catch up with them - yes, it can take a few weeks for the histamine level to creep back up. The bucket analogy: your body requires histamine for proper central nervous system function and digestive processes - so there's always some histamine in the bucket. Add in an undiagnosed allergy, and it gets a bit fuller. Add in hay fever season and a little pet dander...then eat pretty much anything with a little bit of histamine and the bucket spills over.

I love this site! Lots of great info...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-13T02:30:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-37344>

I just have to say for completeness........do not assume a histamine reaction to food means elimination is in play.......it could be another issue. Just did a consult today that had this very issue. This is why education consults can be enlightening at times

### Martha Ray — 2019-08-14T03:20:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/histamine-help-and-more.2351/post-275929>

Hey, I just found this thread because I posted a version of this on a **_Histamine Intolerance Group_**
I belong to on _Facebook_....

_'Try getting more sunlight (sunrises and sunset daily too) and avoid as much artificial lighting (blue light like the LED and florescent lighting --- that has been helping me.... Teaching from Dr. Jack Kruse and learning from his OPTIMAL HEALTH FORUM. Goggle 'Histamine Jack Kruse' and related threads will come up that might help._

_Big histamine trigger for me is spinach so high-oxalate might overflow my 'histamine' bucket. I've lowered high oxalate foods and I avoid all spinach. I do pretty well except in the spring and fall when there are seasonal allergies a histamine factor for me._

_Hope this helps... Oh yes... one other help, metabolic counselor with **Metabolic Healing** put me onto a nasal spray called..._
_**'OMG Nasal Spray'**. The bottle says 'Addresses Nasal Toximation (Marcons maybe?)'. Product is by Dr. Fitt aka Roby Mitchell MD. I hate nose sprays and it was pretty expensive but I really trust the counselor (IMHO he's the best in the industry.) who also happens to personally have histamine issues so I purchased and love the spray... lasts a long time and gentle enough for me. The spray may well be a major help for me since I've been symptom free for same time I've been using. I don't have to use often maybe 3 times a month._
_I do hope this helps.... we have to learn to be our own advocates for our health. No one knows our own bodies as we do and no one cares as much as we and our fellow sufferers do.'_

**Imagine that... sunlight... helping any and everything!**

DOA related gene might be a factor for individuals... but metabolic counselor did not believe a genetic factor for me....

BTW, I too have issues with bone broth, coconut oil and fish if it is not super fresh... and meat leftovers if not eaten the next day. My worst symptoms are (were) flushing rashes, drainage and sore throats.... but like I said I've been greatly helped I believe by the nasal spray, not overdoing oxalates, being careful to eat very fresh food, local season food and getting sunlight.
Yeah, Sunlight what a gift. Thank you Dr. Jack Kruse for helping me see it.

# Thread: Pica?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pica.2365/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-07-11T22:54:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pica.2365/post-36941>

I know this is a weird question, not necessarily paleo/primal specific, but, there are lots of very smart people here, and I thought I'd get some insight.

I've heard that pica is caused by vitamin/mineral deficiencies...is this true? If so, which ones? Will a primal/paleo diet help? Is pica really a symptom of something else?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-12T00:07:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pica.2365/post-36977>

Causes

The scant research that has been done on the causes of pica suggests that the disorder is a specific appetite caused by mineral deficiency in many cases, such as iron deficiency, which sometimes is a result of celiac disease\[3\] or hookworm infection.\[9\] Often the substance eaten by someone with pica contains the mineral in which that individual is deficient.\[10\] More recently, cases of pica have been tied to the obsessive–compulsive spectrum, and there is a move to consider OCD in the etiology of pica.\[11\] However, pica is currently recognized as a mental disorder by the widely used Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-IV). Sensory, physiological, cultural and psychosocial perspectives have also been used by some to explain the causation of pica.

It has been proposed that mental-health conditions, such as obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) and schizophrenia, can sometimes cause pica.\[12\]

However, pica can also be a cultural practice not associated with a deficiency or disorder. Ingestion of kaolin (white dirt) among African-American women in the US state of Georgia shows the practice there to be a DSM-IV "culture-bound syndrome" and "not selectively associated with other psychopathology".\[13\] Similar kaolin ingestion is also widespread in parts of Africa.\[14\] Such practices may stem from health benefits such as the ability of clay to absorb plant toxins and protect against toxic alkaloids and tannic acids.\[15\]

From Wiki on pica.......

### blake@driftinglife.com — 2012-07-12T01:47:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pica.2365/post-37003>

When I first started dating my gf she would devour lemons and ice all day long. Sometimes 5 lemons a day and we would go through a ten pound bag of ice every couple of days. Turns out she was iron deficient and anemic. After her first iron treatment the craving for lemon and ice stopped and hasn't come back. I know people with pica can crave all sorts of things like dirt and other harmful substances, so ice and lemons aren't so bad, but at the rate she consumed ice she would probably not have teeth right now.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-12T06:19:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-issue-fluoride-related.2373/post-37093>

Simply: Fluoride turns to fluorine (can't remember the chemical reaction) which the thyroid uptakes and there is no room left for iodine, a mandatory mineral for thyroid function. Eliminate fluoride and I bet your thyroid will improve a little. On your next labs try to get a FT3. This is the bio-available thyroid hormone. A FT4 would be good too!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-14T04:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-issue-fluoride-related.2373/post-37678>

From Fluoride Alert website (recommended by Russell Blaylock and others, so I'm guessing rally good info):

**How do I avoid fluoride from my tap water?

**WATER FILTERS: One way to avoid fluoride from the tap is to purchase a water filter. However, most water filters will not effectively remove fluoride. For instance, Brita filters will NOT remove fluoride. The two types of filters which will reliably remove more than 90% of the fluoride are [reverse-osmosis](http://http://parentcenter.babycenter.com/refcap/preschooler/pnutrition/64401.html)** filters and [activated alumina](http://http://fluoride.squarespace.com/display/ShowPost?moduleId=707400&discussionId=13836&postId=147373)** filters. Thus, if you are looking for a filter to remove fluoride, make sure it uses "reverse-osmosis" or "activated alumina" technology.

# Thread: Muscle repair?  Pain relief
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-12T21:22:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37238>

I jinxed things by bragging the other day on how good I feel.

Meanwhile, doing my gardening marathon I did some kind of damage to an upper leg/hip. It almost feels like bone pain. Super painful like a backache.

It's causing me to lose sleep, I'm sure I'm inflamed and the stress is probably not helping the old cortisol. I actually had to cancel clients today because I can't get out of the car without wanting to scream and I'm in slow-motion.

I made the mistake of getting on scale this a.m., no change from the last time (5 days ago). I also got TOM so I shall chalk it up to all of the above.

BUT - my point other than oversharing is: Suggestions for pain relief? I usually never ever take anything - but this is BAD and I think getting sleep may be more important.

In addition to what I could send my husband to get at the drug store, I also have some vicodin and some other narcotic I've been saving for years. Warnings? Suggestions? Would a Vicodin interfere with the HCG?

### Lyndra — 2012-07-12T23:22:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37274>

Ouch! Sorry to hear about the injury. I'd be careful of using old medication - it can be either more or less effective than it originally was. Check the expiration date.

I know a great Rolfer if you want to try to go that route (he's great at diagnosing injuries and using manual means to help).

And here's the obvious answer - try icing it. Definitely no heat.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-13T03:30:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37379>

>
>
>
> I know a great Rolfer if you want to try to go that route (he's great at diagnosing injuries and using manual means to help).
>
>
>
> And here's the obvious answer - try icing it. Definitely no heat.

For the record, 6 year old vicodin still works. Aaaaaaaaaaaahhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh!

### colleencoble — 2012-07-13T23:00:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37600>

Oh good! LOL Sorry I didn't see this. Got very bad news yesterday about my best friend's ovarian cancer being back and I've been a puddle on the floor and researching like crazy. Hope you feel better. None of the pain meds interfere with the hcg but you could gain from inflammation so don't panic if you do.

### Tizawee — 2012-07-13T23:05:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37604>

My first reaction was that you might have pinched a nerve while gardening. The fact that the Vicodin works tells me it's not nerve pain. Icing is best, as much as you can handle/have time for. Don't push it too hard while waiting for it to heal or you'll make matters worse.

Hope this helps.

Elin

### kathylu — 2012-07-14T17:15:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37761>

Although you shouldn't walk if the pain is severe, remember that inactivity increases time for recovery. So gentle movement and stretching if you can stand it. Go for physical therapy too...it really does help. Ask the therapist to request a TENS unit rental for you too. TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation) stops pain quite well. Stops local nerves from transmitting pain signals by altering pain "gates". If you use the "pulse" or acupuncture setting for an hour or two, the pain relief can last for hours. Increases endorphins in the brain.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-14T22:41:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37834>

> **Tizawee;37771 wrote:** My first reaction was that you might have pinched a nerve while gardening. The fact that the Vicodin works tells me it's not nerve pain. Icing is best, as much as you can handle/have time for. Don't push it too hard while waiting for it to heal or you'll make matters worse.
>
>
>
> Hope this helps.
>
> Elin

Ooooh, what about the vicodin tells you it's not pinched nerve?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-14T22:42:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37835>

> **kathylu;37928 wrote:** Although you shouldn't walk if the pain is severe, remember that inactivity increases time for recovery. So gentle movement and stretching if you can stand it. Go for physical therapy too...it really does help. Ask the therapist to request a TENS unit rental for you too. TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation) stops pain quite well. Stops local nerves from transmitting pain signals by altering pain "gates". If you use the "pulse" or acupuncture setting for an hour or two, the pain relief can last for hours. Increases endorphins in the brain.

Would a chiropractor count as physical therapy? What do you think of Acupuncture?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-14T22:50:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-37837>

Update! Did Chiro stuff with X-ray in the a.m., he did an adjustment then put on some massage table that rolled across my back/legs. It was nice and relaxing. But - didn't do much to alleviate the problems. Also, all the moving around seeing doctors really made me hurt! I got woozy and almost passed out at the Chiro office.

I went home and laid down quietly to relax, then decided I wanted to see a regular doctor. He looked at the xray too - he said it sounded like two things going on:

1\. bursitis on the side of my leg/hip

2\. A sciatic thing.

I left with a fresh script for vicodin and decided I would take the advil as he suggested (I usually try to avoid medications). I relaxed, got a grip, and cancelled my weekend clients. . . I think that was causing me a tremendous amount of stress - in real estate, there are no days off. Missing an appointment always feels like I just threw away potential paychecks. I had to let that go, then after that and laying quietly I got a grip and decided it was stupid not to ice it.

I'm moving around as much as I can - but I'm still at the point of having to stand or laydown, no sitting. I did have paperwork/follow up stuff to do so I worked for a while today.

I feel much better after regular doc looked at it and didn't see anything permanent wrong.

I think being here with all us sick folks and constantly deep into health is making me paranoid and hypochondriacky.

### kathylu — 2012-07-15T15:06:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-38024>

> **Cavemam;38003 wrote:** Would a chiropractor count as physical therapy? What do you think of Acupuncture?

It depends on the chiropractor. Some of them use PT modalities, some don't. I think soft tissue work (heat, cold, massage, electrical stim) is very helpful for an acute problem. It reduces inflammation and encourages tissue healing. Chiro can be great for a longer term problem, especially if it's functional. Joint manipulation can be great then.

Acupuncture can also provide relief, but a lot depends on the practitioner (and it's expensive). Wouldn't be my first choice. Really, get a TENS. Great non-drug pain relief.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-15T23:06:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-pain-relief.2383/post-38119>

I think soft tissue work (heat, cold, massage, electrical stim) is very helpful for an acute problem. It reduces inflammation and encourages tissue healing. Chiro can be great for a longer term problem, especially if it's functional. Joint manipulation can be great then.
>

Thanks for taking the time to explain. I'll be asking about this!

Hello all. I finally scraped enough money up to get some labs done. I am a 30 year old male, normal weight. I have been feeling awful for the past 2 years.

Symptoms-Can't sleep more than 4 or so hours a night, always cold, absolutely no energy or sex drive at all, bad cystic acne, depression, horrible brain fog, very forgetful, no motivation to do anything, etc etc etc

Here are my labs. My Dr's around here always want to just give some depression medication when i give them my symptoms. You know when something is wrong with you and I know I have a lot more going on then just depression. Any help anyone can give me would be greatly appreciated. I plan on getting an online consult with Jack here in a few weeks as soon as I can find a way to come up with the money. In the meantime, I would like to know at least a general direction as to what might be wrong with me and why I'm feeling so awful. Thank you so much to anyone who takes the time to read this and can assist me in any possible way.

My Results "Optimal" Range

Estradiol **0.9 pg/ml 0.5-2.2

Testosterone **73 pg/ml 72-148

DHEAS **15.2 ng/ml 7-23

Cortisol-morning(5 AM) **6.4 ng/ml 3.7-9.5

Cortisol-noon **3.1 ng/ml 1.2-3.0

cortisol-evening **0.8ng/ml 0.6-1.9

cortisol-night 0.9 ng/ml** 0.4-1.0

PSA 3.1 ng/ml** 0.5-4.0

Free T4 1.3 ng/dl** 0.7-2.5

Free T3 2.5 pg/ml** 2.5-6.5

TSH 3.5 uU/ml** 0.5-3.0

TPO 25 IU/ml** 0-150

Consider managing with out any anti-depression medicines.

Instead, think of

food you eat (you must be a meat eater and able to consume large amounts of fat)(70%fat + 30% protein + incidental carbohydrates) measured by caloric content . At least half of protein from shelfish & fish rest animal protein, eggs, no bird meat)

Circadian Rhythm (day/night, light/dark)

Lipid, steroid, thyroid Hormone Optimization requires frequent vein blood testing, you must be able to arrange for that.

\-----------------------------------

Where this tests were done, give a lab name.

Preferably, scan each (whole) page, attach to your post.

What was tested, blood from vein, blood spot, saliva, urine, something else.

You have about everything too low, except

TSH, PSA, and thyroid antibodies.

\--------------------------------------------------------------------

If you would ever supplement with testosterone,

within a month double check your PSA, if it start going up, stop testosterone, get prostate biopsy.

Before you would start using any supplemental testosterone

you must know status of your RBC, Hct & Hgb. Actually hemoglobin Hbg would suffice.

Some people have high Hgb either before or right after starting testosterone.

Usually they are able to continue testosterone therapy, but in that case, even closer monitoring is required and phlebotomies are employed to lower raising Hbg.

\--------------------------------------------------------------------

Stop eating all grains. That may help in lowering your thyroid antibodies.

Get this test (below),

and stop eating anything that you may discover as alergic.

that may help in lowering your thyroid and other antibodies.

> Get IgE and IgG4 alergy tests,
>
> E-95 Food Allergy Panel (Basic)
>
> A-95 Food Allergy Panel(Extended)
>
> Total 190 foods checked for IgE and IgG4 alergy tests
>
> [http://meridianvalleylab.com/food-allergy-testing/basic-foods-e-panel/](http://http://meridianvalleylab.com/food-allergy-testing/basic-foods-e-panel/)
>
> The E-95 Food Allergy Panel and A-95 Food Allergy Panel can be combined
>
> for a lower cost to you to provide more comprehensive food allergy detection.
>
>
>
> Meridian Valley Laboratory uses a state-of-the-art enzyme-linked
>
> immunosorbent assay technique (ELISA) to measure
>
> serum IgG4 and IGE antibodies for food allergy testing.
>
>
>
> The E95 panel is the basic food allergy test panel and consists of 95 commonly eaten foods. (Combined IgG4 and IgE, by ELISA Method)
>
> The A-95 panel does not overlap with the E-95 panel, and consists of another 95 less commonly eaten foods and seasonings.

Read and follow what is in my post #1 (few times) and read also what is under links there:

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171942#post171942](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171942#post171942)

///

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-13T05:05:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.2395/post-37401>

Are you already going strict with circadian functions? Antidepressents are difficult and time consuming to recover from. I think a huge step in mood anyway will be to read and re-read about following a daily circadian cycle. As soon as the sun comes up, get out and see it. Don't wear sunglasses. As soon as the sun goes down - off with the lights.

If you start doing CT, then you will be able to go to sleep when sun goes down.

If you do that in addition to the high fat diet as Janzs lays out, your brain and mood will really start to function in such a fashion that you can figure this out and then start working on other problems.

Also, make sure to WALK during the day. At least an hour.

You must be off all grains.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-13T07:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.2395/post-37430>

If you want to do antidepressants, I'd follow Dan Kalish's advice and treat them naturally (he has a podcast where he covers this with Sean Croxton).

You don't say what your diet is and/or if you're doing CT and the leptin RX, but I'd be following JanSz advice re: diet and also Cavemam's re: light. Your hormones, including your melatonin and cortisol, look suboptimal which will contribute to poor sleep and depression. Once you go strict with the lights it gets pretty easy and you'll find you relax in the evening and slowly ease into tiredness and then good sleep. Make sure you have all lights blacked out in your bedroom, including electronic lights from clocks/iPods, etc.

I totally relate to the feelings of no energy, brain fog, lack of motivation etc. But I feel totally different since following Dr K's protocols.

Also, you mention acne so I'd be focussed on gut healing, too. Marry the Leaky Gut Rx with the Leptin Rx and CT, follow the natural light cycles, and you'll be away laughing (as we say in New Zealand).

### bennyha — 2012-07-13T19:20:59+0300

Thank you for all your replies. I really appreciate it. I just got done talking to a Dr. and he said he wants to put me on Dr. Wilson's adrenal rebuilder, which is something similar to what I took in the past just about 3 times more expensive. I don't think I want to waste my money with that. He also said he would be open to having me take a small dose of T3 only from a compounding pharmacy and also start a low testosterone cream replacement. Regarding my prostate. I have to get up and pee usually 2 or 3 times a night. I have been like this all my life. And when I drink any liquid I usually have to urinate within half an hour. Does this mean, along with my PSA score, that I probably have an underlying prostate problem?

Regarding diet. I have been eating strict paleo since January. Tons of fish as well. No grains or dairy (outside of ghee) whatsoever. I basically carb cycle, eating more carbs (in the form of sweet potatoes) 3 days a week on the days that I weight train, and then basically 0 carb (only carbs coming from avocados and green veggies-usually about 1 lbs of steamed broccoli). Does this look ok?

Thanks for the advice and assistance all of you have given. I appreciate it so much. Their just isn't good medical advice in the area I'm from. Everyone seems so behind on everything. Any more advice/recommendations I'm completely open to

### bennyha — 2012-07-13T19:36:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.2395/post-37526>

I did the leptin RX and am actually still doing it. I've never been overweight, and have always been extremely fit with single digit bodyfat. But a few years ago I became bulimic. I would eat literally 15,000 calories in a few hours, then wouldn't eat for 3 days out of guilt. Then I followed this viscious cycle every single week for over 2 years. All I could think about was food. And I was never, ever full no matter how much I ate. I would literally throw up from eating too much, then be eating again within 10 minutes because my brain craved food. I was addicted in the worst way. I tried everything under the sun to try and stop this. Nothing worked at all. And I was too ashamed to tell anyone because I looked great on the outside and had single digit bodyfat. So I would eat in isolation. We are talking pounds of meat, potatoes, gallons of ice creams, packages of cookies, multiple pounds of frozen veggies-all in ONE SETTING. Seems unreal thinking back on it. It was the worst 2 year period of my entire life, and I've been through some serious \*\*\*\* in my life. I stumbled across Dr. Kruse's website by the grace of God one day. I have never been very religious, but one day during Christmas break after an epic binge I had had enough. I prayed to God to help me because I couldn't keep living in this manner. It was so draining in so many ways. I read his eating disorder blog, knowing that I had bulimia, and all his blogs on leptin. I pieced together that I had completely trashed my leptin. So I started the Leptin RX, following it to a T, and only had one slip up (way back in March) in that time. Since March, I have not binged or really had the urge to even once, and have regained a normal hunger. I actually can get full again. And I'm not dreaming of my next meal all day long. I simply eat to fuel my body, then I stop. His Leptin RX is truly a miracle in helping with food addictions. I had tried EVERYTHING under the sun, and nothing worked. Then I tried this, and it was the magic potion I had prayed for. I can't thank Dr. Kruse enough for giving me my life back. Now I'm trying to help repair all the damages I had done to my body in the 2 year period ending in 2011.

Sorry for the long post. Just thought I should let everyone know how serious my problem was and how the Leptin RX gave me my life back

### bennyha — 2012-07-13T19:45:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-me-with-my-labs.2395/post-37531>

> **Cavemam;37568 wrote:** Are you already going strict with circadian functions? Antidepressents are difficult and time consuming to recover from. I think a huge step in mood anyway will be to read and re-read about following a daily circadian cycle. As soon as the sun comes up, get out and see it. Don't wear sunglasses. As soon as the sun goes down - off with the lights.
