### Jennie747 — 2012-09-07T12:38:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49945>

Is artificial light in the morning before sunrise as bad for you as it is at night? I tend to be on the computer early in the morning but I do have f-lux on it.

### ealachan — 2012-09-07T15:22:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49964>

> **Jennie747;50490 wrote:** Is artificial light in the morning before sunrise as bad for you as it is at night? I tend to be on the computer early in the morning but I do have f-lux on it.

I asked a similar question a while ago in Ask Jack and was told that, yes, it's still pretty bad for you. I can't get around having to get up before sunrise some days, so I have taken to putting my blueblockers on as soon as I get up those days, and trying to keep the inside lights to a minimum until dawn, at which point I ditch the blueblockers and go outside to get my light exposure. I don't think that's likely optimal, but it's my current work-around.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-07T15:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-49971>

> **Jennie747;50490 wrote:** Is artificial light in the morning before sunrise as bad for you as it is at night? I tend to be on the computer early in the morning but I do have f-lux on it.

I would say that all artificial lightning is bad in some kind of way. Back in the days i worked with electronic components, the industry used to be worried about the impacts it would have on humans. Today we switch all lightning products to LED or LCD and that should worry us BIG TIME.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-07T17:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-50015>

If I remember correctly, many aquatic food species come to the shallows or shore near the time of the full moon. Maybe there is some built in circadian allowance for the extra light from a full moon at this time. Hunter gatherers probably took advantage of this extra food source. So I have decided its okay to stare at a laptop when there is a full moon if you are a werewolf, but only while standing ankle deep in the surf.

Makes me wonder if this going crazy during the full moon stuff came about because of the hurried activities of the hunter gatherers tripping over each other to get to the shallows to grab easy pickin's. Full moon = Aquatic Ape Wal Mart?

### nonchalant — 2012-09-08T00:02:14+0300

> **ealachan;50509 wrote:** I asked a similar question a while ago in Ask Jack and was told that, yes, it's still pretty bad for you. I can't get around having to get up before sunrise some days, so I have taken to putting my blueblockers on as soon as I get up those days, and trying to keep the inside lights to a minimum until dawn, at which point I ditch the blueblockers and go outside to get my light exposure. I don't think that's likely optimal, but it's my current work-around.

Ealachan, ditto for me.

I've replaced some lights with yellow bug lights, and those are the only lights I turn on before sunrise. Even the refrigerator light is a bug light. And I have my blue-blockers on as well until I see that the sky outside is getting light. Then I'll take them off and confirm that I can see blue sky. If yes, the goggles come off.

### steve@wity.net — 2012-09-08T10:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-50211>

> **Glen PDQ;50325 wrote:** I'm not sure where this should really be posted. Been eating paleo for the first 3.5 months and epi-paleo for about 1.5 months including seafood and have lost 35-40 pounds without counting calories. I've been putting on the over-the-glasses blu blockers every evening at sundown and have been getting into bed using the sleep mask more or less about 8-9pm the last few evenings. Yesterday morning I woke up after sleeping for about 7 hours when it seemed a flashbulb went off twice with my eyes shut, 1 second apart. This morning I woke up after sleeping for 8.5 hours when the flash bulb went off once with my eyes closed. This is not external; it is an internal event. Could my brain be activating a new way to use my circadian alarm clock? Jack says he purposefully does not tell us everything we will notice on this path. Is this one of those? Anybody else experience something similar?

Glen

I had this occur last week as well - just the once so far. Happened in the middle of the nght with me. I can only describe it as a "firework" going off in my brain.

I know (because I have tested) that I am seriously short on omega 3's and I'm viewing this as a good sign that the various essential fatty acids are finally getting into the brain. 75% of meals have been fish based for about 1.5 months.

### Destiny — 2012-09-08T13:57:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-50226>

6:55 AM EST - on Fox and Friends - Dr. Alan Greene, a pediatrician - parents are giving melatonin to kids to get them to sleep!

He talked about the importance of avoiding blue light in the evening and using blue blockers!

### Destiny — 2012-09-08T14:20:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-50227>

Interesting article at: [http://www.webrn-maculardegeneration.com/blue-blockers-6.html](http://http://www.webrn-maculardegeneration.com/blue-blockers-6.html)

From the article:

How and where are these glasses used or worn? Are there different lenses for different uses?

In addition to the blue light we are exposed to outdoors (the sky is blue) we have a growing indoor blue light hazard. Compact Fluorescent Light bulbs (CFL) are replacing incandescent bulbs to save energy and our television, computer screens and digital devices are backlit by fluorescent and LED bulbs that have a high blue spectral emission. So the answer is when you are indoors, wear the indoor lenses and when outdoors wear the outdoor sun lens. Doing so will provide critical protection against blue light as well as significantly enhancing contrast providing a visually soothing effect.

Who would benefit from wearing melanin/OLP glasses?

Everyone!

If someone is interested in getting these new lenses how would they go about getting them for those with and without prescription eye wear?

The easiest way is to go to [www.BluTechLenses.com](http://http://www.BluTechLenses.com) and send an email to [info@blutechlens.com](mailto:info@blutechlens.com) Indicate your name, contact information and current eye doctor if applicable. Eye Solutions will make it easy to obtain both the prescription and non-prescription Blu-Tech lenses for indoor and outdoor use.

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-19T15:28:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-52669>

> **Shijin13;50716 wrote:** I just went in and asked if they could help me. I told them at the end of the day I was getting headaches for the fluorescent lights and they says sure. Blue blocker lenses w/antiglare. The quoted me a price and I jumped on it. Eff them if they look at you weird they don't know Jack (pun intended)

I have decided that I need prescription blue blockers. I have many night meetings and sometimes I have to drive at night. Last spring I bought a pair of over-glasses from Low Blue Lights. they are awkward and don't fit, and they look terrible. So I've gotten a regular pair of blue blockers so I take off my glasses, put them on, and can't see.

So yesterday I went to Lenscrafters. I also saw a post where Dr. K said that he got a pair of Oakleys. So I decided that was the way to go, but the lenses I was offered, I don't think will work (but I ordered them-- can change them in the next couple of days).

If anyone knows Oakley, I ordered the VR28, a reddish one. Second choice is gold iridium, but neither of them did what my blue blockers did. Going on their website, they do have one called 'persimmon' which looks orange. I could probably beg Lenscrafters to see if these could be ordered.

Any other suggestions? I may have ordered an expensive pair of sunglasses that won't work for what I need. My night environments usually aren't fluorescent, but lots of regular light. Plus many times I need my computer during these meetings. I do use f.lux, but as we know that ain't optimal.

I appreciate any help on this!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-19T23:13:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-52771>

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-19T23:23:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-52775>

Find a local eye glasses shop w/an opthomologist as part/parcel of the store -

describe exactly what you want to do... you may have to spell it out Block blue light from fluorescent bulbs indoors - you're not looking for sunglasses (you'll have to tell them this several times) - ask them if they have any products that do that... they may have to pick up the phone and call their lab who does the actual lenses to figure out what its, how much it costs etc... the ppl behind the reception desk probably don't know anything and will automatically say no. well, can you check w/the lab that makes your specialty lenses?

In case you haven't noticed I am customer services' TPITA I am the customer they don't want to talk too... its why I have my own account manager for my cell service. yeah. I 'm THAT CUSTOMER. I ALWAYS GET WHAT I WANT.

ETA: I think that's way UHC gave me my dx codes at the beginning of the year.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-20T01:34:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-52814>

> **Cavemam;53400 wrote:** I've had trouble finding this as well. . . any help would be a ppreciated. My husband works in a bunker scenario and I want to get him some. need prescription transitional blue blocking lenses!

I know I'm not in the US, but I am in the backwaters of the world. I just ordered some because of a trip to my parents coming up at the end of the year (they are a statin-loving, carb-eating crowd). I just phoned around until I found an eyeglasses place who said "I've spoken to the optometrist and he said he'll order some in for you. They will be amber in colour. I'll call you when they are in and you can chose your frame and we'll fit them with the lenses." In other words, I just rang until I found a place that didn't need hand-holding and could figure out what I was talking about on their own. It took 3 phone calls.

### Primal Dream — 2012-09-20T15:13:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-circadian-living.2442/post-52882>

# Thread: Supplements for kids?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-07-15T09:40:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/post-38000>

What supplements do you give your kids? My kids' diet is definitely good compared to the vast majority of other kids but still not optimal. We are working hard on it. They have finally stopped resisting fish. But it's not perfect and they have issues we are trying to deal with -- ADD, dyspraxia, allergies.

For supplements, we do a multi, FCLO +butter oil, cal/mag and fish oil. Anything else you guys do? Anything else you think might help?

### tweety — 2012-07-15T18:36:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/post-38067>

Right now it's summer, so we just do the FCLO. But in the fall and winter I will add a multi, D3, and sometimes we use PRP immune spray (which is colostrum). I bought the mag oil and keep meaning to rub it in to their feet, but I always forget.

Their diet is great, but I always question how well they absorb things, or what deficiencies they may have based on my health during pregnancy. I know my youngest complains of occasional headaches and seems to require alot of sleep to function, and since she is second and came when I was already depleted, she is more likely to need some help in terms of nutrients.

But i am also hoping that starting them out early on this way of eating and living will correct some problems they may have from the few years of not living this way.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-15T18:55:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/post-38075>

feed your kids well with real food.......they rarely need supplements unless you let them live a crazy life.

### Sem — 2012-07-15T18:58:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/post-38078>

Why magnesium oil on the feet? What does it do?

### Sem — 2012-07-15T20:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/post-38092>

> **Jack Kruse;38244 wrote:** feed your kids well with real food.......they rarely need supplements unless you let them live a crazy life.

I agree but we are definitely not there yet. I've been working on switching them over to a more optimal diet but it hasn't happened overnight. I've had to switch over things that I thought I was doing right (ie plenty of whole grains and soy!). I've also been working to get them to eat more fish. We have cut out gluten but they are still eating grains although it has diminished significantly. Just cutting out gluten has helped curb their sugar cravings as well, which is good. Since I never cared for it much, it hasn't been something we emphasized until recently. So ... I think they need supplements until we get through this transition phase.

### WillieHolmes — 2012-07-18T10:47:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/post-38967>

I think the supplements should be an organic and should not contain chemicals. Fish, fresh vegetables and good food can be a good supplements for kids. Also, you need to provide a home food to kids instead a fast food like burger and pizzas. Also, the physical exercise is also necessary when you are providing a supplements to kids. The outdoor games will be a best exercise.

### Dovid — 2012-08-07T08:42:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements-for-kids.2448/post-43382>

My better suggestion for the kids not use the supplement to them,because they always put them into the natural diet,all the other artificial diet not give the positive response to the body because each and very organ of the body so much sensitive and not enable to bear the hard things.so be avoid for ever until their maturity age.

# Thread: about five days of doing Leptin RX
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### daniellep40 — 2012-07-15T18:18:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/post-38064>

Hi everyone,

I have a very long history of binge eating and chronic fatigue. I have been on a search of how to resolve it and lose some weight for a very long time. I tried atkins but didn't have the results everyone else had, my weight would not budge. I switched to a paleo plan and now am trying this. I stopped exercising this week and am following it and I have to admit I am a little nervous hoping this will work.

I have a question, black coffee is 0 calories. I looked at the leptin rx frequent questions and it says we shouldn't have black coffee between meals because no snacks are allowed, but it goes on to say 0 calorie liquids don't count as snacks. Does that mean its okay? Its decaff by the way that I drink.

Also my urine has turned very yellow even though i drink tons of water. Has this happened to anyone?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-15T18:56:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/post-38076>

black coffee raises cortisol.......so it modulates the reaction to foods. stop it. Anything that screws up cortisol screws up your hormones and Leptin cycle. This is not about calories.

### talkingdog — 2012-07-16T01:06:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/post-38158>

So, to clarify, you're actually drinking black decaf, not black coffee?

I'm in the same boat, now trying to break a severe caffeine addiction (5 cups +/day) and switching to decaf.

One tool that I have been trying (Julia Ross) for breaking the caffeine addiction is Tyrosine.

Does decaf also raise cortisol (and does it also make the body more acidic?)

Any comment on Tyrosine?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-16T01:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/post-38164>

none.........clear? tyrosine is an AA.....and is part of food or a supplement. If taken with a meal fine.....but someone with an ED maybe not so great idea.

### jib — 2012-07-16T01:30:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/post-38175>

> **Jack Kruse;38245 wrote:** black coffee raises cortisol.......so it modulates the reaction to foods. stop it. Anything that screws up cortisol screws up your hormones and Leptin cycle. This is not about calories.

So I don't get it...there are quite a few of the people here that are doing the BPC in the am with their BAB? You're saying, if you're fat, no coffee, correct?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-16T01:36:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/post-38179>

jib.......this is specific for her with her issues not everyones

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-16T07:18:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/about-five-days-of-doing-leptin-rx.2453/post-38301>

> **Jack Kruse;38333 wrote:** none.........clear? tyrosine is an AA.....and is part of food or a supplement. If taken with a meal fine.....but someone with an ED maybe not so great idea.

Would you explain that some more? I take tyrosine as part of my NT program.

# Thread: Losing hair and leg cramps with CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-hair-and-leg-cramps-with-ct.2456/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NWgirl — 2012-07-15T22:37:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/losing-hair-and-leg-cramps-with-ct.2456/post-38115>

I've noticed that since I've started CT baths that I'm losing way more hair than normal. This could just be a coincidence but thought I'd throw this out there to see if anyone else has seen this. Also I'm having some lower leg and foot cramps, but only in my right leg.

FYI I've taken 9 CT baths in about 2 weeks and the temp is usually 53-55. I am usually in from 30-45 minutes per session. I also do some spot CT most every day.

Thanks!

Kim

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-16T03:08:11+0300

### JanSz — 2012-07-18T17:26:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-help-for-dh-please.2469/post-39007>

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?4433-DHT-gt-3a-3%26%23945%3B-Androstanediol-Glucuronide-(3%26%23945%3B-diol-G)&p=182162#post182162](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?4433-DHT-gt-3a-3%26%23945%3B-Androstanediol-Glucuronide-\(3%26%23945%3B-diol-G\)&p=182162#post182162)

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2552-Lab-help-for-DH-please&p=38617#post38617](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?2552-Lab-help-for-DH-please&p=38617#post38617)

AoS/epiAoS: 0.6 umol/L (ovary syndrome (PCOS) and hirsutism.4

In men, elevated 5AR activity is associated with benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH) and premature baldness.4

5-α Reductase Inhibitors

Some inhibition of 5AR may be desirable when 5AR activity is seen to be quite elevated.

Natural 5AR inhibitors can provide effective moderate inhibition without shutting down 5AR altogether,

which is seen with 5AR inhibiting drugs.

Natural 5AR inhibitors include:

Gamma-linolenic (GLA) , docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), and other fatty acids.5,6

Serenoa repens (Saw palmetto)7,8,9,10

Zinc 11,12

Research on the effects of zinc on 5AR has shown that its inhibitory effects can be potentiated by vitamin B6.

Supplements containing highly concentrated Saw Palmetto extracts may result in over-inhibition of 5AR.

5AR activity can be monitored in a 24-hour urine hormone profile.

Over-inhibition of 5AR decreases the production of dihydrotestosterone (DHT) resulting in increased levels of testosterone.

The consequent aromatization of testosterone Ã  estradiol may cause gynecomastia, a known side effect of 5AR inhibitor drugs.

FinasterideÂ® and DutasterideÂ®

Patent medicines that inhibit 5AR are used to treat

benign prostatic hyperplasia,

prostate cancer,

and baldness (androgenic alopecia).

Two of these patent medicines are finasteride (ProscarÂ®, PropeciaÂ®) and dutasteride (AvodartÂ®).

Currently, millions of men around the world take these drugs (as well as supplements mentioned above)

to inhibit 5AR enzyme and decrease the production of 5a-DHT.

There is evidence to suggest that one of the most potent inhibitors, finasteride, may cause residual impotence

after discontinuation and possibly breast cancer in men.13,14 Finasteride has been on the market since the early 1990s.

Risks of adverse drug reactions (ADRs) to 5AR inhibitors increase with dose.

Common ADRs with these drugs include impotence, decreased libido, and decreased ejaculate volume.

Rare ADRs include breast tenderness and enlargement, and allergic reaction.

Should We Inhibit Production of DHT?

While 5a-DHT has been implicated in male pattern baldness and benign prostatic hypertrophy,

the real concern is prostate cancer. As early as 1986, it was suspected by some that 5a-DHT might be a

primary contributing factor to prostate cancer growth.15

5a-Reductase (5AR) is responsible for conversion of testosterone to 5a-dihydrotestosterone (5a-DHT).

5a-DHT is about three times as potent as testosterone due to its greater affinity for androgen receptors.16

However, recent studies have correlated low levels of DHT with decreased survival in prostate cancer patients.17,18,19

Interestingly, a 2008 study found that giving testosterone to elderly men who had lower than normal levels of testosterone

and elevated levels of DHT resulted in reduced plasma 5a-DHT levels.20

Another study in 2007, a 15-yr follow up of men with prostate cancer,

study shows evidence that low DHT is associated with decreased prostate cancer survival. 21

Over-inhibition of 5α-Reductase Activity

While 5a-DHT is considered to be a potential cause of proliferation and growth in the prostate, its metabolite

5a-androstane-3b,17b-diol (3b-Adiol)

is a differentiating agent that activates estrogen receptor beta (ERb) and may help prevent cancer.

Therefore, caution is warranted when inhibiting 5AR because without adequate DHT the production of 3β-adiol

may be over-inhibited as well.22

Research regarding the benefits of adequate 3β-adiol is growing.

3β-adiol is an androgen that stimulates only estrogen receptor beta (ERb),

which has anti-proliferative and re-differentiation activities.

Among other functions, ERb helps regulate prostate growth and differentiation.23,24,25,26

ERb is also an important modulator of the stress response in the brain.27,28

Over-inhibition of 5AR can result in under-stimulation of ERb,

changing the balance of proliferative/anti-proliferative activity in the prostate and elsewhere.

Heavy metals, such as cadmium and arsenic, may also inhibit the production of 3β-adiol.29,30

These heavy metals have been correlated with increased prostate cancer risks.

Another metabolite of 5a-DHT,

5a-androstane-3a,17b-diol (3a-Adiol),

is a storage form for 5a-DHT and is easily “cycled back” to 5a-DHT.28

At present, it appears that to get an accurate evaluation of “total” 5a-DHT levels (actual and potential)

3aAdiol and 5a-DHT should be added together.

Ratios are important

In the Rancho Bernardo study, a higher testosterone to DHT ratio was associated with a 42% decreased risk of BPH

when comparing the top 3 quartiles to the first quartile (OR 0.58, 95% CI 0.35-0.97, p = 0.04). 31

A 1996 study examined DHT and testosterone levels in men who were also screened for prostate cancer.

The testosterone/DHT ratio tended to be higher in patients with more advanced tumors.

There was an inverse relationship between tumor volume, as defined by PSA level, and 5AR activity.

5AR was defined by DHT level, and the testosterone/DHT ratio.

This trend was most obvious with stage T-3.32 T-stage refers to the level of spread in the prostate and surrounding tissues.

Testing for pro- and anti-proliferative metabolites

Very low levels of 5AR activity as expressed by ratios in urine hormone panels may indicate that the production of 5a-DHT is quite low.

Consider follow-up testing with a serum Testosterone Metabolites Profile to assess the adequacy of 3β-Adiol production.

Other patients who may benefit from evaluation of 3β-Adiol are those using 5AR inhibitors, having elevated testosterone or PSA,

extremely high 5AR activity, or with a family history of prostate cancer.

This profile includes the following important ratios:

Testosterone : 5a-DHT

3β-adiol: (5a-DHT + 3a-adiol)

As a leader in preventive medicine we are very interested in the growing body of evidence that the

ratio of 5a-DHT to 3β-adiol

may much more important than absolute levels alone.

We are proud to be able to be the first and only lab to make testing for these testosterone metabolites commercially available.

This test was developed by Dawn Huo, PhD and Ray Lian, PhD.

### JanSz — 2012-08-28T00:34:24+0300

> **LinD;47948 wrote:** Looking for some guidance as my husband has been on Uroxatral since around 10/2010, after a diagnosis of BPH. He even had a biopsy, but no cancer (PTL). Almost two years later, he is having more trouble. He had an appt with the Urologist on Thurs and they could see 190 cc still remaining in his bladder, plus the prostate is about 41 cm. Depending on his PSA, the dr wants to put him on finasteride.
>
>
>
> Other options to consider??!

Many people are royally screwed by

finasteride=Propecia

finasteride=Proscar

or

duosteride=Avodart

\--------------------------------------------

I retired in 1997 at 57yo, life was good.

In 2003 (age 63) I had PSA~4.5

low testosterone

Erectile dysfunction

fatigue

depression

already large prostate

went to the best MD I could find in Manhattan NYC

He told me to get ready to pack up

offered Cialis and Avodart. (I am going to screw his wife at first opportunity, just to prove him wrong)

I knew already that Cialis would not help.

Was scared of using testosterone because it will cause prostate cancer.

Really ready to kill myself.

So, I tried common sense (my own).

It is easy to get prostate biopsy, I got number of them, no cancer.

From my GP I would get Androgel 1% (because his salesman was pushing Androgel that week).

Latter he tried to give me Testim but I stuck with Androgel.

Note;

I got Androgel because salesman pushed it,

therefore

I was not able to get injectable testosterone that I really want.

Luckily for me my GP and my Osteopath are easy on me, I can get any script for testing that I ask for.

Studying the subject I was able to help myself and give suggestions to others, sometimes they use it.

Currently I do not have ED, feel rather good, am in good mood, want to live as long as possible.

In the mean time I was struggling with my growing prostate.

Yes I had urine remaining in my bladdrer.

Yes I was using Flomax, Urotraxal, they did not really helped.

4/8/2010 got

Green Laser Prostate surgery

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?13628-My-Green-Laser-Prostate-surgery](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?13628-My-Green-Laser-Prostate-surgery)

I was not ready for what happened afterward.

In retrospect, in short

my doctor

[http://www.mskcc.org/cancer-care/doctor/jaspreet-sandhu](http://http://www.mskcc.org/cancer-care/doctor/jaspreet-sandhu)

did excellent job as a surgeon

but

lousy job in getting me ready for what may happen afterward.

I was not ready to erase 9 months out of my life where I was constantly peeing (every 15 min or so)

I was coming in wet pants for doctor's visits. Takes over 1hr to get there.

Had he said that it may happen, I would still got the surgery but would be ready for what is coming after.

Reading blogs I found that it happens often.

I still have to think about bathroom locations, but it is much easier now.

I do not know of better choice.

\---------------------

Possibly I have waited too long and ended with large prostate.

Possibly prostate size and larger injury to burn it out, caused my problems after ward.

My prostate growth may have been influenced by my use of Androgel (that raises DHT)

but I was using Proscar and latter Avodart to get my DHT down.

Most people using Avodart/Proscar do not use Androgel, then are suppressing their DHT to nothing and then some.

\---------------------

If you have more questions, ask.

Or better have your hubby do the asking.

Or either of you may reach me at musclechatroom.

..

One, still unexplored possibility is Pelvic Floor Physical Theraphy.

....

I was already learning Chi-Kong but somehow it was too hard on me and I have not felled any relief.

### mambo — 2012-09-05T08:32:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-help-for-dh-please.2469/post-49559>

### vkiernan — 2012-07-17T01:34:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-ft3-rt3-ratios.2491/post-38554>

I asked Jack the following and his reply following tells me that the ratio isn't necessarily black and white but some shade of grey with other things to consider. I was really excited when I got my FT3 lab result back as I thought I was making progress cuz it had never been that high although I knew I had to wait until my RT3 was back too.

So until I have my next consult and get to ask the question again when he has more info, anyone have any thoughts? My FT3 is 4.4 (1.71-3.70) and my RT3 is 25 (11-32) which gives me a ratio of 17.8. Yes, not the over 20 we are striving for but definitely better than the 7.8 I started with last year. I think I am making progress although the quote below from STTM might tell me otherwise since I am on 2 grains of Armour and have AF. I am not sure what to make of it all and have to wait a week or 2 for more lab results to come in before I can have another consult. Thanks all for your input.

> Jack,
>
>
>
> Got my RT3 results back and my ratio is up to 17.8 w/out going the T3 only route. My question is since it should be over 20, am I shunting all to RT3 yet or is some actually available for use?
>
>
>
> Thanks much.
> not an appropriate question for here

Copied form STTM: "If you are on desiccated thyroid (especially if lower than 3 grains) and find yourself with the free T3 high or above range in the presence of continuing hypothyroid symptoms, or even hyper-like symptoms (anxiety, shakiness), itâ€™s a clue you have adrenal fatigue, aka low cortisol."

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-17T03:25:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-ft3-rt3-ratios.2491/post-38586>

"... it seems to help stimulate some of what should be happening in the brain to maintain healthy circadian rhythm cycles. Likely very importantly, it leads to significant changes in the nitric oxide management in the blood vessels throughout the body. Nitric oxide is what keep the lining of the blood vessels healthy and happy, versus getting damages and creating plaques and clots that produce heart attacks, strokes, etc."

"We have greatly damaged and mismanaged our sleep cycle in modern society. Would restoring sleep to something comparable to several hundred years ago allow the brain to work normally again?

One of the mechanisms of bromocriptine through the brain is to tone down the stress signal being sent out through the nervous system that is abnormally high in diabetics. Well, therapeutic relaxation practice can change the state and function of this same part of the nervous system. Would a 15 minute relaxation practice each morning do the same thing?"

[http://www.iadvocatehealth.org/diabetes\_is\_in\_your\_head\_at\_least\_partly0.aspx](http://http://www.iadvocatehealth.org/diabetes_is_in_your_head_at_least_partly0.aspx)

Adrenals being stressed out due to poor circadian rhythm control is what it sounds like to me.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-17T04:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-ft3-rt3-ratios.2491/post-38602>

Jack says: "It is the first way to treat diabetes using a circadian biology approach. If people think what I am blogging about is off........well then go learn about bromocriptine and the hypothalamus." Jack also said: "Then go back and re read in the CT series :[http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/](http://http://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-7/) see the little shout out for peri-menopausal/menopausal women and Prolactin. When women's PG/E2 ratio is upside down they basically become estrogen collectors and they are physiologically IR. Cycloset is my little secret weapon in my clinic." It regulates glucose production in the liver.

Patty found: "Resetting of circadian rhythm, a novel mechanism of action very different from currently used antidiabetic drugs." Wiki says: "Cycloset lowers blood sugar without increasing insulin. It is a dopamine agonist which is a compound that activates dopamine receptors in the absence of dopamine. Dopamine agonists activate signaling pathways through the dopamine receptor and trimeric G-proteins, ultimately leading to changes in gene transcription. Transcription is the process of creating a complementary RNA copy of a sequence of DNA."

So what did I learn from this? Sounds like Cycloset is a good thing and I need some.LOL Ok, I need another hint. ### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-17T05:20:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-ft3-rt3-ratios.2491/post-38623>

OK, found some good info. I'm having foggy brain issues tonight but you may be able to piece this together. Since our "brokens" seem to be very similar this may help both of us! I hope there are some nuggets that trigger something....

\[0024\] "Hyperprolactinemia is the most common endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis," observed doctors Mah and Webster in their published review \`Hyperprolactinemia: etiology, diagnosis, and management\` in Seminars in Reproductive Medicine. 2002 November; 20(4): 365-74. Doctors Mah and Webster continue and comment that, "a prolactinoma is the most common cause of chronic hyperprolactinemia, once pregnancy, primary hypothyroidism, and drugs that elevate serum prolactin have been excluded. A prolactinoma develops because of inadequate prolactin inhibiting factor (PIF) release from the hypothalamus. All of the neuroendocrine hormones from the hypothalamus that control pituitary hormone production and release are stimulating release factors with the exception of prolactin inhibiting factor (PIF). This is why hyperprolactinemia is the most common endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. Bromocriptine is the first-line therapy for hyperprolactinemia, and has been for over twenty years!

Metabolism and Sunlight

\[0025\] **Two factors are important relative to sunlight's ability to regulate metabolism: the circadian rhythm modulating early morning bright sunlight in the pineal-hypothalamus-pituitary axis where dopamine regulates peripheral metabolism via prolactin secretion or inhibition; and the total ultraviolet irradiation from sunrise to sunset, that regulates the vitamin D1 and D2 conversion to D3. Therefore, there are at least two independent, sunlight-related, mechanisms to distinguish between summer metabolism and winter metabolism.

\[0026\] Other mechanisms that are also survival related but really not thought to be sunlight related influence metabolism, especially winter metabolism. These are: lepin, a hormone produced by fat cells; and ghrelin, a hormone produced by the gastric mucosal cells. Therefore, there are at least four separate physiological systems to regulate systemic metabolism and ensure survival, which are especially important during the winter months. The interactions of the multiple metabolic survival systems render scientific cause and effect relationships of a single system difficult to evaluate.

\[0027\] In terms of effect on insulin resistance, the metabolic syndrome and diabetes type 2, benefit can be anticipated by concurrently impacting both of the sunlight-related metabolic regulatory systems. Therefore, a therapy to modulate the circadian rhythm with a dopamine precursor and dopamine-like neurotransmitter, vitamin D3, is herein described.

Circadian Rhythm, Dopamine & Bromocriptine

\[0028\] Circadian rhythm is controlled by both the early morning light and the duration of daylight. The circadian rhythm is controlled via the effects of that light on the pineal gland as well as the hypophyseal-pituitary axis. The effects associated with decreased light exposure and the pineal gland functioning are well known; with the primary result being a reduction in the release of melatonin. However recent focus has been given to investigating the other half of the equation, the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. Many studies have been performed that now link dopamine, an important neurotransmitter, to weight gain and the metabolic syndrome that are associated with a decrease in seasonal sunlight:**

\[0029\] "Compensatory weight gain due to dopaminergic hypofunction: new evidence and own incidental observations." Reinholz J et al. Nutr Metab (Lond). 2008 December 1; 5:35. \[0030\] Dr. Reinholz and colleagues reported that their; "findings support the crucial role of dopaminergic signaling for eating behaviors and offer an explanation for weight-gain after cessation of activities associated with high dopaminergic signaling. They further support the possibility that dopaminergic medication could be used to moderate food intake." \[0031\] "Deficits of mesolimbic dopamine neurotransmission in rat dietary obesity." Geiger B M et al. Neuroscience. 2009 Apr. 10; 159(4):1193-9. \[0032\] Dr. Geiger and colleagues report that; "results demonstrate that deficits in mesolimbic dopamine neurotransmission are linked to dietary obesity. Depressed dopamine release may lead obese animals to compensate by eating palatable "comfort" food, a stimulus that released dopamine when laboratory chow failed."

\[0033\] The dopamine agonist bromocriptine has been used for years to treat hyperprolactinaemia, a condition associated with abnormally high levels of the hormone prolactin. It is usually caused by diseases of the anterior pituitary gland as well as the hypothalamus, especially tumors in the pituitary known as prolactinomas. Dopamine agonists such as bromocriptine are the main avenue of treatment, since dopamine acts as an inhibiting factor for the release of prolactin. Treatment of hyperprolactinaemia with bromocriptine also however resulted in the discovery that the dopamine agonist has additional effects on body weight and composition.

\[0034\] Many studies have been performed to display the links between bromocriptine, dopamine, and obesity: \[0035\] "Dopaminergic tone and obesity: an insight from prolactinomas treated with bromocriptine." Doknic M et al. Eur J Endocrinol. 2002 July; 147(1):77-84. \[0036\] Dr. Doknic and colleagues report that; "It has recently been shown that increased body weight is associated with prolactinomas and that weight loss occurs with normalization of prolactin levels. On the other hand, decreased dopaminergic tone in humans is well correlated with obesity. The objective of this study was to correlate changes in prolactin levels with leptin and body mass index (BM) in patients with prolactinomas treated with the long-acting dopamine agonist bromocriptine (BC)." \[0037\] They concluded that; "The long-acting dopamine agonist BC, by increasing dopaminergic tone, may influence body weight and likely body composition by mechanisms in addition to reducing hyperprolactinemia in patients with prolactinomas." \[0038\] "Bromocriptine administration reduces hyperphagia and adiposity and differentially affects dopamine D2 receptor and transporter binding in leptin-receptor-deficient Zucker rats and rats with diet-induced obesity." Davis L M et al. Neuroendocrinology. 2009; 89(2):152-62. \[0039\] Dr. Davis and colleagues report that; "The dopamine (DA) D(2) receptor (D2R) agonist bromocriptine (BC) decreases body fat in animal and human models and increases lean muscle mass, improves glucose intolerance and insulin resistance, and reduces triglycerides and free fatty acids. We have previously shown a negative correlation between D2R and body weight in obese individuals and in rodents . . . ." \[0040\] They conclude that; "These observations are all consistent with the existence of unique leptin-DA interactions and the hypothesis that there is hyposensitivity of the DA system in obesity." \[0041\] "Bromocriptine (Ergoset) reduces body weight and improves glucose tolerance in obese subjects." Cincotta A H, Meier A H. Diabetes Care. 1996 June; 19(6):667-70. \[0042\] This describes; "A double-blind placebo controlled study investigated long-term effects of Ergoset, a new quick release formulation of bromocriptine, on body weight, body fat, and glucose tolerance in a group (n=17) of obese subjects who were instructed to follow a moderate hypocaloric diet." \[0043\] Dr. Cincotta concludes that; "When combined with instruction to follow a moderate hypocaloric diet, Ergoset, but not placebo, improves glucose tolerance and promotes significant weight and body fat loss in obese subjects over an 18-week treatment period."

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-17T05:21:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-ft3-rt3-ratios.2491/post-38624>

\[0044\] Along with weight loss effects and prolactin inhibiting actions, bromocriptine has also been found to have positive treatment effects on those conditions associated with the metabolic syndrome as well as type 2 diabetes. The metabolic syndrome is a pre-diabetic state associated with: insulin resistance, increased weight, dyslipidemia, and hypertension. \[0045\] "Activation of dopamine D2 receptors simultaneously ameliorates various metabolic features of obese women." Kok P et al. Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab. 2006 November; 291(5):E1038-43. \[0046\] Dr. Kok and colleagues report; "The metabolic syndrome comprises a cluster of metabolic anomalies including insulin resistance, abdominal obesity, dyslipidemia, and hypertension. Previous studies suggest that impaired dopamine D2 receptor (D2R) signaling is involved in its pathogenesis." \[0047\] Their "results imply that short-term bromocriptine treatment ameliorates various components of the metabolic syndrome while it shifts energy balance away from lipogenesis in obese humans." \[0048\] "Modulation of monoaminergic neural circuits: potential for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus." Pijl H and Edo AM. Treatments of Endocrinology. 2002; 1(2): 71-8 \[0049\] Dr. Pijl reports that, "Bromocriptine favorably affects glucose metabolism and various other components of the metabolic syndrome simultaneously to ameliorate the risk of damage to eyes, neural tissue, kidneys and the cardiovascular system in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus." \[0050\] "Bromocriptine: a novel approach to the treatment of type 2 diabetes." Pijl H. et al. Diabetes Care. 2000 August; 23(8): 1154-61 \[0051\] Dr. Pijl reports, "In vertebrates, body fat stores and insulin action are controlled by the temporal interaction of circadian neuroendocrine oscillations. Bromocriptine modulates neurotransmitter action in the brain and has been shown to improve glucose tolerance and insulin resistance in animal models of obesity and diabetes." \[0052\] From this report of the first human clinical trial of bromocriptine on 22 obese/diabetic patients (2000), Dr. Pijl concluded that; "bromocriptine improves glycemic control and glucose tolerance in obese type 2 diabetic patients." \[0053\] "Bromocriptine--unique formulation of a dopamine agonist for the treatment of type 2 diabetes." Scranton R Cincotta A. Expert Opin Pharmacother, 2010 February; 11(2):269-79 \[0054\] Dr. Scranton reports in the abstract that the "Take Home Message" is, "Bromocriptine-QR is indicated to be used alone or in conjunction with all available treatments for type 2 diabetes. Although the mechanism of action is not fully understood, bromocriptine-QR's action points to a central target in the brain (hypothalamus) which may explain the -observed peripheral improvements in metabolic parameters."

\[0055\] Following the discovery and investigation of the positive effects of bromocriptine on the metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes; additional research has been performed to make the link between the aforementioned conditions, dopaminergic modulation, and circadian rhythm. \[0056\] "Association of the anti-diabetic effects of bromocriptine with a shift in the daily rhythm of monoamine metabolism within the suprachiasmatic nuclei of Syrian hamster." \[0057\] Luo, S. et al. Chronobiotics Investigations. 2000 March; 17(2): 155-72 \[0058\] Dr. Luo states that, "bromocriptine, a dopamine D2 agonist, inhibits seasonal fattening and improves seasonal insulin resistance in Syrian hamsters. Bromocriptine-induced resetting of daily patterns of suprachiasmatic nuclei neurotransmitter metabolism is associated with the effects of bromocriptine on attenuation of the obese insulin-resistant and glucose-intolerant condition."

Read more: [http://www.faqs.org/patents/app/20120088773#ixzz20qGd1dm3](http://http://www.faqs.org/patents/app/20120088773#ixzz20qGd1dm3)

Avidly following this thread. Let me try to recap what you're saying (think of me as a 2nd grader lol!) Are you saying that your brain (SCN) isn't properly synched with seasonal light cycles, and this mismatch becomes more pronounced during the summer solstice (one light extreme), and as a result your HPA axis is suffering and needs more support (pregnenelone/Vit D). I'm wondering if you would similar parallel issues during winter solstice (low light extreme)? So, how does light cycle issues become adrenal issues? What is the hormone(s) involved?

I was reading about hyperprolacti.... (forgetting the full word here), and I read that hypothyroidism is also a cause. Not sure about this connection. This presents a chicken/egg problem in my (confused) mind. It also said hyperprolactemia (word?) is associated with low dopamine levels - dopamine is an antagonist to prolactin. Low dopamine seems to cause excessive eating behaviors... My dopamine was tested a few months back and found to be low (serum), but i was very hypothyroid at the time. I was also found to have high fasting insulin levels (despite low 90s fasting BS), and therefore insulin resistant. I am really looking forward to getting more labs pulled, once I give my thyroid issues a little more time to settle (Armour increases, adding in synthroid due to very low T4 levels despite lots of Armour, DHEA, pregnenelone, now licorice in the am).

So I am thinking summer assists my adrenals or something because I always feel soooo much better.

I've always broken the mold - so to speak. I've always loved winter... its my favorite season, and I feel the best during it, even w/the light issues/low t3 and low VitD. While I love getting out in the sun etc, I dislike heat and humidity, though since I've been working on Cold Adapting - this summer hasn't been so bad (of course prior to this summer I always wore cashmere sweaters and long pants in summer b/c I was cold - something wrong w/that picture maybe???) But I'd rather have a dry heat like AZ! AC not required - all you need is a swamp cooler! I actually did really well when I spent 6wks in Quatar several years ago! but its on par w/Florida in latitude.

I asked Jack a question about people being impacted by adrenals depending on the season - and he said yes some people do better in some seasons and other do better in other seasons...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-17T22:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-ft3-rt3-ratios.2491/post-38826>

So we all need to determine our best season and watch what our hormones do (test, test, test) and adjust accordingly. I have always liked early Fall best in Southern California, not too hot and not too cold...just right! But when I was in colder areas it was always summer. So I'm with you vkiernan!

### chocolate — 2012-07-17T02:43:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron.2493/post-38578>

[http://ic.steadyhealth.com/health\_benefits\_of\_boron.html](http://http://ic.steadyhealth.com/health_benefits_of_boron.html)

> The health benefits of Boron are that it helps you to treat diseases and conditions such as allergies, osteoporosis, arthritis, **candida infections, lupus erythematosis, parasites, sex hormones**, aging and menopausal symptoms.
>
>
>
> Boron can reduce symptoms of allergies, especially if they are related with Rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis is autoimmune disease that can affect not only joints but also other organs. Autoimmune disease is when body attacks itself. If person have rheumatoid arthritis of joints parasite called amoebae can affect their joints. Boron can help you to get rid of amoebae.

### chocolate — 2012-07-17T03:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron.2493/post-38593>

[http://www.dummies.com/how-to/content/boron-a-trace-mineral-necessary-for-good-health.html](http://http://www.dummies.com/how-to/content/boron-a-trace-mineral-necessary-for-good-health.html)

> Key functions of boron:
>
>
>
> **May act on the parathyroid glands to regulate calcium, magnesium, and phosphorus balance.**
>
>
>
> Used to prevent bone loss. Boron is a common ingredient in bone-supporting formulas along with calcium.

### chocolate — 2012-07-17T03:50:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron.2493/post-38594>

[http://www.appliedhealth.com/index.php?option=com\_content&view=article&id=100490](http://http://www.appliedhealth.com/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=100490)

> Boron
>
>
>
> Boron
>
>
>
> In a double blind trial comparing 6 mg/d of "boron" with placebo in the treatment of arthritis, of the 10 patients on boron, five improved while only one of ten in the placebo group improved. The boron had significant benefit in severe "osteoarthritis". The 6 mg of boron was in two tablets containing 25 mg of borax ("sodium" tetraborate decahydrate). The experiment was carried over an eight week "period". There were no side effects noted.
>
>
>
> "Boron and Arthritis: The Results of a Double-Blind Pilot Study", Travers, Richard L., MD, et al, Journal of "Nutritional" Medicine, 1990;1:127-132. (Address: Rex E. Newnham, Ph.D., Cracoe House Cottage, Cracoe Near Skipton, North Yorkshire BD23 6LB, United Kingdom)

### chocolate — 2012-07-17T03:54:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron.2493/post-38597>

[http://www.hammernutrition.com/knowledge/beyond-basics-taking-your-supplement-program-to-a-new-level-part-two.1281.html](http://http://www.hammernutrition.com/knowledge/beyond-basics-taking-your-supplement-program-to-a-new-level-part-two.1281.html)

> Â» Boron
>
>
>
> **Boron isn't as well known as other minerals such as calcium, magnesium, and phosphorus, but they can't do their job without boron. Studies indicate that boron, among its many benefits, helps maintain bone health and aids in the prevention of prostate cancer. Excess supplemental boron does not lead to overproduction of anabolic hormones (as was once suggested by some bodybuilding-specific supplement manufacturers), but taking enough boron does result in adequate production of these hormones. This is vital both for maintaining healthy bone mineral mass and also for ensuring complete recovery from high-volume training, when this mineral may be severely depleted.
>
>
>
> During most of the year, a 2.5 mg dose (obtained from one packet of Premium Insurance Caps) is an adequate daily amount. In general, most sport science nutritionists suggest that no more than 3-6 milligrams of boron per day is required, stating the body has only a micro-appetite for this hormone-enhancing mineral. However, during peak training periods, taking an additional 5 mg of boron may be quite useful because it’s during this time that boron levels may become depleted through more intense and/or higher volume training. Because boron supports adequate production of specific anabolic hormones, and because these hormones are critical for ensuring optimal recovery, this additional boron support goes a long way during these periods of higher volume training.**
>
>
>
> There is no RDA for boron and a safe and adequate daily intake is estimated between 1 and 10 mg. As for overdosing (the effects being manifested as loss of appetite, nausea, diarrhea, and skin rashes), most sources suggest that 100 mg daily or more (some cite amounts as high as 500 mg daily) of boron are required for any toxic effects to appear. Considering that two full packets of Premium Insurance Caps provides 5 mg of boron and that an additional boron capsule provides another 5 mg, the maximum you could take would still fall well within the safe range.
>
>
>
> Boron may not be in the same category as our Daily Essentials products, but it has a definite place in the Hammer Nutrition product line. An additional boron capsule may be very beneficial to support enhanced recovery during peak training periods. It’s inexpensive insurance for those heavy trainers who need it most.

### chocolate — 2012-07-17T04:01:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron.2493/post-38599>

[http://www.health-science-spirit.com/ultimatecleanse.html](http://http://www.health-science-spirit.com/ultimatecleanse.html)

> Borax
>
>
>
> Borax and boric acid contain **boron and are excellent fungicides with additional wide-ranging antimicrobial properties, and also chelating fluoride and heavy metals.**
>
>
>
> Dissolve a lightly rounded teaspoonful (5-6 grams) of borax in 1 litre of good quality water (no chlorine or fluoride). This is your concentrated solution. Keep this bottle out of reach of small children.
>
>
>
> One 1 teaspoon (5 ml) of concentrate is the standard dose with 25 to 30 mg of borax and provides about 3 mg of boron. Start with one dose mixed with drink or food. Gradually increase to the chosen maximum intake. The minimum effective dose when used as fungicide is about 100mg of borax.
>
>
>
> For individuals with low to normal weight the recommended daily maximum amount is 500mg or 100 ml of the concentrated solution; drink spaced out during the day. Heavier individuals may use a maximum of 1 gram or 200 ml of concentrated solution. Take the maximum amounts for up to 1 month and then alternate with another microbicide. However, continue taking a maintenance amount of 2 or 3 doses daily.
>
>
>
> Borax is rather alkaline and in higher concentrations has a soapy taste. You may disguise this with lemon juice, vinegar or ascorbic acid. Also see [http://www.health-science-spirit.com/borax.htm](http://http://www.health-science-spirit.com/borax.htm).

Maybe that is what pulls the fluoride out of the thyroid...

### Grizz — 2013-08-02T12:05:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/boron.2493/post-91364>

Since finding out about Boron, I have put it to the test with several people who are suffering with terrible arthritis. There are a great many people suffering with joint pain in my area of West Central Tennessee, so I am looking forward to the testing results. One poor woman we know has pain in every joint in her body, and is suffering terribly. If Boron helps this woman it will be a true miracle. In another test case, an ENTIRE FAMILY is suffering joint pain. I should know by November if it is helping any of them.

I tried Boron myself with 2 capsules 3mg in the evening with my usual magnesium supplement, and the results were immediate. My sleep improved dramatically. Apparently I was deficient in Boron.

My experience confirms this link:
Boron Cures Insomnia:
01/16/2011: Sheryl from Lawrenceville, Ga. Usa replies: "I have had insomnia for years. I have tried everything... Calcium supplements, melatonin, ambien, benedryl and much more. The ONLY thing that has helped has been Borax, taken as suggested by Ted. I started it for other reasons, but the most amazing thing happened. I slept well for the first time in many years. Give it a try... To all the nay sayers, Borax is only a mineral folks! I have had so many people scared of it, thinking it will harm you. I can testify that I have never felt better! Thanks for this website!"

Seahorse,
I recently discovered that Boron does not hit the bulls eye for arthritis. It is a big factor, but no cigar. The central issue is low PH as in "acidosis." When a person has low PH, the body steals nutrients & minerals from the bones, joints, brain & other parts of the body to try & keep the Blood PH at 7.2.... I will wager that his urine and saliva PH are **WELL BELOW** 7.2 and when he starts boron, it will help to raise the PH. But not enough. He needs a change of diet, liquids & minerals to raise his PH to a normal 7.2. More here:
[http://tinyurl.com/Iodine-PH-Levels](http://tinyurl.com/Iodine-PH-Levels)

Grizz

### markrott — 2013-06-09T16:15:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fish-protein-powder.2517/post-87838>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=38864)
>
> I remember seeing an RCT or some other experiment recently using fish protein. The health improvements were impressive.
> But it seems that any processed product has oxidized or damaged nutrients. Fish protein powder would be great for my relatives, but probably not optimal.

agree with Jack K that fresh whole seafood is best - but that said, hydrolyzing the fish protein into the smaller chain peptides does create highly valuable antioxidant forms of the fish - essentially does what your gut does. 10-20g per day of fish peptides do bring real health benefits. aminomarine is a fish protein powder now available in a form that can be used similar to whey proteins and more cost effective than the tablet options that have been on the market.

# Thread: Girls with eating disorders regain healthy fatty acid levels when their weight normal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-with-eating-disorders-regain-healthy-fatty-acid-levels-when-their-weight-normal.2518/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-18T02:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-with-eating-disorders-regain-healthy-fatty-acid-levels-when-their-weight-normal.2518/post-38876>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/girls-with-eating-disorders-regain-healthy-fatty-acid-levels-when-their-weight-normalizes-12821](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/07/girls-with-eating-disorders-regain-healthy-fatty-acid-levels-when-their-weight-normalizes-12821)

Girls with eating disorders regain healthy fatty acid levels when their weight normalizes

> A study of teenage girls with eating disorders has shown that reduced essential fatty acid levels returned to normal once the girls increased their weight to a healthy level.
>
>
>
> The research, published in the August issue of Acta Paediatrica, suggests that it is not necessary to give omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid supplements to adolescent girls with eating disorders.
>
>
>
> â€œEssential fatty acid status is altered in eating disorders that result in weight lossâ€ explains co-author Dr Ingemar Swenne from Uppsala University Childrenâ€™s Hospital. â€œThis is important because deficiencies in polyunsaturated omega-3 essential fatty acids have been implicated in the development of depression and other mental health issues.â€
>
>
>
> Dr Swenne teamed up with child psychiatrist Dr Agneta Rosling, to analyse the red blood cells of 24 adolescent girls who had suffered from eating disorders and had lost an average of 10kgs. Their average age at the start of the one-year study was 14.3 years.
>
>
>
> The researchers compared the results from the eating disorders group with 39 normal weight girls from local schools.
>
>
>
> Key findings included:
>
>
>
> The girls in the eating disorder group had an average body mass index of 15 at the start of the study and this had risen to 19 at the one-year follow- up. This compared to the 21.2 recorded in the control group of the same age.
>
> Seventeen of the girls had anorexia nervosa and the remaining seven were classified as having an unspecified eating disorder.
>
> Twelve had depression at the start of the study, but this had fallen to two at follow-up. Only two were menstruating at the start of the study, but this had risen to 16 at follow-up.
>
> The girls in the eating disorder group showed marked differences in the levels of fatty acids in their blood cells at the start of the study, compared to the girls in the control group. In particular they had lower levels of essential omega-3 fatty acids.
>
> Once the girlsâ€™ weight normalised, the differences between the two groups became less marked and the girls in the eating disorder group regained more healthy omega-3 fatty acid levels.
>
>
>
> â€œIt is clear from our study that once the girls attending the Eating Disorders Unit received adequate nutrition, normalised their eating behaviours and gained weight, their metabolism and endocrine function improvedâ€ concludes co-author Dr Agneta Rosling.
>
>
>
> â€œThis was sufficient to ensure that their essential fatty acid status improved and, in particular, their omega-3 levels recovered to a more healthy level.
>
>
>
> â€œWe believe that this research indicates that providing girls with eating disorders with omega-3 supplements is unnecessary if they normalise their eating behaviour and weight.â€

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-18T02:27:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-with-eating-disorders-regain-healthy-fatty-acid-levels-when-their-weight-normal.2518/post-38884>

### chocolate — 2012-07-18T04:40:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-with-eating-disorders-regain-healthy-fatty-acid-levels-when-their-weight-normal.2518/post-38907>

I got into a fight at my kid's place two out of three times. The doctor special ordered her meals at one place. They were really nice meals, but the other patients felt badly. The way vulnerable people are treated is so ridiculous. I was allowed to go into a separate room and have her to eat. She was afraid of the garbage they fed. I was assured there was a dietician and the meals were nutritious. OMG, they would make anyone sick. Everyone agrees that food is everything. They would make her sit and drink Ensure. I bet they get paid 25 bucks a can for poisoning someone. I hear the sober livings are the same way. I'd rather drink liquor than eat the food they served at one of the hospitals. The chemicals aren't foreign to the body, at least. Our local hospital turned garbage after the seiu got the cafeteria workers and hospital unionized. The food used to be fabulous and people would go eat there that weren't even patients or family. The blood donations were up for the free parking. Now they offer sh\*t food, free parking, and blackmail patients during their admitting appointment into having blood donors. The free crappy food wasn't a good enough offer.

Maybe the study is trying to offer hope.

Or maybe they are just trying to cop out of prescription grade fish oil or ongoing health monitoring. They need to buy soda pop for welfare recipients. We are entering into an age of new priorities.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-18T05:31:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/girls-with-eating-disorders-regain-healthy-fatty-acid-levels-when-their-weight-normal.2518/post-38934>

> **chocolate;39086 wrote:** I got into a fight at my kid's place two out of three times. The doctor special ordered her meals at one place. They were really nice meals, but the other patients felt badly. The way vulnerable people are treated is so ridiculous. I was allowed to go into a separate room and have her to eat. She was afraid of the garbage they fed. I was assured there was a dietician and the meals were nutritious. OMG, they would make anyone sick. Everyone agrees that food is everything. They would make her sit and drink Ensure. I bet they get paid 25 bucks a can for poisoning someone. I hear the sober livings are the same way. I'd rather drink liquor than eat the food they served at one of the hospitals. The chemicals aren't foreign to the body, at least. Our local hospital turned garbage after the seiu got the cafeteria workers and hospital unionized. The food used to be fabulous and people would go eat there that weren't even patients or family. The blood donations were up for the free parking. Now they offer sh\*t food, free parking, and blackmail patients during their admitting appointment into having blood donors. The free crappy food wasn't a good enough offer.
>
> Maybe the study is trying to offer hope.
>
> Or maybe they are just trying to cop out of prescription grade fish oil or ongoing health monitoring. They need to buy soda pop for welfare recipients. We are entering into an age of new priorities.

\*\*\*\* I lived off Ensure and baby food for so long.... I don't know how Ensure made it past the FDA. Or baby food.

ONe of the reasons they had me leave was b/c what I had to say caused a huge ruckus amongst the other patients. As I spoke in a group setting. oops. At some point in my previous life I wanted to make all this better..... but then I couldn't get better myself. And then my brain went.

# Thread: How exercise improves heart function in diabetics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-exercise-improves-heart-function-in-diabetics.2529/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-18T05:15:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-exercise-improves-heart-function-in-diabetics.2529/post-38929>

[http://feeds.sciencedaily.com/~r/sciencedaily/~3/UWhbJJ3XxFM/120717102403.htm](http://http://feeds.sciencedaily.com/~r/sciencedaily/~3/UWhbJJ3XxFM/120717102403.htm)

> A detailed study of heart muscle function in mice has uncovered evidence to explain why exercise is beneficial for heart function in type 2 diabetes. The research team found that greater amounts of fatty acids used by the heart during stressful conditions like exercise can counteract the detrimental effects of excess glucose and improve the diabetic heart's pumping ability in several ways. The findings also shed light on the complex chain of events that lead to diabetic cardiomyopathy, a form of heart failure that is a life-threatening complication of type 2 diabetes.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-18T05:49:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-exercise-improves-heart-function-in-diabetics.2529/post-38938>

This quote stopped my in my tracks, because it relates to my children.

"An intuitive explanation for why high cholesterol at an early age might be correlated with Alzheimer’s risk has to do with apoE-4. People with that allele are known to have high cholesterol early in life \[39\], and I believe this is a protective strategy on the part of the body. The apoE-4 allele is likely defective in the task of importing cholesterol into the astrocytes, and therefore an increase in the bioavailability of cholesterol in blood serum would help to offset this deficit."

I remember being annoyed at the doc for even checking cholesterol in my pre-teen kids.

# Thread: Researchers publish results of an iron fertilization experiment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-publish-results-of-an-iron-fertilization-experiment.2541/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-18T23:25:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-publish-results-of-an-iron-fertilization-experiment.2541/post-39157>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/18/researchers.publish.results.iron.fertilization.experiment?](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/07/18/researchers.publish.results.iron.fertilization.experiment?)

> An international research team has published the results of an ocean iron fertilization experiment (EIFEX) carried out in 2004 in the current issue of the scientific journal Nature. Unlike the LOHAFEX experiment carried out in 2009, EIFEX has shown that a substantial proportion of carbon from the induced algal bloom sank to the deep sea floor. These results, which were thoroughly analysed before being published now, provide a valuable contribution to our better understanding of the global carbon cycle. An international team on board the research vessel Polarstern fertilized in spring 2004 (i.e. at the end of the summer season in the southern hemisphere) a part of the closed core of a stable marine eddy in the Southern Ocean with dissolved iron, which stimulated the growth of unicellular algae (phytoplankton). The team followed the development of the phytoplankton bloom for five weeks from its start to its decline phase. The maximum biomass attained by the bloom was with a peak chlorophyll stock of 286 Milligram per square metre higher than that of blooms stimulated by the previous 12 iron fertilization experiments. According to Prof. Dr. Victor Smetacek and Dr. Christine Klaas from the Alfred Wegener Institute for Polar and Marine Research in the Helmholtz Association, this was all the more remarkable because the EIFEX bloom developed in a 100 metre deep mixed layer which is much deeper than hitherto believed to be the lower limit for bloom development.
>
>
>
> The bloom was dominated by diatoms, a group of algae that require dissolved silicon to make their shells and are known to form large, slimy aggregates with high sinking rates at the end of their blooms. "We were able to prove that over 50 per cent of the plankton bloom sank below 1000 metre depth indicating that their carbon content can be stored in the deep ocean and in the underlying seafloor sediments for time scales of well over a century," says Smetacek.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-19T03:50:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-publish-results-of-an-iron-fertilization-experiment.2541/post-39258>

Sounds like CPC 4 no?

LOL

### phillipsgeorge@hotmail.com — 2012-07-20T09:12:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-epi-paleo.2556/post-39620>

Going out on a limbic limb here about cooking shellfish; as a kid I was lucky enough (I realise now) to grow up by a marine environment. It was a salt marsh, an estuary tidal area of outstanding natural beauty. We kids would spend all our playdays, like little merpeople, in and out of that refreshing North Sea water (and mud!).

We collected the abundant shellfish that abounded there; cockles, winkles, clams and mussels. But even as children we very quickly learnt that the easiest way into a seashell was to cook it! Some of the older lads would lay a big wood fire on the beach (loads of dry drift wood laying just above the high water line) get it blazing, then knock the fire down into a flat area and lay the shellfish directly on the glowing embers.

30 seconds later: voila!- an open shell with the prized nugget of seafood inside, all ready to be easily picked out of the shell (using an empty pair of mussel shells as a pair of tweezers) and slurped down.

Ok, I suppose they were 'almost raw'. But cooked enough so that the proteins holding those shells so tightly closed had denatured enough to lose their hold. Automatically the shells would open, revealing the tasty morsel inside.

They had a divine smoky flavour (is that the the source of the 'bacon' instinct?) but more importantly; no cut fingers from trying to crack those pesky shells (no oyster shucking knives!) and then scrape out just miserable gobbets of raw seameat with horrid slivers of broken shell to crunch down on.

We would gather our 'prey' all afternoon, then in the early evening sit around the smoky campfire on the beach, eating shellfish till we were stuffed! Happily we would wander home in the evening twilight to the comfort of our beds.

Just try opening a live oyster without a knife!

It's my suggestion that, way back when before the knife, as soon as we got a grip on lighting fires, we cooked our clams and mussels.

[http://www.nordhavn.com/egret/images/april9\_1.jpg](http://http://www.nordhavn.com/egret/images/april9_1.jpg)

My Mum liked seafood so I think I had a good start. But there were always sweets and look at that wheat growing in the first photo's foreground; East Anglia, the cereal bowl of the UK!

I guess most of my teeth had mercury amalgams by the time I was 20.

Then there was 30 years of wine, women and wheat.

10 years on from 50, I've given up one of those, chosen one in moderation and the other; well let's just say I've learnt my lessons!

I have found that Dr K's insights have tied together many loose strands in my search for real health as I stumble through the pharma/fooda fog.

I'm returning to my childhood delights; Sea, Sun, Shellfish, Sleep.....but this time no bread, buns, or biscuits!

(...and no statins!)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-epi-paleo.2556/post-41163>

Well, I bought 1/2 lb large type white looking shrimp today for $7. Also bought some scallops 1/2 lb for $11. yikes! Oh yes, some mushrooms too. I think it's gonna take more than a CD. Expensive Jewelry perhaps? One of those is gonna be breakfast tomorrow. As to expense I can get some fresh frozen white fish from Lake Superior in northern US. It has 1.06gm of DHA per 3 oz serving. I think the company is Superior Fish Company. I had it last week and it is very good and would be good raw I think in a smoothie. Cost is $7/lb. I'll have to check on shipping. Thanks to all for the suggestions!

### Michael — 2012-09-28T12:18:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/defining-epi-paleo.2556/post-54956>

No thanks. I'm OK for fitted kitchens.

# Thread: Anybody else see this article?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-else-see-this-article.2557/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cookworm — 2012-07-19T19:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-else-see-this-article.2557/post-39387>

"The New Science Behind America's Deadliest Diseases" [article](http://http://finance.yahoo.com/news/science-behind-americas-deadliest-diseases-031400919.html) by Laura Landro says that the link to Alzheimer's, heart disease, diabetes, stroke, and cancer might be chronic inflammation. Too bad the article blames high fat (although doesn't differentiate \*which\* fats) as one of the culprits of chronic inflammation, and one of the "cures" is to eat whole grains and dairy. Maybe some day conventional wisdom will catch up. ;-)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-19T20:10:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-else-see-this-article.2557/post-39407>

I think the article I linked about AD at the End of BG 5 blows this one away.......read it.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-19T21:51:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anybody-else-see-this-article.2557/post-39450>

> **Jack Kruse;39593 wrote:** I think the article I linked about AD at the End of BG 5 blows this one away.......read it.

# Thread: Seafood to cure all disease.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-to-cure-all-disease.2558/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MonteD — 2012-07-19T19:24:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-to-cure-all-disease.2558/post-39397>

**TITLE: **The quick and highly effective cure and prevention of many cancers, many â€˜incurableâ€™ physiological, neuro-logical, cardiovascular, late-onset, auto-immune and degenerative diseases, many allergies, influenza and birth defects, by natural nutritional means.

PUBLICATION DATE: 8/14/2004**

LINK: [http://www.fileden.com/files/2012/7/19/3327706/poster10.pdf](http://http://www.fileden.com/files/2012/7/19/3327706/poster10.pdf)**

I've had keen interest in ocean based nutrition for the last few years now. I can't explain how exciting Jack's latest series is for me specifically. I think the trace minerals don't get enough attention really. A lot of research in the past 40 years has gone toward how to use the sea to remineralize: soils-->plants-->livestock-->humans.

Of course, instead of remineralizing the soil it would be FAR more efficient to just eat more seafood. That is the very angle the above PDF is based on.

P.S. I'm glad I saved the PDF. The original website doesn't exist any longer. It's a shame too. It explained just how many times more magnificent fruits, vegetables, and livestock are when they are supplemented with ocean water!

Further reading: [http://remineralize.org/](http://http://remineralize.org/)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-19T20:05:56+0300

Thanks, Monte, I look forward to reading the pdf.

I am so looking forward to our vacation this year. To the Gulf coast, of course! I'm going to sit/stand/float in the water for HOURS.
