# Thread: Let's Talk Seafood . . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 39
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-07-19T20:20:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39412>

BG-5 was finally a blog I could truly wrap my head around without having it spoon fed to me! This alone tells me that it's extremely significant for me . . . my brain desperately needs the seafood!

I've been eating seafood at least once a day for the last few months, but I suppose not as much as I should be. It generally equals about 4-6 oz/day. Based on the blog, it sounds like we should be eating a pound a day!! That, I'm afraid, will totally break the bank, which leads me to my first question:

What are you all finding to be the most affordable, yet still nutrient dense, seafoods?

I know Dr. K has said the worst seafood is better than the best meat, BUT I still struggle with that. It's not that I don't believe him, it's just that I've read so much about the practices of farmed fisheries and it truly disgusts me. Petroleum byproducts used as feed, GMO's, mutated species, PCBs, etc, etc.

So for now I've only been buying wild caught stuff, which is super pricey!

Anybody else uncomfortable with funky fish or am I overanalyzing things?!?! I went to an Asian market yesterday and sure, the stuff was dirt cheap, but it was all farmed in China! YUCK! I try not to buy a thing from China, especially food.

Thoughts?

### ealachan — 2012-07-19T20:22:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39414>

I'm trying to look at it this way: fish is expensive, but healthcare is a hell of a lot MORE expensive. Plus, the more fish I'm eating, the less other meat I'm going to have to buy, so it's kind of a wash in that regard.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-19T20:27:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39416>

My question is I fish for salmon in Lake Michigan and am able to stock my freezer well. How much DHA is in my salmon if it is from fresh water, not salt water? Does it matter? Still a fairly nutrient dense food or?

### BJK77 — 2012-07-19T20:27:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39417>

> **ealachan;39600 wrote:** I'm trying to look at it this way: fish is expensive, but healthcare is a hell of a lot MORE expensive. Plus, the more fish I'm eating, the less other meat I'm going to have to buy, so it's kind of a wash in that regard.

Totally agree, but if you truly can't afford it, what to do? I've been purchasing all organic fruits/veggies and organic, grass-fed meats for my family for years now. My food bill is astronomical and I'm ok with that, but I don't have much more room to budge. Wild Alaskan salmon is $12-20 a pound here. If I eat a pound a day, I'm looking at over $300 a month just for one person and that's on the low end of the $ scale.

I'm curious to hear what others are eating and what they're finding to be affordable, good sources!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-19T20:42:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39425>

Whole wild caught mackerel is cheaper than grass fed ground meat where I shop.

### ealachan — 2012-07-19T20:48:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39428>

> **Jack Kruse;39606 wrote:** Sardines and canned salmon are so expensive???? COME ON people.

a standard-sized can of sardines is 125g = 4.4oz, which is a little more than a quarter of a pound. So you'd need 4 x 125g cans of sardines a day to = 1lb 1.6oz. I bought water-packed sardines last night for $1.69 per can. That's $6.76 per lb.

I know canned salmon goes for about $7 per 8oz can at my local grocery store, so that's certainly not a cheap option, at least for me.

edited to add: this is not me saying that I personally can't afford to eat fish. I was just playing devil's advocate and doing a quick cost analysis.

### Melinda — 2012-07-19T20:53:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39430>

Trader Joes has a 14.75 ounce can (no BPA) of wild caught salmon, with skin and bones, for $2.99. I use two cans and some eggs, spices and few diced veggies to make a salmon loaf. Feeds 4 of us twice and really hides the skin and bone from picky eaters. I up the fats and keep the seafood to about 8oz per person per day. I hope you can find a way in your budget to get to epi-paleo.

### kathylu — 2012-07-19T21:04:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39431>

At Costco: six cans of wild alaskan salmon (7 oz can) for about 10 bucks. Canned smoked oysters (pretty yummy) six cans for about $6. Canned crabmeat for about $9 per pound. Four cans of brisling sardines with skin and bones for around $5. My local asian grocery has 21-25 count shrimp (with or without heads) for $5.50/lb. A variety of wild caught fish from mackerel (3 kinds) to bass to snapper to salmon (Canadian) from $3 to $7 per pound. Frozen sea scallops for $8.99/lb. Fish heads (all types) $2.50/lb. Squid $2.99/lb and they will clean everything to order! Dollar stores (and Aldi) have canned fish for a buck per can.

Look, and you will find.

### jib — 2012-07-19T21:05:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39432>

Anybody ck'd an Aldi's? Think I got sardines in spring water (vs soybean-blec) for a buck and I know they have salmon frozen, but not sure of the source.

### MonteD — 2012-07-19T21:44:39+0300

I don't even touch canned fish anymore. And guess why...it ISN'T** cheaper!!!

### ealachan — 2012-07-19T22:03:04+0300

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-20T07:29:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39610>

Budget is going to be an issue for us, but I believe you can pretty much always make it work. Fish heads (yes, Soul, I'm talking fish heads again) are dirt cheap... and you can simply talk to your fishmonger or wherever you buy your wild caught fish and ask for them. Ditto with the skin they are removing for some of the fillets. This is 'throw away' stuff for most fish places, but it's wonderful nutrition. I figure to keep in our budget, we'll probably need to do the 'fish head smoothie' thing every other day at least. Tonight's dinner was raw roe (also dirt cheap, but really ugly in those sacs!), cooked salmon skins and fish head smoothies. One thing I've realised is that keeping to a budget can mean not having glamorous meals... but they make you feel great!

### nonchalant — 2012-07-20T21:44:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39754>

Has anyone dehydrated fresh fish? I was thinking about this, with the smaller fish. Am I asking for an odorous nightmare stinking up my house?

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T21:53:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-seafood.2559/post-39763>

> **Nonchalant;39941 wrote:** Has anyone dehydrated fresh fish? I was thinking about this, with the smaller fish. Am I asking for an odorous nightmare stinking up my house?

I've done this I think I mentioned it somewhere. Yes, it stinks. I've done it with salmon and smelts. The salmon I eat, the smelts are really dog treats, which I ate some of

The thing with dehydrating fish is that you expose it to warm temps for a long time. So some people would say that that might not be a good idea for PUFAs. The fish still gets cooked, unless you use an Excalibur or other dehydrator that has a precise temperature control option (raw foodie speaking here...). Anything over 118 is dead/cooked, most raw foodies won't go over 115, some won't go over 105. So, the lower the temp, the greater the risk for spoilage of meat/fish which isn't that much of a problem for raw vegans b/c it's all veg after all.

Drying stuff has been part of human's conversation methods for a very long time. Drying in the sun is the best option b/c it helps with preservation, as opposed to using a dehydrator. But then you have bugs that will attack your fish/meat and no control over temps.

I have been thinking about this ...and will take a crack at it.......mostly because I think that it is not magic!

#1 .. Passion ...Dr. K. has a huge passion for life, his day job, his blog, his wife and family and this journey to change medicine and inspire all of us to learn and ask better questions and to become involved in our own healthcare

#2 .... Curiosity ......Dr. K. is infinitely curious about everything - and nothing is sacred

#3 .... Compassion ..... and he listens..... never underestimate the value of listening

#4 ..... Oysters, oysters, oysters and lots of seafood

#5 ....... Lots and lot of C/T to mitigate the damages of his day job and being here and helping us

#6 ..... lots and lots of grounding and sunshine - probably mostly naked!

#7 ....... lots of great water

#8 ....... lots and lots of oxy - in all forms ...... sex, dancing, laughter - you name it - I am positive that he does it
lots! and often!!! This should have been tied to #1 I suspect!

#9 ...... A great work ethic - can't wait to get out of bed in the morning

#10 .... A great play ethic ..... When it is time to play .......Dr. K. lives large ... he squeezes the lemons of life often
and hard

#11 ...... lots of great wine and lots of great food and lots of great music

#12 ..... a circle of friends he trusts and respects and admires and who inspire him to be a better person - and to
expand his boundaries beyond even what he thinks is possible!

#13 .... us ....... I am pretty positive we drive him crazy on a regular basis - but then the lights will go on
for someone and then he must think to himself .... Some of them are actually getting this! and my critics
thought this is an impossible task!

#14 ..... Dr. K. is a regular guy ... but he has been inspired to do better ......

and Dr. K. in turn, is inspiring us to be better and do better....

simple ... but not easy .... but not exactly magic either

What is stopping any one of us from doing all of the above???

we all have a lot of background noise in our life - daily frustrations and disappointments etc ... but we only have one life to live.

So lets all dance at the edge of our life and push our boundaries ...we have nothing to lose and everything to gain!

What have I missed?

Second, now you start to see the world with brand new eyes. Small changes begin to advance and you can clearly see what is bad for humans. Feelings become more clear and you do not get stuck in laws of today, problems start to sink down and be just minor, why? You navigate in a more wakeful state once again...

Third, now you begin to question your lifestyle... Now you see and feel that being in the raw nature gives so much. This is not that you get away, you are coming home. It is not about the silence and peace here, it is about how much life there is and powers that are free. You start to question what the hell i have been doing and start to do radical changes. All of these are tough and harsh when you look at them if you are in a early state, like before the first step above.

Now you start to understand that taking steps back is often good for you even at the price you feel it have... If you are sick, listen to the signals, do not silence them... They are good because they want you to make a change.

As i write this i give you a feeling of my life and how i think but also an idea that to become the new modern human it can mean to ride a horse, fish from a lake and sleep in a tipi outside of the city. Now you can use technology for meaningful things instead of joy and pleasure.

# Thread: CW medicine isn't all that bad
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 36
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-07-20T01:10:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/post-39500>

After a miscarriage we got pregnant last october - an easy pregnancy with no major issues.

At 23:15 on the 18th my wife started getting contractions, and after a while we went to the local hospital for a planned water birth. After 21 hours of contractions the cervix still had some hard edges (at 9.5cm) but my wife was totally exhausted. Last resort before C-section was oxytocin IV drops which worked, and one hour later I could cut the cord on a beautiful daughter, after helping (a bit) with the delivery: 21:15. It was a wicked experience to see nature at work; that hairy head coming closer with every push and my wife being totally lost to the most perfectly honed process a mammal could utilize. (And she only had two 500mg paracetamols as pain relief )

However after a too long period of less effective contractions the womb was exhausted and failed to close itself, causing the bleed from detaching and birthing the placenta to not stop. This is not all that uncommon, and at our local hospital everyone routinely gets an oxytocin shot to help close the uterus which then closes the bleed. All newborns are placed on their mothers belly to find their way to the breast which also helps contracting the uterus. Funny the see how she started salivating after smelling the breast

Trouble was that the uterus didn't contract at all, and the bleeding wouldn't stop. There was no outside tear and only minor (class 1) internal ones so it was pretty obvious what had happened. As it turns out my wife had a really low platelet count (trombocytes) so the blood was to thin to clot properly. Luckily she was too fogged to realize the consequences.

She got more and more pale as the sheets and mattresses were soaking in blood and an ER team tried to halt the bleeds. Even a 100 years ago this would likely mean certain death even close to a hospital, but thanks to a transfusion of two litres rhesus negative A I don't have to be a single parent.

And as if this wasn't tough enough our daughter had an oxygen saturation of only 80 at birth, which triggered screening for heart failure, lung failure or infections. The saturation of newborns can be lowered but below 92 is usually a cause for serious concern. As I know from the experience with CT I'm guessing that even lower might not really be a problem in itself, though.

Luckily the saturation increased by a little on itself by the early morning, but still not enough - so she got a comprehensive blood panel and even an x-ray of the lungs although I was quite hesitant to do that. I guess I figured that if my wife had an undiscovered blood content issue that might also have affected the baby. Thankfully there was nothing out of the ordinary to be seen, and by noon today the saturation was up to 96%.

My girls are still at the hospital and I'm home after 48 hours after the whole thing started; more exhausted and completely drained than I though was possible - but I made it. We all made it though the first hours. I'm very thankful for what I have and that I now have the opportunity to create a family with my soulmate and our baby girl. I will remember this for the rest of life when I see them smile.

Writing this is my way of sorting it- now I'm finally going to have a small breakdown, and get ready to focus on what I have, and not what's missing...

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-20T20:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/post-39708>

> **primat;39885 wrote:** We've been seafood heavy for a year already so switching to epipaleo might not be too hard. The baby will get breastfed as long as possible, but but there is no milk banks in Norway that will give milk to a full term baby. it's frustrating., but no options. now we're in a taxi and there is too much time to think.

Primat - Human Milk 4 Human Babies isn't a milk bank - its a milk share btwn moms. here's their direct link on Facebook for where you're at. [https://www.facebook.com/pages/Human-Milk-for-Human-Babies-Norway/157090461015026](http://https://www.facebook.com/pages/Human-Milk-for-Human-Babies-Norway/157090461015026)

post on there, and let them know you may need help - you'd be surprised how many people will donate - I know ppl in my local area who received milk from Canada, and Mexico - you connect with the donors and then arrange pickup/shipping etc - usually moms will ship at no charge - and may only ask for replacement milk storage bags...

Way better than a milk bank!

additionally - you wifes milk can help her as well, she should drink it!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-22T16:39:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cw-medicine-isnt-all-that-bad.2571/post-40216>

Sorry to hear that........First-line treatment of AML consists primarily of chemotherapy, and is divided into two phases: induction and postremission, also called consolidation therapy. The goal of induction therapy is to achieve a complete remission by reducing the number of leukemic cells to an undetectable level; the goal of consolidation therapy is to eliminate any residual undetectable disease and achieve a cure. Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is usually considered if induction chemotherapy fails or after a patient relapses, although transplantation is also sometimes used as front-line therapy for patients with high-risk disease. Here is the real irony........that little baby maybe your wife's survival card. Make sure you tell all your wife's relatives to go get tested immediately for their compatibility........she is likely going to need a bone marrow transplant.

### SeaHorse — 2012-07-22T22:20:55+0300

the last chapter of Suzanne Somer's book, Knockout, is an interview with the founder of LEF (Life Extension Foundation) about how to supplement/support the body when a person is undergoing CW treatment for cancer. There were many simple, low cost, easy to access things I wish I had known about listed in there. I think you and your wife may find it helpful as an add-on to the other medical intervention you are getting. Lots are focussed on ways to build the body's immunity... there are also other chapters that deal specifically with chemotherapy and how to support it.

Wishing you all the best and sending you a special prayer of healing.

# Thread: Is my testosterone too high (female)?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-testosterone-too-high-female.2572/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jennie747 — 2012-07-20T01:22:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-testosterone-too-high-female.2572/post-39505>

The lab says 79 with a reference range of 14-76. My Doc wrote "OK" next to it. He didn't test estrogen that time, I don't know why, but he did on the previous labs. 6 months previously testosterone was 55, total estrogen was 166.4 but I can't tell which reference range to use as I had a Mirena IUD in at the time and wasn't menstruating, so I don't know where I was in my cycle. They range from 30-650 depending on the cycle phase. Maybe the testosterone was higher after taking out the Mirena?

From reading I wonder if that's causing my adult acne (I'm 46, had it since teen years) and chin hairs? I don't have excess body hair in other places or a deep voice or apple-shape any of the other symptoms I've read about. The only time my acne has ever improved much is when I was pregnant though, that must mean something?

How does one go about lowering testosterone if this is indeed a problem?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-20T01:23:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-testosterone-too-high-female.2572/post-39507>

My daughter was telling me about these little whole dried fish that were setting around the Thai family's house she babysat for as a teen. The little boy used to get up on her lap and try to kiss her. She would just tell him he needed to save his sweet kisses for his mommy (fishies or no). And how lucky he was to have such nice treats, of course. She said the kids always ate them head first. I can't imagine they're any different from anchovies. IDK. I see them in the stores, but I thought they were used in a pestle and mortar with lime, cilantro, and spices for a dressing. That might be good. At least its raw and easy to get your hands on and sounds concentrated. I can't find anchovy sauce or past without bad stuff in it. I know if you feed a rooster fish meal, you have to keep a rake to swat him always. He gets too mean. And he doesn't learn his lesson. He just keeps coming back for more.

### caroline — 2012-07-20T10:49:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39624>

Does ceviche count as raw fish?? theoretically - the lime juice is cooking it I think.

### Destiny — 2012-07-20T14:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39643>

I did smoked salmon for lunch and cooked shrimps for dinner. I have to admit that there was some gag reflex at the end of eating shrimps. It will take some time to get to anything raw. A memo to myself: Love all seafood.

### kathylu — 2012-07-20T14:46:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39644>

> **caroline;39811 wrote:** Does ceviche count as raw fish?? theoretically - the lime juice is cooking it I think.

The lime juice doesn't cook the fish, just gives it a texture similar to lightly cooked fish. That's because the acid in the lime juice precipitates the proteins, changing their structure a bit.

Parasites (as well as viruses and bacteria) are definitely a concern in raw seafood. To eliminate parasites the seafood must be frozen for 7 days at -20 degrees celsius (centigrade). Only 2 hours if you have access to a -70 degree C freezer. To eliminate bacteria and viruses there is not much you can do except cook thoroughly. However, I have been eating raw seafood most of my life (not as often as I would like unfortunately) and I have never gotten sick. Raw oysters are probably the riskiest, they are filled with viruses and vibrio species (bacteria) and I will still eat them raw. Live dangerously!

Maybe we'll evolve by incorporating some new viral sequences into our DNA...

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-20T15:12:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39649>

I just ate my first 1/3 pounds of oysters this morning - undercooked. Can't say I loved them like I love the scallops. In fact, I was pretty happy when they were gone. How do you make oysters so they taste good?? I may just add a few to my morning (allready seafood) BAB, and hopefully I'll acquire a taste for them...

### BJK77 — 2012-07-20T15:49:11+0300

BAB this morning was fit for a champion!! Sardines and oysters with a little beef mixed in to make it more filling all topped with some salmon roe. Headed to Whole Foods today to get some fresh sardines and other goodies.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T15:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39657>

BAB was sauteed sockeye and some sheets of toasted Korean seaweed called laver that I picked up at Hmart yesterday. Hmart has a big fresh fish and salt water tank section. But for now I think I will still buy my seafood at WF. The seafood was looking a little picked over last night at Hmart. I plan on shrimp for tonight.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T19:24:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39696>

> **Glamazon;39844 wrote:** BAB was sauteed sockeye and some sheets of toasted Korean seaweed called laver that I picked up at Hmart yesterday. Hmart has a big fresh fish and salt water tank section. But for now I think I will still buy my seafood at WF. The seafood was looking a little picked over last night at Hmart. I plan on shrimp for tonight.

I'm on my way there, quite a drive for me. And now you're saying it's picked over! Wonder if maybe they got fresh stuff for the weekend?

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T22:24:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39777>

> **Souldanzer;39883 wrote:** I'm on my way there, quite a drive for me. And now you're saying it's picked over! Wonder if maybe they got fresh stuff for the weekend?

I don't know if that was because I went shopping at 8pm and those remaining fish had been probably laying on ice since the morning but it is worth the look. Just be aware that everybody and their dog goes shopping there on the weekend and the crowds are tremendous. Very international in type of shoppers and the whole family goes. Good thing because that means a good turnover of product. The seafood department starts on the back right of the store past the produce section. The store is HUGE! Huge selection of many types of seafood, live, fresh, frozen, and dried in bulk and enormous packages. Lotsa offal too. Mussels are like 4.99 a pound. Yes, they have fresh mackerel. LOL

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T22:28:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39780>

> **Glamazon;39964 wrote:** I don't know if that was because I went shopping at 8pm and those remaining fish had been probably laying on ice since the morning but it is worth the look. Just be aware that everybody and their dog goes shopping there on the weekend and the crowds are tremendous. Very international in type of shoppers and the whole family goes. Good thing because that means a good turnover of product. The seafood department starts on the back right of the store past the produce section. The store is HUGE! Huge selection of many types of seafood, live, fresh, frozen, and dried in bulk and enormous packages. Lotsa offal too. Mussels are like 4.99 a pound. Yes, they have fresh mackerel. LOL

I ended up exiting at Broomfield b/c I wasn't feeling very well. POM does have a lot of choice but I've heard Denver is huge. Another day. My freezer is also kinda full and I need to solve the freezer space issue before I really can stock up... like I should if I have a couple hours round trip!

### Glamazon — 2012-07-20T22:35:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39784>

> **Souldanzer;39967 wrote:** I ended up exiting at Broomfield b/c I wasn't feeling very well. POM does have a lot of choice but I've heard Denver is huge. Another day. My freezer is also kinda full and I need to solve the freezer space issue before I really can stock up... like I should if I have a couple hours round trip!

I don't know if you take I 25 or I 225 down but you want to exit at I 225 and Parker. It's about three stop lights to the north. You want to take a left at the light at Mickey D's. Mickey D's is on the left north corner of the intersection. The parking lot is huge and it is also on the left. It's an all purpose store so you are going to find clothes, chinese medicines, appliances and other furniture there too. It's basically an asian Walmart.

I also want to add you might want to scope out Federal and also Alameda avenues in central west denver south of 6 th avenue. That's where you will find a lot of mom and pop asian markets as well as some bigger markets that have these butcher cases where you buy things like pig uteruses, live fish, and tea smoked duck.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-20T22:44:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39788>

### freesia — 2012-07-20T23:20:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39807>

Ahhh this explains why the sashimi fish I can order comes "deep frozen". So it can be thawed and used right away without the necessery 20 day stay in a regular freezer. Thanks for explaining

> **kathylu;39831 wrote:** The lime juice doesn't cook the fish, just gives it a texture similar to lightly cooked fish. That's because the acid in the lime juice precipitates the proteins, changing their structure a bit.
>
>
>
> Parasites (as well as viruses and bacteria) are definitely a concern in raw seafood. To eliminate parasites the seafood must be frozen for 7 days at -20 degrees celsius (centigrade). Only 2 hours if you have access to a -70 degree C freezer. To eliminate bacteria and viruses there is not much you can do except cook thoroughly. However, I have been eating raw seafood most of my life (not as often as I would like unfortunately) and I have never gotten sick. Raw oysters are probably the riskiest, they are filled with viruses and vibrio species (bacteria) and I will still eat them raw. Live dangerously!
>
>
>
> Maybe we'll evolve by incorporating some new viral sequences into our DNA...

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-21T03:08:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-39869>

Okay, I'm joining today

No breakfast other than tea with coco milk, dinner was Inger's Cocktail with 4oz raw mackerel (no heads left) and 2oz whole smelts (head on), followed by a garden greens salad with graved lachs, desert was a little bowl full of crunchy fried shell-on, head-on, legs-on, eyes looking at me shrimpies. I did great until the shrimp.

Those kinda turned my mind and then my stomach. But I did it and they actually tasted good! I have more of them in the fridge.... hopefully I'll get over myself. I just feel like I'm crunching down on insects  Which I guess would be a good food, too.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-07-24T22:10:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40814>

> **MJ\*;40929 wrote:** That is a very interesting breakfast! How did it go over?

I liked it! Son liked it after he started eating it. He complained at first - but a bit ago we established the "if you don't eat it for breakfast, it goes in the fridge and it's for lunch. If you don't eat it for lunch, it goes in the fridge and it's for dinner" and on and on. . .He went to bed without supper only once, and hunger won out. Besides, I think he actually is starting to like all this stuff!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T22:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40827>

_\*puts a toe back into this thread\*_

3/4lb head-on shell-on shrimp fried in CO, few T of coconut milk in tea, and a couple of spoonfuls of ghee for desert, a sip of kombucha for digestion purposes

### freesia — 2012-07-25T00:03:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40867>

I'm upgrading! (I've already been eating fish for my BAB 4 days out of 7 and for my evening meal 2 x per week.

I'm driven to do this for a few reasons. One is the recent hints that it may affect gut flora positively. That in itself would have so many flow on effects I can't even imagine right now... Another reason is the well studied DHA effect on Glutathione Pdx and superoxide dismutase...again WOW...and already knowing both those enzymes are compromised in my case means I can't really _not_ take part here.

Third reason, well I'm a dummie... For years, back when I was in better health than I am now, I had several occasions per year when I would need to work to critical deadlines. At these times of extra stress, and only these times, I would get a sudden intense desire for shellfish. I didn't grow up by the sea and shellfish was not a regular part of my diet...I didn't ever particularly like shellfish consciously...but my body knew something back then.

My seafood order arrives tomorrow and then I'm in, 100% for 30 days. (Woot!)

I've got frozen seafood coming to compliment the cod and turbot we already get fresh at the market. ..Whole sardines, Whelks (sea snails), Kina Roe, and some southern bluefin tuna which is frozen extra cold and meant to be eaten raw. I can get oysters locally but haven't been doing it often, so will make that more regular....$$$ depending...

Thanks to those who've already sheared pics (YUM) and recipe ideas

### David — 2012-07-25T00:22:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-40875>

My 6AM BAB for the last eight days has been a crab omelet with a side of kippers, plus four strips of bacon and Coffee w/butter, stevia, chocolate, CO and cinnamon. Totals about 60 grams of protein and I am never hungry until dinner at 4PM. Dinner is now scallops, salmon, baked fish or a shrimp dish, plus greens, as well as a small West Indies salad (cold crab + sweet onion marinated in oil and lemon juice).

Woot! Delivery

### nonchalant — 2012-07-25T22:17:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-41069>

Oysters on the half shell. A first for me. I had been concerned earlier about eating oysters in a month without an "R", but I threw caution to the wind. After trying an Inger's Cocktail, it twern't no big deal. Actually, they are good!

### BJK77 — 2012-07-26T03:25:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-41139>

I am so impressed by all of you raw seafoodistas! I totally planned on joining in with the MHS, but just couldn't do it . . . YET! For now I'll continue on with my cooked seafood and the occasional raw oyster or two. I can feel a difference, so as long as I think it's doing something I'll take things slowly.

Today was salmon for BAB and shrimp with avocado, mushrooms and sauerkraut for dinner followed by a raw oyster for dessert -- YUMMO!

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-26T17:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-improved-1-lb-or-1-2-kilo-of-seafood-a-day-challenge-for-30-days.2573/post-41201>

BAB: Inger's Cocktail with 5oz herring head and guts only, chunk of cod liver w/ oil, chlorella and lime juice. 8oz oysters cooked in CO, T of Sweet Fish roe, 2 raw (ceviche) sea scallops, coco milk. Yuck. I can honestly say I didn't like any of it

Dinner last night: 7oz graved lachs with garden greens, spring onions, soaked arame in nori and 2T salmon roe. Some Sea Crunchies. With an Inger's Cocktail as appetizer, lol. That was a 4oz sardine head guts and all, with a few smelts and a cod liver with oil, chlorella and lemon. Coconut milk and a spoon of ghee for dessert. A few almonds, oops.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-23T19:05:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-40406>

"We know experimentally that CMRO2 (cerebral metabolism) is tied directly to microtubule function and mitochondrial ATP production in the brain. The only way we have as neurosurgeons to uncouple neuronal oxygen consumption from cerebral blood flow and not kill neurons is inducing anesthesia. Today we don’t know precisely how anesthesia works, but Stuart Hameroff, MD (an Anesthesiologist at Univ of AZ) believes he does.... We know that volatile anesthetic gases act by Van deer Waals (London Convention) forces in hydrophobic pockets of select brain proteins to ablate consciousness. It’s called the induction of gamma coherence of neuronal microtubules."

"In AD, the neurons are diseased from protein folding defects but the blood vessels can deliver a normal cerebral perfusion pressure. This means oxygen flow is intact in the AD patients’ brain. The neurons however on the dynamic PET/SPECT scans show major hypo-metabolism and hypo-perfusions in the frontal and temporal lobes. Autoregulation is clearly uncoupled in AD." [http://jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/](http://http://jackkruse.com/do-food-electrons-impart-a-quantum-effect/)

Perhaps Alzheimers Disease might also be called Anesthesia Disease (AD).

The two times DH has been under general anesthesia were very disturbing to me. Even after he was released, and he seemed normal, he ended up with zero memory of events for a while. I'd say, from my experience, that is was somewhat similar to Alzheimers.

### Shijin13 — 2012-07-23T19:21:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-40413>

my parents have a friend who had surgery a while back and when he started to comeout of the anesthesia he had some sort of psychotic response to it - and they placed him in a medically induced coma - every time they've tried to bring him out - he has the same response... scary

I've always had a high tolerance for pain... it takes a lot to get to my threshold... its weird... w/both kids my contractions sux'd 5-10min in length and very strong, then 2mins rest...I'd roll that way for 2-3hrs, then stop for 2hrs. hence the 56hr labor w/dd (41hr natural - before I became too dehydrated that the MW had to transfer me)

### Glamazon — 2012-07-23T23:42:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-40478>

> **Nonchalant;40600 wrote:** The two times DH has been under general anesthesia were very disturbing to me. Even after he was released, and he seemed normal, he ended up with zero memory of events for a while. I'd say, from my experience, that is was somewhat similar to Alzheimers.

Could have been the anesthetic agent that was used had induced temporary amnesia. Works in the hippocampus. It is involved with short term memory retention. Nurses rely on ativan IV in the ICU a lot to help keep patients calm but patients end up missing chunks of memory after discharge. Versed is another IV drug that induces temporary amnesia. It is a common agent used for procedures in the hospital.

### Glamazon — 2012-07-23T23:43:53+0300

Red heads do bleed or hemorrhage more easily. A friend of mine did her PhD dissertation on it.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T20:56:56+0300

### nonchalant — 2012-08-14T00:26:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-44778>

> **Jack Kruse;40131 wrote:** gamma coherence is the property of DHA's electron clouds are aligned in neural lipids in such a way that give it properties we do not see out of the lipid layers to allow it to do things we did not expect. It is due to the quantum effects (coherence) of the electrons of one DHA molecule to affect other DHA molecules......

Jack, can you give us a cite for this? I've seen the Hameroff article on gamma coherence, I just can't find a reference for the gamma coherence of DHA specifically. Lots of talk of folding proteins, gap junctions, etc. Perhaps I just don't understand how the neuron works, and I'm missing the obvious? Perhaps it's the membranes? Sigh. I think I need another Inger's cocktail.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-08-14T02:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-44796>

> **Souldanzer;40964 wrote:** children don't develop a sense that they have their own mind until the age of around 7 (iirc)... before that they feel as part of their mother or primary caretaker

not sure I buy this at all.

### Souldanzer — 2012-08-14T03:17:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-had-a-lab.2575/post-44814>

> **Jack Kruse;45108 wrote:** not sure I buy this at all.

somebody taught me that in grad school..... child psychology. Can't remember the name but he was pretty popular. It was related to the concept of understanding that there is "I" vs "you" or "someone else" and that the thinking/mind of the "I" is entirely separate from the mind of the other person. Basically, the guy's point was that children do not develop an understanding of what "mind" means/entails until they are about 7 years old. Before they experience their own mind as part of their mother's mind, they don't get that every person has their own and it is private.

Not married to this theory. Only wrote a term paper about it at some point in my life.

Thinking about how we think in different terms, the one's that were layed out in BG5..... at what point in life is there "enough" DHA to make the thinking process work enough to have a sense of one's own mind as separate from someone elses? I know when my brain went really bad right after TBI/TIA I was having a lot of dissociation symptoms.... things dissolving into.... nothing in particular... more like particles.... had to think hard before I could name and understand a tree (or car, or person, or anything) from the brown and green particles that my eyes perceived. Things didn't appear so separate anymore. Ok, now you're going to think my brain is lost for good  most days when I look at things now they appear complete with form and function in my mind again. It was pretty cool in retrospect, if scary. I still get it a little bit from time to time.

# Thread: 5 week breakthru
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-07-20T20:42:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39718>

So i started on armour 5 weeks ago. 1 grain a day adding one additional grain per week. I am on 5 grains a day now. i was having a very hard time losing weight. i also did ct 4 times a week the first week, then 3 times, then 2 , now 1 time a week (due to a technical problem w my tub). my body temp at the beginning was all over the map but never getting over 98.2 really and id wake up at 96.5

so just a couple days ago i started losing weight again, my body temp when i get up is 97.6 - .8 and climbs to 98.7 during the day. I feel hot all the time now (even when my BT is 98 I am almost sweating).

My question is do I add another grain next week???

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:45:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39722>

> **msclman;39905 wrote:** So i started on armour 5 weeks ago. 1 grain a day adding one additional grain per week. I am on 5 grains a day now. i was having a very hard time losing weight. i also did ct 4 times a week the first week, then 3 times, then 2 , now 1 time a week (due to a technical problem w my tub). my body temp at the beginning was all over the map but never getting over 98.2 really and id wake up at 96.5
>
>
>
> so just a couple days ago i started losing weight again, my body temp when i get up is 97.6 - .8 and climbs to 98.7 during the day. I feel hot all the time now (even when my BT is 98 I am almost sweating).
>
>
>
> My question is do I add another grain next week???

Holy grains. I wouldn't add anymore until you stabilize. This doesn't happen overnight. You also should be tested before you add more but need to let your system settle a bit. Does your doc have you on this much?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T20:46:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39725>

> **Jack Kruse;39907 wrote:** why not........

Really, that much is ok and more??? How does he know when to back off of it then?

### msclman — 2012-07-20T20:56:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39734>

> **vkiernan;39909 wrote:** Holy grains. I wouldn't add anymore until you stabilize. This doesn't happen overnight. You also should be tested before you add more but need to let your system settle a bit. Does your doc have you on this much?

Yes my doctor knows, it was my idea though and my doc is pretty traditional but is giving me some leeway. I'm 220 pounds so although it sounds like a lot, mg/lb dosage is not so bad. no?

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T21:03:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39738>

> **msclman;39921 wrote:** Yes my doctor knows, it was my idea though and my doc is pretty traditional but is giving me some leeway. I'm 220 pounds so although it sounds like a lot, mg/lb dosage is not so bad. no?

Could be since I am half your weight. Seems reasonable when you put it that way. 5 gr seems like so much though. Must be a hdad thing for me. What is going to be the determining factor for you to stop?

### msclman — 2012-07-20T21:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39744>

yes at your weight it is equivalent to 2.5 grains. Im not sure if it works like that or if i just made that up.

I will stop once i feel heavy palpitations, anxiety, racing pulse.

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-20T22:06:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39770>

I'm at 5 grains too - just upped to 5 three days ago. As if that's not enough, I'm also taking 50mcg synthroid. My weight has been steadily dropping since slowly increasing the Armour from 1.5 grains (and implementing the leptin rx strictly). Now I'm within 2 pounds of my ideal weight (I'm 142 pounds). Unfortunately my symptoms are anything but ideal. Chronic low blood pressure, and higher heartrate, and feeling dizzy. It's difficult because these are the adrenal symptoms I get when I don't have enough thyroid hormone, but I'm starting to get nervous that it's because I have too much and somewhere I crossed the ideal point without a change in symptoms. It gets worse as I take more time between dose increases. Now doctor wants me to drop to 4.5 grains (she never approved the last increase) + 50mcg synthroid, and sit where it levels. I don't know how I'm going to survive a few more weeks feeling the way I do right now... Sorry for the post hijack.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-20T22:17:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39775>

> **vkiernan;39912 wrote:** Really, that much is ok and more??? How does he know when to back off of it then?

when he sees no effect at all (Increasing RT3) or he gets side effects from excess thyroid hormone......(excesssive T3)

### vkiernan — 2012-07-20T22:25:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39778>

> **Jack Kruse;39962 wrote:** when he sees no effect at all (Increasing RT3) or he gets side effects from excess thyroid hormone......(excesssive T3)

Got it! Thanks.

It gets worse as I take more time between dose increases. Now doctor wants me to drop to 4.5 grains (she never approved the last increase) + 50mcg synthroid, and sit where it levels. I don't know how I'm going to survive a few more weeks feeling the way I do right now... Sorry for the post hijack.

why the synthroid too? armour has natural t4 in it.

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-21T01:21:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39841>

> **msclman;39987 wrote:** why the synthroid too? armour has natural t4 in it.

Because my labs at 4.5 grains alone showed barely in range for total T4 (5.4, range 5.3-12.3) (free T4 was 1.25 range 0.9 - 1.7), and my Free T3 was mid range (3.6 range 2.3-4.2), and I'm still symptomatic. I think pork thyroid has more T3 proportional to T4 compared to human thyroid (not 100% sure on this though), so increasing the T4 can balance that out. For someone like me whose gland is destroyed by long term auto-immune disease I'm looking to completely replace what my gland would have done. I'm wondering if I'm not absorbing the T4 very well from the Armour. I seem to be getting the T3 pretty well though, so I don't know why it'd be different. I'm nervous that the extra T4 is going to increase my reverse T3 ratio (which has improved from 6 to 15 over the past fwe months). But this would also show me that things are still not right in my body causing my brain to increase reverse T3 levels. At that point, I give in and schedule a consult! For now I'm going to hold everything a few weeks and re-test... How I wish I had my gland back operational!!

funny, im at 82 BPM right now. I never saw that metric so am glad you pointed it out Janszy

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-21T03:26:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/5-week-breakthru.2588/post-39875>

My T3s are okay, but I think that's all I'm absorbing from the Armour. T4 is a storage hormone, but without anything in storage to convert to T3, you go hypoT pretty quickly. I think that's what's happening to me, I'm living off the T3 I take. I (was) mainly symptomatic of hypoT in the morning (low blood pressure, high bpm - adrenals trying to compensate and failing). And as the day would go on (and I'd take the Armour), things would improve. Now, I'm symptomatic all the time, just different symptoms in the morning then the afternoon and evenings (blood pressure comes up a little (100/60), and pulse drops to normal ranges, but I feel dizzy like there's not enough blood going to the brain. You only convert T4 to rev T3 if your body is trying to shut your metabolism down, due to HPA issues, or what not (hazy on the "what-nots" here).

I did a salivary cortisol back in February. It wasn't great, but wasn't horrendous compared to others I've done a few years ago. My numbers were 17 first thing, in range the next 2 (forget exactly where), and high in the evening. I wonder if I messed up the evening one though, because I did it at 10pm before going to bed (and lots of bright lights! Didn't know of JK back then). I take DHEA and pregnenelone per labs drawn, but I don't feel them improving anything for me.

I could be wrong but getting off Armour and onto T3 only would help with symptoms, but not address the underlying issue causing your body to want to shunt everything to reverse T3. That is the real goal in my mind. With high RT3, figuring out what's driving it.

> **msclman;40053 wrote:** dr k would have some great insight for you on this i would definitely give him a call.
>
>
>
> your free t4 looks pretty good. your t3 is ok. did u do a salivary cortisol? I could be way off on this but I thought t4 was really just a storage moecule and that t3 was where the activity was. taking more t4 will convert to rt3 like you said. my rt3 is high as well (b4 armour so I am wondering where the hell it is now). I would love to get compounded t3 and get off the armour but my doc said no.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-22T16:29:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-40213>

Glad you opened up a thread about teeth, Dr. K. I had been wanting to put this in "Ask Jack", but I'll ask here:

I have palatal tori, and it's never been fully understoody why. I had 4 bi's extracted for ortho (I'm 50; it was the early 70's) and had extreme crowding of 23, 24, 25, 26. Did the tori develop due to "pushing" all the teeth together rather than expanding the palate? Or was the crowded palate due to maternal poor nutrition (and smoking, which she did through 3 pregnancies)? Or is it due to the asthma, poor breathing I experienced as a child, or was that a symptom of all the other AI issues I had due to the poor nutrition?

The tori has been relatively unchanged in size or texture since teen years; in fact, I can't remember not having it. But I have no idea when it developed.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-22T16:43:56+0300

### bigknitwit — 2012-07-31T17:54:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-42055>

I have great teeth - no fillings at age 36, although I did have braces. My husband was not so lucky and has had plenty of dental work. Interesting thing is that my older son has had lots of cavities, and his younger brother (2 years younger) has only had 1. His jaw formation is great (looks like no braces needed), whereas older son has already had orthodontal work, and will likely need a little more (he's 11). Did I mention younger son is celiac (both are gluten free), and has hashi's? And I tandem nursed when he was born... Also, younger son has a real innate sense of foods that he should not eat. Turns out a lot of the foods he refuses he is allergic to (we did intradermal allergy testing) - grapes, strawberries, apples, most fruits, some veggies, etc. And over the past few years he's really taken to eating _seafood_, almost daily, whereas older son did not, and does not feel his sensitivities the way younger one does.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-24T21:13:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-60019>

I took my youngest (3.5) to our WAPF dentist this morning for sedation dentistry. She has cavities in all 4 of her back molars (which is devastating to me, but regardless, they need to be filled). She is very "willful," for lack of a better word. When she wants something (or when she doesn't), there is no level of redirection, that will change her perspective. She responds well by learning from her own past experiences (ie. learning the hard way).

I had already spent time explaining to her what was going to happen at the dentist's office. She took the tranquilizing med willingly, and it did sedate her to a certain extent, but not enough. She was happy to sit in the chair, and still chattering on about the blanket, and the TV, and the room, etc! With my older son (who is very much like her - moreso really!), the same med put him pretty much out, and he had no memory of the procedure. She did great for the novacaine shots, but then got very unsettled about the strange feeling of a numb mouth. Unsettled turned to panic after a minute or so of the block in her mouth to hold it open. The dentist had already started to drill one of the cavities, so it took 3 of us keep her still so she could finish the one cavity. Never mind the other ones...

It was not a good morning, to say the least. We will go back in 3 weeks, with a slightly higher dose of tranquilizer, and I'm crossing my fingers it will be enough. In the mean time, I would like to establish a one mile radius around my home - call it the "sugar-free" zone. Given my children eat a pretty solid diet, but that their father has bad teeth, what else can I do for them that might reduce the cavity load? It really is devastating to me (I have none), and disheartening to know their teeth are still rotting...

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-24T21:27:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-60021>

As I recall the conversation with the dentist going, some children will respond to certain sedatives by calming, some by excitation, and some by no notable change. And you can't predict which ones wiill be which. When we were still in the days of many cavities in every child, I just went with the papoose board, with me by their sides, calming them & crying along with them.

When we switched to a WAPF diet, though, all that went away. All the cavities stopped. But it's very possible that it stopped so quickly for my children b/c they may have just happened to have had guts that were ready, or some such. BTW, no one in either side of the family had bad teeth as children, but all of my children did. Heredity plays little role, IMO. Epigenetics is everything. I think that continued cavities in the face of plenty of butter, fCLO, liver, and other K2 sources might imply a malabsorption problem, especially with fat?

BTW, the worse my kids' teeth got, the more I had to eliminate grains, concentrated fruit sources, etc. Don't know if you've done that yet, but nutrient dense is super important. IMO, sugar's biggest contribution to cavities is simply taking the place of the things you need to be eating.

She was happy to sit in the chair, and still chattering on about the blanket, and the TV, and the room, etc! With my older son (who is very much like her - moreso really!), the same med put him pretty much out, and he had no memory of the procedure. She did great for the novacaine shots, but then got very unsettled about the strange feeling of a numb mouth. Unsettled turned to panic after a minute or so of the block in her mouth to hold it open. The dentist had already started to drill one of the cavities, so it took 3 of us keep her still so she could finish the one cavity. Never mind the other ones...
>

> It was not a good morning, to say the least. We will go back in 3 weeks, with a slightly higher dose of tranquilizer, and I'm crossing my fingers it will be enough. In the mean time, I would like to establish a one mile radius around my home - call it the "sugar-free" zone. Given my children eat a pretty solid diet, but that their father has bad teeth, what else can I do for them that might reduce the cavity load? It really is devastating to me (I have none), and disheartening to know their teeth are still rotting...

Why do you need to get them filled?

I am in Finland so it might be different here.. but the dentist of my sister child found lots of cavities in her teeth (I think it was around 10 or so!) when she was around 5 yo or so. He said he will not fill them yet but let her try to suck on xylitol bonbons after every meal. They was her milch teeth that was affected so he did not see too much need to fill them if they caused no pain and the hole was not too huge. Heidi (the name of the girl) sucked on xylitol pills for about half a year and went to the dentist again. All holes were "hardened" as the dentist said and needed no threatment anymore.

I myself have some huge holes according to my dentist (about two years ago.. I was not by the dentist after). I am not filling them. I live right instead. I have had no troubles whatsoever. I believe as long as the tooth is alive there can happend a recovery, a "hardening" of the karies. I want to have as little poison in my mouth as possible. And the fillings are not really healthy, also not the stuff they put on to make the filling hold on the tooth.

If it were my child I would let her suck on xylitol often and give her proper foods and not worry anymore... she will get new, strong teeths coming out soon!

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:35:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-60142>

For me, even 1/2 svg wheat/day was enough to keep me in health disarray. Everything changed when I dropped that half serving.

For my kids, fCLO saved their teeth. I couldn't have done it without it.

My second child was one of those resist-to-the-end kind. That's why I didn't go with sedative for him. If he was going to thrash & scream the whole time, anyway, I didn't want the additional risk of the drug.

It's a whole load of terribly, agonizing decisions for a parent to make, and I don't envy anyone currently in that position.

BTW, a British study found no benefit whatsoever to filling deciduous teeth if the child was not in pain. My kids all have loads of half-decayed, but totally frozen and no longer decaying or hurting teeth that will stay in their mouths till they fall out. Again, a very personal & agonizing decision that I have no intention of judging any other parent on. It's HARD to go against a care provider, period, and you have no guarantee of being right either way.

### CoolSwede — 2014-06-09T13:56:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-130262>

So, here I am bumping an old thread for my own good:

Three years ago I managed to heal an old cavity in one of my upper teeth which had been lingering for a number of years. This was when I had started vit D supplementation. I was quite happy with the bewildered look my dentist gave me when he saw that I had managed to heal that cavity using low-carb and vit D!

Fost forward until two months ago, when my dental hygienist noticed that the cavity was back with a vengeance. Of course I was very disappointed believing that that very cavity was a thing of the past. I talked to my dentist and he agreed that we could wait and see. Unluckily now the tooth has started aching slightly so: enter panic!

Do you have any suggestions about how I should do? Of course I would like to heal the cavity naturally as I did three years ago, but at the same time, I do not want to risk needing a root canal.

Supplements:

1\. Calcium 450 mg daily (due to very low whole blood calcium in latest lab).
2\. Magnesium 300 mg daily (midrange whole blood mag in latest lab).
3\. Thorne K2-MK4 5 mg (5 drops) daily. Started 2 months ago.
4\. Vitamin D3. 7500 IU daily. (205 nmol/l in latest lab).

Remarks:

1\. My whole blood vit A is in the middle of the range. Should I supplement cod liver oil or retinol anyway?
2\. My HDL has dropped to a scaringly low 42. Has my gut becoming a sieve affected my oral health?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/teeth-thread-add-all-things-teeth.2600/post-232479>

Found this thread after bad news from my dentist - I have a few cavities and lots of gum recession. This thread has mostly focused on cavities, but I am sure that gum recession is part of the same process - low redox potential.

I've read this post: [https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/quantum-dentistry-jack-kruse](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/quantum-dentistry-jack-kruse)
So I'm sure that my #1 priority has to be the Redox Rx, and that this should clear up the recession AND cavities.

I was wondering if some fellow mitochondriacs would weigh-in on the following questions. I think I'm on the right track, but wanted to throw these ideas out there and see what other people think/have to add to them.
1\. Lots of dentists have told me that gum recession is due to tooth grinding. If weight-bearing exercise in the earth's magnetic field results in strengthening/increasing bone density, then why would tooth grinding cause generalized gum recession and then bone loss (my jaw bone loss is at 20% already, but I'm only 29)? I suspect tooth grinding is the effect of a quantum process, but is being labeled the cause by modern dentistry.
2\. If our teeth are designed to fit together, how does a disrupted bite alter the quantum mechanics of our teeth, resulting in dental disease of all kinds?
3\. Is gum recession the result of decreased DC current through the tooth, the gum, and the bone beneath it?
4\. If yes, then is the nerve of the tooth meant to energize the perineural water to maintain gum health?
5\. Every dentist I've spoken to has said that gum cannot regenerate - what if the problem is the tooth and its quantum health, rather than the gum, and the gum isn't regenerating because the tooth isn't strong enough? Could we cure gum disease by improving the DC electric current of the tooth?
6\. While we're busy straightening everyone's teeth, are we missing the actual, quantum disease process? Could our modern epidemic of tooth decay be worsened by our obsession with cosmetic dentistry?

Looking forward to your thoughts and advice, fellow mitochondriacs!

palate expander, wisdom teeth removed, midline fracture + scar tissue above front teeth. cranial nerves and occipital region hyper-tonic.

A lot of talk about ”silent infection” in wisdom tooth sockets and meridians associated with those regions of the mouth compromised post surgery…

wisdom teeth taken out in September 2017, in November, presented with the start of a chronic “stye“ issue. Massive staph like infection in the meibiomian glands, internal structures of lower lids. Oil thick like margarine.

Is this a melanin issue in the facial structures? If silent infection of the left over bone and ligament in the wisdom tooth sockets is simmering on low, how can melanin and this perspective local region of the mouth be restored?

### Glamazon — 2012-07-21T21:03:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40049>

[http://youtu.be/u2V0vOFexY4\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;u2V0vOFexY4\]http://youtu.be/u2V0vOFexY4\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;u2V0vOFexY4) A TED talk: _Psychopathic killers are the basis for some must-watch TV, but what really makes them tick? Neuroscientist Jim Fallon talks about brain scans and genetic analysis that may uncover the rotten wiring in the nature (and nurture) of murderers. In a too-strange-for-fiction twist, he shares a fascinating family history that makes his work chillingly personal._

I live about 5 miles from the theater where the killings took place last night. I can't help but think about epigenetic causes like industrial food and toxic environment on what seems to be a rise of psychopathic serial killers in contemporary times.

The killer here looks like he came from a good background. He was here in Denver studying for his PhD in neurosciences. No surface indicators for CW to grab on to give context for this craziness.

Would love to look deeper into his lifestyle.

You know Glamazon, that was the very first thing I thought of when this story broke. This kid apparently was highly intelligent, grew up in a nice environment, and had everything in the world at his disposal. But I, too, wondered just how much more broken he became due to his (I'm certain) SAD diet. He was a typical kid of the 1990's, consuming (I bet) Cheerios, juice boxes, and microwave pizza roll-ups. I'm sure his mom thought she was doing the best she could, as she is a nurse, with all her CW knowledge, so she probably "knows" what are good things to feed him. And he's not overweight, and he was an athlete (track & soccer), so by all accounts, he looked like the picture of health.

I thought of Dr. Terry Wahls' TED talk and it really made me think....

His mother, a psychiatric nurse, immediately knew it was him when the police called her. Had she truly understood the epi-paleo template, could it have saved her son?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-23T04:47:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40339>

Diet is huge, but it is not the only epigenetic switch. Clearly, we know about light and temperature. But the epigenetic literature is FULL of data on the relational aspects that cause epigenetic activation. Huge parts of the brain are wired up before conscious memory is on board, thus the preverbal time period is huge for signaling. Just because the media dubs someone 'from a good home' means about as much as them saying 'they ate a healthy diet.'

lately, I have been doing a lot of psychiatric patient reviews. No where, in any of the hundreds of pages of documentation that I reviewed in the last 6 months, has anyone once mentioned circadian rhthyms, diet, or run some of Dr. K's lab panels. It is scary.

Anecdotally, I can tell you that schizophrenia is showing up in younger and younger children. So are suicidal and homicidal thoughts and intents, violence, and explosive behavior. Kids are showing up with bipolar mania and depression. You also see incidences of genetic components being documented too for these psychotic behaviors. We even have 4 year olds that try to kill themselves. Makes me sad.

We even have 4 year olds that try to kill themselves. Makes me sad.

Wow. This is actually scary.

I watched a program about a couple with a schizophrenic child, maybe 5 or 6 years old. They had another child, and the schizophrenic one was so manic that the couple had to get two apartments in the same complex and switch nights, separating the children, for the sake and safety of their other child.

I was at my friend's house with her 3 year-old the other night. Aside from lack of discipline, this child was up, down, cranky, mad, happy, playful, and downright mean, all within a few hours. Now I realize she's 3, but this was consant mood changes by the minute. What did she eat while I was there...oh let me see...Sprite, SugarBabies, chips with tons of ketchup, rice, cake, whipped cream. Literally, that was what she ate. Now, her mother made beef & chicken fajitas, including veggies, guacamole, and we had berries for dessert. But the little girl only ate the junk, which apparently was fine with mom, 'cause hey, at least she's eating.

### kathylu — 2012-07-24T03:09:55+0300

> **Cavemam;40720 wrote:** ugh, this one kills me. . ."but this is all he will eat". CW has made mothers think if there child goes a few hours without food they might just keel over.

Yeah, I get a lot of criticism for refusing to feed my kid junk food. Of course, he occasionally manages to slip some in. If he refuses to eat, I simply say "ok". If he tells me he is hungry later, then I warm up dinner for him. I've actually had moms say to me "Oh, I could never put my child to bed without dinner!". To which I reply "Why not? It's his choice...there's good food here if he wants it. It won't hurt him to skip a meal either, so I don't worry. He'll just have an extra egg in the morning..."

### BJK77 — 2012-07-24T03:49:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40558>

My son just went to bed hungry and I don't feel bad at all. Does that make me a bad mom? Quite the contrary! I've learned to take a hard stance on food issues. I'll admit to caving in a bit when we're on vacation and under the influence of others (we travel with my parents, what can I say?!?!), but at home there is no such thing as junk food and no snacking at all these days.

The kids get 3 solid, nutrient dense meals per day and they either eat them or don't - their choice. I've had people tell me everything from "you're starving them" to "they're only kids once. let them enjoy it." Yes, I know people think I'm nothing more than a healthnut, but I really don't care. I'm going to do whatever it takes to keep my kids healthy. I see kids constantly who are so strung out on sugar and grains it's unbelievably sad. Kids get put on dangerous drugs everyday because parents are too ignorant to even consider that diet could play a role in how a body functions. It's so sad.

### Souldanzer — 2012-07-24T04:22:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epigenetics-and-psychopathic-killers.2608/post-40564>

Ok, count me in.....

> The orbitofrontal cortex and the serial killer
>
> According to Portales, "what lies behind the eyes" of the serial killer "is the key to his psychopathy". The orbitofrontal cortex is the neural region directly behind the eyes and is the last to develop. Portales quotes authoritative research that, due to childhood traumatic experiences, in some individuals the ortbitofrontal cortex - the tool judgment and decision making - may never completely develop: "individuals who have had damage to \[this\] area can observe social situations, but fail to respond to these situations in an appropriate manner."
>
>
> Dr K
>
> .....you likely have bundle damage in your orbital frontal lobes.

# Thread: G'day mates!  New Member In Corpus Christi, Texas

### LouTheNavi — 2012-07-22T07:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gday-mates-new-member-in-corpus-christi-texas.2613/post-40186>

G'day mates!

I am Lou living in Corpus Christi, Texas. I have been in the Telomere Modification Program since February 2008.

Before I started the program regimen I weighed over 147 kg. Now I weigh 95 kg. The last time I weighed 95 kg was in 1978. My biological age as of 20 July 2012 is 48 years even though my chronological age is 61.

Also prior to starting I had gout and asthma. I had to take 2 medications for gout and also 2 for asthma. Now both conditions are gone and I don't need to take medication for either one.

Looking forward to hearing how others are doing...

Cheers!

# Thread: ED gene
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-07-22T18:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/post-40225>

[http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1095161/The\_5HTTLPR\_polymorphism\_and\_eating\_disorders\_A\_metaanalysis.html#at\_pco=cfd-1.0](http://http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1095161/The_5HTTLPR_polymorphism_and_eating_disorders_A_metaanalysis.html#at_pco=cfd-1.0)

The 5-HTTLPR polymorphism and eating disorders: A meta-analysis

> Eating disorders are influenced by both environmental factors and genes. The 5-HTTLPR polymorphism of serotonin transporter gene has been suggested as a good candidate. This meta-analysis was undertaken: (1) to investigate the association between 5-HTTLPR and eating disorders considered as a whole, including anorexia (AN), bulimia (BN), and binge eating disorder (BED); to extend recently reported findings on the association between 5-HTTLPR and AN-BN.PubMed, ISI, and PsycINFO databases were searched for studies published until October 29. Fifteen studies have been included. Data were analyzed with the Cochrane Collaboration Review Manager Software. Quality of studies and publication bias were assessed.An association between S allele and eating disorders, in particular AN, has been found.To be carrier of the 5-HTTLPR S allele seem to represent a risk factor for eating disorders, especially for AN. However, considering the reported high between-study heterogeneity, future studies should focus on more homogeneous endophenotype.

lots of other articles on the link

### chocolate — 2012-07-22T18:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ed-gene.2617/post-40226>

[http://www.medical-hypotheses.com/article/S0306-9877%2812%2900297-6/abstract](http://http://www.medical-hypotheses.com/article/S0306-9877%2812%2900297-6/abstract)

> Abstract
>
>
>
> Polyunsaturated essential fatty acids (PUFAs) play a pivotal role in mediating cognitive, learning, and memory functions. We propose that PUFAs directly affect the neuronal membrane. PUFAs serve to stabilize and protect the structure and functions of the neuronal membrane. PUFAs exert many effects on the brain with respect to physiology, brain biochemistry, and disorders of the central nervous system. Many of these functions have effects at the cognitive level. This summary demonstrates that a deficiency in brain PUFAs will lead to cognitive deficits, while supplementation of PUFAs can rehabilitate cognitive deficits, as manifested in attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, stress/anxiety, and aging.

We already knew this. Just published the 17th.

### MJ* — 2012-07-23T19:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40419>

[From this abstract:](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9501377)

"Viruses are the most significant cause of shellfish-associated disease"

Since viruses cause epigenetic change, and shellfish carry viruses, will eating more shellfish also cause epigenetic change in us? Will it make us sick or smarter or both? This is just a curious question I've been considering, is there an expense to eating so much shellfish?

### kathylu — 2012-07-24T06:57:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40602>

Yes, viruses often insert sequences in our DNA upon infection. Often it is a random event and no harm comes of it, but sometimes viral sequences can activate other genes we possess. Think oncogene activation, which may lead to cancer, especially lymphomas, leukemias and sarcomas. Even though it can be damaging for the individual, for a mutation of this type to be passed on, the viral gene or gene fragment must be inserted into germ cells (eggs and sperm) then the host must reproduce for it to be stably maintained. Viral gene sequences can also "jump" to other locations taking host cell fragments with them and reinsert themselves. So gene mixing is not infrequent. There is quite a bit of evidence that this does occur. This is certainly one way that evolution occurs. Does it mean you shouldn't eat seafood? Not at all...viruses are everywhere, you can't avoid them. I'd be more worried about acute illness from a virus than genetic or epigenetic change.

[http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2010/01/bornavirus-in-human-dna/](http://http://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2010/01/bornavirus-in-human-dna/)

### MJ* — 2012-07-24T16:39:46+0300

Even though it can be damaging for the individual, for a mutation of this type to be passed on, the viral gene or gene fragment must be inserted into germ cells (eggs and sperm) then the host must reproduce for it to be stably maintained. Viral gene sequences can also "jump" to other locations taking host cell fragments with them and reinsert themselves. So gene mixing is not infrequent. There is quite a bit of evidence that this does occur. This is certainly one way that evolution occurs. Does it mean you shouldn't eat seafood? Interesting article thanks Kathy...germ cell insertion is a factor for anyone of reproductive age when considering all their habits. It is becoming abundantly clear from all that we are learning here that what we do, what our grandparents have done, in terms of what we consume, and are exposed to, and maybe even our physicality, directly effects the health of our offspring. For us as individuals, I agree acute illness is a concern with especially the consumption of raw or tainted seafood. Considering that there is no fossil evidence of the changes from ape to man, Dr. Kruse has contended that epigenetic changes to the individual matter - the speed of which allowed for encephalation of our brains. What we do and are exposed to matters and directly effects our own health and development. I agree it's hard to avoid viruses since they are everywhere, the point of this was that the abstract of this article shows that viruses are especially abundant in seafood. Therefore, by consuming seafood in abundance we are being exposed to more viruses as a result. We have no way of knowing what the exposure to viruses of today will bring tomorrow in terms of epigenetics. My takeaway LoL is this confirms what I'm already doing, cooking my seafood, for more than one reason.
