### kathylu — 2012-07-24T17:38:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-40689>

Well, the two major types of immunity are humoral (antibodies) and cellular (NK, T cells, eosinophils, phagocytes, etc.). I don't think infection by a virus found in seafood would confer any humoral protection against the diseases you mentioned...unless you ate seafood containing Ebola (incredibly unlikely since it's a primate virus) and managed to survive long enough to form antibodies. HIV is a special case, since one of its unique features is its ability to escape a humoral response. Now you might be on to something if the infective agents in seafood were able to activate cellular immunity...which would also provide some nonspecific protection. That is...if you have lots of NK and T cells they may attack many different viral or bacterial agents. In fact, one of the diagnostic criteria for AIDS is a drop in T cell level coinciding with a rise in viral titer.

The bottom line for me...if you cook your seafood, I think any risks are truly negligible. If you eat raw seafood, the risk is higher, so source (clean water) and time of year (more infections occur in war months) becomes important and freezing certainly helps. Can you get sick from raw seafood? Yes. Does it happen frequently? Not really, though outbreaks are certainly reported every year. If you are immunocompromised, cook your seafood. If you are not, use common sense and don't complain if it does happen...you knew the risks. I still eat raw seafood...sushi/sashimi and raw clams/oysters occasionally.

### kathylu — 2012-07-26T18:01:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-41206>

> **Nonchalant;41024 wrote:** So our DNA is passively receiving the sequences? Or does our immune system secret them away there?

Yes to all of that. There are thousands of viruses that are capable of integration into human DNA, many have cleverly made it part of their life cycle. Most, but not all are RNA viruses. Think herpes (DNA) virus that infects, an RNA intermediate integrates into neurons and may lay dormant for many years before being excised and starting viral replication (thus becoming infectious) again. There are thousands of HERV's (human endogenous retroviruses)...they make up roughly 8% of our genome. After integration many viral sequences are made "silent" by host cell processes (methylation, deletional recombination, histone deacetylation, etc), but as Dr. K has pointed out, many sequences have radically modified our epigenetics. HERV sequences can act as promoters and enhancers, turning our genes on and off. They are implicated in multiple forms of cancer and in autoimmune disease.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-08-04T08:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seafood-and-viruses.2628/post-42817>

How likely is it that our gut flora would incorporate the bits of genetic material from the large amount of viruses in seafood? Perhaps the genetic material in seafood is more likely to cause changes in our gut symbionts first.

# Thread: Weston Price on Fish and Sea Food
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-07-23T23:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40484>

Jack mentioned Price in a recent blog post. Strangely, just before I'd got to that point I found comments of Price's coming into my head.

It's interesting the importance he sees in the maritime element in the Hebridean Islanders' diet. Informally, I believe Price was known for recommending seafood to inquirers looking for dietary advice as one of the most nutritious foods one could eat.

He also mentions fish and shellfish when discussing Polynesian Islanders -- still today very large and healthy people with a reputation at rugby that belies the size of their populations. (Genetic momentum?)

And there are some interesting comments with reference to African tribes living largely on fish:

> The Government of Kenya has for several years sponsored an athletic contest among the various tribes, the test being one of strength for which they use a tug-of-war. One particular tribe has carried off the trophy repeatedly. This tribe resides on the east coast of Lake Victoria and lives very largely on fish. The members are powerful athletes and wonderful swimmers. They are said not to have been conquered in warfare when they could take the warfare to the water. One of their methods is to swim under water to the enemy's fleet and scuttle their boats. They fight with spears under water with marvelous skill. **Their physiques are magnificent. In a group of 190 boys who had been gathered into a government school near the east coast of Lake Victoria **only one boy was found with dental caries, and two of his teeth had been affected.
>
>
> [http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price9.html](http://http://www.journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price9.html)
>
>
>
> In the same chapter Price also has this interesting note on the Dinka:
>
>
>
> Dinkas, Jebelein, Sudan. This tribe lives on the Nile. Its members are not as tall as the Neurs. They are physically better proportioned and have greater strength. They use fish from the Nile and cereals for their diet. They decorate their bodies profusely with scars.****

****

The Nuer (I take it that's who he means by "Neurs") are cattle herders, living at that time on mostly milk, blood, and meat. It's interesting that these people should be a bit taller than the fish-eaters, but not so well-proportioned or strong.

Perhaps most striking is what's said about the Maori of New Zealand, however. In chapter 15, Characteristics of primitive and modernized dietaries, we have:

The native Maori in New Zealand, used large quantities of foods from the sea, wherever these were available.** Even in the inland food depots, mutton birds were still available in large quantities. These birds were captured just before they left the nests. They developed in the rockeries about the coast, chiefly on the extreme southern coast of the South Island. At this stage, the flesh is very tender and very fat from the gorging that has been provided by their parent. The value of this food for the treatment of tuberculosis was being heralded quite widely in both Australia and New Zealand. In the primitive state of the islands large quantities of land birds were available and because of the fertility of the soil and favorable climate, vegetables and fruits grew abundantly in the wild. Large quantities of fern root were used. Where groups of the Maori race were found isolated sufficiently from contact with modern civilization and its foods to be dependent largely on the native foods, they selected with precision certain shell-fish because of their unique nutritive value**.

A splendid illustration of the primitive Maori instinct or wisdom regarding the value of sea foods was shown in an experience we had while making examinations in a native school on the east coast of the North Islands. I was impressed with the fact that the children in the school gave very little evidence of having active dental caries. I asked the teacher what the children brought from their homes to eat at their midday lunch, since most of them had to come too great a distance to return at noon. I was told that they brought no lunch but that when school was dismissed at noon the children rushed for the beach where, while part of the group prepared bonfires, the others stripped and dived into the sea, and brought up a large species of lobster. The lobsters were promptly roasted on the coals and devoured with great relish. Other sea foods are pictured in Fig. 74.

Chapter 12 is devoted to the Maori -- "Isolated and modernized New Zealand Maori".

Price notes that an earlier researcher had found that:

> the incidence of caries in the Maori \[was\] 1.2 per cent in a total of 326 skulls. This is lower even than the Esquimaux, and **shows the Maori to have been the most immune race to caries, for which statistics are available.
>
>
> [http://journeytoforever.org/farm\_library/price/price12.html](http://http://journeytoforever.org/farm_library/price/price12.html)
>
>
>
> He describes the unmodernized Maori as being, physically, "on a pedestal of perfection".
>
>
>
> He concludes:
>
>
>
> The Maori race developed a knowledge of Nature's laws and adopted a system of living in harmony with those laws to so high a degree that they were able to build what was reported by early scientists to be the most physically perfect race living on the face of the earth.**

So eat up.

"What a man! What a brain! And all this is due to fish you say?"

1 minute 40 seconds

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NjbR73qSukY\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;NjbR73qSukY\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NjbR73qSukY\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;NjbR73qSukY)

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T00:54:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40501>

"Where groups of the Maori race were found isolated sufficiently from contact with modern civilization and its foods to be dependent largely on the native foods, they selected with precision certain shell-fish because of their unique nutritive value."

I'll bet those shellfish tasted best to them. The Maori knew that those shellfish were most satisfying.

### vlynnb — 2012-07-24T04:43:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40575>

So interesting. My daughter is married to 1/4 Maori. He is in very good physical shape, a little shorter than most but very muscular. He and his father, who was a professional Rugby player, love junk food and are still in good shape, apparently. But what do they crave, even in the inter-mountain west? Fish, fish, and more fish. His dad loves to splurge on shrimp for the barby. I think I will keep encouraging the fishing trips for steel-head and salmon that they live for! Maybe more and more fish will someday alter their taste for junk food. I am afraid that is unrealistic though.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-24T05:14:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40584>

You have to admit, we excel at making empty food taste fabulous. Pinnacle of human achievement.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-24T07:53:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40611>

Unfortunately, the SAD diet is alive and well here in NZ. Maori and Pacific Islanders in NZ are over-represented in every health risk category, including obesity and all the other diseases of modern life. You just can't substitute fried fish and chips for kai moana (the Maori term for seafood) and not flip epigenetic switches in the wrong direction. And, of course, our Western doctors deal with their obesity and health issues by pushing exercise, low fat, high carb diets on them (and all the rest of us). What remains, however, is a devotion to the land and the sea. Maori, like Native Americans, do not believe anyone owns the water but believe it is a human right to have access to it and that we are caretakers of the land. I hope that the epi-paleo information can take hold in the Maori community... we have a bicultural society here (not in name only) and the influence could be huge.

Uncle Sam will never admit that the Indians were tall, lean and healthy just two generations ago. If ever someone wanted proof that humans weren't designed to eat a grain-based diet, look at the American Indian population-almost all of them are battling overweight, diabetes, and heart disease.

>
>

> Price notes:
>
>
>
> The rapid degeneration of the Australian Aborigines after the adoption of the government's modern foods provides a demonstration that should be infinitely more convincing than animal experimentation. It should be a matter not only of concern but deep alarm that human beings can degenerate physically so rapidly by the use of a certain type of nutrition, particularly the dietary products used so generally by modern civilization.

Whenever I think about these kinds of issues I find it frightening that Price can have been documenting all this so carefully and in such detail, and yet everyone has gone on in much the same way heading for the edge of the cliff. It's truly mind-boggling.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-24T14:22:22+0300

That is the definition of an environmental mismatch where you supposedly "smart brain" intellectualizes a concept and screws it up over your gut instinct. Inner guidance should come through our feelings and body wisdom and not via intellectual understanding. Pay attention to your inner guru. When it speaks to you, you need to listen intently. The mind can no longer be thought of confined to the skull. It resides in every cell of our body. Every thought is a biochemical equivalent.

I was irritated and decided to wait a bit, then dive into some cerviche. Then I realized I wasn't hungry any more. I looked up, and saw it was too late to eat, so I didn't. I went to bed after another hour.

This is very different from my behavior just a few months ago, when Candida or whatever was influencing my meals. Eating lots of seafood seems to be in the process of redefining my behaviors and tastes, and thoughts.

### Michael — 2012-07-24T15:51:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weston-price-on-fish-and-sea-food.2632/post-40666>

> **Jack Kruse;40825 wrote:** That is the definition of an environmental mismatch where you supposedly "smart brain" intellectualizes a concept and screws it up over your gut instinct. Inner guidance should come through our feelings and body wisdom and not via intellectual understanding. Pay attention to your inner guru. When it speaks to you, you need to listen intently. The mind can no longer be thought of confined to the skull. It resides in every cell of our body. Every thought is a biochemical equivalent.

IOW, we should also be listening to the Zen/Ch'an Buddhists (and perhaps the Taoists which is probably where this type of thinking made its way into Chinese Buddhism from). The point of those riddles, the koan, I understand, is not "smart alec" responses as Westerners tend to believe -- what "Zen" often seems to mean in the mouth of a Westerner. The point is that you _can't solve them. You think and think over the koan with your analytical mind until you have to give up. Sooner or later, and this seems to be influenced by your meditation practice, an intuitive answer will present itself to you.

It's not the answer itself that matters. So buying something like _The Gateless Gate or The Blue Cliff Record_ and reading some of the traditional koans, responses, and commentary on them is beside the point. It's the process. And the "sitting"_ of course.

I'm sure I should develop my intuitive side more.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T01:26:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs.2634/post-40509>

Your thyroid numbers suck! But you know that....

TSH - too high

FT3 - too low (s/b around 4.0-4.2)

FT4 - too low (s/b at least 3.0 but really higher)

rT3 ratio - too low

You are converting your FT4 to rT3 and NOT FT3. I can't remember, of course, do you have adrenal problems? Your iron looks a little weak to me which is another reason our bodies want to make rT3 instead of FT3. IIRC, you want the iron or ferritin to be near 100 and definitely need your saturation to be around 35%.

WOW, Vitamin B12 is great! But your preg and DHEA both look low. DHEA should be around 250-380 according to Dr. Dzugan. Preg s/b 200-230. Aren't you taking those?

> **Destiny;40700 wrote:** Hi Patty! Yes, I know the thyroid is not great. I have a bit of adrenal problem since it is OK in the morning, low at noon and evening and high at night.

### Destiny — 2012-08-05T14:01:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-labs.2634/post-43026>

So I had my consult and it was great. It gave me a clear direction on what to do first, second, etc. The good news is that my problems are straightforward and fixable.

I need to find a doctor who will prescribe T3. I will try my GP first. Then, my compounding pharmacy is next - to give me a few doctors who prescribe compounded T3.

After that, I will have to concentrate on getting more progesterone, estrogen and testosterone. I am presently on BHRT - progesterone and estrogen but it is not enough. I am seeing my Obgyn next week. If she is not open to that, I will need to find a new one.

I could also benefit from Metformin and Cycloset but that would come after the addressing the first two big issues - thyroid and then my female hormones.

Another unexpected benefit of the consult was that my husband expressed an interest in getting his own labs.

### ealachan — 2012-07-24T20:38:11+0300

signs I have:

hair and nails greatly improved and growing super fast

feet used to be super rough / dry all the time, are now smooth as a baby's backside

skin is clearer, with better coloring

generally calmer, less prone to anxiety attacks

thinking far more clearly (though my posts don't always show it...haha)

libido has returned from a very long hiatus

no carb cravings - haven't TRULY craved much of anything besides shrimp and steak for months now

can easily get by on two meals a day most days

usually sleep very well now (used to wake up a lot in the night, tossing and turning)

wake up before alarm most days, able to get up and get going with much more ease than previously

losing weight, though not rapidly by any means

> Here is what Dr. K says in the "My Leptin Perscription" about knowing when you are LS:
>
>
>
> A. Men will notice quick weight loss.
>
> B. Women will notice mood changes first (calmer/sleepy) and their sleep will improve. Their clothes will fit differently but weight may not change drastically initially because of effects on the pituitary. This will change too if they continue moving forward.
>
> C. You will notice a change in your sweating pattern.
>
> D. You will notice you have better recovery from exercise and your energy levels seem to have risen.
>
> E. Your hunger is gone and so are your cravings.
>
> F. When you awaken you will feel very refreshed like you slept well.
>
> Generally when the signs are all present, I then really push HIIT exercise with heavy weights.
>
>
>
> 6\. (QUILT SURVIVABILITY) = (Total Energy – Growth and immunity expense) X (RESOURCES) X (efficiency) X (awareness of our environment).
>
> Stated in levee form where:
>
> Cell longevity = LS – IGF-1 + immunity X Food Quality X leakiness of Mitochondria X environmental cues

Sounds like to me you most definitely are LS.

# Thread: Circadian T3 Method for Healing Adrenals
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 30
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-07-24T16:25:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40671>

[http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/t3-circadian-method-for-adrenals/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/t3-circadian-method-for-adrenals/)

Has anybody tried this method of dosing T3 (or Armour) early in the AM? This guy says most cortisol for the day is made in the last 4 hours of sleep. He started taking T3 in those hours (you have to play with the timing and dosing) to produce a better adrenal response (measured by blood pressure, heart rate, and temperatures). People are no longer needing their hydrocortisone as a result...

I am thinking of trying it but I am hesitant because I will have a hard time gonig back to sleep after waking to take the dose. And especially because of all that Jack says about the greatness of restful sleep, etc, which I finally seem to be getting. So, does the potential benefit out weigh the cost?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-24T18:13:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/circadian-t3-method-for-healing-adrenals.2649/post-40698>

I've been taking my meds this way since the early part of this year. I tried it with the natural thyroid meds (NDT) and it made an immediate difference with my energy upon waking; I had a lot more! I am now on T3 and still doing it. It takes some work, with monitoring your heart rate, BP, temp, etc. I personally didn't have any problem going back to sleep with either the NDT or T3, but I know some people do. Get Paul's book and read on how he did it so you will know all the details of the program. I really like it and so do mnay others but it's not just a "take a pill" type program. Also it doesn't interfere with the BAB in the morning!!!

The only way to know if it will interfere with you falling back to sleep is to try it. Some people just cannot do it because it keeps them awake. I am quite a light sleeper and surprisingly I barely open my eyes when I take the pills and I am back to sleep almost immediately.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-07-25T20:47:18+0300

When you start the T3 CM method you should take the dose about two hours before you usually wake up. You adjust the timing back as well as the dose, as you see how your body reacts. There is a lot too this method but I think you took it too early to start. Also because of the fillers in Armour you should break it up, chew it if you will, before ingesting. It helps with the absorption. I personally do not like the brand Armour and much prefer NP Thyroid by Acella for a natural product.

### JackStapleton — 2012-09-23T16:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/for-those-that-count-grams-of-protein-for-bab-best-practice.2650/post-53584>

I was simply eating a REALLY BIG breakfast and assumed I had it covered. I went and looked it up this morning. Looks like I was wrong. An egg has 6 grams of protein. Two slices of bacon have 3. So if I ate **6 eggs and 6** slices of bacon I would only get to 45 grams of protein, not enough if 50 grams is the goal.

Apparently my idea of big was not nearly big enough. Are people really eating more than 6 eggs and 6 slices of bacon for breakfast??? Wow.

I thought I was already pigging out with 4 eggs and 5 slices of bacon, which seems already double the "norm". So I added a cup of chili. Still not at 50 grams. Only 47.

# Thread: How far down the food chain did we go for our big brains?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MJ* — 2012-07-24T19:07:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-40717>

Here is a very interesting [article](http://http://www.algaeindustrymagazine.com/algae-101-part-eleven-did-algae-make-us-human/) on alge and it's potential role in encephalation. Could alge be something we should include in our diets? Is anyone supplementing with Spirulina?

"Down the food chain:

Rather than moving up the food chain to game meat, early hominoidsâ€™ first step may instead have been down the food chain when they ingested algae in their drinking water. Consuming algae may have been intentional but more likely was incidental because the tiny algal cells were visible only in the sense that they turned the water slightly green. The lakes and wetlands in the Rift Valley where humans developed larger brains are home to some of the oldest lakes and wetlands on Earth that produce plentiful natural stands of the high protein and nutrient rich spirulina algae. Spirulina is the best selling algal nutritive supplement on the market today because it provides a complete set of essential nutrients. A hominoid tribe on the lee side of an algae lake may have ingested several grams of algae daily in their drinking water. These few grams of algae would not have provided sufficient roughage or protein for a full diet. Algae would have acted as a natural food supplement to supply the essential nutrients, vitamins and antioxidants that provided the green spark for encephalation."

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-07-25T18:08:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41014>

Very high in protein...most of the lipid content is GLA.

DHA content?

### David — 2012-07-25T23:13:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41086>

WiKiPedia sez: "Spirulina's lipid content is about 7% by weight, and is rich in gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), and also provides alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), linoleic acid (LA), stearidonic acid (SDA), eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) and arachidonic acid (AA)."

### WannaBeOptimal — 2012-07-26T01:13:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41108>

Looks like DHA is pretty far down on the totem pole.

### chocolate — 2012-07-26T03:08:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-far-down-the-food-chain-did-we-go-for-our-big-brains.2654/post-41131>

I have some and take it if it crosses my path. I don't take it regularly.

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-07-24T19:57:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40749>

The purpose of this thread is to have a consolidated place to discuss our attempts to optimize methlyation and related biochemical pathways. Many of us have done the 23andMe test and/or methlyation panels and compared the results to information available on-line based on the work of Dr. Amy Yasko (who primarily uses this info to treat autistic children). Use this thread to discuss test results, share information and articles, and to talk about the dietary and supplementation strategies.

If you are interested in reading up on this topic here are some great links over on this thread:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1866-Methylation-issues-and-the-Leaky-Gut&highlight=methylation](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1866-Methylation-issues-and-the-Leaky-Gut&highlight=methylation)

### freshveggies — 2012-07-24T23:44:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40856>

HI, I have been searching for 6 years as to why my health has been going down hill and I think it is pointing to my methylation defects expressing themself. I have run the 23 and me panel and a methylation panel from Health Diagnositics. Here are my methylation snips from 23 and me:

ACE--Not available

CBS A360 -/-

CBS C699T +/- (CBS generates sulfur breakdown products (sulfite and sulfate, which stimulate the stress/cortisol “fight or flight” response)

COMT H62H -/-

Comt V158M -/-

COMT L136L

COMT-61 -/-

MOA A +/+

MTHFR C677T +/-

MTHFR A1298C -/-

MTHFR 03 P39P -/-

MTR A2756G -/-

MTRR H595Y -/-

Mtrr K350A -/-

MTRR R415T -/-

MTRR S257T N/A

MTRR A919G -/+

MTRR-11 A664A +/-

NOS 3 N/A

SOUX S370S N/A

SOUX A628G n/a

VDR BSM TAQ -/-

VDR Fok I N/A

ACAT1-02 -/-

AHCY-01 -/-

AHCY-02 -/-

AHCY-19 -/-

BHMT-01 n/a

BHMT -02 -/-

BHMT -04 -/-

BHMT-08 -/+

SHMT C1420T -/-

The ones that are +/+ are Homozygous and there is 2 copies of the mutation. One from each parent.

\-/+ is Heterozygous one copy of the mutation

\-/- no mutation

Here is my Health Diagnostics test:

I did Health Diagnostics test: March 2012

Gutathione oxidized .51 range .16-.50

Glutathione redued 3.3 range 3.8-5.5

s-adenosylmethionine 237 range 221-256

s-adensoylhomocysteiine 56.8 range 38-49

folic acid derviates

5-CH3-THF 15.0 range 8.4-72.6

10-Formyl-THF 4.3 range 1.5-8.2

5-Formyl-THF 4.90 range 1.2-11.70

THF 1.15 range 06-6.80

Folic Acid 18.5 range 8.9-24.6

Folic Acid (WB) 17-3 range 9.0-.35.5

Folic Aci active RBC 417 range 400-1500

Since I have a CBS and BHMT defect, it is recommended that I start with working on them. My diet is suppose to be low sulfur and I will be adding in TMG, acetyl L-Carnitine, Yucca, gaba.

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-07-25T03:07:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40897>

From the anecdotal evidence that I have read only having 1 copy of CBS is not too too bad. That said there is some stuff below on your CBS issue. I like your idea of the sulfur dip strip testing and monitoring to see how your diet impacts those levels. I think that will help you know if you need to focus on a low sulfur diet or not.

I too have two MTRRs, but I only share one with you - MTRR-11. From what I have read 415 is the tough one, so we both dodged the bullet on that one! But MTRR means you do not recycle B12 very well. Since you are COMT-/- you can go with Mb12 or a mix of methly, hydroxy and andenoyl. Some people recommend working up to 3-8 mg/day.

What have you been using for B12 and how much?

MTHFR C677T one means you will not use folic acid very well, and need to take folinic & mthf folate (Yasko says 200mg of each a day, others have used more and seen benefit).

CBS C699T. This one means you will end up with high ammonia and high taurine if you eat "too much" protein. That is where the urine amino acid test might come in handy.

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-07-25T03:18:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40901>

Oh I also read that alpha ketoglutarate will help lower the ammonia levels. Personally I have not tried this one.

From: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha-Ketoglutaric\_acid](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha-Ketoglutaric_acid)

"...studies that show excess ammonia in the body can combine with alpha-ketoglutarate, reducing problems associated with ammonia toxicity"

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-07-25T03:52:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40906>

Just a little bit more....

COMT-- = needs lots of methyl groups. So methyl B12 is a very good thing. SAMe and DMG would be two others to consider adding.

BHMT 8 +- is not too severe, but DMG would probably be a good supp to consider adding if there are any attention/focus issues (and only after you have a good amount of B12 in place first).

MAO ++. That is the enzyme that breaks down serotonin. Can means either high serotonin (aggressive, sometimes short tempered or "prickly") or serotonin swings (moody, with some of those moods aggressive/prickly). Support for this is frequent dosing of very low dose 5htp and/or st johns wort. Some people recommend trying 1/2 cap of a 50mg 5htp capsules 4x a day. I know it sounds counterintuitive to take 5htp for high serotonin (5htp turns into serotonin), but it helps regulate the body's biofeedback (high serotonin turns off serotonin production, which causes swings).

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-07-25T04:39:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40910>

Supposedly I'm supposed to go easy with the methly donors like TMG because I am COMT+/+. I have not tried the others. What's curious to me is that even though I have CBS, I'm supposed to start NAC and ALA (high in sulfur) to boost my glutathoine levels. We shall see - I have not added those in yet. Next week. I'm debating between yucca and alpha ketoglutarate for the ammonia issue. But will probably hold off few weeks before adding something more.

But we are all different, so listen to your body and see how it responds to the supplements.

This is the vitamin I am using for the MTFR issues: [http://www.douglaslabs.com/pdf/pds/200571.pdf](http://http://www.douglaslabs.com/pdf/pds/200571.pdf) which supposedly has a balance of the Bs and the co-factors needed. In addition, I'm also taking a little folate and a trans-dermal formula with 5-MTHF, mB12, and MSM. I think we are trying to drive all the forms of folate up because I'm just low across the board.

Missing from 23andMe are Yasko's rsids for NOS, SUOX, and ACE. And I mis-spoke earlier I do have that pesky MTRR R415T. Darn. Per above - just taking Bs and co-factors to get the methlyation cycle running. Also will be adding NAC and ALA to try and boost the glutathoine.

Before starting the supplements, my methlyation panel was as follows:

**Methylation Panel Results (reference range):**

Glutathione (oxidised) 0.43 umol/L (0.16-0.50)

Glutathoine (reduced) 2.5 umol/L (3.8 - 5.5)

s-Adenosylmethoinine (RBC) 235 umol/L (221-256)

s-AdenosylHomocystine (RBC) 48.8 umol/L (38.0-49.0)

5-CH3-THF 8.8 nmol/L (8.4-72.6)

10-Formyl-THF 3.7 nmol/L (1.5-8.2)

5-Formyl-THF 4.20 nmol/L (1.20-11.70)

THF 0.44 nmol/L (0.60 - 6.80)

Adenosine 17.3 10^3 M (16.8-21.4)

Yikes - LOTS to work on in this space but at least I have some SAMe. I plan to take the supplements for 2 more months before retesting. The question in my mind is should I do any other tests in the interim. I also wish I knew how important the ammonia over-production thing is. I do suffer from a lot of brain fog, but that could be for so many reasons.

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-07-25T05:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-40923>

Ok - one last note in case it helps anyone out there. When I first started trying to take these Bs my body flipped out. I was dizzy, had a giant headache, felt like I was on the verge of puking all the time. Oh and I got rashes all over my body, followed by strange itchy bumps. They don't call me the rash queen for nothing. So I had to back off and slowly, slowly, slowly build up the amounts. It has been about a month and I'm almost up to a daily dose.

Still get the occasional rash. Still feel a little over stimulated during the day. Feel way too jittery at night which has sleep in the toilet.

When I try to add in the NAC I swell up. Hands, feet and lower legs are puffy. Yuck.

Hoping that this will decrease over the next few weeks. If not I will have to consider if things need tweaking.

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-07-29T18:38:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-41804>

On trying to combat the high protein diet with Yucca (per the yasko camp), I did some googling last night. Most of the publications are focused on how this is used for controlling ammonia in large scale farms (pig, chicken) but what I read made me stop and think...

[http://livestocklibrary.com.au/handle/1234/19910](http://http://livestocklibrary.com.au/handle/1234/19910)

It seems that the molecules that do the "ammonia reduction" are the saponins. To which I will have to say no thank you. Saponins are known for causing leaky gut. Cordain has quite a bit of approachable info on this. And it you google saponins and leaky gut you will find a ton of info.

There has to be an another approach out there. Back to the books.

from the middle of post #257

_ammonia is high, so is taurine. Meaning, his CBS+ is actually a factor, now that he is digesting protein (Tanya & Shannon, thought this might be relevant for you). He went from red low on ammonia last test to halfway up yellow high this time, that's a huge swing in 4 months. Yasko's solution is a low protein diet. Not going there, so trying some other stuff. I've added alpha ketoglutarate back in - it's great at eating ammonia, although it can produce glutamates in the process. Oddly, for us, at the moment, that would be fine. I'm increasing **curcumin - that's what Yasko uses to push cysteine towards producing glutathione, rather than taurine**.

### SeaHorse — 2012-08-05T02:41:52+0300

I spent a few hours reading these sites Devo...they were very helpful and I'm starting to piece together some of the food intolerances and the methylation issues. I threw out all my supplements because of the additives and now I wish I had my b12 with intrinsic factor bottle back. I'll just have to start again slowly and see how it goes.

I hadn't realized that the methyl groups were used to inactivate histamine....that's a big puzzle piece right there.

The dopamine/serotonin pathway is particularly important for me...my mother had Parkinsons and depression and I've certainly had long periods of depression as well. My neurotransmitter test came back with normal serotonin and high dopamine...I don't feel the effects of this however...is it the same issue as with hormones...you may test with normal levels but if you don't have the proper receptors and/or methylators you don't experience the benefits? And then I was also reading that high dopamine leads to stress as well as low states...

thanks again for pointing me in the right direction for research!

### Szara.loring@gmail.com — 2012-08-09T14:25:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-43852>

I'm having trouble with the sulfur containing supplements like NAC. I was told that to really understand if I need to restrict protein, I should get the NOS and SUOX genes sequenced.

CBS is the beginning of the transsulfuration pathway and SUOX is the end. If you have an upregulation (CBS) at the beginning and you have a change that causes a slow down at the end (SUOX) the intermediates will collect and your body will be over burdened. The other thing you may be interested in is how well your body can turn excess ammonia into urea. If you have a SNP change in NOS it will impact how well the body detoxifies ammonia to urea.

My MD is not a fan of the sulfur/sulfite or ammonia test strips. He feels that first we need to understand all the key genes and then perhaps look at a Urine amino acid test before making dietary adjustments. Until we know I'm to leave the diet as is and avoid sulfur containing supplements. I don't eat a lot of veggies because my body does not like them.

I'm trying a little experiment where I have protien and veg at breakfast and dinner and then I have something like bulletproof tea or a coconut keto shake as a snack in the early afternoon. I'm thinking about trying a 3:1 ketogenic diet. Right now now I'm probably at 2:1. (grams fat: grams protein + grams carbs).

### Astrid — 2012-08-13T09:48:54+0300

I found the tekst on high tryptophane foods it was on The heartfixer site.

How best to address the MAO A R297R abnormality is not clear to me.\* As serotonin metabolism is adversely affected, individuals with the R297R defect should avoid large doses of high tryptophan foods (see appendix).\* High doses of St. Johnâ€™s Wort, often taken to address depression, could lead to mood swings as serotonin levels fluctuate.\* Dr. Yasko recommends frequent dosing in small amounts of St. Johnâ€™s Wort, 5HTP (a tryptophan metabolite), and the Mood S RNA formula if serotonin support is needed.\* If serotonin production is impaired on the basis of BH4 deficiency secondary to a Methyl Cycle abnormality, as the abnormality itself is addressed, BH4 levels should stabilize, hopefully normalizing serotonin production.\* \*

Bud I think there is some conflicting information here if you campare it to onther information. Yakso says if you are comt and vdr taq positive like I'm for both +/- it is acting like comt ++ then you should ovoid high thyrocine foods an go for The high trypthophane foods.

What they both don't mention is that when you eat foods with tryptophane The tryptophane stays inactive if you don't combine it with carbohydrates. It are the carbohydrates that are The switch to activafe The tryptophane or the thyrocine. So in my opinion it are not the kind of proteÃ¯ns you eat that make The differance.

Astrid

Good article. I just learned something about a close friend of mine who suffers from schizophrenia. She "may" have a copper imbalance. The symptoms fit her to a tee. From this article to another about copper toxicity I learned this: " There exists a high copper personality. Positive traits include a warm, caring, sensitive, emotional nature, often with artistic orientation and a child-like quality. Often high-copper people are young-looking. Many traditionally feminine traits are associated with copper such as softness, gentleness and intuitiveness. This may relate to the qualities of metallic copper, which include softness, malleability and an excellent conductor of electricity.

When the personality is not fully integrated or the copper becomes too high, negative traits show up. These include spaciness, racing thoughts, living in a dream world and naivetÃ©. Other qualities include childishness, excessive emotions, sentimentality, a tendency to depression, fearfulness, hidden anger and resentments, phobias, psychosis and violence. Artists, inventors and other high-copper types often "live on the edge", in part due to their high copper level." The article is here: [http://drlwilson.com/articles/copper\_toxicity\_syndrome.htm](http://http://drlwilson.com/articles/copper_toxicity_syndrome.htm)

This article might also explain why it's harder for some smokers to quit.

When the bible reads, we will never know God from start to finish, it tells me how much we don't know and that we will be learning forever. How complicated "He" made us. I, highly, doubt we are going to be the one's to fix ourselves completely.

### Sujoe123 — 2017-03-31T05:23:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimizing-methlyation.2658/post-211444>

Maybe I am missing something but I am homozygous for CBS 699. I waste B vitamins and my ammonia levels are high. Some doctors believe in limiting B6, but I disagree. I might not know what I'm talking about but there are extremely many other reactions in the body that require B6 and if you've got one reaction using it up then the others are left short-changed. Why not just tweak all the supplements up to as fast as your body needs them. I do the sulfite strips and I'm fine as long as I keep my molybdenum intake up. I use p5p at 50 mg per day in 3 divided doses ( I am homozygous in folate path too...so MF and folinic and M B12) and nibbles of niacin 3 times per day. I supplement with creatine, NAC, glutamine, glycine and P. serine for this pathway....my hair does not fall out by the hundreds but insomnia is horrible due to MAO and CBS snips.

# Thread: Need a place to vent
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-07-25T08:22:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-40951>

So I've been working really hard on my health this year and feel like I'm getting nowhere. I lost about 25 pounds with hcg earlier in the year. Have kept it off. Have cut out sugar, grains, most dairy, doing Leptin Rx. Working in Ct but it is hard!

I was on the Nuvaring and went off of it to try to get a good hormone picture. Got TOM once and haven't since. Doc gave me progesterone to bring it on but so far no luck. Some initial labs showed positive ANA, thyroid in the low end of the range, high cholesterol. Will be going back soon to run more labs but was hoping for period first.

Anyways, I am waking up everyday feeling worse. I have joint pain that been getting worse and am losing hair. Feeling super despondent about t all. I have had really awful luck with doctors in the past and I am so scared that they are going to put me on drugs that will just make me worse. I already got a prescription for statins that I trashed.

I just thought that, after all this work, I would be doing better but I'm not. I am not sure who to turn to or where to go for help. I'm only 42 and I have two little boys. I don't want to wake up everyday exhausted, in pain and sad.

### ealachan — 2012-07-25T12:35:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-40954>

I'm sorry you're feeling so rough...\*hugs\*

If your doctor is not treating your pretty obvious thyroid issues, is it maybe time for a new doc? Or are you able to ask for a referral to a specialist, possibly? You shouldn't have to live life feeling this way.

Hi Sem! You are right, it should not be that difficult to find a good doctor but it is. We take care of our families, work and then try to learn so we could improve our health. Don't give up, be a pest if needs to be! I am sure most of the doctors thought of me that I was a crazy lady that was asking for all kinds of labs and wanted copies too!! It took me seeing 3 doctors (some multiple times) to get the labs I needed (a sort of). You need to look for another doctor.

### vkiernan — 2012-07-25T14:09:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-40961>

Sem, Sounds like you need more testing and new doc. Good things will happen but you gotta know what to work on or you are flying blind. You may have to pay out of pocket but in the end it will be worth it. You don't have to take the drugs they put you on. It is always in your hand. If you can't find a good doc, keep looking. A lot of us are going on 3 or more in 1 year.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-25T14:28:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-40964>

attittude........it is critical.

No, not yet. I still have to sort all that out. I know the people I have now will just put me on synthroid. I'm gathering that's not necessarily the best option? I have been so messed up by bad meds that, frankly, I'd rather go without than go take the chance. I'm proceeding with caution.

### Lyndra — 2012-07-25T18:47:37+0300

### janagram — 2012-07-25T18:59:13+0300

### DarleenMB — 2012-07-25T19:00:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-41027>

> **Sem;41212 wrote:** No, not yet. I still have to sort all that out. I know the people I have now will just put me on synthroid. I'm gathering that's not necessarily the best option? I have been so messed up by bad meds that, frankly, I'd rather go without than go take the chance. I'm proceeding with caution.

they can only do that with your permission. Tell them NO synthroid. If you need to go on thyroid meds tell them you will on take the real thing. Remember your doc is not the 'boss' of you. You are PAYING HIM/HER to be your partner in your health care. You have the right to make your voice heard.

Took me a very long time to learn that. And a lot of lost body parts.

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-25T19:17:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-41033>

> **Sem;41163 wrote:** So I've been working really hard on my health this year and feel like I'm getting nowhere. I lost about 25 pounds with hcg earlier in the year. Have kept it off. Have cut out sugar, grains, most dairy, doing Leptin Rx. Working in Ct but it is hard!
>
>
>
> I was on the Nuvaring and went off of it to try to get a good hormone picture. Got TOM once and haven't since. Doc gave me progesterone to bring it on but so far no luck. Some initial labs showed positive ANA, thyroid in the low end of the range, high cholesterol. Will be going back soon to run more labs but was hoping for period first.
>
>
>
> Anyways, I am waking up everyday feeling worse. I have joint pain that been getting worse and am losing hair. Feeling super despondent about t all. I have had really awful luck with doctors in the past and I am so scared that they are going to put me on drugs that will just make me worse. I already got a prescription for statins that I trashed.
>
>
>
> I just thought that, after all this work, I would be doing better but I'm not. I am not sure who to turn to or where to go for help. I'm only 42 and I have two little boys. I don't want to wake up everyday exhausted, in pain and sad.

I'm so sorry for your pain, it can be so frustrating. I've been fighting Docs for many years now and it is not easy. So many answers here in this forum, very giving people. If you don't have a science or medical background it can be overwhelming at times. I believe one of the secrets of success in life is persistence..just don't give up!!

I wish I had answers for you. One thought I had, if you don't mind posting what area of the country you are in, someone may know good Docs in your area. Hang in there, I second Dr. K....attitude is key.

### colleencoble — 2012-07-25T21:13:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-41057>

Darlene is very right. Just go in with a smile and tell the doc firmly that you want Armour, or at the very least T3 added to a dose of synthroid. But you really should get your reverse T3 checked first since you should be on straight T3 if that's out of whack. Your cholesterol will come down, you will feel great. Depression is often caused by low thyroid too. Fixing that fixes a lot of things.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-26T00:52:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-a-place-to-vent.2664/post-41103>

Sem, I'm really sorry this is bringing you down. I agree with others that your medical support isn't good and you need good support. This is obvious, but in case you haven't... maybe a consult with Dr K? Since you've got labs, he can be really helpful in pointing you in the right direction, both in terms of what you need from a doc and what you can get doing asap yourself. I totally know the angst of trying to find a doc who gets it (there are a total of 2 functional medicine docs in this country!) but I found the consult so empowering... I am full steam ahead on getting my questions answered and finding someone who can support me in the right way (even if I have to help shape that!). My children are a main reason I choose to fight hard instead of giving up! Hang in there.

# Thread: Vacation a la Kruse
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation-a-la-kruse.2667/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2012-07-25T15:03:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation-a-la-kruse.2667/post-40973>

I had a consult with Jack the day before I left for a week vacation on Cape Cod. I had already envisioned a healthy week of eating right , exercising and resting. After my consult i was further inspired. so i went to the sea and ate seafood, all of it , almost every meal. For me that's only two meals a day. Breakfast was cooked at our cottage; eggs , leftover fish from the night before, lacto fermented veg. Dinner was mostly eaten out. Cape Cod is one place where you can go out to dinner and eat not only clean paleo, but epi-paleo. So I enjoyed much fruta de mer. I got lots of sun, used no sunblock, didn't burn. I got dhea cream to bring with me and started using that daily. The ocean temps were in the low sixties which didn't make anyone happy but me. So I had at least 2 CT sessions a day, plus cold shower at night. I biked, kayaked, swam, walked on the beach, rested on the beach. Went to bed shortly after sunset and woke up with the sun.

During a guided Kayak tour we paddled past eroded dunes. In the exposed face of the dune we could see two interesting things.There was a dark horizontal line in the sand that was the original forest floor before the colonists came and cut down all the trees which allowed the blowing sand to bury the forest floor. The other cool thing was an exposed native american shell midden that was many feet thick and 40 -50 ft long. This was where they came to eat shellfish for hundreds if not thousands of years. This was very striking given Jack's recent blogs on human coastal dwelling. Our affinity with the ocean is no coincidence. It is our source.

Well I am done with vacation and I feel really good. I am kind of sad that it seems impossible to live the way we should.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-25T15:58:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation-a-la-kruse.2667/post-40980>

@Dali nice post and I agree......we need to go against the grain to get what we all need.

THANKS

### CarolMorris — 2012-07-25T20:43:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vacation-a-la-kruse.2667/post-41050>

Dali, really enjoyed your post. I'm Boston born and my grandmother had a little house on the Cape. My dad was transferred back and forth from East Coast to West Coast twice. Redondo Beach and Huntington Beach in the West and Boston and NJ in the East.

Did you have scrod and fried clams??

I dod not have scrod or fried clams. I did have clams, oysters, mussels raw and steamed, lobster, sole, bigeye tuna, sea bass, cod, shrimp, bluefish pate, swordfish, squid scallops. I was only there for a week.

# Thread: You're only as healthy as your cell membrane
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-07-25T19:36:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/post-41040>

Came across this today while listening to an interview of Pam Killeen, who works in addiction therapy.

It struck me as the kind of thing Jack might say.

Here's the interview:

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio/2012/06/13/addiction-the-hidden-epidemic-with-pam-killeen](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio/2012/06/13/addiction-the-hidden-epidemic-with-pam-killeen)

It was really quite interesting. Among other things, she seemed to be saying that the damage vegetarianism does starts at the cell membrane.

I was also interested to hear what she said about copper. In her work, Pam tries to balance neurotransmitters, like Julia Ross and others do, but she says she's not found a person witha addiction problems who hasn't also got some degree of copper toxicity.

This is a new one to me, but here's what she says. The level of copper we're exposed to is supra-normal, because we get it through the water supply from copper piping (obviously), from copper sulfate (which is often added as a flocculant by water companies), and, she says -- and this, too, is quite new to me -- because of the fluoridation of water supplies that happens in some jurisdictions, since fluoride ions will grab hold of copper. So what she says is that if levels of zinc are low -- as they are for people on poor diets -- that copper becomes a problem.

It's a fun interview. As well as the what I used for the subject line of this, she had some pretty quotable lines. She said she calls the "four food groups" the "four food lobbies".

And this on talk therapy: "You can't talk someone out of a biochemical imbalance".

### KiwiLauren — 2012-07-26T01:46:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/post-41114>

I listened to that podcast a while back, too. I have also heard Chris Kresser and others talk about copper toxicity and how it can cause serious neurological problems, including psychosis. Another reason to eat epi-paleo!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-07-26T02:30:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/post-41121>

I've decided to give up all my supplements in favour of epi-paleo (don't actually KNOW the specifics as I haven't listened to the webinar) however my take on it is much increased seafood with bits of pastured beef and pastured bacon (haha!), fermented veg, fresh veg, lesser amounts of butter and coconut oil (but still use them in cooking) (As he said that too much saturated fat will blunt the assimilation of DHA in the brain...I FEEL WEIRD FOR SAYING THIS B/C IT SOUNDS DANGEROUSLY CLOSE TO CW, HOWEVER I KNOW IT'S NOT THE SAME AND THE EXPLANATION IS NOT CW). Pro-biotic and some inulin from benefibre. This should take care of everything.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-07-26T02:31:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/post-41122>

I'm hoping that my magnesium and zinc intake will be sufficient from my seafood. I have lots of variation including shell fish as well.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-26T02:50:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/post-41124>

In the webinar we talked at length about the science behind the Epi-paleo Rx.......and the key NT involved. BG 5 tells you all about Copper.

### Michael — 2012-07-26T08:01:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/post-41165>

Could you get too much in the modern world, given that you get it from sources that weren't there in the past, such as municipal water?

### IceBarbie — 2012-07-26T21:03:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/youre-only-as-healthy-as-your-cell-membrane.2676/post-41301>

My former chiropractor who is now practicing Homeopathy, told me how she became copper toxic due to a plumbing issue in her old home. It was awhile back so I'm fuzzy on what happened exactly but it had something to do with something in her well and her copper pipes in combination. So she was taking Zinc to balance the effects of the copper toxicity. I do remember she said it had caused her to gain weight or not be able to lose weight too until she figured it out.

How to buy and store avocado.

How to recognize ripe avocado. Ripen just right, not too much not to little.

### nonchalant — 2012-07-26T01:13:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado.2685/post-41107>

I get the green ones, and toss them all into the crisper of the fridge. When I want one, I pull it out a day or two ahead of time. Its skin turns dark by that time, and usually it is great inside.

Be sure the ones you buy have a tight skin. If they are loose, the insides will be brown.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-07-26T01:35:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado.2685/post-41111>

once its ripe this is how I eat the fruit.....cut it in half and remove the pit and fill the pit hole with some balsamic vinegar.....and get a spoon and go to town on it. Great meal replacement on the road.

### PrimalPam — 2012-07-26T02:00:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado.2685/post-41115>

One of my favorite dinners is something I call my "Fat Salad". Huge bowl of greens (kale, spinach, etc...after last night's webinar, I'll add sea veggies), 1 whole avocado, crabmeat or shrimp, and drizzled with olive oil or CO and balsamic vinegar. Yum.

### ealachan — 2012-07-26T02:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado.2685/post-41123>

> **JanSz;41322 wrote:** Need pointers.
>
>
>
> How to buy and store avocado.
>
> How to recognize ripe avocado. Ripen just right, not too much not to little.
>
>
>
> Costco sells them, I have mixed luck.
>
>
>
> ,,

how to tell they're ripe: go for darker green ones that yield SLIGHTLY to gentle pressure. If they're squishy, they're old and no good. You can also sometimes tell by looking at the narrow end of the fruit - if it has a bit of the stem left, you can try to flick it off with the tip of your thumb. If the stem bit flicks off easily and the spot underneath it is greyish green, it's ripe. If the stem doesn't flick off at all or if the spot is a bright light green beneath the stem, it's likely under-ripe. If it's brown or dark grey under the stem, it's likely old and yucky.

If you buy them under-ripe (ie: hard), you can put them in a paper bag and close it tightly - this will usually ripen them within a day or so. If you cut one open and it's got lots of dark grey streaks, it's not going to be good. Because avocados are primarily fat, they go rancid when they go bad. Yuck!

### BJK77 — 2012-07-26T03:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado.2685/post-41135>

> **ealachan;41339 wrote:** You can also sometimes tell by looking at the narrow end of the fruit - if it has a bit of the stem left, you can try to flick it off with the tip of your thumb. If the stem bit flicks off easily and the spot underneath it is greyish green, it's ripe. If the stem doesn't flick off at all or if the spot is a bright light green beneath the stem, it's likely under-ripe. If it's brown or dark grey under the stem, it's likely old and yucky.
>
>
>
> If you buy them under-ripe (ie: hard), you can put them in a paper bag and close it tightly - this will usually ripen them within a day or so.

+1 on all this. I just learned about the tip to flick the end off and check for a good, green color and have been doing this for the last few batches I bought with great success. I also like to get them more under-ripe than over and put them in a brown paper bag. It beats buying rotten, rancid ones.

I had an avocado tonight with dinner. Smashed it up and topped it with sauerkraut with shrimp and mushrooms on the side. Sounds gross, but oh so yummy and totally nutrient dense meal. I could feel my gut healing as I ate it

### Sandra — 2014-01-25T07:48:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/avocado.2685/post-106995>

Avocados freeze really well too. Mash them up and freeze them in ice cube trays or just blobs on a plate. Just thaw them out before you use them. Great in smoothies or just blobbed onto salads or as spreads.
